title: Sertraline as a rare cause of interstitial lung disease
case_summary:
A 36-year-old Caucasian man was admitted to a psychiatric ward for severe depression and alcohol misuse. He had abstained from alcohol for 2 weeks prior, was a non-smoker with no recreational drug use, and had no past medical history. Seven weeks before admission, he had started sertraline 100 mg daily; he took no other regular medications. Twelve days into his admission, he developed a dry cough, dyspnea, and left-sided pleuritic chest pain. He was hypoxic (SpO? 85% on room air, falling to 76% on exertion), tachycardic (106 bpm), and febrile (38.1  degreesC); blood pressure and respiratory rate were normal. Auscultation revealed reduced air entry bilaterally with bibasal crepitations. 

An urgent chest radiograph showed diffuse bilateral air-space opacification. Intravenous antibiotics were started, but there was no clinical improvement over 48 hours. Screening for infection--including blood cultures, sputum culture, urinary Legionella and pneumococcal antigens, and respiratory PCR te

title: Giant coronary fistula aneurysm presenting as a progressing left-sided asymptomatic mediastinal mass with systolic dominant Doppler flow: a case report
final_diagnosis: giant coronary artery aneurysm associated with coronary fistula
case_summary: A 67-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of an abnormal mediastinal shadow discovered on a routine chest radiograph. Seven years prior, her radiograph was normal, but over the past four years serial films demonstrated progressive protrusion of the left third aortic arch. A CT scan performed two years ago revealed a 4-cm mass in the left mediastinum, although it was not reported at the time. She denied chest pain, dyspnea, or other cardiovascular symptoms and had no relevant medical history. On current contrast-enhanced CT, the mass measured approximately 5 cm abutting the left pulmonary artery and was initially considered to represent lymphoma, neurogenic tumour, or germ cell tumour. PET-CT showed no uptake of 18F-FDG. Arterial-phase images demonstrated internal contrast enhancement equivalent to that of a blood vessel and areas of thrombus. The patient was referred to cardiology for further assessment.

title: Unusual presentation of Loeys-Dietz syndrome: A case report of clinical findings and treatment challenges
case_summary: A 35-year-old Hispanic woman presented with 48 hours of severe hypogastric abdominal pain and lower GI bleeding characterized by diarrhea with clots and hematochezia unrelieved by over-the-counter medications. Her history included childhood episodes of lower GI bleeding that resolved spontaneously, hypothyroidism, and polycystic ovary syndrome treated with hormonal contraceptives for five months. On examination, she was tachycardic (112 bpm), normotensive, and 90% on room air; she had diffuse abdominal tenderness and a clear systolic murmur. Laboratory tests revealed hypoalbuminemia (3.1 mg/dL) and an elevated D-dimer (10 000 ?g/mL) with a normal thrombophilia and autoimmune workup. Colonoscopy demonstrated tortuous hemorrhoidal veins and dilated vessels in the colon and terminal ileum, raising suspicion for mesenteric venous thrombosis versus ischemia. Contrast-enhanced thoracoabdominal CT showed segmental pulmonary emboli with bilateral infarctions, portal vein atresia, a shunt from

title: Unusual Presentation of Recurrent Early Stage Endometrial Carcinoma 28 Years after Primary Surgery
case_summary:
A 72-year-old woman was referred after detection of a rectal mass on routine endoscopic surveillance. She denied abdominal pain or changes in bowel habits. Her past medical history was remarkable for:
? 1985: hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy for early-stage, low-grade endometrioid endometrial carcinoma (FIGO Ib) treated with intravaginal brachytherapy.  
? 1989: left thyroid lobectomy for papillary carcinoma followed by I-131 therapy.  
? 2001: endoscopic resection of bladder papillary carcinoma.  
? 2003: right hemicolectomy for pT3 N1 colonic adenocarcinoma followed by adjuvant chemotherapy.  

Genetic testing revealed microsatellite instability due to a heterozygous somatic MLH1 mutation. In 2013, colonoscopy showed a right-sided extrinsic compression of the rectal wall with intact mucosa. Pelvic MRI demonstrated a 4.7 ? 3.5 cm intramural mass located 6.5 cm from the anocutaneous plane and 3 cm from the anorectal junction, with a nearby 11 mm focus in perivisceral fa

title: Vascular Ehlers?Danlos syndrome: A null COL3A1 variant found in a patient with loin pain without marked cutaneous features (case report)
case_summary:
A 27-year-old man presented with acute-onset severe left-sided loin pain for 1 day. He had a similar episode 2.5 years earlier treated as pyelonephritis and a transient bilateral lower-limb weakness 1 year ago with unrevealing neuroimaging; both had since resolved. He also recalled prior groin and lower-limb pain. He denied fever, urinary or bowel symptoms, trauma, easy bruising, smoking, and substance use. His paternal aunt died of suspected acute coronary syndrome in her 50s.

On examination, his blood pressure was normal at presentation but rose to 159/74 mmHg over 3 days. He had no syndromic facies, disproportionate limb proportions, joint laxity, or signs of vasculitis or rheumatologic disease. The skin of his posterior calves appeared slightly lax, thin, and translucent; other areas were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed a creatinine of 131 ?mol/L; complete blood count, coagulation profile, liver function tests, renal function tests, and rheumatologic investigations were o

title: Three-Layered Optic Disc Hemorrhages in Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension
case_summary:
A 42-year-old obese woman presented with after-images in her left eye after bright light exposure and transient blurred vision with position changes. She denied regular headaches, pulsatile tinnitus, or any sudden?onset severe headache. 

Visual acuity was 20/20 in both eyes. Humphrey 24-2 SITA-Fast visual fields showed nonspecific depressed points in the right eye (mean deviation [MD] -6.18 dB) and an enlarged blind spot in the left eye (MD -4.84 dB). 

Dilated fundus examination revealed moderate bilateral optic disc edema and preretinal, intraretinal, and subretinal hemorrhages around the left optic disc. Blood pressure was 140/100 mm Hg, and hemoglobin was normal. 

Magnetic resonance imaging and magnetic resonance venography of the head demonstrated signs of raised intracranial pressure without evidence of a mass lesion or venous sinus thrombosis. A lumbar puncture showed an opening pressure of 32 cm of water with normal cerebrospinal fluid contents.

title: Adenosquamous Carcinoma of the Pancreas
case_summary: A 60-year-old woman presented with a 4-month history of girdle-type epigastric and left hypochondrial pain radiating to the left lumbar region, associated with anorexia and an 8-kg weight loss. She had no significant medical history, but smoked (30 pack-years). Her father had recently been treated for pancreatic adenocarcinoma. On examination she appeared fatigued and depressed; abdominal examination revealed no masses, hepatomegaly, ascites, or lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies showed anicteric cholestasis (2? upper limit of normal) and a C-reactive protein level of 34 mg/L (normal <5 mg/L). Tumor markers were within normal limits (CEA 2.7 ng/mL; CA 19-9 32 IU/L). A CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis demonstrated extensive involvement of the pancreatic tail with locoregional extension to the posterior wall of the stomach and a subcentimeter lesion in liver segment VI. Endoscopic ultrasound revealed a 5.3?4 cm, well-defined, hypoechoic homogeneous lesion at the junction of the

title: Carotid Cavernous Sinus Fistula with Contralateral Feed Not Diagnosed by Virtual Assessment or by Non-Invasive Vascular Imaging
case_summary:
A 67-year-old woman presented with a 1-year history of progressive right proptosis, conjunctival injection, and left frontal headache. During the COVID-19 pandemic, her condition was initially diagnosed via virtual visits as thyroid-associated orbitopathy, although she was biochemically euthyroid. She had no history of smoking, hypertension, or head trauma; her ocular history included mild hyperopia treated with bilateral laser iridotomies.

Over several months, she developed right-eye vision loss, persistent redness, and diplopia on eccentric gaze. She reported hearing an intracranial bruit. Best-corrected visual acuities were 20/140 (right) and 20/40 (left). She could not identify any Ishihara plates with the right eye but read all with the left. Pupils were equal, with a right 2/4 relative afferent pupillary defect. There was no eyelid retraction. Examination revealed 2 mm of right-sided proptosis, a 25 percent reduction in right abduction, and prominent 360 degrees dilated episcler

title: A Rare Case of Gastric Variceal Hemorrhage Secondary to Infiltrative B-Cell Lymphoma
case_summary:
A 73-year-old man with hyperlipidemia, hypertension, and obstructive sleep apnea presented with 3 days of coffee-ground emesis and melena, accompanied by dizziness, weakness, palpitations, and fatigue. In the emergency department, he was hypotensive and tachycardic; his hemoglobin was 3.4 g/dL and INR 1.29. He received aggressive fluid resuscitation and multiple units of packed red blood cells, with stabilization of vital signs.

An emergent esophagogastroduodenoscopy revealed isolated bleeding gastric fundal varices, which were treated with argon plasma coagulation. He was started on intravenous protonix and octreotide drips and transferred for evaluation of presumed cirrhosis and possible transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt placement. He denied any history of liver disease, injection drug use, or current alcohol use, though he recalled one episode of hematemesis 40 years earlier requiring transfusion. He also reported a 38-lb weight loss over the prior 6 months.

Repeat end

title: Acute Testicular Ischemia following Endovascular Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm Repair Identified in the Emergency Department
case_summary:
A 72-year-old man with a history of abdominal aortic aneurysm presented to the emergency department two days after endovascular aneurysm repair with a stent graft. He reported persistent right flank, right lower abdominal, and right testicular pain since surgery. He was discharged less than 24 hours earlier on a bowel regimen and pain medications. Despite regular bowel movements and analgesia, the pain continued. He denied fever, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting, dysuria, hematuria, or diarrhea.

On examination, vital signs were normal. The right testicle was exquisitely tender on palpation, in normal vertical alignment, and had an intact cremasteric reflex bilaterally; testicular elevation did not relieve pain. The abdomen was mildly distended with diffuse tenderness worse on the right, without peritoneal signs or costovertebral angle tenderness. Femoral and dorsalis pedis pulses were present and symmetric.

Scrotal ultrasonography showed a mildly enlarged rig

title: Spontaneously disappearing right atrial mass in a preterm infant: a case report
case_summary:
A male infant was born at 25 weeks and 5 days of gestation by spontaneous vaginal delivery, weighing 1000 g. Maternal chorioamnionitis was clinically suspected; the mother received intravenous antibiotics, and placental histopathology later confirmed maternal and fetal amniotic fluid infection without thrombi.

Apgar scores were 9 and 9. The neonate was managed initially with nasal continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP), but at 3 hours of age was intubated for increasing FiO2, received surfactant, and was extubated at 17 hours of age back to CPAP with FiO2 of 0.21. Chest radiography at 3.5 hours showed respiratory distress syndrome; a repeat film at 27 hours showed right upper lobe collapse/consolidation.

Admission laboratory studies at 21 hours of age included C-reactive protein (CRP) 26 mg/L, white cell count 9.5?10^9/L, platelets 153?10^9/L, and negative blood cultures; surface swabs grew Group B Streptococcus. CRP fell to 10 mg/L at 66 hours. A central venous line was placed a

title: Two cases of reversible male infertility due to congenital adrenal hyperplasia combined with testicular adrenal rest tumor
case_summary: A 41-year-old man was evaluated for 2 years of infertility. His height was 1.58 m, weight 65 kg (BMI 23.9 kg/m?). On examination, both testes were atrophic (9 mL by Prader orchidometer) with pea-sized indurations palpable at the epididymal heads bilaterally. Scrotal ultrasound revealed bilateral heteroechogenic, hypovascularized masses near the testes. Semen analysis showed oligoasthenozoospermia (volume 2.6 mL; concentration 7 ? 10^6/mL; motility 14%). Serum follicle-stimulating hormone was 2.7 mIU/mL, luteinizing hormone 1.3 mIU/mL, testosterone 4.3 ng/mL, and estradiol 13 pg/mL. Tumor markers, including human chorionic gonadotropin, alpha-fetoprotein, and lactate dehydrogenase, were within normal limits. Pelvic MRI (T2-weighted) showed irregular, heterogeneous low-signal-intensity lesions with surrounding high-signal normal parenchyma in both testes. Contrast-enhanced CT scan demonstrated diffuse, irregular enlargement of both adrenal glands without other abnormalities.

title: A rare case of omental cake
case_summary:
A 59-year-old woman was referred to endoscopy after a small amount of fresh haematemesis during an episode of vomiting associated with a stomach "bug." She denied weight loss or abdominal pain. Her medical history included multiple myeloma--with normal renal function and calcium levels--and a hysterectomy for fibroids. She reported no history of asbestos exposure or mesothelioma.

An initial upper GI endoscopy revealed lumpy gastritis in the cardia and fundus, and biopsies were taken. Four weeks later, histologic examination of those biopsies identified blue rubber bleb naevus disease. A second endoscopy showed ongoing bleeding from the vascular lesions; she was treated with an intravenous proton-pump inhibitor and remained stable.

After multidisciplinary-team discussion, a volumetric CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was obtained (arterial phase for chest and upper abdomen; portal venous phase for the entire abdomen and pelvis). The scan demonstrated no mass in the stomach, o

title: Partial thrombosis of the corpus cavernosum - a malignancy mimicker
case_summary:
A 49-year-old man with mixed-type gastric adenocarcinoma diagnosed three months earlier, who underwent total gastrectomy with Roux-en-Y reconstruction and chemotherapy, presented with perineal discomfort after discontinuing anticoagulation. He was initially treated for acute epididymitis with antibiotics when the discomfort evolved into severe pain that worsened with urination. He denied hematuria, urinary tract infections, trauma, drug abuse, and intracavernous injections. On examination, there was tenderness of the right perineum along the ischiopubic ramus. Laboratory tests, including white blood cell count, coagulation profile, C-reactive protein, and urinalysis, were normal.

Non-contrast and contrast-enhanced CT of the pelvis showed asymmetric enlargement of the proximal right corpus cavernosum appearing hypodense with a peripheral hyperdense rim of enhancement, causing mass effect on the left corpus cavernosum; no pelvic metastases or collections were identified. Doppler ultraso

title: Erdheim?Chester disease of multisystem involvement with delayed diagnosis: A case report and literature review
case_summary:
A 31-year-old man was incidentally found to have a moderate, asymptomatic pericardial effusion on echocardiography in early 2017. A subsequent CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis demonstrated aortic, mediastinal, and pericardial thickening, bilateral pleural effusions, and a soft-tissue mass encasing the bilateral ureters and extending around the thoracic and abdominal aorta. Blood tests showed a white blood cell count of 14.5?10^9/L, neutrophils 9.99?10^9/L, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 16 mm/h, and CRP 9.45 mg/L. Antinuclear and ANCA antibodies were negative; IgG4 level, liver, and renal function were normal. Physical examination revealed distant heart sounds, no palpable lymphadenopathy or masses. 

Based on imaging and inflammatory markers, the patient was diagnosed with retroperitoneal fibrosis (RPF) and treated with intravenous methylprednisolone followed by oral prednisone and tamoxifen; cyclophosphamide was added later. Despite 6 months of therapy, repeat CT showed no r

title: Incidental deep soft tissue leiomyoma of the groin - a case report and comprehensive review of literature
case_summary: A 53-year-old otherwise healthy man presented with a 4-month history of swelling of the right foot and ankle. He denied trauma, fevers, weight loss, or claudication. On examination there was non-pitting edema of the right foot and ankle without erythema or warmth. Duplex ultrasound of the leg veins excluded deep vein thrombosis. Radiographs of the ankle and serologic tests were unremarkable, with no evidence of arthritis. One month later, because of progression of the edema to the entire leg, a CT scan of the pelvis and groin was performed and revealed a hyperechogenic structure in the right groin compressing the common femoral vein and causing focal venous narrowing. Duplex sonography confirmed a 3 cm mass dorsal to the common femoral vein. MRI of the groin showed a 26?24?54 mm lesion that was mildly hyperintense on T2-weighted images, isointense to muscle on T1, and demonstrated slight contrast enhancement. A fine-needle biopsy revealed proliferating spindle cells but did not provide

title: Small-Vessel Thrombotic Vasculopathy Secondary to Paradoxical Emboli Traversing a Patent Foramen Ovale: A Vasculitis Mimic
case_summary:
A 20-year-old Vietnamese man presented with a 2-week history of paresthesias followed by duskiness and pain affecting digits of both feet. The changes occurred without preceding trauma or systemic symptoms such as fevers. 

He had no significant past medical history, took no regular medications, was a nonsmoker and nondrinker, and had no history of recreational drug use. He had recently become unemployed and spent most of his day sedentary at home.

On examination, he was afebrile and other vital signs were unremarkable. He had strong popliteal, posterior tibial, and dorsalis pedis pulses bilaterally. There were no calcinoses, Raynaud's phenomenon, sclerodactyly, telangiectasias, malar rash, synovitis, aphthous ulcers, or livedo reticularis. Heart sounds were dual with no added sounds, and lung fields were clear. Nailfold capillaroscopy did not identify dilated capillary loops or dropouts.

Laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 13.8?10^9/L, neutrophil count of 11.4?10^9/L,

title: Suction thrombectomy of a massive, hypermobile (type C) right atrial thrombus: a case report
case_summary:
A 25-year-old man was referred for management of a persistent right atrial mass noted on transthoracic echocardiography performed one month earlier for preoperative risk assessment. During that month he had received apixaban 5 mg twice daily without change in the size of the mass. 

His medical history was notable for relapsed acute myeloid leukemia treated with chemotherapy and bone marrow transplantation (currently in remission), chronic steroid use for graft-versus-host disease complicated by avascular necrosis of the left hip repaired two months earlier, and a prior right subclavian tunneled catheter and Port-a-cath placed for chemotherapy that was removed two years ago.

On examination, vital signs were within normal limits on room air. Jugular venous distention was present, and the remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable.

Transesophageal echocardiography showed a 3.0 ? 1.3 cm hypermobile mass attached by a thin 1.8 cm stalk to the lateral right atrial wall at the

title: Acute cholecystitis associated with Brucella melitensis bacteremia: A rare intraabdominal manifestation of brucellosis
case_summary:
A 26-year-old man with no significant medical history presented with a 21-day history of right upper quadrant abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and malaise. He had moved from northeast Nebraska to California three months earlier to work on wind farms in the Mojave Desert. Shortly after arriving, he developed intermittent low-grade fever, nausea, vomiting, malaise, and abdominal pain, which he treated with over-the-counter cold medications and ibuprofen. 

Ten days before presentation to our center, he was admitted to a local hospital in Tehachapi Valley, California, for worsening symptoms and was found to have rhabdomyolysis (creatine kinase 1451 U/L) presumed secondary to exertion and dehydration; he received intravenous fluids. Laboratory studies showed only mild elevations in liver enzymes. Abdominal ultrasound and CT scan demonstrated fatty infiltration of the liver and hepatosplenomegaly. Urine and blood cultures were negative, and he was discharged after six days.

Three days lat

title: Kikuchi-Fujimoto disease in children: two case reports and a review of the literature
case_summary:
A previously healthy 16-year-old Chinese girl presented with 4 days of progressive fever, cough, headache, tender right cervical lymphadenopathy, intense fatigue, dizziness and two syncopal episodes. She had been treated with oral amoxicillin-clavulanate for 4 days without improvement. On exam, she was febrile, with a 3-cm tender right retro-angulo-mandibular lymph node, hyperemic pharynx, and no hepatosplenomegaly. Initial neck ultrasonography showed multiple bilateral hypo-anechoic, hypervascularized lymph nodes (largest 2.7 cm). CRP was 2.06 mg/dL and WBC count was 3690/mm3. Intravenous cefotaxime was started, with partial regression of nodes and resolution of fever.

Six days later, she redeveloped twice-daily fevers up to 41  degreesC, with painful enlargement of cervical lymph nodes and new tender nodes in the inguinal and axillary regions. Labs showed WBC 1930/mm3, neutrophils 760/mm3, ESR 33 mm/h, ferritin 1303 ng/mL, and LDH 857 U/L. Thoracic X-ray and abdominal ultrasound wer

title: Hemiparesis Caused by Cerebral Venous Sinus Thrombosis during the Postpartum Period: A Case Report; Negelle Arsi General Hospital and Medical College, Ethiopia
case_summary: A 29-year-old woman, gravida 4 para 4, at two months postpartum, presented with a three-week history of headaches and blurred vision and one day of right-sided body weakness. She had been treated twice for presumed migraine headaches without relief. There was no personal or family history of thromboembolism. On examination, blood pressure was 100/60 mm Hg; other vital signs were normal. She was alert (GCS 15/15). Motor strength was 4/5 in the right upper extremity, 4+ in the right lower extremity, and 5/5 on the left. Meningeal signs were absent. Laboratory tests, including complete blood count, random blood sugar, organ function tests, and viral markers, were all normal. A contrast-enhanced head CT scan revealed a filling defect along the superior sagittal sinus, resembling a delta sign, as well as a tubular filling defect along the posterior aspect of the superior sagittal sinus.

title: Intra-abdominal fetus in fetu presenting at 31 weeks gestational age
case_summary: A 23-year-old G0P0 woman was referred at 31 weeks 4 days' gestation for evaluation of a fetal abdominal mass. Serial prenatal ultrasound examinations showed a heterogeneous echogenic mass in the left hypochondriac region at the upper pole of the left kidney, measuring 45 ? 50 ? 42 mm at 31+4 weeks, 48 ? 58 ? 50 mm at 34+3 weeks, and 80 ? 74 ? 66 mm at 39+0 weeks. The mass contained calcified structures with shapes suggestive of a skull, spine, and femur, which became more apparent as the fetus grew. Color Doppler imaging demonstrated a branch arising from the fetal abdominal aorta with an umbilical arterial waveform supplying the mass.

title: The role of contrast enhanced ultrasound in the differential diagnosis of segmental testicular infarction
case_summary: A 43-year-old man presented with 24 hours of left testicular pain that had intensified before evaluation. He reported no fever, trauma, or urinary symptoms. On examination, the left testicle and epididymis were tender with mild swelling; no discrete mass was palpable. Laboratory studies showed mild leukocytosis with neutrophilia. A scrotal Doppler ultrasound demonstrated a normal right testis and, in the upper pole of the left testis, a mildly hypoechoic, heterogeneous, ill-defined lesion with no internal vascularity but a peripheral area of increased Doppler signal; the remainder of the left testis was slightly hyperemic with normal echotexture and the epididymis appeared normal. Contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS) confirmed total avascularity of the lesion with a hyperenhancing peripheral rim and normal enhancement elsewhere. The patient was started on antibiotics, managed conservatively, and underwent strict imaging follow-up. Tumor markers (LDH, AFP, ?-hCG) were negative. A thoraco

title:
Intravascular Large B-Cell Lymphoma Presenting as Acute Axonal
Polyneuropathy: A Case Report and Literature Review
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_summary: A 60-year-old man with hypertension and diabetes presented with acute low back pain radiating bilaterally to the legs, progressing over several days to difficulty ambulating. He was initially treated for a presumed urinary tract infection without relief and later received a spinal steroid injection. Two weeks after onset, he developed urinary incontinence and bilateral leg paralysis. During a 2-week hospitalization, CT pulmonary angiography diagnosed a pulmonary embolism. MRI of the brain and spine revealed abnormal foci of enhancement in the lumbar spine and at T12. A skeletal survey was negative, and a CT-guided bone marrow biopsy showed no malignancy. Lumbar puncture demonstrated 13 white blood cells/mm3 (82% lymphocytes), protein 132 mg/dL, and negative PCR testing for herpes simplex virus and West Nile virus. One day of intravenous steroids improved pain but not weakness; steroids were stopped. He then developed bowel incontinence and was treated with IV immunoglobulin and transfe

title: Fournier's gangrene after missed acute perforated appendicitis: A case report
case_summary:
A 25-year-old man initially presented 15 days before current admission with epigastric pain, fever, chills, and oral intolerance; his symptoms transiently improved and he was discharged. Five days later, he developed right flank pain radiating to the testis, hypogastric discomfort, dysuria, scrotal swelling, and discolored urine, prompting referral to urology and readmission to the emergency department. 

On evaluation, he reported fever, chills, dyspnea, chest pain, and myalgia. He had no significant past medical history, drug use, allergies, smoking, or addiction. Vital signs were: temperature 37.5 degreesC, pulse 135 beats/min, respiratory rate 28 breaths/min, and blood pressure 70/50 mmHg. Cardiac and pulmonary examinations were unremarkable. Abdominal examination revealed erythema of the right flank that was soft to palpation, tenderness in the hypogastric and right lower quadrant regions, and ecchymosis of the left scrotum without crepitus. An anaerobic odor was noted.

Laborator

title: Polyarteritis Nodosa with Bilateral Asynchronous Testicular Necrosis: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 55-year-old man with history of bilateral popliteal deep-vein thromboses 6 months earlier, hypertension, and end-stage renal disease secondary to focal segmental glomerulosclerosis was being evaluated off warfarin for renal-transplant workup.  

During hemodialysis, he developed acute left testicular pain; he had also noted left shoulder pain radiating down his arm since the evening before. Scrotal ultrasound with Doppler showed poor perfusion of the left testicle without torsion. Surgical exploration revealed an ischemic left testicle; a left orchiectomy was performed. Pathology showed interstitial hemorrhage and focal atrophy without malignancy.  

Three weeks later, he had acute right-sided scrotal pain and left lower extremity pain; ultrasound again showed absent flow in the right testicle. A right orchiectomy was performed, and pathology again showed acute interstitial hemorrhage and focal atrophy.  

The patient was anticoagulated with enoxaparin, warfarin, and aspirin, but a c

title: Synovial sarcoma: A radiologic case report
case_summary:
A 33?year?old man with no significant medical history presented with 3 months of progressive right leg pain, calf swelling, and difficulty walking. He denied trauma, fever, chills, chest pain, abdominal symptoms, or urinary changes. Physical examination revealed tenderness and edema of the right lower leg. A venous ultrasound showed a large cystic mass in the medial thigh compressing the distal femoral vein and associated deep venous thrombosis of the distal femoral and popliteal veins. He was started on apixaban. 

At surgical follow?up, examination disclosed a 5???5?cm firm, non?tender mass in the right upper medial thigh without inguinal lymphadenopathy. Contrast?enhanced CT of the right thigh demonstrated a 4.6???4.0???3.2?cm mass in the Hunter's canal. MRI of the thigh revealed a 4.8???4.2???5.1?cm lesion along the superficial femoral vessels that was heterogeneously hyperintense on T2-weighted images, hypointense on T1-weighted images, exhibited large areas of solid enhancement,

title: En bloc resection of pararectal ectopic thyroid tissue: a case report
case_summary:
A 66-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of a right pararectal mass that had been incidentally noted 14 years earlier and was followed with serial CT scans. The lesion grew from 19 mm to 34 mm and appeared hypervascular on the most recent CT. She was asymptomatic until recently developing mild right groin pain. Her medical history was notable only for a dysplastic rectal polyp resected 2 years earlier; there was no history of malignancy. 

On examination, vital signs were normal, and the physical exam was unremarkable. A right extraluminal mass was barely palpable on digital rectal examination. A recent 18F-FDG PET scan showed no hypermetabolic activity in the mass. Initial imaging led to the suspicion of a gastrointestinal stromal tumor or a neuroendocrine tumor. A Gallium-68 Dotatate PET scan demonstrated high somatostatin receptor expression. Pelvic MRI revealed an isodense, slightly heterogeneous lesion on T1-weighted images, with persistent T2 hyperintensity and restricted

title: Pleural empyema by Parvimonsd micra in an immunocompetent patient: a case report
journal: Revista Peruana de Medicina Experimental y Salud Publica
case_summary:
A 28-year-old man presented with a 34-day history of persistent fever, chills, weight loss, and progressive dyspnea. He reported moderate left rib?cage pain and palpitations early in the course. Five years earlier, he had completed treatment for pulmonary tuberculosis that was resistant to isoniazid, streptomycin, and ethionamide. On day 4 of illness, a chest radiograph was interpreted as normal despite ongoing fever (38 degreesC), chills, and leukocytosis with neutrophilia. During the ensuing month, he also noted abdominal discomfort and continued weight loss. Serologic testing for HIV 1-2 and three sputum smears for acid-fast bacilli were nonreactive and negative, respectively; abdominal ultrasound and stool studies were unremarkable.

On admission to the hospital, he appeared thin and moderately dehydrated, with a heart rate of 110 bpm, blood pressure of 100/70 mmHg, and oxygen saturation of 96% on room air. Examination revealed abolition of vesicular breath sounds over the middle a

title: Embolic Stroke due to a Common Carotid Artery Thrombus in a Young Patient with Severe Iron-Deficiency Anemia without Thrombocytosis
case_summary:
A 41-year-old man with no vascular risk factors presented with acute stuttering right upper extremity weakness and paresthesias, mild slurred speech, and right facial droop. He reported intermittent right arm numbness and tingling over the past month and denied trauma. His past history was notable for rectal bleeding; he took no medications. Family history was negative for neurologic or hematologic disease.

On examination, he was afebrile, blood pressure was 139/82?mm?Hg, and heart rate was 54?beats/min regular. Conjunctival pallor was noted, and stool testing was hemoccult-positive. Neurologic examination yielded an NIH Stroke Scale score of 2 (mild right lower facial droop and right upper extremity pronator drift). 

Noncontrast head CT revealed an old right frontal wedge-shaped embolic infarct. CT angiography of the head and neck showed a 1.5 ? 0.8?cm thrombus adhering to the wall of the left common carotid artery 4?cm proximal to the bifurcation without evidence of atherosclerosis

title: Hysterectomy with Fetus In Situ for Uterine Rupture at 21-Week Gestation due to a Morbidly Adherent Placenta
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
case_summary:
A 32-year-old G5T2P1A1L2 at 21 weeks and 3 days of gestation presented to labor and delivery triage with acute lower abdominal pain and a presyncopal episode. She denied fever, nausea, vomiting, changes in bowel habits, vaginal bleeding, contractions, or membrane rupture. Her obstetrical history included one classical cesarean for stillbirth, two elective cesareans, and a prior uterine dehiscence noted at surgery, as well as referral for a suspected abnormally adherent placenta after an anatomy scan showed a complete anterior placenta previa with loss of the placental-myometrial interface, multiple large irregular lacunae, and marked vascularity abutting the bladder.

On arrival, she was awake and oriented but hypotensive (80/40 mmHg) with a heart rate of 71 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Fetal heart rate was 145 beats/min. She received a fluid bolus, after which blood pressure improved to 92/51 mmHg. The abdomen was soft with tendern

title: Bilateral Adrenal Hemorrhage and Adrenal Insufficiency in the Context of Polycythemia Vera: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_summary:
A 60-year-old man with a 14-year history of polycythemia vera and hypertension was admitted with acute severe abdominal pain, nausea, and transient loss of consciousness. He reported vague abdominal discomfort intermittently over the past decade. His medications included aspirin, hydroxyurea, and intermittent losartan; he had poor adherence to phlebotomies and other therapy. On arrival, blood pressure was 110/75?mmHg; he was lethargic and confused. Physical examination showed epigastric and periumbilical tenderness, splenomegaly, and left costovertebral-angle tenderness. Laboratory studies were notable only for chronic leukocytosis consistent with his underlying disease; other values were within normal limits. 

An abdominopelvic CT scan with contrast demonstrated splenomegaly and a 40???23?mm hypodense lesion in the right adrenal gland (55?HU) and a 60???35?mm ill-defined lesion in the left adrenal gland, without evidence of vascular thrombosis. Two days later, after supportive care,

title: A tibioperoneal trunk mycotic pseudoaneurysm successfully treated with ligation
journal: Journal of Vascular Surgery Cases and Innovative Techniques
case_summary:
A 68-year-old man presented with 3 days of left leg swelling, erythema, and increasing pain on a background of 2 months of generalized fatigue.  
Laboratory studies showed a leukocytosis of 15.5 ? 10^3/?L with 21% bands.  
Duplex ultrasound was negative for deep venous thrombosis.  
Computed tomography of the leg revealed only subcutaneous edema at the site of the rash.  
A skin biopsy demonstrated perivascular lymphocytic inflammation without evidence of vasculitis.  
Blood cultures grew Streptococcus mutans.  
Transthoracic echocardiography identified a probable mitral valve vegetation with mild to moderate regurgitation; the patient received 4 weeks of outpatient intravenous ceftriaxone.  
One month later, echocardiography showed resolution of the vegetation but persistent moderate mitral regurgitation. The patient continued to report left foot paresthesia.  
Electromyography suggested possible left sciatica neuropathy or lumbosacral plexopathy, but lumbar spine magnetic resonance i

title: Case report of unique presentation of spontaneous calf hematoma
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: A 26-year-old man presented with a one-month history of gradually worsening pain and swelling in the right calf. Two months earlier, he had undergone external fixation and vascular repair of a left tibial open fracture, followed by a rotational flap; postoperative prophylaxis included rivaroxaban. Three weeks postoperatively, he had a flap debridement and resumed rivaroxaban. Two months later, he experienced mild posterior calf pain that improved with elevation and was treated for presumed cellulitis after Doppler ultrasound showed patent vessels and minimal soft-tissue edema. Two weeks after that, the pain and swelling progressed despite conventional therapy. On examination, vital signs were normal. The right calf was uniformly swollen, warm, and tender without discoloration; distal pulses were present. Laboratory studies showed normal CBC, renal and liver function, and bleeding time; activated partial thromboplastin time was prolonged at 41.6 s (reference, 23-32 s). Ultrasound of the

title: Clozapine Intoxication Mimicking Acute Stroke
journal: Clinical Practice and Cases in Emergency Medicine
case_summary:
A 66-year-old man with hypertension and hyperlipidemia was brought to the emergency department by his wife for altered mental status. Two hours before arrival, he had consumed two glasses of wine and taken a nap; he was asymptomatic before the nap. On awakening, he exhibited dysarthria and extremity discoordination.

Home medications included bupropion, dutasteride, lisinopril, and tolterodine. 

Vital signs were: blood pressure 115/78 mm?Hg, heart rate 83 beats/min, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 36.8? degreesC, and oxygen saturation 95% on room air.

On examination, he had waxing and waning alertness but could intermittently follow commands. Pupils were reactive but sluggish, with horizontal and vertical nystagmus. Facial symmetry was intact, the tongue was midline, and speech was dysarthric. Motor strength could not be formally assessed; he moved all extremities symmetrically. He had bilateral dysmetria on finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing and truncal ataxia when

title: External jugular vein aneurysm presenting as submandibular neck mass
case_summary: An 80-year-old woman was referred for bilateral intermittent ear pain. She described mild, left submandibular neck swelling with tenderness that resolved within 2-3 days; these episodes were long-standing but had worsened over several months. Her medical history included hypertension managed with enalapril and amlodipine and hyperlipidemia managed by diet. On examination, her blood pressure was 161/88 mmHg; other vital signs were normal. Physical examination and laryngoscopy were unremarkable except for subtle fullness in the left submandibular region without a discrete mass. A contrast-enhanced CT scan showed a 1.8 ? 1.4 ? 1.8 cm hyperdense lesion within the left submandibular gland. Because of the discrepancy between imaging and exam, an ultrasound of the neck was performed, and during real-time ultrasound, the lesion was found to be vascular.

title: A case of intra-abdominal hemorrhage secondary to peliosis hepatis
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 58-year-old woman with a long history of heavy alcohol consumption and oral contraceptive use presented with two days of sharp, nonradiating abdominal pain aggravated by palpation and sitting up and relieved by walking or standing, accompanied by vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal distention, and weakness. On initial evaluation she was hypotensive and tachycardic and found to have an acute abdomen. During emergent laparotomy in the Turks and Caicos, approximately 2 L of dark blood was evacuated, and peri-hepatic packing was performed. Transferred in hypovolemic shock, she underwent relook laparotomy with removal of packs, evacuation of 400 cc of blood, and identification of a large right subcapsular hematoma without a palpable left-lobe lesion. On postoperative day 3, she developed a right pleural effusion requiring tube thoracostomy, and hypokalemia was corrected. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed:
- A massive nonenhancing hyperdense right subcapsular hematoma.
- An irregular,

title: A Rare Case of Popliteal Venous Aneurysm
case_summary:
A 21-year-old male football player presented with several months of left popliteal fossa discomfort following minor trauma, aggravated by palpation and daily activities. On examination, there was no palpable mass or signs of inflammation in the popliteal fossa, but palpation of the anterior left knee elicited pain. There were no signs of peripheral arterial disease. Vital signs were normal, and chest and abdominal examinations, blood pressure measurement, chest radiography, oxygen saturation, and electrocardiography were unremarkable.

Magnetic resonance imaging of the left knee without contrast showed a 4 ? 2 cm oval formation with heterogeneous hyperintensity on T1- and T2-weighted sequences in the popliteal fossa adjacent to vascular structures, suggestive of a blood-containing lesion. Color-Doppler ultrasonography demonstrated a mixed echogenicity mass in the left popliteal fossa with no detectable arterial or venous flow.

title: Impending central retinal vein occlusion in patient with idiopathic cutaneous leukocytoclastic vasculitis
journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case_summary:
A 27-year-old Indian American man presented with five days of constant blurry vision, primarily in the lower visual field, of the right eye. His ocular history included bilateral LASIK and moderate myopia (?5.0 diopters). Medical history was significant for idiopathic cutaneous leukocytoclastic vasculitis diagnosed five years earlier by skin biopsy, with direct immunofluorescence showing IgM, IgG, and C3 deposition in dermal vessels; the disease had flared twice, with purpuric rash and abdominal pain, and responded to colchicine, antibiotics for Helicobacter pylori, and systemic prednisone.

On examination, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/20 in both eyes; intraocular pressures were 13 mm Hg OD and 14 mm Hg OS. Slit-lamp examination showed no anterior chamber cells or flare. Dilated fundus examination of the right eye demonstrated mildly enlarged, tortuous veins, optic nerve hemorrhage, and intraretinal hemorrhages temporal to the macula. Spectral-domain optical coherence tomography

title: Clot or not? An unusual case of false positive CTPA and an approach to diagnosis
case_summary:
A woman in her sixties with a history of chronic bronchiectasis since childhood (postpertussis) and frequent chest infections presented with acute onset of shortness of breath on minimal exertion and new left-sided pleuritic chest pain. On examination, she was tachycardic, tachypnoeic, and hypoxic. An arterial blood gas on room air showed pH 7.40, pO2 8.78?kPa, and pCO2 5.48?kPa. Laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 14.9???10^9?/L and a C-reactive protein level of 133?mg?/L. She was started on antibiotics for a presumed lower respiratory tract infection, but because her presentation differed from previous infective exacerbations, pulmonary embolism was suspected.

A chest radiograph showed hyperexpansion, ring and tram-line shadows, coarse lung markings, and architectural distortion consistent with her known bronchiectasis, with no new changes. A CT pulmonary angiogram demonstrated multiple filling defects within pulmonary arteries. Review of the lung windows of the same s

title: Severe fetal intracranial hemorrhage: Congenital Cytomegalovirus infection may play a role? A case report and review of literature
case_summary:
MR is a male neonate born at 38 weeks' gestation by elective cesarean delivery. Birth weight was 2350 g (5th-10th percentile), length 45 cm (5th percentile), head circumference 31 cm (<5th percentile). Apgar scores were 8 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes.  
Maternal history was notable only for immunity to rubella, negative toxoplasma serology, and a single CMV serology at 11 weeks (IgG positive, IgM negative). A maternal febrile episode occurred in the periconceptional period; there was no trauma, and no use of cocaine, warfarin, or anticonvulsants. No family history of bleeding disorders or stroke.  
Fetal ultrasound at 20 weeks suggested intracranial hemorrhage, confirmed by fetal MRI at 25 weeks, which showed a 2.3 cm porencephalic cyst in the right frontotemporal-insular region with ex vacuo enlargement of the right lateral ventricle, reduced right cerebral hemisphere volume, and abnormal micropolygyria of the right frontal cortex.  
At birth, examination revealed microcephaly, jaundice,

title: A neonate with spontaneous arterial limb ischemia and an aneurysm of the oval foramen: a?case report
case_summary: A male neonate, second twin of a dichorionic gestation, was delivered at 37 weeks' gestation by cesarean section (Apgar scores 9, 10, and 10; birth weight 2980 g) after an uneventful pregnancy. Approximately 3 hours after birth, his right foot developed sharply demarcated white discoloration that quickly turned purple, became swollen, and was painful. On subsequent examination, some distal phalanges of the left hand also appeared purple. His vital signs remained normal. Laboratory studies at admission showed an activated partial thromboplastin time of 38.5 s, prothrombin time of 14.6 s, fibrinogen of 2.4 g/L, a venous hematocrit of 0.56 L/L, C-reactive protein of 0.7 mg/L, and a normal complete blood count; blood cultures were negative. Doppler ultrasound on days 1 and 3 showed patent femoral and crural arteries without a peripheral focus for embolism. A pediatric cardiologist was consulted to evaluate for a cardiac source of thromboembolism.

title: Successful percutaneous management of hypothenar hammer syndrome with thrombosuction and catheter-directed intra-arterial thrombolysis
journal: Journal of Vascular Surgery Cases, Innovations and Techniques
case_summary:
A 36-year-old man who is an avid weightlifter with a history of anabolic steroid use presented with a 2-hour history of sudden-onset pain in the fourth and fifth fingers of his left hand. He felt a "popping" sensation in his palm while performing heavy squeezing wrist exercises and immediately noted pain. Within minutes, the affected fingers became numb, cold, and turned progressively blue. 

On examination, ulnar artery pulsations were absent, and there was profound cyanosis with early signs of ischemia in the fourth and fifth fingers. The distal left palm was tender, and the affected fingers were cool. No carotid or subclavian bruits were detected. The remainder of the examination was unremarkable. 

Laboratory tests, including a complete blood count, metabolic panel, coagulation assays, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and C-reactive protein, were all within normal limits. An electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm with no evidence of atrial fibrillation. Transthoracic echocardi

title: Cholesterol crystal embolism to the gastrointestinal tract: a catastrophic case
case_summary:
A 62-year-old man with severe peripheral arterial disease underwent bilateral common femoral endarterectomy and aortobifemoral bypass for incapacitating claudication due to extensive atherosclerosis. On postoperative day 1, he developed melena and hematochezia. He became hypoxic, hypotensive, and acidotic with rising lactate levels. Emergency colonoscopy revealed severe ischemia of the proximal colon and severe circumferential ischemic proctitis.  
On postoperative day 2, exploratory laparotomy showed necrosis of the distal ileum and cecum, which were resected; dusky areas in the remainder of the small bowel and colon were left in situ pending re-evaluation. On postoperative day 3, the patient progressed to multi-organ failure. A second-look laparotomy revealed diffuse necrosis of the small bowel and ascending colon, and these segments were resected. The patient became profoundly acidotic and hyperkalemic intraoperatively, and he died shortly after return to the intensive care unit.

title: Prenatal presentation of glutaric aciduria type II: A case report with radiologic, clinical, biochemical, molecular, and pathological phenotyping
case_summary:
A 29-year-old nulliparous woman with a consanguineous partner was referred at 21 weeks' gestation for evaluation of fetal renal anomalies. Detailed obstetrical ultrasound showed placenta praevia, bilaterally large undifferentiated hyperechoic kidneys with multiple corticomedullary micrometric cysts, and a markedly hyperechoic liver with tiny bile-containing cystic structures. Fetal growth and amniotic fluid volume were normal. The patient declined amniocentesis; carrier testing for PKHD1 was negative.

At 25 weeks, ultrasound demonstrated a narrow fetal thorax, and oligohydramnios developed by 27 weeks. At 29 weeks, imaging revealed an enlarged cavum vergae and an unusually trabeculated subarachnoid space. A cesarean delivery was performed at 36 weeks for placenta praevia; the male infant weighed 3050 g and had Apgar scores of 7, 7, and 7.

The infant developed respiratory distress with respiratory acidosis and was transferred to the neonatal intensive care unit. Cerebral sonography su

title: Intravascular Lymphoma with Progressive CNS Hemorrhage and Multiple Dissections
case_summary:
A 47-year-old woman with a history of delayed gastric emptying presented with daily migratory paresthesias ascending her right side, each lasting about 20 minutes and accompanied by headache and confusion. During the preceding month, she had been admitted elsewhere with right-sided paresthesias, worsening migraines with visual aura, and intermittent somnolence. An initial contrast-enhanced brain MRI showed only minimal nonspecific subcortical T2/FLAIR hyperintensities. Transferred for concern of focal motor seizures, she had no lymphadenopathy, skin lesions, or edema. On examination, she was somnolent but arousable, and when alert scored 22/30 on the Montreal Cognitive Assessment, with deficits in attention, language, and abstraction. The remainder of the neurologic exam was unremarkable.

Laboratory evaluation, including cerebrospinal fluid analysis, was unrevealing. Repeat MRI of the brain 13 days later revealed two new diffusion-restricted foci in the left centrum semiovale consiste

title: Vascular leiomyoma of the thigh: Classic presentation of a rare tumor with imaging and pathology correlation
case_summary:
A 55-year-old woman with no significant past medical or surgical history presented with a large, painless mass in the proximal right thigh noted after recent intentional weight loss. On examination, there was a firm, mobile, 15 cm mass in her right upper inner thigh without Tinel's sign or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Ultrasound of the mass demonstrated a lobulated, predominantly hypoechoic solid lesion with circumscribed margins and internal vascularity on Doppler imaging.

MRI revealed a sharply demarcated intermuscular mass located between the sartorius and adductor longus muscles. The lesion was isointense to slightly hyperintense relative to muscle on T1-weighted images and homogeneously hyperintense on T2-weighted fat-saturated sequences, with strong homogeneous contrast enhancement. The superficial femoral artery was identified along the posterior aspect of the mass, with more than 180 degrees of its circumference encased, but without luminal invasion or occlusion. The femoral vei

title: Vitamin K Deficiency Presenting in an Infant with an Anterior Mediastinal Mass: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_summary:
A 28-day-old term male infant, born at home without vitamin K prophylaxis, was referred to the emergency department for sudden ecchymosis of the left hemiscrotum. He was exclusively breastfed and had been well until 26 days of age, when parents noted acute discoloration of the right testicle that migrated to the left over 24 hours. During that time he became progressively fussy, with decreased oral intake and pallor, but no fevers, emesis, or rash.

On examination, he appeared lethargic, pale, and jaundiced, with purpura of the left hemiscrotum and oozing from peripheral IV attempts. He had increased work of breathing with retractions, normal cardiac and abdominal exams, and an appropriate neurological exam for age.

Laboratory studies revealed mild indirect hyperbilirubinemia, anemia (hemoglobin 8.4 g/dL), mildly low platelets (143 ? 10^3/?L), markedly prolonged prothrombin time (> 80 s) and partial thromboplastin time (> 250 s), with normal fibrinogen. He received intravenous vitamin

title: A multidisciplinary approach to carotid body tumors surgical management
case_summary: A 32-year-old man was referred for evaluation of an asymptomatic, 7 cm right lateral neck mass noted on routine examination. He had no family history of paragangliomas or multiple endocrine tumors. On ultrasound, the mass was hypoechoic and hypervascular, located at the carotid artery bifurcation on the right. A computed tomography angiogram demonstrated a well-circumscribed tumor encasing the internal and external carotid arteries at the bifurcation, consistent with a Shamblin III lesion. Biochemical testing with 24-hour urine catecholamines was negative.

title: Case report of pulmonary brucellosis complicated by pleural effusion
case_summary: A 32-year-old woman, two weeks postpartum after cesarean delivery for intrauterine fetal demise at 7 months' gestation, presented with a 5-day history of progressive dyspnea, fever, productive cough with blood-tinged sputum, and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. She denied weight loss, appetite change, joint symptoms, rash, or tuberculosis exposure. On examination she was febrile to 38.6? degreesC, tachycardic at 120?bpm, respiratory rate 24/min, blood pressure 120/75?mm?Hg, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Chest auscultation revealed right infrascapular dullness and diminished breath sounds. Hemoglobin was 9.4?g/dL, leukocyte count 9.6?10^6/mL, platelets 635?10^3/mL, CRP 1.6?mg/L, ESR 63?mm/hr, and arterial blood gas showed respiratory alkalosis. Initial chest radiograph demonstrated right costophrenic angle obliteration with underlying consolidation. She was treated for health-care-associated pneumonia with piperacillin/tazobactam and levofloxacin. On hospital day 2 her dyspnea

title: Epithelioid hemangioendothelioma involving the superficial femoral artery and femoral vein
journal: Journal of Vascular Surgery Cases, Innovations and Techniques
case_summary:
A 64-year-old Hispanic woman was referred for evaluation of acute-onset right calf claudication of 1 month's duration, now accompanied by anteromedial thigh pain and numbness and tingling on the dorsum of the right foot. She denied fevers, chills, night sweats, weight loss, trauma, infection, or intravenous drug use. Her medical history was notable for hypertension, hyperlipidemia, active tobacco use, and invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast (pT1a, cN0, cM0, ER+, PR+, HER2?) treated 1 year earlier with partial mastectomy, radiation, and exemestane. On examination, there was no palpable mass or skin change, but there was significant tenderness of the proximal medial right thigh. Peripheral pulses were 3+ bilaterally, and motor and sensory function were intact.  

A computed tomography angiogram of the right thigh showed a 16-mm lesion abutting the proximal superficial femoral artery and femoral vein. The initial differential diagnosis included thrombosed pseudoaneurysm, neoplas

title: Spontaneous Splenic Rupture Complicating Severe P. falciparum Infection: A Case Report and Literature Review
case_summary:
A 32-year-old man from a malaria-endemic region presented with 7 days of dull, aching left-sided upper abdominal pain associated with intermittent fever, rigors, night sweats, anorexia, and generalized aches. He denied cough, dyspnea, chest pain, diarrhea, jaundice, itching, epistaxis, or trauma. He had a history of prior uncomplicated P. falciparum infections treated with oral therapy.  
On examination, he was confused and pale, with a temperature of 39.8  degreesC, pulse rate of 110 beats/min, blood pressure of 90/55 mmHg, and oxygen saturation of 91% on room air. Chest auscultation was normal. Abdominal examination showed localized left upper quadrant tenderness and guarding without generalized peritonitis.  
Initial management included high-flow oxygen, intravenous fluids, analgesia, blood cultures, and empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics. Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 8 g/dL, white cell count 5,000/mm^3, platelets 170,000/mm^3, normal coagulation profile, and normal rena

title: Renal dedifferentiated liposarcoma with intra-caval tumor thrombus: A rare case
journal: Indian Journal of Urology : IJU : Journal of the Urological Society of India
case_summary:
A 65-year-old man presented with an abdominal lump and right flank pain of 4 months' duration. He reported no hematuria or lower urinary tract symptoms. On examination, a hard, nodular mass was palpable in the right hypochondrium, epigastrium, and right lumbar region. 

Ultrasonography with Doppler study, contrast-enhanced CT scan, and MRI revealed an upper and mid-pole right renal mass measuring 20?16?10 cm, extending from the subdiaphragmatic area to the aortic bifurcation. There was associated retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Areas of doubtful fat density were noted within the mass. A tumor thrombus was seen in the inferior vena cava, extending from the intrahepatic portion to the right common iliac vein. 

On the basis of the imaging findings and caval involvement, a clinical diagnosis of renal cell carcinoma with venous extension was made.

title: Case report: Renal infarction by paradoxical embolism through the patent foramen ovale as an unusual cause of post-operative abdominal pain after sleeve gastrectomy
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 50-year-old woman presented with acute left-sided abdominal pain 9 days after an uncomplicated laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy (BMI 41). Her post-operative course had been uneventful; she received standard DVT prophylaxis including calf compressors, graduated compression stockings for 3 days, and enoxaparin 40 mg daily. On day 9, while walking on a treadmill, she developed sudden left flank and epigastric pain. 

Vital signs: BP 140/80 mmHg; HR 68 bpm; RR 17/min; SpO2 98% on room air; T 36.0  degreesC. Physical examination revealed localized tenderness in the left upper quadrant and epigastrium without peritoneal signs.

Laboratory studies: WBC 5.5 ? 10^9/L; Hb 130 g/L; CRP 6 mg/L; normal liver and renal function tests.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed an anterior-inferior left renal wedge infarction and incidental bilateral renal and hepatic cysts. Renal Doppler ultrasound demonstrated normal arterial and venous flow without evidence of renal thrombus. 

Cardia

title: Non-Gestational Choriocarcinoma with Widespread Metastases Presenting with Type 1 Respiratory Failure in a 39-Year-Old Female: Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_summary:
A 39-year-old Caucasian woman presented with a one-month history of progressive dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain. Her past medical history included anxiety, depression, heavy cigarette and cannabinoid smoking, and one prior term pregnancy 16 years earlier. On examination, she was in respiratory distress, requiring 9 L/min of oxygen to maintain normal saturations. Lung auscultation was clear; there were two discrete masses in the right breast and tenderness in the upper abdomen.

A chest radiograph revealed multiple pulmonary opacities suggestive of metastases and a left pleural effusion. Contrast-enhanced CT showed extensive metastatic lesions in the lungs, liver, spleen, kidneys, pancreas, and subcutaneous tissues of the right chest wall, left flank, and breast, including a dominant 12-cm mass in the lingula amid marked emphysema. The uterus and adnexa appeared normal.

Breast ultrasound identified a 22?8 mm hematoma and a 12?11 mm vascular lesion, which was biopsied. Laboratory studi

title: Successful use of daily intravenous infusion of C1 esterase inhibitor concentrate in the treatment of a hereditary angioedema patient with ascites, hypovolemic shock, sepsis, renal and respiratory failure
journal: Allergy, Asthma, and Clinical Immunology : Official Journal of the Canadian Society of Allergy and Clinical Immunology
case_summary: A 51-year-old man with type I hereditary angioedema (HAE; onset in late teens), Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease, and congenital aortic stenosis was admitted for scheduled aortic aneurysm repair. He was bridged from warfarin to heparin. On hospital day 3, he developed sudden left flank and severe abdominal pain. He was agitated, disoriented, pale, and peripherally cold. Vital signs: temperature 35.2  degreesC, heart rate 91 bpm, blood pressure 70/40 mm Hg, respiratory rate 26 breaths/min. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 43 g/L (118 g/L two days earlier), hematocrit 0.24, leukocyte count 22 ? 10^9/L, blood glucose 8.6 mmol/L, creatinine 290 umol/L (64 umol/L five days earlier), and zero urine output. Management included discontinuation of heparin, sedation, intubation, mechanical ventilation (FiO2 0.40, PEEP 10), renal replacement therapy (RRT), intravenous fluids, red-cell transfusion, norepinephrine, and 2000 units of plasma-derived C1-inhibitor (pdC1-INH) concentrate. Abdominal CT re

title: Carotidynia: A Rare Diagnosis for Unilateral Neck Pain Revealed by Cross-Sectional Imaging
case_summary: A 34-year-old man presented to the emergency department with a 5-day history of bitemporal headache and 24 hours of intense, throbbing, continuous left neck pain radiating to the ipsilateral mandibular and auricular region. The pain was localized over the left carotid bifurcation and worsened with neck movement and palpation. Physical and neurologic examinations were normal. He denied trauma, recent infection, or chiropractic manipulation; his medical history was otherwise unremarkable. Laboratory studies--including complete blood count, coagulation tests, electrolytes, glucose, renal and liver function tests--were within normal limits. The white-cell count was 7 g/L (reference, 4-10 g/L), C-reactive protein was 6.3 mg/L (reference, 0-10 mg/L), and erythrocyte sedimentation rate was mildly elevated at 11 mm/h (reference, 0-10 mm/h). Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck revealed soft-tissue thickening around the distal common carotid artery, the carotid bulb, and the proximal internal carot

title: Critical Limb Ischemia in a Young Man: Saddle Embolism or Unusual Presentation of Thromboangiitis Obliterans?
case_summary:
A 32-year-old man was referred for right critical limb ischemia. He had a 3-month history of bilateral intermittent claudication and chronic alcoholism. He had smoked approximately 10 cigarettes daily since age 10 and 10 cannabis joints daily since age 12; he reported no other cardiovascular risk factors or superficial thrombophlebitis. On examination, the right leg was extremely painful and pale, with absent pedal pulses bilaterally and mild sensory loss on the right. Allen's test of the upper extremities was negative.  
An echo Doppler study suggested bilateral common iliac occlusion and a three-vessel occlusion of the right lower leg. CT angiography showed an intraluminal aortic and iliac clot and bilateral distal tibial vessel occlusions. The patient was anticoagulated with intravenous heparin. A transthoracic echocardiogram did not detect any proximal source of emboli.  
He underwent a right iliofemoral embolectomy with selective right popliteal, tibial, and peroneal embolectomy a

title:
Contrast-enhanced ultrasound in the diagnosis of a solitary neck
mass: a case report of metastatic seminoma
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
case_summary:
A 35-year-old previously healthy man presented with a 3-month history of an incidentally discovered mass in his right neck. He reported no fever, cough, tingling, itching, testicular pain, or any other symptoms. Physical examination revealed an isolated, firm but soft, 6-7 cm mass in the right upper neck; no other abnormalities were noted, and testicular examination was normal. 

Contrast-enhanced computed tomography of the neck showed a 50 ? 29 mm soft-tissue mass in the right upper neck, encasing the right internal jugular vein. Neck ultrasonography revealed a hypoechoic, irregular mass approximately 68 ? 18 ? 50 mm in size, located deep to the right sternocleidomastoid muscle and lateral to the common carotid artery and internal jugular vein, with a clear boundary but multiple small nodular components; Doppler imaging showed no obvious internal blood flow. 

Contrast-enhanced ultrasound of the mass demonstrated heterogeneous hyperenhancement during the arterial phase and hypoenhance

title: Fibroadipose Vascular Anomaly [FAVA] - A Distinct Entity and Not Just a Malformation!
case_summary:
An 11-year-old girl presented with a 4-year history of a painful swelling in the anterior right thigh. It began as a small, deep lump in the mid-thigh and gradually enlarged, causing severe local pain and limiting knee motion. Over the past year, her mother noted a limp with pelvic and shoulder drop toward the affected side. She had previously undergone an inconclusive incisional biopsy and received intralesional sclerotherapy without benefit.

On examination, there was a tender, deep intramuscular soft-tissue mass in the anterior thigh, restricting active and passive knee flexion to between 10 degrees and 40 degrees. Calf muscle circumference was reduced on the affected side, and limb-length measurement showed over 2 cm shortening.

High-resolution ultrasound demonstrated an ill-defined hyperechoic area within the anterior compartment muscles and a few tubular vascular channels on power Doppler, suggestive of a vascular malformation. Multiplanar MR imaging revealed a large (approximat

title: Laparoscopic resection of a primary hydatid cyst of the adrenal gland: a case report
case_summary:
A 62-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of left flank pain. Her medical history was unremarkable. On examination, vital signs were within normal limits and the physical examination was unremarkable. Complete blood count, electrolytes, eosinophil count, serum biochemistry, and urinalysis were all within normal limits.

A plain abdominal radiograph showed a 3-cm round opacity with evidence of calcifications in the left upper quadrant. Chest radiography was normal. Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated a calcified heterogeneous cystic mass in the retroperitoneal area between the superior pole of the left kidney and the spleen. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed a solitary cystic mass with a calcified wall within the left adrenal gland that showed no enhancement after intravenous contrast. No other intra-abdominal or intrathoracic masses were identified.

MRI of the abdomen with T2-weighted imaging demonstrated a lesion within the left adrenal gland characterized by a hypoint

title: Prostatic biopsy-related rectal bleeding refractory to medical and endoscopic therapy definitively managed by catheter-directed embolotherapy: a case report
case_summary: An 82-year-old man with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (on coumarin anticoagulation) presented with an episode of gross hematuria. Physical examination revealed a diffusely indurated prostate on digital rectal exam. Contrast CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed normal kidneys and bladder and an enlarged prostate. Laboratory studies revealed a normal white-cell count and a prostate-specific antigen level that had risen from 4.0 to 9.4 ng/mL. Transrectal ultrasound of the prostate demonstrated a hypoechoic, hypervascular, subcapsular lesion in the peripheral zone, with a differential diagnosis of carcinoma or granulomatous inflammation. Seven days after temporary cessation of anticoagulation, an ultrasound-guided transrectal prostate biopsy was performed as an outpatient. Pathology showed diffuse carcinoma of the peripheral prostate. Three days later, the patient had massive rectal bleeding with hemodynamic shock (blood pressure 50/30 mmHg, heart rate 100 bpm), which responded to resuscit

title: Rare Case of an Epithelial Cyst in an Intrapancreatic Accessory Spleen Treated by Robot-Assisted Spleen Preserving Distal Pancreatectomy
final_diagnosis: Epithelial cyst in an intrapancreatic accessory spleen
case_summary: A 21-year-old woman of Philippine descent was referred for evaluation of right upper quadrant abdominal pain of one year's duration. She had no prior medical history. Physical examination and laboratory tests were normal. An abdominal ultrasound showed cholecystolithiasis and incidentally revealed a cystic lesion in the tail of the pancreas. Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated a 24 mm cystic lesion in the pancreatic tail abutting the spleen, with thickening and enhancement of the caudal cyst wall. MRI of the pancreas showed a 31 mm cystic lesion that was hyperintense on T1-weighted images, consistent with prior hemorrhage; the cyst wall enhanced in the venous phase, with focal central enhancement in the arterial phase, and the rim exhibited diffusion restriction on diffusion-weighted imaging. At a multidisciplinary conference, a solid pseudopapillary neoplasm with prior hemorrhage was considered most likely; cystic neuroendocrine tumor and mucinous cystadenoma were also included in the d

title: Relapsing TIPIC Syndrome after Administration of an mRNA-Based COVID-19 Vaccine
case_summary:
A 49-year-old woman presented with acute left-sided, ibuprofen-resistant neck pain localized over the left common carotid artery. The pain began 3 weeks earlier and worsened over the past 8 days; she had no focal neurologic deficits. Her medical history was notable for hypertension and dyslipidemia, and she had received her second mRNA-based COVID-19 vaccination (elasomeran, mRNA-1273) in the left arm 7 days before symptom onset. An outpatient carotid ultrasound showed wall thickening of the left common carotid artery, prompting referral.

On admission, CT angiography demonstrated a semilunar hypodense thickening of the left common carotid artery with slight luminal narrowing. MRI showed periarterial STIR-signal enhancement in the middle third of the left common carotid artery on postcontrast T1-weighted Dixon sequences, along with a membranous structure within the artery and ventrally accentuated circumjacent contrast enhancement. High-resolution ultrasound revealed a 30 mm long, medi

title: Liver Microabscesses in a Premature Neonate With Eosinophilic Colitis
case_summary:
A 33 4/7-week, 2.22-kg female neonate was born after spontaneous vaginal delivery following premature rupture of membranes. She had an unremarkable physical examination at birth and passed meconium within 24 hours. Maternal breast milk feeds were initiated by gavage on day 1 of life. 

On day 4, after episodes of bilious emesis, she was made nil per os. Examination showed a distended, tender abdomen without rebound and normoactive bowel sounds; the remainder of her exam, including genitourinary assessment, was normal. Laboratory studies on day 4 revealed a white-cell count of 21.1?10^9/L with 14% eosinophils. 

By day 5, she developed frank bloody stools. Serial abdominal radiographs showed nonspecific bowel gas fullness without pneumatosis. An upper gastrointestinal series ruled out malrotation. Although abdominal distention resolved by day 6, leukocytosis, eosinophilia, and hematochezia persisted. Family history was notable for a sister with milk-protein intolerance who developed hem

title: Acute compartment syndrome of the thigh after total knee arthroplasty: a case report
case_summary:
A 57-year-old Caucasian woman with osteoarthritis of the right knee underwent elective total knee arthroplasty under spinal anesthesia. Her medical history included obesity (BMI 33.4), type II diabetes, hypertension, and prior percutaneous coronary intervention. Preoperative knee range of motion was 0-10-90 degrees with varus alignment. During surgery, local infiltration analgesia with ropivacaine, adrenaline, and tranexamic acid was used; no tourniquet or drain was employed. The procedure was uncomplicated, and mobilization began on the evening of surgery.

On postoperative day 1, she reported severe right-leg pain requiring escalation of analgesics. On day 2, she developed massive pain in the mid-right thigh that did not improve with additional opioids, worsened with flexion, and was accompanied by circumferential swelling and tenderness. Because of her body habitus and the atypical location, an ultrasound to exclude deep-vein thrombosis was attempted but proved ambiguous. An interdi

title: Adenovirus-Associated Thrombotic Microangiopathy and Necrotizing Interstitial Nephritis in a Renal Transplant Recipient: A Case Report and Review
case_summary:
A 45-year-old man with end-stage renal disease of unknown etiology underwent deceased-donor kidney transplantation. Induction immunosuppression consisted of rabbit anti-thymocyte globulin and methylprednisolone; maintenance therapy included mycophenolate mofetil, tacrolimus, and prednisolone. The surgery was prolonged with significant hemorrhage and required blood transfusions and inotropic support. The patient remained anuric postoperatively; renal Doppler ultrasound showed patent arterial flow without obstruction. Tacrolimus trough levels were within target range.

On day 6, a biopsy showed acute tubular injury; diuresis began by day 10. On day 16, a urinary leak led to re-exploration; the accessory renal artery was thrombosed and the distal ureter necrotic. The necrotic tissue demonstrated only ischemic necrosis without rejection. Urine output recovered.

By day 28, the patient developed fever spikes and rising creatinine. Tacrolimus levels, blood counts, peripheral smear, and LDH w

title: Reactivation of latent Histoplasma and disseminated cytomegalovirus in a returning traveller with ulcerative colitis
case_summary:
A 62-year-old man of Bangladeshi origin presented with 2 weeks of lethargy, fever, productive cough and weight loss. His medical history included well-controlled ulcerative colitis managed with long-term prednisolone and azathioprine, type 2 diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia and chronic hepatitis B. He admitted to taking higher than prescribed doses of prednisolone for the preceding 2 months. He had travelled to Bangladesh 6 months earlier without illness.

On examination, his temperature was 34.8  degreesC; there was a non-tender, erythematous left calf overlying a saphenous vein harvest site. Initial laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 116 g/L, white-cell count 6.2 ? 10^9/L and C-reactive protein 138 mg/L. A lower-limb ultrasound excluded deep venous thrombosis, and CT of the chest, abdomen and pelvis demonstrated a rectal mass and a right basal pneumonia.

Empirical intravenous flucloxacillin was started for presumed cellulitis. Over the first week, fever rose to 40  degreesC, C

title: Permissive Hypotension in a Patient with Severe Hypernatremia: A Case Report
journal: Clinical Practice and Cases in Emergency Medicine
case_summary:
A 64-year-old woman with contractures of unknown etiology, osteoporosis, psoriatic arthritis, bullous pemphigoid, paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia, hypertension, and mild cognitive impairment, who is a nursing-home resident with limited mobility, was brought to the emergency department because of three days of decreased oral intake, progressive confusion, and generalized weakness. Family noted she had skipped meals at a birthday celebration and had "off" speech on the phone. On the day of presentation, a nurse found her confused and unable to call her family, and EMS was activated.  

On arrival her blood pressure was 101/64 mm Hg (30 minutes later 75/63 mm Hg), heart rate 137 beats/minute, respiratory rate 34 breaths/minute, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, temperature 36.6  degreesC, and weight 35.3 kg. She was confused, moaning, and withdrawing to pain. She appeared cachectic with bitemporal wasting, sunken eyes, and had purulent right ocular drainage. Mucous membranes were

title: Endovascular management of renal artery aneurysm rupture in pregnancy - A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 41-year-old primigravida at 22 weeks' gestation (conceived via IVF for endometriosis) presented with sudden-onset severe left-sided abdominal pain of 90 minutes' duration while sitting, associated with nausea and dizziness but no urinary or gastrointestinal symptoms. She reported normal fetal movements. She had hypermobile wrists but no other signs of connective tissue disorder and no relevant personal or family history.

On examination she was afebrile and tachycardic, with local peritonism in the left renal angle and left iliac fossa despite opioid analgesia. Her body habitus was slim. The uterus was soft and non-tender. Vaginal examination showed a closed os without discharge or bleeding, with extreme tenderness on left adnexal palpation. There were no palpable abdominal masses and bowel sounds were normal.

Arterial blood gas analysis revealed a deep metabolic and lactic acidosis with respiratory compensation, which partially responded to fluid resuscitation. Urinalysis showed no

title: Black Urine and Methemoglobinemia in the Setting of Sepsis Due to Clostridium Perfringens
case_summary:
A 50-year-old man presented to the emergency department with diffuse body pain of 2 hours' duration. He described severe (10/10) abdominal pain, worsening at his usual sites of metastasis--including the jaw, bilateral scapulae, chest wall, and left inguinal region--and unrelieved by his home analgesics. His medical history was notable for hypothyroidism and metastatic melanoma (brain, lung, liver, and bone); he had completed seven cycles of carboplatin-paclitaxel several months earlier, with recent disease progression.  

Home medications included temozolomide, esomeprazole, acetylcysteine, atorvastatin, dexamethasone, gabapentin, levetiracetam, levothyroxine, magnesium lactate-pyridoxine, metoclopramide, ondansetron, oxycodone, and sitagliptin-metformin.  

On examination, he appeared ill and in pain but was alert and oriented. Vital signs were: temperature 37.3 degreesC, blood pressure 126/96 mmHg, heart rate 105 beats per minute, and SpO2 95% on room air. Cardiovascular and pulmonar

title: Segmental Arterial Mediolysis (SAM) Leading to Chronic Renal Insufficiency
journal: International Journal of Nephrology and Renovascular Disease
case_summary:
A 45-year-old Caucasian man with a history of chronic back pain and erectile dysfunction, social alcohol use, and occasional smoking presented with 3 days of progressively worsening right lower-quadrant abdominal pain and headaches.
