Initial vital signs were notable for a blood pressure of 124/86 mmHg; serum creatinine was 1.0 mg/dL (eGFR 90 mL/min/1.73 m2). Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated a right lower-pole renal infarct and perivascular inflammation around the celiac artery. Workup for thromboembolism (ECG, transthoracic echocardiography with bubble study, D-dimer, coagulation studies) revealed no abnormalities. Serologic evaluation for systemic vasculitis (ESR, CRP, ANCA, ANA) was negative. He was discharged on apixaban.

Sixteen days later, he returned with recurrent right flank pain, blood pressure 151/88 mmHg, creatinine 1.3 mg/dL (eGFR 65.7), ESR 32 mm/h, CRP 12.3 mg/dL. Urinalysis was bland. CT angiography showed extension of the right renal infarct i

title: Diagnosing rare intraductal biliary neoplasms - Intraductal papillary neoplasm of the bile duct: A case report with typical imaging findings
case_summary:
A 45-year-old woman presented with a three-month history of a dull, dragging abdominal pain. She denied weight loss, jaundice, fever, pruritus, anorexia, or pale stools. On examination, she had hepatomegaly without a palpable gallbladder mass. Laboratory tests showed mild elevations in total bilirubin (1.5 mg/dL; direct 0.9 mg/dL), ALT 100 IU/L, AST 90 IU/L, and ALP 198 IU/L. Tumor markers were within normal limits (CEA 1.5 ng/mL; AFP 13 ng/mL; CA 19-9 18 IU/mL). The white-cell count was 6000/uL with eosinophils <5%.

Transabdominal ultrasound revealed a large, well-defined cystic lesion in the left biliary segments, with dilated segmental ducts and an irregular isoechoic papillary mural thickening projecting into the lumen; no vascularity was seen. Hydatid (IgG 0.3; normal <0.9) and amoebic (IgG 0.47; normal <0.9) serologies were negative.

Contrast-enhanced MRI of the upper abdomen demonstrated:
 ? A well-defined cyst at the hepatic hilum containing an isointense, frond-like soft tis

title: Segmental arterial mediolysis: a rare cause of an acute abdomen
case_summary:
A 43-year-old man presented with sudden-onset epigastric pain radiating to the umbilical region, accompanied by nausea and vomiting. He was afebrile and hypertensive (160/103 mmHg). Examination revealed a soft abdomen with mild epigastric tenderness. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 14.9?10^9/L, neutrophils 11.46?10^9/L, lactate 2.5 mmol/L, and otherwise unremarkable hematologic and metabolic panels. 
Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated narrowing and hypodensity of the superior mesenteric artery just distal to its origin, raising concern for thrombus. CT angiography revealed fat stranding around the artery, nonatherosclerotic segmental narrowing, post-stenotic dilatation, and a dissection flap. Based on these findings, a working diagnosis of vasculitis was made, and the patient was treated with low-molecular-weight heparin and intravenous steroids. A vasculitic screen was negative. He was discharged on rivaroxaban and a tapering dose of oral stero

title: CT diagnosis and endovascular management of spontaneous rupture of uterine artery in pregnancy
case_summary:
A 30-year-old primigravida at 34 weeks of gestation, with no significant past medical or surgical history, woke with sudden onset of stabbing lower abdominal pain, vomiting, and lightheadedness. She reported an absence of fetal movements and denied trauma or vaginal bleeding. On arrival, her blood pressure was 70/40 mmHg; heart rate was normal. She was fluid resuscitated. Examination showed a diffusely tender, distended abdomen with cold, clammy extremities. Obstetric evaluation revealed a closed cervix, no signs of labor, and absent fetal heart sounds. Artificial rupture of membranes yielded clear fluid. Bedside ultrasound confirmed no fetal heartbeat or movement. A provisional diagnosis of placental abruption with fetal demise was made. Six hours later, the stillborn infant and placenta were delivered vaginally. 

A few hours postpartum, the patient developed severe abdominal pain similar to her initial pain, became hypotensive and tachycardic, and had generalized abdominal distensio

title: Interstitial pneumonitis secondary to leuprorelin acetate for prostate cancer
case_summary:
A 66-year-old Caucasian man with metastatic prostate cancer presented with a two-month history of gradually worsening shortness of breath, nonproductive cough, and fatigue. He was a retired non-smoker with no history of chemical exposures and a past medical history significant for gastroesophageal reflux and osteoarthritis. One week after starting cyproterone acetate (50 mg twice daily), he noticed mild dyspnea on exertion (Modified Borg Dyspnoea Scale 3), malaise, and fatigue. Initial examination, chest radiography, and inflammatory markers were normal, and he was managed as an outpatient. Five weeks later, he received an intramuscular injection of leuprorelin acetate (30 mg), and one day afterward he developed acute severe dyspnea (Modified Borg Dyspnoea Scale 8), extreme exhaustion, and a drop in exercise tolerance from several kilometers to 50 m. He remained immobile for five days before presenting to the hospital.

On admission he was afebrile, with arterial oxygen saturation of 9

title: Obstructive shock in a 47 year old female with a deep venous thrombosis due to intravascular leiomyomatosis: a case report
case_summary:
A 47-year-old Asian woman was transferred with a two-month history of episodic chest pain, dyspnea, bilateral lower-extremity swelling, cyanosis, and transient loss of consciousness. Two years earlier she had undergone an abdominal hysterectomy for uterine fibroids. Two months before transfer she had a brief loss of consciousness; two weeks before she noted gradual pleuritic chest pain and leg edema; and one day before she developed shortness of breath and a nonproductive cough. She had no known risk factors for thromboembolism.

At the outside hospital she appeared cyanotic and hypotensive, with a heart rate of 110 bpm, systolic blood pressure of 70 mm?Hg, respiratory rate of 34 breaths per minute, and dusky skin. An electrocardiogram and serum troponin were normal. Arterial blood gas on 100% oxygen showed pH 7.25, PaCO? 22 mm?Hg, and PaO? 506 mm?Hg. The leukocyte count was 12.9?10?/?L; other hematologic, renal, liver, and coagulation studies were within normal limits. A head CT scan

title: May-Thurner Syndrome--a Rare Cause of Extensive Pelvic DVT, but Is there More to Know?
case_summary: A 28-year-old African American man with a history of tobacco use presented with a 3-day history of lumbar back pain and posterior left lower extremity pain. He denied recent trauma, prolonged immobilization, prior thromboembolism, or a family history of clotting disorders. Vital signs showed a heart rate of 104 bpm. On examination, the left thigh was warm and tender, with moderate nonpitting edema of the left lower extremity. Laboratory testing revealed a white-cell count of 10.9 ?10^3/uL, platelets 743 ?10^3/uL, C-reactive protein 286 mg/L, lactate 2.6 mmol/L, and a D-dimer >4.40 ug/mL. An electrocardiogram showed left ventricular hypertrophy and sinus tachycardia. Point-of-care ultrasound of the left leg demonstrated noncompressibility of the left common femoral vein, the saphenofemoral junction, the confluence of the superficial and deep femoral veins, and the popliteal vein. Computed tomography angiography of the chest was negative for pulmonary embolism. Computed tomography angiog

title: Morel-Lavall?e lesion diagnosed 25 years after blunt trauma
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: A 44-year-old Hispanic man with no significant medical history presented with progressive pain, a sensation of heaviness, and difficulty walking due to swelling of his left thigh. At age 19, he had sustained blunt trauma in a four-track vehicle accident and had been diagnosed with a left-thigh hematoma, which had been drained at that time. Twenty-five years later, he noted gradual onset of left-thigh swelling. On ultrasound, there was a lobulated hypoechoic fluid collection without internal vascularity in the left lateral thigh. MRI demonstrated a well-defined lesion between the fascia and subcutaneous fat of the left lateral thigh, with centrally hyperintense signal suggestive of proteinaceous fluid, heterogeneous nodular components, and a hypointense peripheral rim. He underwent open biopsy and drainage; the specimen contained pleomorphic eosinophilic material with inflammatory cells and proteinaceous debris, and both Gram stain and cultures were negative. One month later, he develop

title: Collet-Sicard Syndrome from Thrombosis of the Sigmoid-Jugular Complex: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_summary:
A 32-year-old man presented with a gradual-onset, severe right-sided headache that resolved spontaneously within 24 hours, followed by dysphagia, dysphonia, and right-sided neck pain. He reported a recent upper respiratory infection with frontal sinus pain, fevers, and mucopurulent nasal discharge. He had no significant medical history, denied illicit drug use, was a non-smoker, and drank alcohol moderately.

On examination, the right neck was tender along the deep cervical chain without lymphadenopathy. Ear, nose, and neck movements were normal. Neurologic examination revealed dysfunction of the right cranial nerves IX, X, and XII: absent right gag reflex, right soft palate paralysis with uvula deviation to the left, hypotonic right tongue deviated to the right on protrusion, and right vocal cord palsy with pooling of saliva in the hypopharynx. The trapezius and sternocleidomastoid muscles had normal strength, and the remainder of the neurologic examination was normal. Audiometry was

title: Endosalpingiosis of urinary bladder: report on a rare entity
case_summary:
A 46-year-old woman presented with right flank pain, dysuria, and hematuria. Her past medical history was notable for an abdominal hysterectomy 15 years earlier. General and pelvic examinations were unremarkable. Urinalysis showed 6-8 red blood cells and 4-6 pus cells per high-power field but no other abnormalities.

An ultrasound of the abdomen and pelvis revealed a focal hypoechoic polypoidal lesion measuring 3.4 ? 2.8 cm along the anterosuperior wall of the urinary bladder, containing tiny hyperechoic foci; the outline of the adjacent bladder wall appeared indistinct. The uterus was not visualized; a cystic area was noted in the left adnexa, and both ovaries were not seen.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed a well-defined soft-tissue-density lesion with calcific specks along the anterosuperior bladder wall that demonstrated heterogeneous enhancement in the early arterial and venous phases. The intraluminal component merged with the bladder wall, causing minimal l

title: Diagnosis of unicuspid aortic valve after loss of consciousness during cesarean section: A case report
case_summary: A 35-year-old woman (G2P1) at 38 weeks' gestation was admitted for a repeat elective cesarean delivery under spinal anesthesia. Her past medical and obstetrical history were unremarkable; preoperative electrocardiography was normal. Initial vital signs after spinal anesthesia (0.5% hyperbaric bupivacaine 11 mg with fentanyl 10 ug intrathecally plus prophylactic phenylephrine infusion) were stable: BP 120/70 mmHg, HR 60 beats/min, SpO2 99%. Twelve minutes into the operation, during placental removal, she suddenly lost consciousness with up-rolling of the eyes, developed apnea, and her SpO2 fell to 88%. Mask ventilation with 100% oxygen was initiated, and spontaneous respiration returned after 2 minutes, with BP 105/67 mmHg and HR 89 beats/min. On recovery, she was asymptomatic, pupils were equal and reactive, and there were no focal neurologic deficits. Estimated blood loss was 740 mL, and arterial blood gases remained normal. Given concerns for pulmonary or amniotic fluid embolism and

title: Role of advanced imaging in primary hepatic neuroendocrine tumor with borderline raised AFP and negative chromogranin staining: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: A 68-year-old man presented with a 4-day history of abdominal fullness and discomfort and a 2-week history of reduced appetite. He had hypertension managed with amlodipine and no history of liver disease. On examination, he had minimal right upper quadrant tenderness without rebound or guarding. Laboratory testing showed a GGT of 264 U/L (normal 11-49 U/L), elevated ALP, SGOT, and SGPT, and a borderline AFP of 17.5 ng/mL (normal 0-15 ng/mL). Abdominal ultrasound revealed mildly altered hepatic echotexture and a non-obstructive right renal calculus. A triphasic contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen demonstrated an irregular exophytic lesion with solid and cystic components in segment III of the left hepatic lobe measuring 76 ? 64 ? 93 mm; the lesion showed minimal heterogeneous arterial enhancement with progressive enhancement in the portal and venous phases and exerted mass effect on the stomach. To exclude an extrahepatic primary malignancy, upper and lower gastrointestinal endoscopy, a

title: Drug-Drug Interaction of Ergotamine with a Combination of Darunavir, Abacavir, and Lamivudine Causing a Fatal Vasospastic Ischemia
case_summary:
A 50-year-old woman with HIV for 6 years, newly started 2 weeks ago on abacavir-lamivudine and darunavir, was admitted with 4 days of lethargy and a painful cold cyanotic right hand. Her partner found her very drowsy on the floor and summoned emergency services. Earlier that week, a general practitioner treated her arm pain as lateral epicondylitis with NSAIDs. 

On admission, her Glasgow Coma Scale was 13 (conscious but sleepy, slow speech, oriented), cranial nerves intact. She had a cyanotic right hand up to the wrist and cyanotic toes on the right foot; no pulses were palpable in any limb; no edema. Blood pressure was 140/70 mmHg, heart rate 80/min, oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Abdominal exam was unremarkable; hyperactive bowel sounds; no fever; normal urine output.

Laboratory studies showed WBC 11.5?10^9/L, CRP 24 mg/dL, CK 2285 U/L, sodium 121 mEq/L, chloride 87 mEq/L, lactate 0.7 mmol/L; troponins were negative; D-dimer was 2.2 mg/L. HIV viral load was 42 copies/mL and CD4

title: An unexpected cause of a swollen pacemaker pocket: a case report
case_summary: A 70-year-old man with extensive nicotine use and a remote history of laryngeal squamous cell carcinoma (7 years prior) and bladder urothelial carcinoma (10 years prior) underwent dual-chamber pacemaker implantation for sick sinus syndrome 9 months ago. Postoperative checks on days 1, 2, and at 3 months showed normal pacemaker function and a clinically unremarkable pocket. Three weeks before presentation, he tested positive for COVID-19 infection, but his fatigue, dyspnea, and cough persisted and worsened rather than improving. At a routine cardiology visit 9 months after implantation, he reported a new, painless but bothersome swelling over the pacemaker site. On examination, his blood pressure and heart rate were normal, and there were no signs of cardiac decompensation or cachexia. Chest ultrasound revealed a soft tissue mass with increased vascularity surrounding the device. CT imaging confirmed a soft-tissue mass around the pacemaker pocket, and haematoma or infection was excluded

title: Vanishing Bile Duct Syndrome in a Patient with Uterine Cancer and Paraneoplastic Systemic Sclerosis
case_summary:
A 55-year-old woman with a history of hypertension presented with two months of progressive dyspnea and bilateral lower-extremity edema. She denied chest pain, abdominal pain, gastrointestinal symptoms, fevers, or weight loss. She was a lifetime nonsmoker and drank alcohol socially (<2 drinks/month). Medications included losartan 50 mg daily.

On admission, liver-function tests showed an alkaline phosphatase of >500 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 70 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 65 U/L, total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL, and an international normalized ratio of 1.16. A CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast revealed a uterine mass. Endometrial biopsy confirmed endometrial carcinoma. Workup for liver disease included a negative hepatitis panel, normal iron studies, negative smooth-muscle antibody, and an unremarkable right-upper-quadrant ultrasound.

During outpatient evaluation, her alkaline phosphatase rose to 714 U/L, with aspartate aminotransferase 120 U/L, alanine aminotr

title: Bilateral Adrenal Hemorrhage in a Patient with Antiphospholipid Syndrome during Chronic Graft-versus-host Disease
case_summary: A 56-year-old man with follicular non-Hodgkin's lymphoma underwent allogeneic HSCT in 2006 complicated by acute and chronic GVHD, and subsequently developed antiphospholipid syndrome manifesting as pulmonary embolism and deep vein thrombosis. He was maintained on warfarin. In 2015, he developed 2 days of rhinorrhea, cough, sore throat, right upper abdominal pain relieved by sitting upright, nausea, and anorexia. He was afebrile; blood pressure was 135/58 mmHg. Examination showed mild upper-abdominal tenderness without rash or petechiae. On day 3, platelet count was 128?10^3/?L, PT/INR 1.97, aPTT 98.5 seconds, and a mixing test remained positive for lupus anticoagulant. By day 5, he was hypotensive (95/64 mmHg), sodium was 119 mmol/L, CRP 11.75 mg/dL, and aPTT 112.7 seconds. Blood cultures and fungal, mycobacterial, and viral antigen tests were negative. CT on day 3 was unremarkable; repeat CT on day 5 showed bilateral adrenal enlargement with hemorrhagic features. T2-weighted MRI confi

title: Chronic Ischemic Gastritis in a Patient With a History of Cancer and Atherosclerotic Disease
case_summary: A 74-year-old woman with a history of breast cancer, carotid atherosclerosis, hypertension, and a 40-pack-year smoking history presented with a 5-month history of postprandial abdominal pain and vomiting associated with a 10-kg weight loss. Physical examination showed sarcopenia and tenderness in the epigastrium without guarding or rebound; examination of the heart, lungs, and extremities was unremarkable. Laboratory studies revealed vitamin D deficiency and a modest elevation in transaminases; other values, including pancreatic enzymes, were within normal limits. An abdominal ultrasound demonstrated gallbladder calculi without signs of acute cholecystitis. Because of her oncologic history, an esophagogastroduodenoscopy was performed; it showed diffuse gastric mucosal hyperemia, multiple ulcers with signs of recent bleeding, and no mass lesions. Histopathology revealed chronic active gastritis with negative staining for Helicobacter pylori, and the patient denied any use of nonsteroida

title: Gastric Adenocarcinoma Presenting as Chronic Back Pain: A Case Report
journal: Clinical Practice and Cases in Emergency Medicine
case_summary:
A 76-year-old woman with a history of hypertension and hypothyroidism presented to the emergency department with intermittent left upper back pain for one year that acutely worsened over the past two days. She denied chest pain, dyspnea, fever, chills, or gastrointestinal bleeding but admitted to occasional heartburn and an unintentional 10-pound weight loss over six months. Four days earlier, she had flown into the area and reported a brief episode of pallor with weakness, lightheadedness, and nausea. She had no trauma history.

On arrival, her temperature was 36.9  degreesC, heart rate 90 bpm, blood pressure 101/76 mm Hg, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. She appeared mildly uncomfortable but non-toxic. Conjunctival pallor was noted. The lungs were clear; cardiovascular exam was normal. The abdomen was soft with mild epigastric tenderness but no guarding or masses. The back was atraumatic with no vertebral, paraspinal, or scapular tenderness and

title: Isolated extratesticular hematoma from intraoperative positioning during lumbar spinal surgery
case_summary: A 33-year-old man was admitted after a frontal motor-vehicle collision with airbag deployment. He had a brief loss of consciousness, a mild headache, and moderate low-back pain radiating to the left leg. On examination, he had a small forehead bruise, lumbar paraspinal tenderness, and could not lift his left leg more than 6 inches due to pain, though strength, sensation, and plantar and dorsiflexion were intact. Initial CT showed an acute traumatic pseudoaneurysm of the thoracic aorta, traumatic subluxation of L5 on S1, and a left L5 transverse-process fracture. MRI demonstrated severe spinal canal narrowing at L5-S1 with soft-tissue edema, a hematoma at the left L5 neural foramen, and nerve expansion. The aortic injury was successfully stented. On hospital day 3, he underwent posterior lumbar decompression with fusion and skin-flap reconstruction in the prone position for 5 hours. At the end of the procedure, the scrotum was noted to be trapped between the right thigh and the table, w

title: Hepatoid adenocarcinoma: A wolf in hepatocellular carcinoma's clothing
journal: JGH Open: An Open Access Journal of Gastroenterology and Hepatology
case_summary: A 57-year-old woman with Child-Pugh B cirrhosis from prior alcohol excess underwent routine surveillance. Her ?-fetoprotein level was 5330 mcg/L (normal <9 mcg/L), but liver ultrasound showed no focal lesion. A multiphase contrast-enhanced CT scan revealed a 4 cm lesion in segment 2 of the liver that lacked arterial-phase enhancement and showed only peripheral enhancement in the portal-venous phase. A follow-up liver MRI confirmed a similar 4 cm lesion in segment 2 without arterial enhancement and with peripheral portal-venous enhancement. No other lesions were identified.

title: Gastrointestinal Beriberi Mimicking a Surgical Emergency in a Well-Nourished Patient: A?Case Report
journal: Mayo Clinic Proceedings: Innovations, Quality & Outcomes
case_summary:
An 81-year-old man with a history of rheumatoid arthritis on prednisone and no history of alcohol abuse presented in spring 2018 to the emergency department with severe lethargy and was intubated for airway protection. He was hypotensive, requiring norepinephrine support. On examination, his abdomen was soft but distended. Laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 10.8?10^9/L with a neutrophil shift, a creatinine level of 2.1 mg/dL (baseline unknown), and metabolic acidosis (arterial pH 7.29, PCO2 39 mm Hg, bicarbonate 18 mmol/L, base deficit 7 mmol/L). The serum lactate concentration was 6.8 mmol/L. Liver function tests were within normal limits, and the serum albumin level was 2.3 g/dL. Computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis was unremarkable. Transthoracic echocardiography showed no thrombus and a left ventricular ejection fraction of 45%. Ammonia levels, acetaminophen values, and a comprehensive toxin panel were unremarkable.  
Because of worsening lactic acidosis

title: Aggressive vascular tumor mimicking posttraumatic hematoma: A case report of kaposiform hemangioendothelioma on the nose
case_summary:
A 2-year-old boy was referred for reevaluation of a nasal purpuric lesion that persisted 6 months after he fell on his nose. Immediately after the fall, a hematoma formed on the nasal dorsum, but the lesion failed to resolve and even expanded during heat exposure. There was no prior nasal abnormality.

On examination, there was a firm, red-purple swelling of the skin and subcutaneous tissue over the nasal dorsum extending into the paranasal area, with small pearls of clear fluid on the surface. Endonasal inspection revealed congested mucosa and crusting. Vital signs were normal, and laboratory tests showed no thrombocytopenia or coagulopathy.

Magnetic resonance imaging showed subcutaneous infiltration of the nasal dorsum extending into the nasal cavity and anterior ethmoid sinus, with bright heterogeneous enhancement on T1-weighted images after gadolinium and no connection to intracranial dural sinuses. Doppler ultrasound revealed rich vascularization without signs of lymphangioma or

title: Painless Livedoid Vasculopathy in a Patient with G20210A Prothrombin Gene Mutation
case_summary:
An 87?year?old Caucasian woman was admitted for chronic painless nonhealing skin ulcers over both malleoli and bilateral shin swelling of approximately one year's duration, which had worsened over several weeks. She denied fever, chills, dyspnea, chest pain, cough, abdominal pain, or changes in bowel habits. Her history was notable for hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and resection of a right middle lobe lung mass more than ten years earlier. Her medications included spironolactone, allopurinol, aspirin, carvedilol, rosuvastatin, and pentoxifylline.

On examination, there were two lateral malleolar ulcers bilaterally, each about 1 mm deep, 1 cm high, and 0.8 mm in diameter, with sharp margins and slight erythema but no discharge or induration. Bilateral lower leg varicosities and shin edema were present without warmth or calf tenderness; Homan sign was negative. Pedal and posterior tibial pulses were palpable. Cardiovascular, pulmonary, abdominal, neurologic, HEENT, and ophthalmologic exa

title: Pelvic Actinomycosis and Diagnostic Complexity: Case Report with Literature Review
case_summary:
A 48-year-old woman presented to the emergency department in March 2023 with a several-day history of progressive abdominal pain, distension, and features of intestinal obstruction. She was afebrile and had no significant past medical history. 

Abdominopelvic CT scan revealed an enlarged right adnexa containing multiple fluid-filled cysts; thickening of the left uterine wall contiguous with the left bladder wall; colonic wall thickening with hyperemia; and heterogeneous peritoneal fluid collections. 

On physical examination, the abdomen was distended and tender. Bimanual pelvic examination demonstrated indeterminate solid tissue and firm adnexal adhesions. Transvaginal ultrasound showed a thin, linear endometrium; an enlarged right ovary; and the left ovary densely adherent to the sigmoid colon.

Laboratory studies revealed leukocytosis (white blood cell count 15.02 ? 10^3/uL) and elevated C-reactive protein (40.49 mg/L). 

Given the high suspicion for a gynecologic malignancy based

title: Anticoagulation therapy for pulmonary embolism involving a myxoma mimicking, giant type C thrombus: A case report
case_summary: A 76-year-old man with essential hypertension, treated daily with lercanidipine and valsartan, presented after 8 days of progressive dyspnea preceded by blood-tinged sputum. He denied fever, chills, night sweats, chest pain, or toxic exposures. On examination: T 36.6  degreesC; HR 108 bpm; RR 26 /min; BP 125/72 mmHg; SpO? 89% on room air. Bilateral basal fine moist rales and unilateral lower limb swelling were noted; there were no signs of right-sided heart failure. Laboratory studies showed WBC 4.3?10?/L (neutrophils 69.9%), hemoglobin 115 g/L, CRP 36.7 mg/L, NT-proBNP 525 pg/mL, and D-dimer 2.25 mg/L (upper limit 0.05 mg/dL). Arterial blood gas on FiO? 35% revealed PaO? 78 mmHg, PaCO? 38 mmHg, and SaO? 94%. Immunological tests and tumor markers were normal. CTPA demonstrated extensive bilateral pulmonary emboli in the main, lobar, and segmental pulmonary arteries. Bedside transthoracic echocardiography revealed a 5.85?4.32 cm mobile mass attached to the posterior wall of the right at

title: Anti-Tubular Basement Membrane Antibody Nephritis Manifesting in a Patient With Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia: A Very Rare Case Report
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_summary: A 69-year-old man with hypertension, cutaneous basal cell carcinoma, and prostatic adenocarcinoma treated with proton therapy and leuprolide, and newly diagnosed chronic lymphocytic leukemia was referred for nephrology evaluation due to progressive worsening of kidney function. Three months earlier his serum creatinine was 1.1 mg/dL; at presentation it was 2.5 mg/dL, and he had a mild non-anion gap metabolic acidosis. He was not taking any medications and had no history of urinary retention or obstruction. A CT scan showed a homogeneously enhancing soft-tissue mass in the mediastinum and retroperitoneum, measuring 9.7 ? 8.3 cm, encasing the aorta, celiac axis, superior mesenteric and renal arteries, and partially involving the inferior vena cava. Initial considerations included monoclonal gammopathy, tumor lysis syndrome, and renal ischemia from vascular encasement. Laboratory studies showed normal serum uric acid, lactate dehydrogenase, phosphorus, and calcium levels. Serologic tests

title: Case Report: Klippel-Trenaunay Syndrome - Recurrent Venous Thromboembolism and Vascular Malformation
case_summary:
A 27-year-old man, long labeled as having Ehlers-Danlos syndrome since age 10, was referred for evaluation of recurrent unprovoked pulmonary embolism and deep venous thrombosis. He reported right lower extremity varicose veins, multiple lipomas, and hemihypertrophy. His mother first noted limb asymmetry at age 4 years. 

On examination, vital signs were normal. Cardiac and respiratory exams were unremarkable. He had a high-arched palate, small fused tongue, hyperelastic skin, and generalized joint hypermobility. An anterior abdominal mass was palpable. Musculoskeletal exam revealed hypertrophy of the left-hand middle finger, bilateral genu valgum, right leg hemihypertrophy, prominent varicose veins, and multiple lipomas.

Laboratory tests, including inherited and acquired thrombophilia panels (protein C, protein S, antithrombin III, lupus anticoagulant, antiphospholipid antibodies, Factor V Leiden, and prothrombin G20210A mutation), were all negative.

Doppler ultrasonography of the ri

title: Gastric intramural hematoma subsequent to thoracic aortic dissection: Case report and literature review
case_summary:
An 87-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with acute, intense thoracic pain radiating to her back and upper abdomen, accompanied by nausea, dyspnea, sweating, and a hypertensive spike. Her history included hypertension and prior hip arthroplasty complicated by pulmonary embolism, for which she received temporary anticoagulation and then daily aspirin with cilostazol. On examination, her chest radiograph showed an enlarged mediastinum. Despite morphine and nitroglycerin, her pain persisted but she remained hemodynamically stable. Cardiac enzymes and electrocardiogram were normal, and the D-dimer was elevated at 8.02 ?g/mL. CT angiography of the chest demonstrated ectasia of the ascending aorta (4.0 cm), an aneurysm of the descending aorta with mural thrombus, and mediastinal fluid, without evidence of pulmonary embolism. Echocardiography revealed mild aortic insufficiency. She was admitted to the ICU and managed conservatively.

Three days later, she developed severe ep

title: Epstein-barr virus (EBV)-positive inflammatory pseudotumor-like follicular dendritic cell sarcoma (IPT-like FDCS) presenting as thrombocytopenia: A case report and literature review
final_diagnosis: Inflammatory pseudotumor-like follicular dendritic cell sarcoma
case_summary: A 67-year-old woman presented with a one-month history of bleeding from the mouth, nose, and skin. Two weeks earlier, imaging had detected a splenic mass. She had no prior medical history. On examination, the spleen was not palpable. Laboratory testing revealed severe thrombocytopenia (platelet count, 1 ? 10^9/L), which did not respond to recombinant human thrombopoietin, intravenous immunoglobulin, or methylprednisolone. A contrast-enhanced abdominal CT scan showed splenomegaly with a 57 ? 58 mm patchy low-density lesion. In the arterial phase, the lesion exhibited moderate peripheral enhancement and central hypoenhancement; in the venous phase, there was slightly reduced enhancement centrally. A preliminary diagnosis of splenic hamartoma was made, and the patient was scheduled for laparoscopic splenectomy.

title: Multiple stenting using anchoring technique with balloon guiding catheter for common carotid artery dissection after aortic arch replacement: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: A 68-year-old man underwent total aortic arch replacement for a distal arch aneurysm. Postoperative contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated a left common carotid artery dissection adjacent to the vascular graft. He was initially asymptomatic and managed conservatively. One month later, he developed recurrent episodes of right-sided hemiparesis lasting less than 24 hours. MRI of the brain showed no infarction. Repeat CT angiography revealed diminished true-lumen flow into the aneurysm but extension of the false lumen into the ipsilateral internal carotid artery. Digital subtraction angiography localized the entry point 7 cm distal to the graft, confirmed persistent false-lumen flow to the internal carotid artery, and demonstrated reduced cerebral blood flow.

title: Peripheral Retinal Neovascularization with Vitreous Hemorrhage in HIV Retinopathy
case_summary:
A 53-year-old African-American woman with AIDS was referred for possible retinitis in her left eye. She had been diagnosed with AIDS eight years earlier during hospitalization for pneumocystis pneumonia, cryptococcal meningitis, and herpes zoster; her CD4+ count at that time was 11. Initial fundus examination then revealed diffuse intraretinal hemorrhages and cotton wool spots in both eyes, and fluorescein angiography showed an enlarged foveal avascular zone without peripheral nonperfusion. In the intervening years, she had a stroke and pulmonary embolism; a hypercoagulability workup was normal except for mildly reduced protein S activity.

Her current complaint was a gradual, painless decrease in vision in her left eye for one week. On examination, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/20 in the right eye and 20/40 in the left. Intraocular pressures were normal. Pupillary reflexes were intact without afferent defect; a left homonymous visual field defect consistent with her prior stroke

title: Emergency bedside cesarean delivery: lessons learned in teamwork and patient safety
case_summary: A 30-year-old G3P2 woman at 38 weeks 6 days of gestation was admitted for elective induction of labor because of concerns about intrapartum anticoagulation. At 10 weeks' gestation she had developed an idiopathic lower-extremity deep venous thrombosis, an inferior vena caval filter was placed, and therapeutic subcutaneous unfractionated heparin was administered for the remainder of pregnancy. Twelve hours before admission her last heparin dose had been given; on admission her activated partial thromboplastin time was 70 seconds (reference, 21-33 seconds). Neuraxial analgesia was contraindicated, and labor was managed with cervical ripening using misoprostol and intermittent intravenous fentanyl. Fetal heart-rate tracings remained reassuring. Six hours later, her aPTT remained prolonged at 80 seconds. After discussion, an intravenous remifentanil patient-controlled analgesia infusion was initiated, with a basal rate of 0.04 ?g/kg per minute (2 ?g/min) and a bolus dose of 0.8 ?g/kg (40 ?g

title: Cesarean Scar Endometriosis: An Uncommon Surgical Complication on the Rise? Case Report and Literature Review
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
case_summary:
A 37?year?old G3P3 woman was referred for evaluation of a subcutaneous mass in her lower abdominal wall. Her obstetrical history included three lower?segment cesarean deliveries--two elective at term without complications and one emergent at 32 weeks for placenta previa hemorrhage with a healthy infant. Seven months after the last cesarean, she noted a slowly enlarging mass beneath the transverse scar, associated with mild pain and occasional brown leakage that coincided with her menses. 

On examination during menstruation, vital signs were normal. Palpation revealed a tender, oval, subcutaneous mass measuring approximately 80 ? 35 mm directly beneath the cesarean scar. At the center of the mass was a 2?mm diameter skin orifice. When the skin on either side of the orifice was compressed, thick brown fluid was expressed. 

A sonographic examination of the abdominal wall showed a heterogeneous hypoechoic mass with scattered echogenic spots and a few small arterial vessels surrounding it

title: Breast to Brain: A Case Report and Literature Review of Leptomeningeal Carcinomatosis
case_summary:
A 72-year-old Hispanic woman was brought to the emergency department by family for 3 days of worsening confusion, urinary incontinence, and difficulty ambulating after an unwitnessed fall. She had been noted to be mildly altered at home 3 weeks earlier but remained independent in activities of daily living. Over the past 3 days, she developed rapid neurologic decline, inability to perform ADLs, urinary incontinence, and echolalia.

Her medical history was significant for breast cancer diagnosed 4 years earlier on screening mammogram. She underwent bilateral mastectomy and axillary lymph node dissection, which revealed invasive lobular carcinoma of the left breast (estrogen-receptor positive, progesterone-receptor positive, HER-2 negative) with nodal metastases (pathologic stage T2N2M0) and ductal carcinoma in situ of the right breast. She received 12 cycles of paclitaxel, external beam radiation to the left axilla, and daily anastrozole for 3 years.

In the ED, noncontrast CT of the he

title: Life-threatening bleeding from dissecting Intramural Hematoma of Esophagus (IHE) treated by trans arterial embolization.
final_diagnosis: dissecting intramural hematoma of the esophagus
case_summary: An elderly man with diabetes, ischemic heart disease, and peripheral vascular disease had sudden cardiovascular collapse after several episodes of hematemesis. He underwent 20 minutes of cardiopulmonary resuscitation with defibrillation and inotropic support, regained circulation, and was admitted to the intensive care unit intubated and ventilated. Upper endoscopy showed marked luminal narrowing of the esophagus due to diffuse submucosal swelling and bluish mucosal discoloration without evidence of perforation, suggesting a submucosal hematoma. Contrast-enhanced CT aortography demonstrated a diffusely swollen esophagus and a small focus of contrast extravasation in the distal esophageal wall on arterial-phase images with progressive pooling on delayed-phase images, indicating active hemorrhage.

title: Prenatal features of aneurysm of the posterior cerebral artery: a case report
case_summary:
A 28-year-old primigravida was referred at 36 weeks of gestation for suspected fetal ventriculomegaly. Her obstetric, medical, and family histories were unremarkable; serologic testing for TORCH infections was negative, and a mid-trimester anatomic survey was normal. 

At 34 weeks, ultrasound showed a male fetus with dilation of the bilateral lateral ventricles (18 mm and 10 mm), as well as the third and fourth ventricles. Adjacent to the left lateral ventricle was a well-circumscribed, round anechoic cyst measuring 13 ? 10 mm; Doppler assessment was not performed at that time. Fetal biometry was appropriate for gestational age.

At 36 weeks and 5 days, ventricular measurements were unchanged, but the left parieto-occipital cyst had enlarged to 32 ? 27 ? 25 mm, with surrounding hyperechoic areas consistent with pericystic hemorrhage; the left lateral ventricle contained an irregular hyperechoic collection suggesting intraventricular hemorrhage. Color and spectral Doppler ultrasound dem

title: Adult Onset Still's Disease: A Case Report with a Rare Clinical Manifestation and Pathophysiological Correlations
case_summary: A 51?year?old never?smoker dentist presented with 15 days of left chest pain, mild exertional dyspnea, dry cough, evening fevers up to 38.5  degreesC, and 5 days of diffuse arthralgias. Her medical and family histories were unremarkable. On admission she was febrile (39.2  degreesC) and tachypneic (25 breaths/min). Chest examination revealed decreased expansion, dullness to percussion, and reduced vesicular sounds at both lung bases. Arterial blood gas showed pH 7.44, PaO? 67 mmHg, PaCO? 29 mmHg. Laboratory studies: WBC 12 000/uL, hemoglobin 12.7 g/dL, ESR 33 mm/h. Chest radiograph demonstrated a left pleural effusion, and empiric ampicillin-sulbactam with azithromycin was started. Thoracentesis yielded 5000 WBC/mm? (38% neutrophils, 30% lymphocytes, 20% eosinophils). Blood, urine, and sputum cultures, stains and cultures of bronchial wash, and tuberculin skin testing were all negative. CT of the chest and abdomen, abdominal ultrasound, mammography, and fiberoptic bronchoscopy ruled ou

title: Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome Following Chemotherapy and Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor Combination in a Patient with Small-Cell Lung Cancer
case_summary:
A 64-year-old man with extensive-stage small-cell lung cancer (T1N2M0), hypertension, ischemic heart disease, and diabetes was admitted for his first cycle of carboplatin (AUC 5) and etoposide (100 mg/m2 on days 1-3) with atezolizumab (1200 mg on day 1). The day after treatment, he was found with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 5/15 and a right upper-limb tonic-clonic seizure that became generalized. Despite clonazepam and levetiracetam, focal seizures persisted, prompting transfer to the intensive care unit. On arrival, his blood pressure was 206/108 mmHg, heart and respiratory rates were elevated, and he was afebrile. Neurologically, he had right hemiplegia, right facial paralysis, and a pyramidal syndrome; blood glucose was normal.

Electroencephalography showed left occipital status epilepticus. Noncontrast and contrast-enhanced brain CT revealed no evidence of stroke, hemorrhage, or metastases; the Circle of Willis was patent with mild carotid bulb stenosis. Brain MRI on a 3 T scann

title: Desmoid fibromatosis post-cervical spine surgical intervention
case_summary:
A 34-year-old woman presented with several months of progressive difficulty swallowing and an enlarging mass on the left side of her neck, associated with pain radiating to her left shoulder and mild shortness of breath. Her history was notable only for cervical spinal surgery with instrumentation at C5-C6 three years earlier; there was no family history of cancer or history of smoking or alcohol use. 

On examination, she was hemodynamically stable. The chest was clear, abdomen soft, and neurological examination normal. There was an immobile, non-tender, non-pulsatile swelling on the left side of the neck.

Laboratory studies revealed a normal blood cell count, electrolytes, renal, liver, and thyroid function tests; the C-reactive protein level was elevated at 21.9?mg/L (normal <5?mg/L), and alpha-fetoprotein was within normal limits.

Cervical spine radiographs showed a well-aligned spine with a radio-opaque metallic disc spacer at C5-C6 and slight rightward tracheal displacement.

title: Spontaneous intramedullary abscesses caused by Streptococcus anginosus: two case reports and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 72-year-old man with hypertension, arthritis, a remote history of Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia, and a history of deep venous thrombosis presented with acute bilateral lower extremity weakness. Four days before admission, he experienced right-sided abdominal pain; an abdominal CT scan was unrevealing. He then developed lower back pain and right lower extremity weakness. While under evaluation at an outside hospital, he acutely lost motor function in the left lower extremity. A spinal MRI showed intrinsic T2 hyperintensity extending from C7 to L1 with prominent intradural, extramedullary serpiginous flow voids. He was transferred for suspected ruptured spinal arteriovenous malformation.

On arrival at our institution, he reported complete sensory loss beginning at the sternum but denied fevers, chills, or night sweats. Examination revealed 5/5 strength in both upper extremities, 0/5 strength in both lower extremities, sensory level at ~T7, and absent rectal tone. A lumbar drain was

title: Idiopathic pseudoaneurysm of the popliteal artery with endovascular treatment: A case report
case_summary: A 90-year-old woman was admitted with acute pain and swelling of her left knee of approximately 24 hours' duration. She was bedridden with multiple comorbidities and unable to provide a reliable history. There was no known history of trauma, surgery, or vascular intervention. On examination, she was emaciated, with incipient flexion contractures of both knees. Vital signs were within normal limits. Examination of the left leg revealed a pulsatile mass in the popliteal fossa and moderate swelling of the lower leg; there were no signs of bone fracture or critical limb ischemia. A lateral radiograph of the left knee showed posterior displacement of a calcified structure in the popliteal fossa but no fracture. Duplex ultrasonography demonstrated a 6.8 cm extraluminal hematoma with central turbulent flow and marginal thrombus, supplied by turbulent flow from a calcified and mildly dilated popliteal artery (0.69 cm diameter) with monophasic distal flow. Based on these findings, a working dia

title: Massive spinal epidural infantile hemangioma, image findings, and treatment: a case report and review of literature
case_summary:
A 3-week-old female neonate was referred for evaluation of lower-extremity weakness. She was born at term (3.145 kg) via cesarean delivery for breech presentation; maternal history was notable only for enoxaparin use for antiphospholipid syndrome. On examination, she had a prolapsed anus, a midline lumbar dimple, absent deep-tendon reflexes, preserved primitive reflexes, flaccid distal paresis of the right leg, proximal and distal paresis of the left leg, and lax anal sphincter tone.

MRI of the brain and spine revealed a large infiltrative, avidly enhancing lesion occupying the spinal canal from T10 to S1 (8.3 cm craniocaudal), with bilateral dumbbell?shaped extraspinal components through expanded neural foramina, abutting the psoas muscles and involving adjacent paraspinal musculature. The mass was hyperintense on T2-weighted images, hypointense on T1-weighted images, and exhibited extensive flow voids; there was no fat signal, and T2 hyperintensity in both psoas muscles suggested po

title: Lateral lung hernia following a low-energy trauma without bony injuries: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: An 82-year-old man presented with a 3-day history of dry cough, shortness of breath, and breath-dependent left-sided chest pain after stumbling and falling onto his left side. His past medical history included arterial hypertension, deep-vein thrombosis, chronic renal failure, and type II diabetes. On examination, there was a large, tender ecchymosis over the left ninth rib and fine inspiratory crackles at the left lung base. Vital signs were normal. Laboratory studies showed a C-reactive protein level of 16 mg/L; other values were unremarkable. A chest radiograph demonstrated left basal opacification without pleural effusion, pneumothorax, or bony injuries. He was treated for presumed left-sided pneumonia with antibiotics for 10 days. Despite therapy, he continued to have chest pain and dyspnea and had a persistently elevated CRP of 51 mg/L. Given his history of deep-vein thrombosis, a CT scan of the thorax was performed to exclude pulmonary embolism.

title: Diagnosis and Management of Spontaneous Lumbar Venous Retroperitoneal Hematoma in Setting of Deep Venous Thrombosis: A Case Report and Algorithm
case_summary: A 58-year-old homeless man with hypertension, type II diabetes, and a history of prior venous thromboembolism presented with 1 week of progressive weakness and fatigue. His only medications were atenolol, lisinopril, and metformin; he was not on anticoagulants. He denied hemoptysis, dyspnea, chest pain, headache, nausea, or vomiting. In the emergency department, his heart rate was 113?bpm and the remainder of his physical examination was unremarkable. He had an infrarenal inferior vena cava (IVC) filter placed 15 years earlier and was unaware of the indication. While in the ED, he suddenly became unresponsive; ACLS was initiated for 11 minutes, after which he was intubated and transferred to the ICU. Serial hemoglobin measurements revealed a drop from 12.3?gm/dL to 6.8?gm/dL. Given concern for acute blood loss, a noncontrast CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was obtained, showing a large left-sided retroperitoneal hematoma contiguous with the aorta and IVC, mass effect on the b

title: Densities in the left innominate vein after removal of an implantable venous device: a case report
case_summary: A 19-year-old woman with a history of acute lymphoblastic leukemia diagnosed at age 15 underwent placement of a left subclavian venous access port with the catheter tip in the superior vena cava. She completed two years of chemotherapy, then relapsed 10 months after the end of treatment. During re-induction chemotherapy, she developed renal failure, encephalopathy, and required mechanical ventilation and dialysis. After recovery she received multiple cytotoxic agents. She later presented with fever and malaise; her C-reactive protein level was 462 mg/L. Differential diagnosis included sinusitis, deep neck infection, and line infection. Blood cultures were negative. CT of the neck showed a submental abscess that was drained but her fever persisted. Three and a half years after placement, her central venous catheter was removed without complication. Cultures of the catheter tip were negative, but her C-reactive protein remained elevated (169 mg/L) despite resolution of fever. Three month

title: Primary hepatic epithelioid hemangioendothelioma masquerading as metastases: A rare case report
case_summary:
A 42-year-old man presented with a 2-month history of dull abdominal pain. He denied fever, jaundice, or vomiting and had no history of alcohol use. On examination, there was a tender, palpable mass in the epigastric region.

Laboratory studies showed elevated alkaline phosphatase, SGPT, and SGOT, with normal alpha-fetoprotein, carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), and Cancer Antigen 19.9. Viral hepatitis serologies (HBsAg, HCV Ag) were negative, and other blood tests were within normal limits.

Abdominal ultrasound revealed hepatomegaly with multiple heterogeneously hypoechoic and mildly hyperechoic lesions of varying sizes in both liver lobes. Color Doppler demonstrated flow signals predominantly at the periphery of the lesions. Shear wave elastography of the lesions showed stiffness values ranging from 140.8 to 159.0 kPa, suggesting a malignant process.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen showed multiple well-defined, heterogeneously hypodense lesions scattered throughout the liver, some

title: Pulmonary arterial venous malformations as primary manifestation of gestational trophoblastic neoplasia
case_summary:
A 33-year-old G4P3013 woman with a recent pregnancy of unknown location initially managed with two doses of methotrexate in April 2018 had a slow decline of ?-hCG to normal levels. In June 2018 she was found to have a pulmonary arteriovenous malformation and underwent coil embolization. She was followed with weekly ?-hCG levels until they were undetectable in July 2018, then was lost to follow-up.

In October 2018 she presented with right shoulder pain, abdominal pain, and shortness of breath. On examination she had abdominal tenderness with rebound. Her quantitative ?-hCG level was 1011 mIU/mL. Transvaginal ultrasound showed free pelvic fluid, and liver enzymes were elevated (AST 193 U/L, ALT 270 U/L). Because of concern for a ruptured ectopic pregnancy, she underwent emergent laparotomy. At surgery there was 650 cc of hemoperitoneum, but both fallopian tubes appeared normal; the bleeding source was a ruptured subcapsular hepatic hematoma without active bleeding at that time.

Postope

title: Testicular adrenal rest tumours in an adult patient with congenital adrenal hyperplasia
case_summary: A 23-year-old man with known congenital adrenal hyperplasia presented with right-sided testicular pain and a large right testicular lump. He reported adherence to prednisolone and fludrocortisone. On examination, both testes were diffusely swollen, enlarged, and nodular; there were no signs of hyperandrogenism or Cushing syndrome. Laboratory studies showed elevated ACTH and 17-hydroxyprogesterone levels, with normal urea, electrolytes, and morning testosterone. Scrotal ultrasound demonstrated that normal testicular parenchyma was entirely replaced by ill-defined hypoechoic hypervascular masses, with gross bilateral enlargement. A CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed no distant metastases, and the partially imaged testes were hyperattenuating and avidly enhanced relative to skeletal muscle. MRI revealed both testes enlarged to 7 cm with lobulated margins, homogeneous iso- to hypointense replacement of parenchyma that was hyperintense to skeletal muscle on T1 and T2, intense

title: Fever of unknown origin as the first presentation of candida vertebral osteomyelitis: A case report
case_summary:
A 70-year-old woman was evaluated for an incidentally discovered distal pancreatic mass. Endoscopic ultrasound with fine-needle aspiration revealed adenocarcinoma. Her medical history was otherwise unremarkable. She underwent distal pancreatectomy, splenectomy, and adrenalectomy for vascular involvement, and a right internal jugular venous port was placed intraoperatively.

On postoperative day 5, she developed fever. Abdominal ultrasonography showed a peripancreatic fluid collection, which was drained under ultrasound guidance; cultures were negative, and she remained on antibiotics with persistent low-grade fever. By day 21, she had high-grade drenching fevers and respiratory distress. Chest CT angiography demonstrated partial thrombosis of the superior vena cava with air bubbles, suspicious for an infected thrombus. The port was removed; cultures of the port grew Candida species on two occasions, while three sets of simultaneous blood cultures were negative. A follow-up abdominal CT

title: Bilateral superficial temporal vein thrombosis after acute carbon monoxide poisoning and prolonged immobilisation: a case report
case_summary:
An 81-year-old woman was found on the floor of her home after being missing for two days. Firefighters measured 180 ppm CO in the atmosphere and 234 ppm in her exhaled air. On arrival at the emergency department, her blood pressure was 143/92 mmHg, pulse 97 beats/min, temperature 35.7  degreesC, and Glasgow Coma Scale score was 13 (eyes to voice, confused but able to answer questions). Her medical history included left breast carcinoma resected 30 years earlier and a known duplicate superior vena cava. She had no personal or family history of venous thromboembolism.

On examination, both temples were swollen and tender; palpation of the scalp over the left parietal bone worsened the pain. Temporal pulses were palpable, and sinus palpation was nonpainful. Cardiopulmonary and upper airway examinations were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed a carboxyhemoglobin level of 13% (normal 0.5-1.5%), arterial blood gas with PaO? 31.3 mmHg (35-40 mmHg), PaCO? 38.9 mmHg (36-44 mmHg), oxyhemog

title: Recurrence of Primary Vascular Leiomyosarcoma Five Years after Initial Diagnosis in the Lower Extremity
case_summary: A 58-year-old woman presented with a three-week history of a painless mass in her left leg accompanied by lower-extremity edema. On examination, a firm mass was palpable deep to the skin along the course of the short saphenous vein. There were no other skin lesions or systemic symptoms. Routine laboratory tests were within normal limits. Venous ultrasonography revealed a luminal mass in the short saphenous vein. Magnetic resonance imaging of the leg showed a 15 ? 10 ? 7 mm heterogeneous intravascular mass extending toward the Achilles tendon, findings that were thought to represent thrombosis of the short saphenous vein.

title: Adenoid cystic carcinoma of the trachea mimicking asthma
case_summary:
A 58-year-old woman with a history of bronchial asthma since childhood, well controlled without hospital admission or physician review for over 10 years, was admitted with 2 days of worsening dyspnoea associated with mild wheezing and cough. She denied weight loss or chest pain. On examination, she was tachypnoeic but speaking in sentences, with diffuse wheeze on chest auscultation and no stridor. There was no salivary gland or lymph node enlargement. She was treated as an asthma exacerbation but did not improve and remained hypoxic despite therapy. To exclude pulmonary embolism, a CT pulmonary angiogram was obtained, which showed:  
? A 12 mm mass protruding into the posterior aspect of the trachea with enlarged pre-carinal and subcarinal lymph nodes  
? Complete collapse of the left lower lobe and right middle lobe  
? A right upper lobe pulmonary embolism  
She was also noted to have difficulty clearing tracheal secretions despite initial measures.

title: A case report of MoCD etiology in a neonate: A novel homozygous MoCS2 variant
case_summary: A male neonate was born at 36 weeks 6 days' gestation to consanguineous parents by cesarean delivery after an uneventful pregnancy. Apgar scores were 8 and 9. Birth measurements were normal, and he was discharged home after 24 h in good health. On day 3 of life, he presented with poor feeding and "frequent blinking." The mother then witnessed episodes of rapid, pedaling-like "rowing" movements with tonic posturing of the left upper limb, intense face flushing, fixed staring gaze, tachycardia to 190 beats/min, and oxygen desaturation lasting 30 s, which ceased after 0.1 mg/kg IV lorazepam. On examination he was icteric, lethargic, hypoactive, and had an absent suck reflex. An arterial blood gas showed metabolic acidosis. He was admitted to the NICU, where seizures recurred and were refractory to 0.1 mg/kg lorazepam and 40 mg/kg levetiracetam; elective intubation and IV midazolam (0.2 mg/kg bolus, 5 ?g/kg/min infusion) then stabilized the seizures and improved the acidosis. Initial labs

title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia Presenting with Asymptomatic Liver Lesions and a History of Early-onset Myocardial Infarction and Multiple Intracranial Aneurysms
case_summary:
A 49-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of an incidental low-density area on liver CT, initially suspected to represent peliosis hepatis. Her history included an acute myocardial infarction treated with percutaneous coronary intervention at age 49 and treatment of a large left internal carotid aneurysm and right middle cerebral artery aneurysm at age 45. Laboratory studies showed a normal blood count and no elevation in liver enzymes. Contrast-enhanced CT and MRI of the liver revealed an early and persistently enhancing hypervascular nodule in segment 7, multiple small enhancing lesions consistent with telangiectases, and early opacification of the portal and hepatic veins, indicating arterioportal and arteriovenous shunts. There were no signs of portal hypertension, although the portal vein was dilated. Follow-up ultrasound showed stability of the nodule.  

One year later, she presented with anemia (hemoglobin, 6.0 g/dL). Gastrointestinal endoscopy was negative, but capsule e

title: Transient visual field loss after COVID-19 vaccination: Experienced by ophthalmologist, case report
journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case_summary: A 42-year-old Thai male ophthalmologist with well-controlled dyslipidemia received his second dose of CoronaVac vaccine at 12:50 pm. Thirty minutes of post-vaccination observation were uneventful, but ten minutes later he noted blurred, foggy central vision that expanded over minutes to obscure the entire left visual field without darkening. There was no headache, weakness, or other neurologic symptoms. He presented to ophthalmology 10 minutes after onset. Visual acuity was 20/20 OU. Anterior segments and fundi were normal. There was no intraocular inflammation or relative afferent pupillary defect. Macular OCT was unremarkable. Automated perimetry (24-2) demonstrated a left congruous homonymous hemianopia respecting the vertical midline. Stroke evaluation was initiated; neurologic examination was normal except for a blood pressure of 150/90 mmHg. Brain MRI with DWI showed no evidence of acute infarction, hemorrhage, significant stenosis, arteriovenous malformation, or aneurysm. Approx

title: Preoperative diagnosis of a "humanoid" fetus in fetu using multimode ultrasound: a case report
case_summary:
A 25-day-old male infant, born at 34 weeks' gestation, was referred with a one-month history of an enlarging abdominal mass. Antenatal ultrasonography had detected the mass in utero. After birth, the infant developed progressive abdominal distension and was initially thought to have a teratoma on ultrasound at a local hospital. 

On examination, there was a 5 cm?4 cm palpable mass in the right upper abdomen; the remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed total bilirubin 98.7 ?mol/L (reference 5.0-28.0), direct bilirubin 17.9 ?mol/L (reference <8.8), indirect bilirubin 80.0 ?mol/L (reference <20), alpha-fetoprotein >1210 ng/mL (normal for age 1164-118 850), and neuron-specific enolase 21.48 ng/mL (reference <15). Echocardiography revealed a patent foramen ovale; chest radiography was normal.

To further characterize the lesion and its blood supply, the infant underwent multimodal abdominal ultrasound (grayscale high-frequency US, color Doppler flow

title: Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis Complicated by Hypertrophic Pachymeningitis Presenting with Simultaneous Multiple Intracerebral Hemorrhages
case_summary:
A 50-year-old woman with a 2-year history of granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) complicated by hypertrophic pachymeningitis (MPO-ANCA positive) presented with acute-onset headache and nausea. Her GPA had been managed with corticosteroids and methotrexate after inadequate response to cyclophosphamide. 

On presentation, vital signs were within normal limits. A noncontrast brain CT showed only widening of the Sylvian fissures and bilateral frontal lobe atrophy compared with a study 2 years earlier. Twenty-four hours later, she developed impaired consciousness (Japan Coma Scale III-100) and disorientation. Physical examination was otherwise unremarkable. Laboratory tests revealed a C-reactive protein level of 2.5 mg/dL; tests for PR3-ANCA and MPO-ANCA were negative. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed no cardiac source of embolism. Conventional cerebral angiography showed no vascular irregularities, stenosis, aneurysm, or evidence of sinus thrombosis. T2*-weighted brain MRI showed

title: Symptomatic uterine leiomyomatosis with intracaval and intracardiac invasion: Video case report
case_summary: A 31-year-old Black woman, nulliparous and obese, presented with a history of pelvic pain and dysmenorrhea. Transvaginal ultrasonography showed an enlarged heterogeneous uterus. In May 2017, she underwent a complete hysterectomy; the specimen weighed 13.9 pounds, and pathology revealed leiomyomas without malignancy. One month later, she developed dyspnea and chest pain. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a mass presumed to be a thrombus in the right atrium. At sternotomy, the lesion was deemed unresectable; biopsy revealed "a fusocellular neoplasm, without atypia, richly vascularized, with areas of stromal fibrohyalinization, and the immunohistochemical analysis revealed angioleiomyoma." Magnetic resonance imaging of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis identified a vascularized lesion within the inferior vena cava extending 19 cm from the iliac confluence to the right atrium, with an external component around the right gonadal vein. MRI also showed a 2.5-cm lingular lung nodule; pe

title: Ceftaroline-related acute eosinophilic pneumonia
journal: Lung India : Official Organ of Indian Chest Society
case_summary:
A 65-year-old woman was admitted with a 3-day history of progressive shortness of breath at rest and on exertion, productive cough, and fever to 103 degreesF. She had no history of asthma, COPD, or smoking. Her medical history was notable for coronary bypass grafting, ascending aortic aneurysm repair, and bioprosthetic aortic valve replacement one year earlier, complicated by MRSA sternal osteomyelitis and mediastinitis requiring debridement. She completed a 6-week course of vancomycin but had recurrent MRSA bacteremia; antibiotic susceptibilities showed a vancomycin MIC of 2 ug/mL and a ceftaroline MIC of 0.5 ug/mL. Five weeks into a planned 6-week course of intravenous ceftaroline (600 mg every 12 h), she developed her current respiratory symptoms.

On examination, she was dyspneic, tachypneic, and orthopneic. Diffuse inspiratory crackles were present throughout both lung fields. Arterial blood gas on 8 L nasal cannula showed a PaO? of 64.8 mmHg. She was transitioned to high-flow nas

title:
Newborn With Enlarged Erythematous Mass on Back: Case Report and
Review of Medical Literature
final_diagnosis: Subcutaneous fat necrosis of the newborn
case_summary:
A 7-day-old term male infant was brought for evaluation of progressively enlarging lumps on his back. He was born at 39 weeks' gestation after an uneventful pregnancy; Apgar scores were 7 and 7 at 1 and 5 minutes, and his initial newborn examination noted subcutaneous nodules in the thoracolumbar region. At 2 hours of life he developed tachypnea, grunting, and fever, was treated with antibiotics in the NICU, and was discharged on day 5. His mother observed that the back nodules continued to grow. On day 7, examination revealed multiple, firm, nontender, erythematous nodules on the upper back. Vital signs were temperature 37.4 degreesC, heart rate 132/min, and respiratory rate 56/min. Laboratory studies showed WBC 21,900/mm3 with 41% neutrophils and left-shifted forms, platelets 436,000/mm3, D-dimer 4.36 mg/L, and normal coagulation indices; blood cultures were obtained.  
Ultrasound of the back demonstrated an 18 cm ? 18 cm ? 5 cm hypervascular, bilobed, predominantly solid mass with s

title: Varicocele due to renal arteriovenous malformation mimicking a renal tumor: a case report
case_summary:
A 64-year-old Asian man presented with gradual left scrotal swelling for 2 years. His medical history was notable for hypertension treated with felodipine and perindopril, type 2 diabetes treated with glimepiride, and chronic hepatitis B on nucleos(t)ide analogs. He denied any history of renal trauma, biopsy, or surgery. He did not smoke or drink alcohol.

On admission, blood pressure was 141/92 mm Hg, heart rate 85 beats/min, temperature 37.1  degreesC, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Physical examination showed a grade 3 left varicocele; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies revealed normal blood counts and chemistries; urinalysis showed 2+ glucose with normal white and red blood cell counts. Scrotal ultrasound confirmed a grade 3 left varicocele. Renal ultrasound demonstrated a 3.7 ? 4.2 cm low-echogenic mass with abundant blood flow in the upper pole of the left kidney.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed a 3.6 ? 4.3 cm irregular le

title: A case report of bilateral persistent sciatic artery with thrombosis in a 47-year-old woman: A rare vascular anomaly
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: A 47-year-old woman with a history of essential hypertension managed with valsartan presented with acute left leg pain and discoloration of the forefoot. Two weeks earlier, she had received a leuprolide acetate injection for uterine fibroids. The pain began as mild but progressed to severe rest pain in the calf and distal foot, accompanied by mottling of the toes. On examination, pulses were absent in the left lower extremity, and the forefoot appeared mottled, suggesting ischemia. CT angiography of the lower limbs showed monophasic flow in the proximal left leg. The images revealed bilateral persistent sciatic arteries, aneurysmal dilatation of the left sciatic artery, hypoplastic femoral arteries, and complete thrombosis of the proximal left sciatic artery with collateral vessel filling distally.

title: Rare direct embolism of urothelial carcinoma causing acute mesenteric ischaemia during remission
case_summary:
An man in his early eighties presented with sudden severe abdominal pain and vomiting, on a background of multiple self-limiting episodes of similar pain for which no radiologic or endoscopic cause had been identified. His medical history included therapeutic anticoagulation for bilateral lower-limb deep-vein thrombosis, a cardioembolic stroke, and a diagnosis of bladder carcinoma in situ (T1) eight years earlier treated by transurethral resection and intravesical Bacillus Calmette-Gu?rin, with no recurrence on annual surveillance cystoscopy and biopsy (last performed eight months before presentation). 

On examination, he had an acute abdomen with generalized peritonism. A computed tomography angiogram of the abdomen demonstrated a thrombus in a distal branch of the superior mesenteric artery without evidence of bowel compromise. The provisional diagnosis was mesenteric arterial occlusion by thrombosis; differential diagnoses included mesenteric venous thrombosis, arterial embolism, a

title: Multiple hemorrhagic intraparenchymal tumors presenting with fatal intracranial hypertension: A rare manifestation of systemic epithelioid hemangioendothelioma
case_summary:
A 24-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presented with a 7-month history of cough and blood-streaked sputum and a 1-month history of progressive visual disturbance in the right eye. An ophthalmologic evaluation was normal, but brain magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated multiple intracranial lesions, and she was referred for further evaluation.

On examination, she was alert and oriented. Visual acuity was counting fingers in the right eye and 30/50 in the left eye; there were no other focal neurologic deficits. A noncontrast head CT scan showed multiple 5-15 mm high-density intra-axial nodules with surrounding edema in both cerebral hemispheres and cerebellum, with obscuration of sulci consistent with increased intracranial pressure. Emergency cerebral angiography excluded vascular malformations and sinus thrombosis but revealed a prolonged intracranial perfusion time. Brain MRI demonstrated that the nodules were hypointense to isointense on T1-weighted sequence

title: Better 90 Minutes Late than Never: Differential Diagnosis on MRI Scanning in a Case of Hepatic Angiosarcoma
case_summary:
A 71-year-old man presented to the emergency department with dyspnea and epigastric pain and reported non-specific abdominal symptoms. He had no known exposure to carcinogens. On examination, vital signs were within normal limits. Laboratory tests revealed elevated alanine and aspartate transaminase levels, thrombocytopenia, and anemia; ?-fetoprotein was negative, and renal function was normal. An unenhanced thoracic CT performed to evaluate his dyspnea incidentally demonstrated hypoattenuating masses in both hepatic lobes on native and contrast-enhanced images, without a definitive diagnosis. He was referred for further evaluation with abdominal MRI. 

On T2-weighted HASTE images, multiple rounded hepatic lesions exhibited heterogeneous internal architecture and hyperintensity, the largest measuring 5.1 cm in segment VII. On T1 in- and out-of-phase images, the lesions had heterogeneous areas of high signal consistent with hemorrhage. Diffusion-weighted imaging (b = 800 s/mm?) showed m

title: Profoundly Delayed Emergence due to Pulmonary Embolism
case_summary:
A 12-year-old Black female (weight 130.5 kg; BMI 47.4 kg/m^2) with well-controlled asthma, obstructive sleep apnea, and genu valgum underwent elective distal femoral and proximal tibial osteotomies with internal fixation under general anesthesia. She received midazolam 2 mg IV premedication; anesthesia was induced with propofol 200 mg, lidocaine 100 mg, and succinylcholine 200 mg, followed by endotracheal intubation. A lumbar plexus block and popliteal block were placed on the operative limb. Anesthesia was maintained with sevoflurane in O_2/air (expired concentration 2.1-2.5%) without EEG bispectral monitoring. Two tourniquets were applied sequentially on the operative leg; use of compression devices on the nonoperative limb was not documented.

After the second tourniquet deflation (approximately 4 hours into the case), pulse oximetry fell to 60%, end-tidal CO_2 decreased to 10-15 mmHg, and heart rate rose to 120-140 bpm. The patient was ventilated with 100% O_2 and sevoflurane was d

title: Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome due to nonadherence to antihypertensive treatment: A case report from Nepal
case_summary:
A 66-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with a 1-hour history of altered sensorium, bilateral upper-limb weakness, and slurred speech. She denied any seizure activity, tongue biting, or incontinence. Her medical history was notable for type 2 diabetes mellitus (metformin), hypertension (amlodipine and losartan), and primary hypothyroidism (levothyroxine), all of which she had taken for 10 years. She reported stopping her antihypertensive medications 1 week earlier. She did not smoke or drink alcohol, and her family history was noncontributory.

On examination her blood pressure was 210/110 mm Hg, pulse 115 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, SpO2 95% on room air, and Glasgow Coma Scale score 13/15. Her random blood glucose was 140 mg/dL. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations were unremarkable. Neurologic examination revealed motor strength of 3/5 in the left upper limb and 5/5 in the right upper limb; 5/5 in the left lower limb and 4/5 in the right lower

title: Case report of multiple mesenteric mycotic aneurysms after perforated appendicitis
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: A 69-year-old man with hypertension (on lisinopril and metoprolol), hyperlipidemia (on simvastatin), and treated prostate cancer presented with continuous right lower abdominal pain and signs of peritonitis. He had quit smoking six months earlier and had no allergies. On admission, the white-cell count was 16.2?10^9/L and C-reactive protein was 231 mg/L. Abdominal ultrasound and contrast CT scan showed a perforated appendix with two appendicoliths and two small abscesses; the abscesses were deemed too small for percutaneous drainage. Intravenous amoxicillin/clavulanic acid was started, and laparoscopic appendectomy confirmed purulent peritonitis. A free appendicolith was removed, the appendix was stapled off at the cecal base, and local irrigation performed. Postoperatively, leukocyte count and CRP declined slowly. On postoperative day 8, the CRP increased again and hemoglobin fell from 14.5 g/dL to 11.3 g/dL. CT angiography revealed a new right lower abdominal abscess and multiple ane

title: Preoperative Direct Puncture Embolization of Castleman Disease of the Parotid Gland: A Case Report
case_summary: A 27-year-old woman with no significant medical history noted a slowly enlarging mass in the right parotid region over several months. On examination, there was a firm, non-tender swelling behind the right mandibular angle with overlying rose-colored skin; facial nerve function was intact. Ultrasonography showed a 5-cm solid mass within the right parotid gland with marked intraparenchymal hypervascularity and low peripheral vascular resistance. Ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration yielded blood fibrin material with lymphomonocyte elements but was non-diagnostic. Three months later, MRI of the neck demonstrated an oval, hypervascular mass in the posterior right parotid gland displacing the internal and external carotid arteries medially. Given the atypical localization, hypervascular imaging features, and inconclusive cytology, the working diagnosis was an atypical vagal paraganglioma, and preoperative embolization was planned prior to excision.

title: Growing Teratoma Syndrome: A Rare Outcome
case_summary:
A 26-year-old man was referred for evaluation of a right testicular mass noted on self-examination several weeks earlier. He had no significant medical history or prior testicular trauma.  
On examination, the right testis was enlarged without tenderness. Serum tumor markers were obtained, and a testicular ultrasound was performed. The ultrasound revealed a 32?cc right testicle with highly heterogeneous parenchyma containing multiple hypoechogenic, hypervascular nodules and a moderate hematocele. Doppler imaging also showed bilateral varicoceles.  
An abdominal ultrasound demonstrated a 4?cm superior retroperitoneal lymph node with surrounding fat stranding. A contrast-enhanced thoracoabdominopelvic CT scan confirmed a lateroaortic retroperitoneal lymphadenomegaly and a 2?cm left supraclavicular lymphadenomegaly, without other metastatic lesions.  
The patient underwent right radical orchiectomy. Histologic examination showed a mixed germ cell neoplasm with seminomatous, embryonal carc

title: Hepatic falciform ligament clear cell myomelanocytic tumor: A case report and a comprehensive review of the literature on perivascular epithelioid cell tumors
case_summary:
A 29-year-old woman was admitted for evaluation of a ruptured liver mass. Four months earlier she had undergone a cesarean section followed by L-carnitine therapy. She had no history of hepatitis or cirrhosis. On examination, the right upper abdomen was slightly distended. A 10-cm hypogastric surgical scar was present. A firm, smooth, non-tender 5?6 cm mass was palpable in the right upper quadrant. Laboratory studies showed normal liver and renal function, electrolytes, and a normal ?-fetoprotein level.  
Ultrasound revealed a heterogeneous hyperechoic mass measuring 15.5?9.6?14.2 cm in the right hepatic lobe, with an irregular anechoic area, a clear margin, and no detectable blood flow on Doppler imaging. Plain CT showed a large right anterior hepatic lobe mass with mixed high and low internal densities. Dynamic contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated uneven arterial enhancement, more homogeneous enhancement in the venous and equilibrium phases, a large non-enhancing area within the mass, m

title: A rare case of hibernoma in the posterior thigh of an elderly patient: A surgical case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: A 70-year-old woman presented with a 2-year history of a slowly enlarging mass in the posterior aspect of her left thigh. She was afebrile, with preserved general health. On examination, the lesion measured approximately 7?12 cm, was firm, mobile relative to the skin but fixed to deeper tissues, non-tender, exhibited prominent collateral venous circulation, and there was no regional lymphadenopathy or distal neurovascular deficit. A standard radiograph of the thigh was unremarkable. Soft-tissue ultrasound demonstrated a heterogeneous, well-vascularized mass with fatty characteristics, raising suspicion for liposarcoma. MRI confirmed a 9?19 cm mass displacing the femoral vessels medially; it was hypointense on T1-weighted images, hyperintense on T2 and fat-saturation sequences, contained areas of necrosis and hemorrhage, and showed marked gadolinium enhancement, especially on fat-saturation sequences.
