title: Fistula between the Thoracic Duct and an Unusual Vessel Aneurysm Branching Off the Abdominal Aorta Revealed by Aneurysm Rupture: A Case Report
case_summary: A 42-year-old man was referred for sudden onset of severe back and chest pain. He had a history of dyslipidemia but no history of traumatic injury; he had received acupuncture on his back several weeks earlier. On admission, his blood pressure was 125/69 mmHg, pulse 91 bpm, and temperature was normal. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 15,400 cells/?L, hemoglobin 13.3 g/dL, and C-reactive protein 0.05 mg/dL. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography revealed a post-mediastinal hematoma and an unusual vascular structure branching from the abdominal aorta at the level of the superior mesenteric artery. This vessel formed an aneurysm from which unidentifiable channels extended both cranially and caudally alongside the aorta; the cranial channel appeared to track toward the left subclavian circulation.

title: Paraganglioma of the anterior superior mediastinum: Presentation of a case of mistaken diagnosis so long and review of the literature
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: A 59-year-old woman was noted to have an anterior mediastinal mass on chest CT performed for cough 9 years ago. At that time, the 31-mm lesion (CT value ~44.5 HU) lay anterior to the trachea, slightly compressing and shifting it to the left. A contrast-enhanced CT scan showed enlargement of the mass and "the presence of a single enlarged lymph node (lymphoma, Metastasis, or Inflammation)." 18F-FDG PET-CT demonstrated an upper paratracheal lesion with a maximal diameter of 50 mm and SUVmax 6.27. Four different hospitals rendered a diagnosis of Castleman disease, and no biopsy or treatment was undertaken. Annual CT scans documented slow growth; over the past six months she developed progressive neck tightness and dyspnea. A recent CT showed a single 46 ? 51 ? 72 mm mass with uniform density (CT value ~40 HU), a distinct border, and compression of the trachea and great vessels. On examination, temperature was 36.1  degreesC, pulse 71 bpm, respiratory rate 17 bpm, blood pressure 133/73 mmH

title: Angiomatoid fibrous histiocytoma (AFH) unusual clinical presentation and unique radiological findings
case_summary: An 8-year-old Yemeni boy with no prior medical history presented with dark skin discoloration over a slow-growing, mobile subcutaneous mass on the mid-lateral aspect of his left thigh that had been present for 6 months and enlarged most over the prior 2 months. The lesion appeared after a suspected penetrating trauma. He reported intermittent low-grade fevers, predominantly at night, along with weakness and fatigue. On examination, there was a 5?5 cm dark brown patch with a central hypopigmented, scaly area over a firm, non-tender, mildly warm swelling. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 7.9 g/dL, C-reactive protein 102 mg/L, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 42 mm/h, and a normal white-cell differential. Plain radiographs of the left femur revealed a subtle soft-tissue opacity without radiopaque foreign body or gas. Ultrasound demonstrated a 3.6?2.4?1.9 cm complex predominantly cystic lesion with interspersed vascular channels showing arterial spectral Doppler waveforms and occasional

title: A case of fatal fulminant fat embolism syndrome saved by VA-ECMO in the acute phase of multiple trauma
case_summary:
A 79-year-old woman injured in a traffic accident was admitted to the emergency department. On arrival she was hypoxemic (oxygen saturation 90% on a 10 L mask), alert (GCS 15), and otherwise hemodynamically stable. During evaluation her consciousness abruptly declined to GCS 4 (E2V1M1) and she became hypotensive (BP 67/46 mmHg). CT imaging revealed an open tibiofibular diaphyseal fracture and a pelvic fracture with contrast extravasation. She underwent immediate transcatheter arterial embolization of the pelvic vessels. Two hours after injury, external fixation of the tibiofibular fracture was performed, and she was transferred to the ICU. 

In the ICU, her level of consciousness and oxygenation did not improve. Two hours after ICU admission she developed cardiovascular instability requiring intravenous norepinephrine and dobutamine. Despite increasing norepinephrine to 0.32 ? and dobutamine to 3.85 ?, she remained hypotensive and moderately hypoxemic (PaO2/FiO2 ratio 111). Transthorac

title: A pair of two atrial myxomas mimic thrombus in left atrial appendage and cause ischemic stroke in a young adult: a case report
case_summary:
A 56-year-old man was referred with acute dysarthria and left hemiparesis. His history included arterial hypertension and permanent atrial fibrillation, for which he was not anticoagulated due to a previous intracerebral hemorrhage. On examination, he was alert and oriented, with slurred speech but no aphasia, residual spastic hemiparesis of the left limbs, left-sided ataxia, and cardiac arrhythmia on auscultation. 

Laboratory studies were notable only for a marginally elevated creatinine (1.44 mg/dL) and troponin (18 ng/L). Cranial CT showed no acute infarction or hemorrhage. MRI demonstrated diffusion restriction in the left corona radiata extending into the precentral gyrus, consistent with acute ischemia, plus hemosiderin in the right thalamus from prior hemorrhage. Neurosonography showed no significant stenosis.

The stroke was initially attributed to cardioembolism from atrial fibrillation. Given his contraindication to oral anticoagulation, he was referred for left atrial appen

title: A case report of metformin?associated lactic acidosis
case_summary:
A 73-year-old woman with a 20-year history of type 2 diabetes treated with metformin 850 mg twice daily and glimepiride 4 mg once daily, chronic kidney disease (stage 3A; baseline eGFR 54 mL/min/1.73 m^2), hypothyroidism on levothyroxine, and psychiatric disease on mirtazapine and quetiapine presented with 5 days of generalized fatigue, nausea, and decreased urine output, plus 1 day of shortness of breath and abdominal pain. She also noted relative constipation for 10 days and had not followed up in a year.

On arrival, she was drowsy (GCS 15), temperature 37.4  degreesC, heart rate 104 beats/min, blood pressure 80/50 mmHg, respiratory rate 32 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 94% on room air, and random blood glucose 159 mg/dL. Physical examination showed an irreducible para-umbilical hernia and mild suprapubic tenderness; there was no lower-limb edema.

Laboratory results: hemoglobin 12 g/dL, WBC 9.8 ? 10^9/L, platelets 272 ? 10^9/L, CRP 11.8 mg/L; serum sodium 140 mmol/L, potassium 3.6

title: Massive adrenal vein aneurysm mimicking an adrenal tumor in a patient with hemophilia A: a case report and review of the literature
case_summary: A 58-year-old white man with a history of hemophilia A (baseline factor VIII activity 12%), type 2 diabetes mellitus, and prior testicular cancer presented with bright red blood per rectum. During evaluation, abdominal CT revealed an incidental 18 ? 17 ? 16 cm heterogeneous, hemorrhagic mass in the left suprarenal fossa exerting mass effect on the stomach, pancreatic tail, and left kidney. Physical examination was notable only for a large, firm, nonpulsatile, nontender mass in the left upper quadrant. He was normotensive, not tachycardic, normokalemic, and had no cushingoid features or gynecomastia. He received recombinant factor VIII to maintain activity between 80% and 100%. Comprehensive biochemical evaluation for a functional adrenal lesion--including plasma metanephrine (<0.20 nmol/L), normetanephrine (0.22 nmol/L), 17-hydroxyprogesterone (74.6 ng/dl), estradiol (30 pg/ml), dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate (43 ?g/dl), dexamethasone suppression tests, adrenocorticotropic hormone (27

title: Massive pseudotumor of unknown etiology in a cemented metal-on-polyethylene total hip arthroplasty: a case report
case_summary:
An 86-year-old woman presented with acutely worsening left hip pain superimposed on a 10-year history of progressive pain, an enlarging left pelvic mass, and shortening of her left lower extremity. She had undergone a primary left total hip arthroplasty in 1983 using a cemented metal-on-polyethylene implant. Her past medical history included aortic valve replacement, pacemaker placement, and pelvic radiation therapy for rectal cancer 8 years earlier. 

On examination, she was wheelchair bound. A large, firm, tender, well-circumscribed mass occupied her left pelvis and upper thigh, with no neurovascular deficit. She was unable to sit upright or ambulate due to mass effect. 

Laboratory studies showed C-reactive protein of 5.3 mg/dL (normal <1 mg/dL), white blood cell count 7,400/mm3, and hemoglobin 7.8 g/dL. 

Plain radiographs demonstrated progressive, destructive lytic changes of the left hemipelvis with superior migration of both femoral and acetabular components. A computed tomograp

title: Sonographic and Endoscopic Findings in Cocaine-Induced Ischemic Colitis
case_summary:
A 41-year-old man with a history of intravenous drug abuse was admitted for diffuse abdominal pain and bloody stools. One year earlier, he had been diagnosed with chronic hepatitis C (genotype 1A) without cirrhosis. He reported intermittent, self?limited episodes of abdominal pain and vomiting over the previous three months; his most recent cocaine use was the day before presentation. On examination, he was febrile (38.3  degreesC) and had tenderness in the right lower quadrant, maximal at McBurney's point. Laboratory tests showed a C?reactive protein level of 14 mg/dL and a white blood cell count of 14 500/uL.  
Abdominal ultrasound was performed to evaluate suspected appendicitis and demonstrated segmental, circumferential thickening of the ascending colon with preserved layers, limited hyperechoic mesenteric fat, and weak color Doppler signals in the affected segment. A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the abdomen confirmed inflammation of the ascending colon and cecum, possibly extendi

title: Myoepithelial hamartoma as a solitary mass in the pancreatic parenchyma: the first case report
case_summary: A 41-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of a pancreatic head mass incidentally detected by ultrasonography during a routine health check. She was asymptomatic, with no significant past medical or family history, and her physical examination was unremarkable. Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated a 2.3?1.5 cm well-demarcated mass in the head and uncinate process of the pancreas, showing low attenuation in the early and late arterial phases and high enhancement in the portal venous phase; there was no bile duct or vascular invasion. Serum tumor markers were within reference ranges: CEA 1.8 ng/ml (0-5), CA 19-9 9.2 U/ml (0-37), chromogranin A 45.21 ng/ml (24-97), insulin 5.2 uIU/ml (2-25), and glucagon 28.7 pg/ml (40-130). Owing to concern for a potentially malignant or borderline tumor, a pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy was performed. On gross examination, a 2?1 cm firm, well-encapsulated, lobulated, whitish mass without hemorrhage or degenerative change was identified in

title: New-Onset Ulcerative Colitis in a Young Caucasian Woman with Unclassified Arteritis
case_summary:
A 32-year-old Caucasian woman presented with two weeks of throbbing right-sided neck pain radiating to her jaw, acutely worse for two days, along with transient nonspecific visual changes and bilateral upper-extremity paresthesia. An outpatient carotid Doppler ultrasound showed 50-69% stenosis of the right carotid artery. Clinic laboratories revealed an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 67 mm/hr and a C-reactive protein level of 2.7 mg/dL. She was started on methylprednisolone 125 mg every six hours and admitted for further evaluation.

Her history included anxiety (on escitalopram), hyperlipidemia (on atorvastatin), and a prior massive transfusion for placental abruption. She denied fevers, chills, chest pain, abdominal pain, headaches, or limb claudication.

On examination, temperature was 36.6  degreesC, pulse 81/min, blood pressure 109/64 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, and SpO? 100% on room air. She was alert and without distress. The neck was supple but exquisitely tender to light

title: Unusual Bilateral Neck of Femur Stress Fracture in a Healthy, Non-athletic Individual - ACase Report and Literature Review
case_summary:
A 50-year-old housewife presented with a 4-month history of bilateral anterior groin pain (left greater than right), progressive limp, and difficulty sitting cross-legged or squatting. Twenty days before this presentation, she became unable to bear weight on the left side. She had no history of trauma, excessive activity, or prior fragility fractures, and no comorbidities or medications; she was two years postmenopausal. On exam, there was bilateral hip tenderness (left > right), external rotation deformity and 1 cm shortening of the left lower limb, severely limited left hip motion, and pain at extremes of right hip motion. Straight leg raise was intact on the right but not possible on the left; neurovascular exam was normal.  
An anteroposterior pelvis radiograph obtained 20 days earlier showed bilateral inferomedial femoral-neck sclerosis with cortical breach on the right and an undisplaced complete fracture line on the left; the neck-shaft angles were 130 degrees on the right and 1

title: Stroke-like manifestation of a spontaneous spinal epidural hematoma with spontaneous resolution: a case report
case_summary:
A 28-year-old man presented with sudden onset of severe neck pain radiating to both shoulders and the right lower limb, associated with numbness over both upper limbs and the right lower limb. He had no history of trauma, anticoagulant use, or systemic symptoms. On initial examination, he was ambulant with localized paravertebral and shoulder tenderness but no midline spinal tenderness; strength and sensation were full in all four limbs, cranial nerve and cerebellar exams were normal, Lhermitte's sign was positive, and Hoffmann's sign was negative. Cervical spine radiographs were normal, and he was discharged after analgesia.

He re-presented 2 days later with recurrent neck pain (pain score 8/10), dyspnea, and chest pain, after a fall due to right-sided limb weakness that began 9 hours earlier. He remained continent and had no headache, vision changes, or speech problems. Examination revealed tenderness over the lower cervical and upper thoracic spine; strength was 5/5 in the left lim

title: Intracranial hemorrhage induced uncontrolled seizure in a deceased donor liver transplant patient: a case report
case_summary: A 61-year-old man with hepatitis B-related cirrhosis, portal vein thrombosis, and prior portal-systemic encephalopathy was admitted for elective living-donor liver transplantation. His postoperative regimen included aspirin and nafamostat mesilate for thrombosis prophylaxis; prostaglandin E1 for graft perfusion; tacrolimus and prednisolone for immunosuppression; and ampicillin/sulbactam, fluconazole, and nystatin for infection prophylaxis. On postoperative day (POD) 6, the patient's mental status deteriorated and motor strength was decreased in all extremities (upper, grade III; lower, grade I). By POD 7, lower-extremity strength improved to grade III. A neurologic consultation on POD 7 revealed a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 12 (E4, V2, M6) and equal, reactive pupils. On POD 8, he had a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting 2 minutes with rightward eye deviation; the seizure aborted after lorazepam. Levetiracetam (2000 mg/day) and pregabalin (150 mg/day) were started, but brief seiz

title: Endovascular Treatment of a Right-Sided Ureteroiliac Fistula in a Patient with a Simultaneous Left-Sided Ureteroileal Fistula
case_summary:
An 80-year-old woman with a history of rectal cancer treated in 2008 by liver metastasectomy followed by Hartmann's procedure with intraoperative radiotherapy (5?5?Gy) and adjuvant capecitabine, oxaliplatin, and bevacizumab presented in January 2011 with intermittent gross hematuria. Since December 2009 she had bilateral JJ stents for ureteral strictures, replaced regularly. In November 2010 she required drainage of a pelvic abscess after stent occlusion complicated by Candida glabrata infection. Her medical history also included type 2 diabetes mellitus and angina pectoris.

On presentation, her hemoglobin was 7.2?mmol/L and creatinine was 370??mol/L. Renal ultrasound showed bilateral hydronephrosis. Because of persistent hematuria and suspected stent occlusion, cystoscopy and ureteral stent exchange were performed: removal of the right double-J catheter revealed pulsatile arterial bleeding from the right ureteral orifice. The stent was changed, bleeding subsided, and she received fou

title: A giant superficial myofibroblastoma involving the vagina and pelvis: A case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 27-year-old woman (G0P0) presented with a 1-year history of progressive abdominal girth enlargement and a 1-month history of a large pelvic mass. She noted minimal vaginal spotting for 2 days after menses but denied abnormal discharge or changes in menstrual volume or cycle length. She had no hormone therapy or prior gynecologic procedures. Serum tumor markers, including CA 19-9, CA 125, CA 153, CA 742, CEA, AFP, LDH, HE4, and NSE, were all within normal limits.

On pelvic examination, a large tender pelvic mass measuring approximately 15 ? 17 cm was palpated protruding about 3 cm into the vagina; the vaginal walls were smooth, and the cervix was displaced laterally. Transabdominal and transvaginal ultrasound demonstrated a heterogeneous cystic-solid mass (17 ? 11.7 ? 8.7 cm in the extraperitoneal pelvis; 3.6 ? 6 cm in the vaginal wall) displacing bladder, uterus, and cervix posteriorly and to the right. The solid areas were hypoechoic with multiple cord-like anechoic bands. Color Do

title: Gemcitabine-Induced Extensive Skin Necrosis
case_summary: An 82-year-old woman with metastatic pancreatic carcinoma receiving Gemcitabine chemotherapy (1000 mg/m2 weekly for three weeks per cycle; total dose 10.8 g) presented with a 20-day history of severe pain, paresthesia, and swelling of her lower limbs. On examination, she had extensive necrotic ulcerative lesions on the posterior aspects of both legs and surrounding edema. She was afebrile. Laboratory tests showed mildly elevated inflammatory markers (C-reactive protein 6.8 ng/dL), leukocytosis (12.6 ? 10^3/?L), and mild anemia (hemoglobin 9.5 g/dL). An autoimmune panel (p-ANCA, c-ANCA, ANA, ENA, anti-DNA, complements, cryoglobulins, anticardiolipin) and coagulation studies (fibrinogen, INR) were normal. A swab of the lesions showed no evidence of infection. An arterial and venous Doppler ultrasound of the lower limbs was normal.

title: Leg massage during pregnancy with unrecognized deep vein thrombosis could be life threatening: a case report
case_summary:
A 25-year-old primigravid Thai woman at approximately 25 weeks' gestation, with no known underlying disease and no prenatal care, underwent a traditional Thai leg and foot massage at a commercial facility. About 5-10 minutes into the massage, she experienced sudden onset of dyspnea, altered consciousness, a brief convulsion, and collapse. Two nurses on site initiated basic life support, and emergency medical services arrived 20 minutes later to find her pulseless. She was intubated and received continuous CPR en route to the hospital, arriving approximately 25 minutes after CPR began.

On arrival, electrocardiography showed pulseless electrical activity. Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 11.7 g/dL, hematocrit 36.0%, white blood cell count 12 850 cells/mm?, platelets 159 000 cells/mm?, prothrombin time 10.5 s (INR 0.95), activated partial thromboplastin time 29.7 s (ratio 0.93), fibrinogen 100 mg/dL, and lactate 3.14 mmol/L. Urinalysis was normal. Arterial blood gas analysis demons

title:
Rotation-hinged knee prosthesis for the treatment of Charcot
arthropathy: a case report and literature review
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
case_summary:
A 54-year-old man was admitted with a 20-day history of left knee swelling and deformity. He denied any trauma. The knee was mildly painful; he could flex and extend but could not bear weight. An earlier local X-ray reportedly showed a displaced fracture of the left tibial plateau.

His medical history was notable only for a positive serum Treponema pallidum antibody. He had no known diabetes or other systemic illness.

On examination, vital signs were stable. There was no spinal deformity or limitation of spinal movement. Upper limbs had normal strength and sensation. Left knee inspection showed swelling and varus deformity. There was a positive floating patella test, mild tenderness, and percussion pain. Range of motion was 120 degrees of flexion to 20 degrees of extension. Left lower-extremity muscle strength was graded 4/5; right was 5/5. The American Knee Society clinical score was 45; functional score, 30. There was hyperpigmentation of the left leg, shallow hypoesthesia over the

title: Spontaneous radial artery pseudoaneurysm in an infant due to idiopathic medial hypoplasia - a case report
journal: Case Reports in Plastic Surgery & Hand Surgery
case_summary:
A seven-month-old boy was referred for evaluation of a painless mass of the left wrist. His parents first noted a 4-5 mm soft tissue swelling four months earlier, which had progressively enlarged. He had an uncomplicated birth history, no trauma, vascular access, or known connective tissue or rheumatologic disorders.  
On examination, there was a 1 cm bluish, non-tender, pulsatile mass over the left radial artery. A Doppler-assisted Allen test raised concern about arterial patency.  
Arterial duplex ultrasound showed a 9 ? 9 mm lesion, suspected to be a true aneurysm, with normal distal flow. Magnetic resonance angiography demonstrated a 1.1 ? 0.8 ? 1.0 cm mass arising from the radial artery, with retrograde filling from the superficial palmar arch and thrombosis proximal to the lesion.  
Surgical exploration revealed a multi-lobular, focally bluish arterial mass adherent to the flexor carpi radialis sheath. Trial clamping of the radial artery proximally and distally confirmed brisk di

title: The Case of the Rare Malformation and Rare Variant: An Infant with a Self-Embolized Torcular Dural Sinus Malformation and a Concomitant Prothrombin Variant
case_summary:
A male infant was delivered at 35 5/7 weeks' gestation via induced vaginal delivery after partial placental abruption. The mother, of European descent with hypothyroidism, had an unremarkable pregnancy until a 19-week anatomy scan that demonstrated a small intracranial cyst. A follow-up fetal MRI at 25 weeks showed a large, posterior midline, extra-axial vascular structure--a giant venous lake at the torcular herophili and posterior superior sagittal sinus--with evidence of partial thrombus, mass effect on adjacent cerebral and cerebellar parenchyma, noncommunicating hydrocephalus, and effacement of the prepontine cistern.

At birth, the infant was macrocephalic and minimally responsive but was hemodynamically stable, fed by mouth, and breathed room air. On day 1, brain MRI revealed a 5.8 ? 5 ? 8.8 cm posterior midline extra-axial structure with restricted diffusion, hydrocephalus without transependymal flow, and cerebellar tonsillar herniation 9 mm below the foramen magnum. Given the

title: Reversible Cerebral Vasoconstriction Syndrome with Intracranial Hypertension: Should Decompressive Craniectomy Be Considered?
case_summary:
A 42-year-old woman with a history of chronic migraine treated with nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and ergotamine-caffeine presented with a sudden, diffuse "thunderclap" headache that began immediately after swimming. The headache was described as explosive and unusual and resolved spontaneously over 8 hours. On initial examination, her vital signs, general physical examination, and neurologic examination were all normal. 

A noncontrast head CT scan was normal. Lumbar puncture revealed oxyhemoglobin and 290 red blood cells per mm3, and a diagnosis of subarachnoid hemorrhage was made. A cerebral angiogram was scheduled for the next day.

On hospital day 1, she developed severe headache with acute hypertension, followed by aphasia and right hemiplegia. Her level of consciousness declined rapidly, necessitating endotracheal intubation. An emergent CT scan showed a left rolandic intracerebral hemorrhage.  
 
Urgent cerebral angiography revealed diffuse segmental narrowing of the cer

title: Emergency multimodality imaging for a giant cystic renal artery aneurysm in a lung adenocarcinoma patient with iodinated contrast media allergy: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 36-year-old man with a 2-year history of right-sided lung adenocarcinoma with pleural metastases presented with intermittent lower back pain for approximately 6 months. The pain occurred 4 to 5 times per week, was rated 3/10 on a visual analog scale, worsened with bending or prolonged sitting, and improved with rest. He denied urinary symptoms or hematuria. His only notable allergy was to iodinated contrast media. He was a never-smoker, drank alcohol in moderation, and was receiving targeted therapy and immunotherapy for his lung cancer.

On examination, temperature was 36.6  degreesC, heart rate 71 beats/min, respiratory rate 17 breaths/min, blood pressure 122/70 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Abdominal and flank examinations were unremarkable, and there were no signs of renal dysfunction.

Non-contrast CT of the abdomen revealed a 3.8 ? 2.4 cm soft tissue-density lesion in the right renal hilum and sinus, with a suggestion of connection to the right renal artery. Gray

title: Superficial granulomatous pyoderma of the leg improved after conservative management with Unna boot and intralesional steroid injections
case_summary:
A 27-year-old Pakistani woman presented with a 20-year history of an indolent, nonhealing ulcerating eruption on her left leg. The lesions began as painful acneiform papules on her lower thigh, then gradually extended toward her ankle, enlarged, ulcerated, and healed with scars. She reported pathergy, noting new lesions after waxing leg hair. Numerous courses of antibiotics, colchicine, antituberculosis medications, and dapsone at outside institutions did not produce resolution. 

On examination, the left leg showed confluent, scarred plaques in areas of prior ulceration and multiple punched-out superficial ulcers with associated tenderness; the ulcers lacked undermined borders. There was no regional lymphadenopathy, and the remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable.

Three 3?3?4-mm punch biopsy specimens revealed irregular epidermal hyperplasia containing neutrophilic microabscesses adjacent to an ulcer, along with suppurative and granulomatous inflammation in the papilla

title: Paget's disease with tumefactive soft tissue extension mimicking a chronic subdural hematoma
case_summary:
A 71-year-old woman with hypertension, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and a history of transient ischemic attack underwent an upper endoscopy for reflux disease. She awoke with a brief episode of bilateral upper extremity weakness, marked clumsiness of her left hand, left extremity numbness, and word-finding difficulty; all symptoms resolved within 20 minutes. She reported mild recurrent headaches over the past few years and firm nodules on her scalp that had been under observation. She denied vision or hearing changes, nausea, vomiting, or seizure activity. She had a 7-pack-year smoking history and a history of alcohol abuse. 

A head and neck CT angiogram showed no vascular stenosis, occlusion, aneurysm, or acute infarction. Non-contrast head CT demonstrated a 6-mm isodense extra-axial collection along the right convexity, with slight mass effect but no midline shift. The bone windows revealed sclerosis of the calvarium with focal inner-table erosions and periosteal reaction,

title: Femoral nerve compression secondary to a ganglion cyst arising from a hip joint: a case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 57-year-old woman presented with a 6-month history of progressively worsening pain in the right groin radiating down the medial thigh to the anterior knee and medial lower leg and foot. The pain was aggravated by stair climbing and walking and was not relieved by nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drugs. Over the preceding 2 months, she became unable to climb stairs or walk long distances because of paresthesias and hypoesthesia of the medial side of the right lower leg and foot. 

On examination, there was tenderness over the anterior right hip without palpable mass or muscle atrophy. Neurologic examination revealed altered sensation to light touch over the medial right lower leg and foot. Internal rotation, external rotation, and extension of the right hip were painful and markedly limited. Vascular examination of the right lower limb was intact, and the straight-leg-raising test was negative. 

Laboratory studies, including blood sugar, serum electrolytes, erythrocyte sedimentation rat

title: Lateral geniculate artery pseudoaneurysm after arthroscopic medial meniscectomy
journal: Journal of Vascular Surgery Cases, Innovations and Techniques
final_diagnosis: Pseudoaneurysm of the lateral inferior genicular artery
case_summary:
A 32-year-old man with no prior medical or surgical history presented to the emergency department with a 3-week history of a pulsatile mass at the lateral aspect of his left knee. Three weeks earlier, he had undergone an uncomplicated arthroscopic medial meniscectomy. Since then, he had noticed that the mass was becoming increasingly painful and appeared to be enlarging. On examination, he was hemodynamically stable, and routine laboratory tests were within normal limits. 

Point-of-care CT angiography of the left lower extremity demonstrated a nonspecific blush of contrast in the region of the lateral left knee, but the study was limited by motion artifact. Concerned about a possible arterial injury, clinicians obtained dedicated anterolateral color Doppler ultrasound (CDU) of the lateral left knee. CDU revealed a heterogeneous, echogenic collection measuring approximately 2.9 cm ? 2.3 cm ? 1.5 cm with an adjacent small vessel. Spectral Doppler imaging showed pulsatile, to-and-fro flo

title: A cause of recurrent strokes: carotid webs detected by CT angiogram
case_summary:
A 38-year-old Middle Eastern man with a history of an ischaemic stroke 15 months earlier presented with sudden onset of dense right-sided hemiparesis, right facial droop, and aphasia. At initial presentation he had headache and dysphasia and a CT scan then showed a left temporo-parietal infarct; he returned to full independence on clopidogrel and atorvastatin. On this admission, non-contrast CT of the head demonstrated an acute left M1 occlusion. He received intravenous alteplase followed by successful mechanical thrombectomy. 

CT angiogram of the intracranial vessels confirmed an occlusion of the left middle cerebral artery (M1 segment) and revealed two separate foci of soft-tissue filling defects arising from the posterior wall at the origins of the left and right internal carotid arteries. Contrast-enhanced MRA of the neck showed a hemorrhagic-appearing plaque at the origin of the left internal carotid artery, with no high-grade stenosis or evidence of arterial dissection. Axial fa

title: Spontaneous mesenteric hematoma occurring during antithrombotic therapy and responsive to surgical treatment: a case report
case_summary:
A 63-year-old man with a 3-day history of abdominal pain presented in shock. His medical history was notable for atrial fibrillation, stroke, and popliteal and internal iliac artery aneurysms, for which he was taking cilostazol and rivaroxaban. On arrival his heart rate was 102 beats/min, blood pressure 73/48 mmHg, and he was alert but in distress. The abdomen was diffusely tender without peritoneal signs or external trauma. Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 9.0 g/dL, lactic acid 5.7 mmol/L, and C-reactive protein 1.18 mg/dL; other values were unremarkable.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed a 95 ? 250 mm mass along the sigmoid colon mesentery with heterogeneous internal hyperdensity compressing the colon; there was no obvious extravascular contrast leakage, no identifiable responsible vessel, no aneurysm in or around the hematoma, and no free air. After initial stabilization with transfusions, the patient was managed conservatively.

On hospital day 1 he developed peritone

title: A Case of Craniocervical Junction Arteriovenous Fistulas with a Brainstem Mass Lesion on Imaging: Case Report and Literature Review
case_summary:
A 34-year-old man presented with intermittent headache and hoarseness for 10 days. The headaches were intense during attacks but improved with rest. He had no dizziness, nausea, vomiting, limb weakness, limb twitching, swallowing difficulty, or choking when drinking. On examination, he was alert and slightly hoarse in speech; pupils were equal, round, 2.5 mm, and reactive to light. Limb strength was grade V with normal tone.  
Noncontrast CT of the head showed an abnormal density in the brainstem, and CT angiography showed no significant vascular abnormalities.  
Brain MRI revealed a lamellar lesion in the brainstem, measuring approximately 19 mm ? 19 mm on axial images, with slightly long T1 and T2 signals. The lesion enhanced markedly after contrast administration. FLAIR imaging showed hyperintensity, and T2 images demonstrated flow?void signals with prominent anterior and posterior vascular markings around the brainstem. MRI raised the possibility of an arteriovenous fistula affecti

title: A case of pulmonary tumor thrombotic microangiopathy following lymphedema on the lower extremities
case_summary:
A 55-year-old man with hypertension and type 2 diabetes, and a 34-pack-year smoking history (quit 1 year ago), underwent repair of a right inguinal hernia without complications. One month later, he developed swelling of the right lower extremity, lower abdomen, and scrotum. Abdominal CT, contrast-enhanced CT to detect venous thrombus, blood tests, and urologic evaluation were unrevealing. Lymphoscintigraphy demonstrated lymphatic leakage from the scrotum to the right lateral thigh and impaired lymphatic drainage of both lower extremities. He was diagnosed with postoperative lymphedema.

One month later, he presented with mild dyspnea on exertion. Chest radiography showed mild bilateral middle and lower lung-field infiltrates. High-resolution CT revealed thickening of the broncho-vascular bundles, a tree-in-bud pattern, and interlobular septal thickening. Interstitial pneumonia, including hypersensitivity pneumonia and sarcoidosis, was suspected; however, blood tests were unremarkable a

title: Gastric Signet Ring Cell Adenocarcinoma With Metastasis to the Testicles and Leptomeninges
case_summary:
A 38-year-old man with a history of unprovoked deep vein thrombosis on rivaroxaban, alcohol use, and 22 pack-year tobacco use presented with right lower-extremity swelling and right groin pain radiating to the right testicle. He was hemodynamically stable and afebrile. Physical examination showed right lower-extremity edema and slight tenderness of the right testicle. 

Given concern for occult malignancy in the setting of recurrent unprovoked DVTs, a CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis was obtained, which showed diffuse gastric wall thickening, extensive retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, and nodularity with stranding along the peritoneal surfaces. Scrotal ultrasound demonstrated a 0.9 ? 0.7 ? 0.8-cm, well-marginated hypoechoic lesion in the upper pole of the right testicle. 

The patient underwent a right orchiectomy. Histopathological examination revealed infiltrating signet ring cell adenocarcinoma involving both the testicular parenchyma and spermatic cord with angiolymphatic invasion

title: Chronic contained ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm with a rare presentation of lower limb neuropathic claudication: a report of two cases
final_diagnosis: Chronic contained rupture of abdominal aortic aneurysm
case_summary:
A 62-year-old man with hypertension presented with 6 months of sharp, constant lower back pain rated 7-8/10 that radiated to the posterior and lateral thighs. The pain was aggravated by increases in intra-abdominal pressure (cough, hip motion) and initially responded to over-the-counter analgesics but later became refractory. He noted a pulsatile periumbilical mass that appeared concurrently with his pain. He denied limb coldness, pallor, cyanosis, swelling, recent infection, or trauma. His medications included an oral antihypertensive; he smoked two pack-years; BMI was 27 kg/m2.  
On examination, vital signs were stable. He appeared pale. Palpation revealed a pulsatile periumbilical abdominal mass with a localized bruit; lower-limb pulses were normal. The remainder of the exam was unremarkable.  
Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10 g/dL; infection markers were within normal limits.  
Duplex ultrasound demonstrated an infrarenal aortic aneurysm measuring 73 ? 70 mm with intralumina

title: Long overlooked: Adult VACTERL association unmasked by a large patent ductus arteriosus
case_summary:
A 27-year-old man presented with a 3-day history of worsening breathlessness and cough. He was conscious, well oriented, with a respiratory rate of 28 breaths per minute and oxygen saturation of 93% on room air, corresponding to New York Heart Association class IV symptoms. Routine blood tests showed mild relative neutrophilia and polycythemia. He had a history of recurrent respiratory tract infections since childhood.

A frontal chest radiograph revealed gross cardiomegaly with prominent pulmonary vascular markings bilaterally. A 2D echocardiogram demonstrated global hypokinesia, dilated cardiac chambers, a D-shaped left ventricle, severe ventricular dysfunction (ejection fraction 30%), and a grossly enlarged main pulmonary artery. The left ventricular internal dimensions were 6.4 cm in diastole and 4.4 cm in systole; interventricular septal thickness at end-diastole was 1.43 cm.

Contrast-enhanced CT pulmonary angiography confirmed marked dilation of the main pulmonary artery (approx

title: A Dynamic Multimodality Imaging Assessment of Right Ventricular Thrombosis in a Middle-Aged Man with Lymphocytic Interstitial Pneumonia: The Additive Role of Tissue Doppler Imaging
case_summary:
A 46-year-old man with anti-Mi2 dermatomyositis and lymphocytic interstitial pneumonia on azathioprine, prednisone, rituximab, and oxygen therapy presented to the emergency department with progressive dyspnoea, chest pain, and malaise. On arrival, SaO2 was 66% on room air, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, heart rate 102 bpm, and temperature 36.3  degreesC. Arterial blood gas showed PaO2 39.6 mmHg, PaCO2 27.2 mmHg, pH 7.3, and lactate 7.7 mmol/L. Laboratory tests revealed WBC 13,900/uL, neutrophil-lymphocyte ratio 18.6, creatinine 1.57 mg/dL, troponin I 0.42 ng/mL, CRP 58 mg/L, D-dimer 4215 ug/L, and NT-proBNP 6474 pg/mL. ECG demonstrated sinus rhythm, mild right-ventricular conduction delay, and deep T-wave inversion in right precordial and inferior leads. Chest radiographs showed diffuse fibrosing interstitial lung disease without focal consolidation.

An urgent transthoracic echocardiogram revealed severe right-ventricular dilatation (RV/LV basal diameter ratio 2.4), mild RV hypokinesia (

title: An Egyptian child with erythromelalgia responding to a new line of treatment: a case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 34-month-old boy was evaluated for a 2-year history of intermittent, bilateral, intense pain, warmth, and redness of his feet. Each episode lasted minutes to hours and was precipitated by exercise or warmth (for example, covering his legs with blankets) and relieved by cooling (cold water). The attacks were symmetrical and confined to the feet without proximal extension. There was no history of similar family symptoms, medication use, or blood transfusions. 

On multiple visits, he appeared well with normal vital signs. There was no pallor, jaundice, cyanosis, organomegaly, or lymphadenopathy. During an episode, both feet were visibly red and warm; the remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed:  
? Complete blood count with differential: normal  
? Serum immunoglobulin E: 11 IU/mL (normal)  
? Cholesterol: 146 mg/dL; triglycerides: 49 mg/dL  
? Blood urea nitrogen: 16 mg/dL; creatinine: 0.7 mg/dL  
? Liver enzymes: ALT 22 U/L; AST 34 U/L  
? U

title: Macrodystrophia Lipomatosa: An Unusual Cause of Localized Gigantism
case_summary:
A 20-year-old man presented with a history of painless, progressive enlargement of his entire left upper limb since childhood. He had difficulty using the limb for daily activities and underwent partial debulking surgeries at ages 13 and 18, with subsequent regrowth. There was no relevant family history or other systemic symptoms.

On examination, the left upper limb was markedly enlarged--predominantly on the radial aspect involving the thumb, index finger, and thenar region--with a mid-arm circumference of 130 cm (four times that of the right). The overlying skin was thickened, nontender, and nonpulsatile. There were no nodules, caf?-au-lait spots, pitting edema, palpable bruit or thrill, or dilated veins. The contralateral limb and lower extremities were normal.

Plain radiographs of the left upper limb showed asymmetric soft-tissue prominence along the lateral aspect of the arm, forearm, and hand, including the first and second digits. Duplex ultrasound demonstrated no evidence of

title: "Black Esophagus" or Gurvits Syndrome: A Rare Complication of Diabetic Ketoacidosis
case_summary:
A 65-year-old man with a history of coronary artery disease, diabetes mellitus, and peripheral vascular disease was admitted with acute onset of altered mental status, agitation, and confusion. His temperature was 97.8  degreesF, blood pressure 114/55 mm Hg, heart rate 84 beats/min, respiratory rate 16/min, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air. On examination, he was alert but disoriented to time, place, and person, and was agitated and uncooperative. Laboratory studies revealed a blood glucose level of 989 mg/dL; arterial blood gas showed pH 7.2, PCO2 22.2 mm Hg, HCO3 9.8 mEq/L, PO2 90.7 mm Hg, and an anion gap of 30. Serum acetones were positive; urinary ketones were 15 mg/dL, and urinary glucose exceeded 1000 mg/dL, consistent with diabetic ketoacidosis. CT scans of the head, abdomen, and pelvis, as well as a chest radiograph, were unremarkable. He was started on an insulin infusion and supportive care.

On hospital day 3, he developed coffee-ground emesis, tested positive for feca

title: Arteriovenous malformation associated with trophoblastic retention post-cesarean section: A case report and review
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman with a history of a term cesarean delivery 3 years ago presented with 2 months of persistent low-grade metrorrhagia following a spontaneous miscarriage. Initial evaluation showed a plasma ?-HCG level of 13,500 mIU/mL. On referral, she was hemodynamically stable, afebrile, with no anemia. A pelvic examination revealed a normal cervix and no active endocervical or endometrial bleeding. Repeat plasma ?-HCG was 7100 mIU/mL, and hemoglobin was 12.1 g/dL. Transvaginal ultrasound revealed an anteverted, anteflexed uterus of normal size, a thin regular endometrium, and a heterogeneous, richly vascularized lesion in the anterior isthmus over the cesarean scar measuring 62 ? 56 ? 29 mm. Color Doppler imaging showed multiple dilated vessels with venous velocities over 50 cm/s and low-resistance arteries with systolic peaks over 100 cm/s. A high-flow vascular malformation associated with retained trophoblastic tissue was suspected.

title: The Rarest Variant Type Of Groin Cystic Mass In Adult Female: Encysted Hydrocele Canal Of Nuck
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
final_diagnosis: Hydrocele of the canal of Nuck
case_summary:
A 25-year-old woman presented with a one-month history of a right-sided, palpable inguinolabial swelling that had gradually increased in size and was associated with pain. She denied any history of hernia or trauma. On examination, there was a 5???4?cm right inguinolabial bulge that was tender, irreducible, without overlying skin changes, and with a negative cough impulse. Routine blood tests were unremarkable.  
Abdominal ultrasonography revealed a well-defined, septated cystic lesion measuring 5.8???3.1?cm in the right inguinolabial region without internal or peripheral vascularization. A contrast-enhanced CT scan confirmed a comma-shaped, cystic structure in the same location, without obvious communication to the peritoneal cavity.  
Based on the clinical presentation and imaging, a working diagnosis of femoral hernia was made, and elective surgical exploration was planned.

title: Multimodality Image-Guided Embolization of Bronchial Artery Pseudoaneurysm in a Patient With?Aortopathy
case_summary:
A 59-year-old man was referred for evaluation of a progressively enlarging thoracic para-aortic mass. He had undergone noncontrast CT at an outside hospital, which revealed a 5-cm para-aortic mass in the right posterior mediastinum initially thought to be of venous origin because of its proximity to the azygos vein. The patient was asymptomatic, and the clinical examination was unremarkable.

Medical History: Hypertension, nasal skin cancer; prior Bentall procedure, resection of coronary artery ostial aneurysms, and aortoiliac bypass for aortic and bilateral iliac dissection.

The differential diagnosis of posterior mediastinal masses included neurogenic tumors, infectious spondylitis, meningocele, thoracic aortic aneurysm, aortic dissection, dilated azygos vein, esophageal varices or tumors, lymphoma, and lymphadenopathy.

Investigations: Time-resolved, cardiac MR angiography demonstrated a 5.5 ? 4.5 cm, partially thrombosed mass to the right of the proximal descending thoracic aorta,

title: Approach to Diagnosis of TFE3-rearranged Renal Cell Carcinoma in a Limited Resource Setting: A Case Report
journal: Journal of Kidney Cancer and VHL
case_summary:
A 19-year-old woman presented with dysuria and a progressively enlarging right flank mass that became palpable on examination. A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the abdomen showed a large, lobulated, heterogeneously enhancing mass arising from the right kidney, with internal calcifications, hypodense necrotic areas, and extension into the right renal vein and inferior vena cava. Chest CT for metastatic workup revealed several subcentimeter, noncalcified pulmonary and pleural-based nodules, with no pleural effusion or mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

The patient underwent right radical nephrectomy. Gross examination of the bivalved specimen revealed a 20.3 ? 14.4 ? 11.7 cm ill-defined, irregularly shaped, firm to friable, lobulated, yellow-tan mass with extensive hemorrhage and necrosis, almost completely replacing the renal parenchyma, and a large tumor thrombus in the renal vein.

Microscopic sections showed a tumor with predominantly lobulated, nested, and papillary-fused architecture, cen

title: Grzybowski's Generalized Eruptive Keratoacanthomas in a Patient with Terminal Kidney Disease--An Unmet Medical Need Equally Ameliorated by Topical Imiquimod Cream and Lapacho Tea Wraps: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 51-year-old Caucasian man presented with a 10-month history of intensely pruritic papules on sun-exposed areas of his extremities, causing sleep disturbance. He had no prior dermatologic evaluation and no personal or family history of cancer. His medical history was significant for arterial hypertension, hyperuricemia, coronary heart disease, idiopathic end-stage kidney disease (with two kidney transplants in 1990 and 2001 complicated by rejections), peritoneal dialysis (2007-2017), and ongoing hemodialysis since 2017. He also carried a factor V Leiden mutation. His medications included felodipine, bisoprolol, clonidine, ramipril, urapidil, cholecalciferol, aspirin, allopurinol, phenprocoumon, thyroxine, iodine, fluvastatin, calcium acetate, omeprazole, and sevelamer carbonate. 

On examination, there were approximately 100 scaly, ulcerated papules and nodules measuring 3-20 mm scattered over the sun-exposed upper and lower extremities. There were no signs of chronic actinic damage,

title: Pneumoperitoneum as a result of a ruptured splenic abscess
case_summary: A 48-year-old woman with a history of diabetes mellitus, coronary artery disease, and psoriasis presented with a 2-day history of acute diffuse abdominal pain. On arrival, she was tachycardic (heart rate 130/min), hypotensive (blood pressure 78/45 mm Hg), afebrile, and fully oriented. She received intravenous fluids with improvement in blood pressure and heart rate. Initial laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 8.3 ? 10^3/uL, arterial pH of 7.29, and lactate of 3.5 mmol/L. An acute abdominal radiograph demonstrated free air beneath the right hemidiaphragm. Examination of the abdomen showed distension, tenderness, guarding, and signs of peritonitis. Because her vital signs stabilized after resuscitation, a contrast-enhanced abdominal CT scan was obtained to localize the source of the pneumoperitoneum.

title: CT and MR imaging of a huge retroperitoneal synovial sarcoma: A case report
case_summary: A 29-year-old man with no prior medical history presented with a 1-month history of progressively worsening severe back pain unrelieved by analgesics and associated with unintended weight loss. On examination, there was a large, palpable mass in the left abdomen. Routine blood and urine tests were within normal limits. Abdominal ultrasound revealed a large heterogeneous soft?tissue lesion occupying the left abdomen with marked vascularity on Doppler imaging. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen showed a 190 ? 133 ? 227 mm left retroperitoneal mass of heterogeneous density and enhancement, containing central necrotic fluid collections, multiple calcifications, and no fatty component. The mass extended from L2 to S4, eroded the left transverse processes of L4 and L5, infiltrated adjacent paravertebral and iliacus muscles, displaced the small bowel and colon medially, abutted the anterior abdominal wall without breach, and reached the lower pole of the left kidney. MRI demonstrated heterog

title: False lumen thrombus following aortic dissection diagnosed as the source of repeat lower extremity emboli with angioscopy: a case report
case_summary: A 71-year-old woman with hypertension and a history of subarachnoid hemorrhage underwent hemiarch replacement for Stanford type A acute aortic dissection. Two months later, she presented with intermittent claudication of the left leg. Examination revealed absence of the left femoral pulse, with pain and coldness of the foot but no sensory or motor deficits. An electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated normal cardiac function without intracardiac thrombus or atrial enlargement. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and lower extremities revealed occlusion extending from the left common femoral artery through the superficial, deep femoral, and popliteal arteries. A diagnosis of acute arterial occlusion of the left lower extremity was made, and emergency thrombectomy was performed. Warfarin was started (target INR, 1.8-2.5). One month later, her left leg pain recurred, and emergency thrombectomy was repeated; warfarin and aspirin were administered. O

title: Palpitations, headache and night sweats caused by a retroperitoneal mass: case report and short review
case_summary:
A 31-year-old woman from West Africa was referred after routine blood tests showed marked inflammation (C-reactive protein, 352 mg/l) and anemia (hemoglobin, 7.2 g/dl). Over the preceding 2 weeks, she had experienced malaise, fatigue and night sweats, as well as palpitations and persistent headaches. In the emergency department, she was tachycardic, and examination revealed a painful, palpable infraumbilical mass.  
Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated a 5 cm prevertebral, smoothly rounded soft-tissue mass with smooth edges and internal vascularization on color Doppler. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen in arterial and portal venous phases showed a heterogeneously enhancing nodular mass caudal to the aortic bifurcation, without evidence of invasive growth or distant metastases.  
Positron emission tomography-CT one hour after injection of 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose revealed inhomogeneous intense uptake within the lesion, with a maximum standardized uptake value of 12.2, and no FDG-avid loco

title: Unilateral delayed post-hypoxic leukoencephalopathy: a case report
case_summary:
An 81-year-old man with newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukemia and chronic kidney disease (stage G2), hypertension, and hyperlipidemia was admitted for cytoreductive therapy. On hospital day 13, he suddenly developed right-sided hemiparesis and global aphasia (NIHSS score 17). Noncontrast head CT showed no early ischemic changes (ASPECTS 10), and CT angiography demonstrated an occlusion of the left cervical internal carotid artery without intracranial large-vessel occlusion. He was transferred for endovascular therapy under general anesthesia. Angiography revealed a subpetrous dissection of the left internal carotid artery; aspiration thrombectomy achieved complete reperfusion (eTICI 3) with an onset-to-recanalization time of 265 minutes. 

After extubation, his aphasia and hemiparesis improved immediately. Twenty-four-hour NCCT showed neither infarction nor hemorrhage. On day 2 after treatment, his NIHSS score was 5. On day 10, MRI showed no diffusion-weighted or FLAIR abnormalities,

title: Primary splenic angiosarcoma: a rare entity often associated with rupture and hemoperitoneum
case_summary:
An 80-year-old woman presented with a 6-month history of progressive abdominal pain, weight loss, fatigue, and pallor. Her medical history included hypertension and atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. On examination, she had marked pallor and massive splenomegaly (spleen palpable 10 cm below the left costal margin, firm, non-tender) without lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies showed:
? Hemoglobin 8.9 g/dL with mild microcytosis (MCV 79.3 fL) and reticulocyte count 1.36%  
? White-cell and platelet counts within reference ranges  
? Renal and liver function tests normal; ferritin 206 ng/mL (RR 13-150), transferrin saturation 10% (RR 20-50)  
? Folic acid and vitamin B12 levels normal  

An abdominal CT scan demonstrated a diffusely heterogeneous, massively enlarged spleen (24 ? 11 ? 18 cm; volume 2470 mL) with hypovascular areas; the liver was unremarkable, and there was no abdominal lymphadenopathy or venous thrombosis. Chest CT showed cardiomegaly and coronary atherosclerosis. Upp

title: Eosinophilic angiitis presenting with a true, fusiform, temporal artery aneurysm
case_summary:
A 51?year?old man was referred with a five?week history of an enlarging lump in the left temporal area. He reported onset shortly after forceful nose blowing during an upper respiratory illness. He had unintentionally lost about 3 kg and complained of an occipital headache but denied any head trauma. His medical history included seasonal rhinitis; he had previously used a corticosteroid inhaler. Family history was notable for systemic lupus in a sister, rheumatoid arthritis in his mother, and cerebrovascular disease in his father. He was a former smoker (15 pack?year history), having quit one year earlier.

On examination, vital signs were normal. A 4-cm firm, nonpulsatile swelling was palpable over the left temple without overlying skin changes. Neurologic examination was unremarkable. Initial laboratory studies showed a normal complete blood count except for an elevated eosinophil count of 1.8 ? 10^9/L. Renal and liver function, immunoglobulins, and C-reactive protein were within nor

title: Calcinosis cutis in the setting of severe COVID-19 infection
case_summary: A 51-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia was admitted with acute respiratory distress syndrome secondary to COVID-19. His hospital course was complicated by a post-COVID-19 inflammatory response syndrome, marked by high fevers, a leukemoid reaction, acute renal failure requiring renal replacement therapy, deep venous thrombosis and pulmonary embolism (managed with heparin), and pneumoperitoneum. On hospital day 89, dermatology consultation was requested for a right upper-arm lesion of unknown duration that was occasionally tender but otherwise asymptomatic. Examination revealed indurated pink-yellow linear columns of coalescing papules arising from an inferior central plaque with an overlying angulated eschar, spanning most of the right upper arm. Laboratory studies showed a blood urea nitrogen concentration of 87 mg/dL, a phosphorus concentration of 5 mg/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 44 mm/h, and a negative antinuclear antibody test.

title:
Gallbladder protrusion through the groin region--a very unusual
femoral hernia
case_summary:
An 81?year?old Caucasian woman presented with a 10-month history of a right groin bulge and mild exertional pain. She had no systemic disease and no prior episodes of acute cholecystitis. Surgical history included urinary incontinence surgery 12 years earlier and a left Lichtenstein hernioplasty 10 years earlier without recurrence. She had known thoracic and abdominal aortic aneurysms planned for endovascular repair and a cystocele.

On examination, there was a firm, nonreducible bulge in the right inguinal region that did not increase with coughing; palpation elicited pain.

Ultrasound of the groin revealed an elongated tubular structure containing multiple calculi, originating from the right hypochondrium and projecting into the femoral canal.

title: An Extremely Rare Case of Bonneau Syndrome with Novel Cardiac and Eye Manifestations
journal: Saudi Journal of Medicine & Medical Sciences
case_summary:
A female infant was born at 35 weeks of gestation to first-degree cousin parents after a pregnancy complicated by polyhydramnios. Antenatal ultrasound had shown intrauterine growth retardation, polysyndactyly, and complex cardiac malformations. At birth, her weight was 1.6 kg, length 40 cm, and head circumference 29 cm (all <3rd centile); Apgar scores were 7 and 10. Physical examination revealed arched eyebrows, depressed nasal bridge, hypertelorism, low-set ears with simple helix, and hirsutism. Ophthalmic findings included microphthalmia, cataract, and vitreous hemorrhage. Limb anomalies comprised broad, proximally inserted thumbs; cutaneous syndactyly of the second to fourth fingers bilaterally; postaxial polydactyly of both hands; broad big toes; and syndactyly of the second to fourth toes bilaterally. She had central cyanosis, a soft systolic murmur at the upper left sternal border, and an oxygen saturation of 80% on 30% FiO?.  

Laboratory and imaging studies showed:
? Electrocar

title: The Use of Resuscitative Endovascular Balloon Occlusion of the Aorta in a Case of Suspected Septic Distributive Shock: A Case Report
journal: Journal of the American College of Emergency Physicians Open
case_summary:
A 30-year-old Ukrainian soldier was admitted to a Level 1 trauma center 20 days after an explosion injury that resulted in complex bilateral lower-leg fractures managed with external fixation and approximately 20% BSA mid- to deep dermal burns. On admission, multiresistant Gram-negative bacteria and MRSA osteomyelitis were diagnosed in both legs. After burn care and skin grafting, he underwent intramedullary reaming and irrigation of both lower extremities for osteomyelitis.

Just before the end of this operation, with a central venous catheter, arterial line, and spontaneous breathing, he developed sudden circulatory collapse without major blood loss. Push-doses of theodrenalin-cafedrin were given, followed by adrenaline and noradrenaline infusions (0.1 ?g/kg/min each), balanced fluids, and an increase in FiO? to 100%. Despite these measures, his blood pressure fell to 35/20 mm Hg and end-tidal CO? decreased from 38 to 20 mm Hg. Ventilator settings (PSV, PEEP 7 mbar, PIP 20 mbar, RR 1

title: Splenic rupture in community acquired pneumonia: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 49-year-old woman presented with low back pain radiating down her right leg following three weeks of intractable cough productive of green sputum. She denied chest pain, shortness of breath, fevers, neurologic symptoms, or urinary or bowel complaints. She had completed a course of oral cephalexin prescribed by her general practitioner. Her medical history was notable only for hypertension and chronic low back pain. She smoked 20 cigarettes per day (15 pack-year history). 

On examination, she was afebrile, with normal vital signs and no respiratory distress. Lung auscultation revealed bronchial breath sounds and coarse crepitations in the right mid- to lower zones. Her abdomen was soft and non-tender. Neurologic examination of the lower limbs was normal. Laboratory tests showed a white-cell count of 19.2 ? 10^9/L with neutrophilia. A chest radiograph demonstrated obscuration of the right heart border, consistent with right middle lobe consolidation. 

She was diagnosed with community

title: Adult Wilms' Tumour: Case Report and Review of Literature
journal: Journal of Kidney Cancer and VHL
case_summary:
A 43-year-old Caucasian man presented with a 4-week history of right upper quadrant and right flank pain radiating to his right testicle. There was no preceding trauma, haematuria, or other systemic symptoms. His medical history included a resected osteoid osteoma of the left femur in 2007 and an L4/5 microdiscectomy. He was a non-smoker with no allergies and took no regular medications. On examination, the right flank was full in contour with minimal tenderness. Laboratory studies showed a haemoglobin concentration of 140 g/L, a creatinine level of 116 umol/L, and an estimated glomerular filtration rate of 66 ml/min.  
An abdominal ultrasound revealed a large right renal subcapsular haematoma measuring 7 ? 5 ? 7 cm compressing the renal cortex. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen showed an enlarged right kidney with a mass in the lower pole measuring 7.4 ? 7.5 ? 9.2 cm and associated paracaval lymphadenopathy causing compression of the right renal vein and inferior vena cava. A repeat

title: Spontaneous rupture of the ovarian vein in association with nutcracker syndrome: a case report
case_summary:
A 77-year-old Japanese woman (para 7; menopause at age 48) with hypertension (on nifedipine and candesartan) and chronic hepatitis C (normal liver function tests) presented with sudden onset of left lower abdominal pain. A primary care CT scan showed a 7.5 cm elliptical mass in the left adnexal region, atrophic ovary, and findings suggestive of a retroperitoneal hematoma initially interpreted as torsion of a left ovarian cyst, and she was transferred for surgical management. 

On admission: height 144.5 cm; weight 57.2 kg; BMI 27.4; blood pressure 112/50 mm Hg; pulse 72 bpm; temperature 37.1  degreesC. Physical examination of the abdomen and pelvic exam revealed no palpable masses or tenderness. Transvaginal ultrasound confirmed an atrophied uterus, could not visualize adnexa, and identified the left pelvic hematoma. 

Initial labs: hemoglobin 9.5 g/dL; hematocrit 28.4%; WBC 7970/?L; CRP 0.05 mg/dL; urinalysis showed mild occult blood. The hematoma remained stable in size during 11 day

title: Vacuoles, E1 enzyme, X-linked, autoinflammatory, and somatic syndrome in the intensive care unit: a?case report
case_summary:
A 70-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and prostate cancer in remission presented in October 2019 with a right leg deep vein thrombosis treated with rivaroxaban. One month later he developed persistent fever (38.5  degreesC) and a non?itchy, painless papular rash on all four limbs. By year's end the rash had rapidly extended centrifugally with erythematous margins and purplish centers. Skin biopsy confirmed Sweet's syndrome. CT imaging of chest and abdomen showed no malignancy. Laboratory studies revealed CRP 187 mg/L and polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia (20.9 g/L); JAK2 mutation and infectious work?up were negative. Prednisone induced temporary remission, but skin eruptions recurred with each taper through November 2020.

At end 2020 he was rehospitalized for asthenia, weight loss, recurrent rash, and periorbital edema. CRP was 257 mg/L. He developed laryngeal pain, left ear helix pain, and acute unilateral hearing loss (suspected polychondritis), right eye episcleritis, chemosis, an

title: Cerebral proliferative angiopathy depicted by four-dimensional computed tomographic angiography: A case report
case_summary:
A 53-year-old man presented with two months of progressive left-sided weakness.  
He had no headache, seizure, or cutaneous abnormalities, and his medical, family, and social histories were unremarkable.  
Laboratory studies were within normal limits.  
Unenhanced CT showed right hemispheric brain atrophy, especially in the frontal lobe, with subcortical spotty calcifications.  
Contrast-enhanced CT revealed diffuse abnormal vessels in the right hemisphere.  
MRI demonstrated no restricted diffusion or hemorrhage, and confirmed parenchymal atrophy.  
On MR angiography, the vascular network comprised arterial components fed by the right anterior, middle, and posterior cerebral arteries with strong flow signals, and venous components draining into cortical veins and the deep venous system with slightly strong flow signals.  
Based on these CT and MR imaging findings, diffuse arteriovenous shunts, such as arteriovenous malformation and diffuse pial or dural arteriovenous fistula, were con

title: Clostridium myonecrosis -- a rare and underdiagnosed condition in the elderly: a case with severe skipping lesions and an overview of treatment guidelines
case_summary:
An 84?year?old woman living independently was found on her bedroom floor and brought to the emergency department, complaining of mild diffuse pain (VAS 3/10). She had no recent trauma and a history only of chronic sotalol use. Initial vital signs were: blood pressure 149/61 mm Hg, pulse 60 beats/min, oxygen saturation 98%, and temperature 35.4  degreesC. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal examinations were unremarkable. Neurologically, she was alert and oriented with normal speech, although communication was limited by hearing loss; she could not recall a fall. The right foot and lower leg were cold, with delayed capillary refill and absent peripheral pulses. The medial aspect of the left lower leg showed light reddish discoloration without blisters or crepitus; pulses were palpable and temperature was normal. Both hips were painful on movement, and posterior skin and spine examination revealed no abnormalities.

Initial arterial blood gases and labs (returned within 1 hour) showed hemoglob

title: Pulmonary embolism or COVID?19 pneumonia? A case report
case_summary:
A 63-year-old Iranian woman with a 2-year history of dementia presented in January 2022 with progressive confusion, disorientation, severe agitation, visual and auditory hallucinations, and impaired sleep over the preceding 2 weeks. Five days before admission she was diagnosed with delirium superimposed on dementia at an outpatient clinic; her prior psychotropic medications were stopped, and clonazepam, haloperidol, and quetiapine were started without improvement.

On admission she was afebrile; blood pressure 120/70 mm Hg; heart rate 96 bpm; respiratory rate 18 breaths/min; oxygen saturation 92% on room air; and BMI 32. She was restless, incoherent, and disoriented to time, place, and person. Laboratory studies showed hypokalemia (K 3.2 mmol/L), elevated C-reactive protein (24 mg/L), erythrocyte sedimentation rate (23 mm/hr), creatine phosphokinase (923 IU/L), lactate dehydrogenase (639 U/L), and a mild respiratory alkalosis (pH 7.47, pCO2 34.7 mm Hg). Complete blood count, metabolic

title: Gastrointestinal Manifestations of CLOVES Syndrome
case_summary:
A 34-year-old man was referred for a several?year history of hematochezia accompanied by abdominal discomfort, tenesmus, and urgency. He denied nocturnal stooling, weight loss, infectious or extraintestinal symptoms. Family and social histories were noncontributory.

Outpatient MRI of the abdomen showed diffuse mural thickening and mucosal hyperenhancement of the distal colon and rectum. Laboratory studies revealed a mild normocytic anemia (hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL) and mild thrombocytopenia (platelets 140?10^9/L); electrolytes, creatinine, ferritin, liver enzymes, celiac serologies, and C?reactive protein were normal.

On examination, there was a discrepancy in limb size, ectatic veins on the arms, and port?wine skin lesions. The abdomen was soft and nontender without organomegaly; digital rectal examination was normal.

Two years earlier, a geneticist had confirmed a diagnosis of CLOVES syndrome based on limb overgrowth, port?wine stains, superficial vascular malformations, macrodactyly,

title: Neonatal adrenal hemorrhage presenting as an acute scrotum: A case report on the rare presentation of right adrenal hemorrhage and contralateral left scrotal hematoma
case_summary:
A male infant (birth weight 3450 g) was born at 39 weeks' gestation by uncomplicated vaginal delivery in cephalic presentation. There was no history of birth trauma. Apgar scores were 9 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes. On day 1 of life, left scrotal swelling and bluish discoloration were noted. Physical examination showed a tender, swollen left scrotum without abdominal distention.

Scrotal ultrasonography with power Doppler demonstrated a normally echogenic left testis with preserved vascular flow and a collection of hematic fluid in the left scrotum. Abdominal ultrasonography revealed a heterogeneous fluid collection beneath the right lobe of the liver.

An abdominal CT scan was performed and identified a right adrenal hematoma without evidence of hemoperitoneum.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, platelets 234 000/mm^3, normal coagulation tests, total bilirubin 5.4 mg/dL, and direct bilirubin 0.5 mg/dL.

title: Massive uterine fibroid: a diagnostic dilemma: a case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 46-year-old nulligravid German woman presented with a 15-year history of slowly increasing abdominal girth and two episodes of polymenorrhea and menorrhagia. She denied bowel or bladder complaints. Her past avoidance of medical care led to sporadic consultations during which no imaging was performed, and her abdominal enlargement was attributed to weight gain.

On examination, her BMI was 32.1 kg/m2. She appeared well, with no pallor or edema. Her abdomen was massively distended and pendulous, with flank fullness, and an irregular mass extending from the pelvis to the xiphisternum. A vaginal examination was not possible because of patient anxiety.

Transabdominal ultrasound revealed a huge intraabdominal mass, impaired appearance of the right kidney, compensatory enlargement of the left kidney, and a small amount of ascites. An urgent CT scan showed a 32 ? 27 ? 34 cm heterogeneous mass occupying the abdominopelvic cavity that could not be visibly separated from the uterine cavity, bl

title: Hepatic abscess caused by esophageal foreign body misdiagnosed as cystadenocarcinoma by magnetic resonance imaging: A case report
case_summary:
A 51-year-old woman with a 1-year history of poorly controlled diabetes presented with a 15-day history of chills and fever up to 38.8  degreesC despite prior outpatient antibiotics. She denied abdominal pain, vomiting, or weight loss. On admission, her temperature was 36.7  degreesC, blood pressure 115/71 mmHg, heart rate 90/min, and respiratory rate 18/min. Physical examination revealed slight tenderness under the xiphoid process without rebound or guarding; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (24.67 ? 10^9/L) with neutrophil predominance (91.2%), hypoalbuminemia (28.2 g/L), elevated alanine aminotransferase (84 IU/L), aspartate aminotransferase (67 IU/L), and D-dimer (14.68 mg/mL); tumor markers CEA and CA19-9 were within normal limits. Viral hepatitis and HIV screens were negative.

A prior plain CT scan showed a low-density mass in the hepatic porta, and a subsequent color Doppler ultrasound revealed a 6.5 ? 4.3 cm hypoechoic

title: Successful Thrombolytic Therapy for?Mechanical Mitral Valve Thrombosis?Using Bivalirudin-Based Thrombolytic Regimen
case_summary:
A 43-year-old woman presented with a 2-week history of progressive dyspnea. She was afebrile, with a heart rate of 87 beats per minute, blood pressure of 97/70 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation of 94% on room air. Physical examination revealed elevated jugular venous pressure and a muffled mechanical closure click. Her medical history was notable for rheumatic heart disease, mitral valve replacement with a 27-mm bileaflet mechanical prosthesis 9 years earlier, a prior episode of prosthetic valve thrombosis treated with tPA 8 years ago, and a reported allergy to porcine products. Home warfarin therapy was chronic, but her INR on admission was subtherapeutic at 1.3.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL and brain natriuretic peptide 241 pg/mL. Electrocardiography revealed normal sinus rhythm with nonspecific ST-segment changes. Chest radiography showed mild interstitial prominence. CT scan of the chest with contrast demonstrated bilateral moderate pleural effusions and an enlarged p

title: Imaging?based diagnosis of intrapancreatic accessory spleen: A case report
case_summary:
A 60?year?old man was referred after a routine abdominal ultrasound identified a mass in the tail of the pancreas. His medical history was notable for hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, coronary artery disease (with three stents), and a cerebrovascular accident in 2006; he had no history of malignancy. He reported no abdominal pain, weight loss, or symptoms suggestive of a functioning pancreatic neuroendocrine tumour. Physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed normal serum amylase (51 U/L), lipase (18 U/L), carcinoembryonic antigen (0.8 ug/L), and carbohydrate antigen 19-9 (8.1 kIU/L). White blood cell count and C-reactive protein were within normal limits.

A triple?phase contrast?enhanced CT scan revealed a 25 ? 17 mm, ovoid, well?demarcated, hypervascular lesion in the pancreatic tail with no ductal involvement. On noncontrast images, the lesion's density matched that of the spleen. In the arterial phase, it showed an inhomogeneous "zebra" enhancement patt

title: Portal Cavernoma during Pregnancy
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
case_summary:
A 32-year-old woman, gravida 3, para 2, was referred at 13 weeks of gestation after an incidental finding on abdominal ultrasonography. At 11 weeks, she had been admitted for back pain and fever; pyelonephritis was diagnosed and treated with intravenous cefalexin. A routine abdominal scan revealed solid, hyperechoic material within a dilated portal vein, with increased caliber and multiple serpentine channels. She was hemodynamically stable and asymptomatic at presentation.

On examination, blood pressure was 100/50 mm Hg, heart rate 65 bpm, and respiratory rate 20 breaths per minute. There was mild splenomegaly; the abdomen was nontender with normal bowel sounds and no ascites. Obstetrical ultrasound confirmed a single viable fetus consistent with 13 weeks of gestation, and first-trimester aneuploidy screening was low risk. The patient's history was notable for a gastric sleeve procedure one year earlier for obesity; there was no history of liver disease.

Laboratory tests showed:
- H

title: Diabetic myonecrosis: a diagnostic challenge in patients with long-standing diabetes
journal: Journal of Community Hospital Internal Medicine Perspectives
case_summary:
A 51-year-old woman with a >20-year history of poorly controlled type 1 diabetes mellitus complicated by dialysis-dependent renal failure, retinopathy, and neuropathy presented with sudden onset of left thigh swelling and constant severe pain for 7 days. She denied trauma, vascular interventions, fever, or weight loss. Her medical history included coronary artery disease, stroke, hypertension, and mild cirrhosis from hepatitis C.

On examination, the left thigh was erythematous, edematous, and tender on the medial aspect, with a circumference 9 cm greater than the right. The overlying skin was otherwise normal, and she was afebrile. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 11 000/?L, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/h, a C-reactive protein level of 1.3 mg/dL, and a creatine phosphokinase level of 338 U/L.

Plain radiography of the thigh showed no gas or calcification. Lower-extremity arterial and venous Doppler studies showed no vascular occlusion, and blood cultures w

title: Gallbladder volvulus--acute cholecystitis 'with a twist'
case_summary:
A 92-year-old Caucasian woman presented with sudden-onset epigastric and right upper quadrant pain that awoke her from sleep. The pain was constant, radiated to her back, and was immediately followed by profuse vomiting. She denied fever, rigors, urinary or bowel symptoms and had no prior similar episodes. Her medical history included hypertension, gastroesophageal reflux disease, a previous transient ischaemic attack with left carotid endarterectomy, peripheral vascular disease, an L2 vertebral crush fracture, and a remote open appendicectomy. 

On examination, she was afebrile with normal vital signs. The abdomen was soft and non-distended, with diffuse right-sided tenderness and a palpable gallbladder in the right iliac fossa. Laboratory tests showed a white-cell count of 13.2?10^9/L; electrolytes, renal function, and liver-function tests were within normal limits. 

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated a distended gallbladder with prominent wall thickening an

title: Dyspnoea at Term in an Obese Mother
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
case_summary:
A 35-year-old European woman, gravida 1 para 0, with an unplanned pregnancy presented for antenatal care at 35 weeks' gestation. Her medical history included asthma managed with a salbutamol inhaler. At presentation, her weight was 147 kg (BMI 54 kg/m2). An oral glucose tolerance test was normal, and throughout antenatal visits she remained normotensive with no proteinuria. She reported dyspnoea on exertion, bilateral leg swelling, and marked weight gain, which were attributed to her pregnancy.

At 40 weeks + 3 days, she developed bilateral leg erythema, oedema, and tenderness; Doppler ultrasound excluded deep venous thrombosis, and she received antibiotics and physiotherapy. A fetal ultrasound estimated the weight at 4755 g and noted polyhydramnios. At 41 weeks + 1 day, after prelabour rupture of membranes and 6 hours of unsuccessful oxytocin induction, an emergency caesarean section was performed. Intraoperatively, subcutaneous abdominal oedema and ascites were noted. A healthy male
