title: Colon cancer metastasis mimicking a hilar cholangiocarcinoma: a case report and literature review
case_summary:
A 69-year-old man was referred for evaluation of hilar bile duct stenosis. His history was significant for laparoscopic sigmoidectomy 4 years earlier for stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous liver metastases. He underwent partial hepatectomy of segment 7 and radiofrequency ablation of segment 8 lesions, and, two years later, resection of a local post-RFA recurrence. He had received no adjuvant chemotherapy.

On presentation, vital signs were within normal limits. Laboratory studies showed cholestatic liver enzyme elevation. Enhanced CT demonstrated wall thickening and enhancement of the hilar bile duct. Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography, endoscopic retrograde cholangiography, and intraductal ultrasound revealed a mass protruding into the lumen of the hilar bile duct. Brush cytology returned class IV cells. Based on imaging and cytology, a hilar cholangiocarcinoma (Bismuth type II) was diagnosed. The patient underwent right portal vein embolization followed b

title: Spontaneous rectus sheath hematoma revealed by abdominal pain during pregnancy: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 32-year-old gravida 6, para 3 woman at 31 weeks of gestation presented with irregular uterine contractions and right-sided abdominal pain that was worsened by movement, palpation, and coughing. She denied recent trauma or coughing spells. Her previous obstetric and surgical history included three vaginal deliveries and one cesarean; two prior spontaneous abortions were unexplained. 

On examination, she was hemodynamically stable. The uterus was appropriately enlarged for gestational age. There was moderate rigidity and tenderness of the right abdominal wall, with blue discoloration over the lateral abdomen and flank. No masses were palpable. Vaginal examination showed a closed cervix and no vaginal bleeding. Fetal heart rate was 140 beats per minute without uterine contractions.

Laboratory studies revealed hematocrit 25.2%, hemoglobin 8.4 g/dL, platelet count 132 000/mm3, INR 1.01, and APTT 30 s. Leukocyte count and urine protein were normal. Obstetric ultrasound showed a posterior

title: Incidental descending thoracic aortic thrombus: the conundrum of medical versus surgical therapy
journal: Journal of Community Hospital Internal Medicine Perspectives
case_summary:
A 65-year-old woman with a history of sigmoidectomy and end colostomy for postoperative anastomotic leak presented to the emergency department with four days of progressive dyspnea, orthopnea, and new pitting edema. Four days earlier she had been treated for acute hypoxic respiratory failure and clinical congestive heart failure with two doses of IV furosemide and discharged home.  
On re-evaluation, vital signs were within normal limits. Physical examination was notable for bilateral pitting edema to the mid-shin and bibasilar crackles.  
Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis and hemoglobin of 11 g/dL; electrolytes and renal function were normal, and urinalysis was unremarkable.  
A CT angiogram of the chest to exclude pulmonary embolism revealed no pulmonary emboli but demonstrated an irregular, homogeneous 15.5 mm ? 12.8 mm filling defect along the right side of the descending thoracic aorta, at the 9 o'clock position opposite the carina, without other aortic wall abnormalities.

title: Diffusion-weighted MR imaging and assessment of ovarian carcinomas with vaginal deposit accidentally detected during pregnancy
case_summary: A 40-year-old woman, gravida 5 para 4 at 32 weeks of gestation, presented with repeated episodes of vaginal bleeding. On abdominal examination, the uterine fundal height was disproportionately large for her gestational age. Transabdominal ultrasound revealed a single viable intrauterine fetus consistent with 32 weeks' gestation. Screening of the adnexal regions showed bilateral complex masses with high vascularity measuring 8 ? 6 cm on the right and 12 ? 8 cm on the left. Transvaginal ultrasound identified a highly vascular polypoidal mass projecting into the upper vagina, indistinct from a bulky cervix, and solid peritoneal deposits in the pouch of Douglas. A non-contrast pelvic MRI performed one week later showed bilateral complex cystic adnexal masses and an upper vaginal mass on T2-weighted images. On diffusion-weighted imaging, these masses exhibited persistent bright signal intensity and low apparent diffusion coefficient values. Laboratory tests were within normal limits except

title: A unique case of xanthogranulomatous cholecystitis complicated by multiple liver abscesses and portal vein and hepatic artery thrombosis and occlusion
case_summary:
A 64-year-old Caucasian man with non-insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, chronic renal failure, chronic hepatitis C, and coronary artery disease presented initially with diabetic ketoacidosis. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen showed hypodense areas in the medial and lateral segments of the left hepatic lobe extending toward the hilum, concerning for an evolving phlegmon of the liver. Right upper quadrant ultrasound revealed thrombosis of the left portal vein. He was anticoagulated with heparin and transitioned to warfarin.

Two weeks later, he was readmitted with sepsis. Image-guided drainage of the presumed liver abscess yielded no purulent fluid; a solid lesion was biopsied, and pathology showed organizing hematoma with acute and chronic inflammation. Repeat CT imaging demonstrated persistent thrombosis of the left portal vein, anterior branches of the right portal vein, and the right hepatic artery.

Because of the persistent left hepatic lesion and concern for biliary obstructi

title: Fine-Needle Aspiration Cytology of Parathyroid Carcinoma Mimic H?rthle Cell Thyroid Neoplasm
case_summary:
A 31-year-old man noted a palpable 1.5 cm nodule in the lower pole of the right thyroid lobe. Thyroid function tests were normal. An initial fine-needle aspiration (FNA) without ultrasound guidance showed intrathyroidal lymphoid hyperplasia. Over the next 5 years, the nodule slowly enlarged without compressive symptoms. Repeat FNA yielded a H?rthle cell lesion. The surgeon planned a right thyroid lobectomy.  
Preoperative studies revealed:  
? Serum calcium 13.5 mg/dL (8.5-10.1) and phosphorus 1.9 mg/dL (2.5-4.5).  
? Parathyroid hormone level 1,859 pg/mL (15-65).  
? Renal function normal.  
On dedicated neck ultrasound, a 2.8 cm heterogeneous, hypervascular mass was seen posterior to the right inferior pole of the thyroid. A Tc-99m sestamibi SPECT scan showed increased uptake corresponding to this mass.  
History elicited 7 years of chronic knee pain. Bone radiographs demonstrated generalized demineralization, multiple osteolytic lesions in the distal femur, proximal tibia, fibula, a

title: The IRIS paradox: Imaging findings in a case of PJP?IRIS
case_summary:
A 57?year?old woman with a history of HIV infection who had discontinued antiretroviral therapy 2 years earlier presented with a 2-week history of progressive dyspnoea and cough. Her oxygen saturation was 90% on room air, and her temperature was 37.6 degreesC. A CT pulmonary angiogram showed diffuse bilateral ground-glass opacities with well-demarcated peripheral sparing. Laboratory studies revealed an HIV viral load of 4.2 ? 10^6 copies/mL, a CD4 count of 50 cells/mm^3, and an induced sputum PCR for Pneumocystis jirovecii of 2.8 ? 10^8 copies/mL. She was treated with intravenous trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole and high-dose prednisone, with clinical recovery. After 3 weeks, she was switched to prophylactic dosing of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, and bictegravir-emtricitabine-tenofovir alafenamide was initiated on hospital day 16. She was discharged on day 18 and prednisone was stopped after 20 days.

Twenty-one days after starting antiretroviral therapy, she returned with a 5-day histo

title: Angiographic Embolization with Histoacryl in Combination with Direct Injection of Bone Cement of an Intraosseous Venous Malformation of the Mandible: Report of a Case with 22-Year Follow-Up
case_summary:
A 45-year-old woman presented with a 3-year history of painless swelling of the left mandibular body, causing facial asymmetry.  
On examination:  
? Well-demarcated, hard swelling of the left mandibular body and angle with palpable submandibular lymphadenopathy  
? Intraoral hard bulge in the left posterior buccal vestibule; the left second premolar was Grade 3 mobile; no sensory deficits  
History: the left mandibular first and second molars had been extracted for mobility without bleeding.  
Panoramic radiograph: well-defined multilocular radiolucent lesion from the mental region to the ramus, containing small loculations and fine trabeculae.  
Laboratory studies: bleeding time 2 min; platelet count 322 ? 10^3/?L (both normal).  
Biopsy via the extraction socket of the mobile premolar revealed no hemorrhage, and histology showed thin-walled dilated vascular spaces of variable diameter lined by a single layer of endothelial cells with mild inflammatory infiltrate.  
CT scan: expansil

title: Bilateral Episcleral Corkscrew Vessels: Expedition into the Unknown ? Case Report
case_summary:
A 66-year-old woman presented with chronic asymptomatic bilateral conjunctival hyperaemia for over 1 year, unresponsive to topical therapies. Her medical history included treated arterial hypertension, dyslipidaemia, atrial fibrillation, and type 2 diabetes.  
On examination, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/20 OU. Biomicroscopy revealed prominent bilateral episcleral corkscrew vessels. Intraocular pressures by Goldmann applanation were 27 mm Hg OD and 28 mm Hg OS; pachymetry measured 501 um OD and 499 um OS. There was no proptosis; ocular motility was full; pupils were equal and reactive. Gonioscopy showed open angles with blood in Schlemm's canal OD. Funduscopy revealed healthy optic nerves (cup-to-disc ratios ~0.2 OD, 0.1 OS) and no vascular abnormalities. No carotid or ocular bruits were heard. The patient denied headache, diplopia, pulsatile tinnitus, orbital pulsation, head or neck trauma, and had no family history of glaucoma.  
Optical coherence tomography of the retinal ner

title: Peliosis Hepatis with Hemorrhagic Necrosis and Rupture: a Case Report with Emphasis on the Multi-Detector CT Findings
case_summary:
A 42-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with acute, diffuse abdominal pain. Three days earlier she had fever and right upper quadrant pain treated at a local clinic. She had no significant medical history. Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (11.2?10^3 cells/uL), elevated C-reactive protein, markedly elevated liver enzymes, and hemoglobin of 7.8 g/dL requiring transfusion.  
Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated a large, heterogeneous subcapsular lesion in the right hepatic lobe and a perihepatic fluid collection. The initial differential diagnosis included a subcapsular hepatic abscess or a mass such as a cavernous hemangioma.  
Triphasic multidetector CT revealed an approximately 9-cm, low-density, irregular lesion in the right hepatic dome that was largely nonenhancing; however, multiple irregular, linear, septa-like densities were isoattenuating to adjacent liver on all phases. Adjacent to this was a heterogeneous hyperdense subcapsular collection, consistent with

title: Arteriovenous malformation presenting as a lip swelling
case_summary:
A 27-year-old man with no significant medical history presented with a 3-month history of a swelling in his left upper lip that had grown rapidly over the past month. He denied trauma, pain, bleeding, ulceration, or color change. On examination, the lesion was a 6.0 cm ? 2.5 cm soft, pulsatile, immobile mass with well-defined borders; it was nonreducible and noncompressible, and there were no signs of inflammation (no erythema, heat, or discharge). Initial considerations included abscess and vascular tumors.  
 
Magnetic resonance imaging (axial T2-weighted) showed diffuse linear-tubular signal?void areas in the soft tissue of the upper lip. MR angiography (coronal maximum-intensity-projection) demonstrated a vascularized mass in the upper lip with arterial supply from distal branches of the external carotid artery. MR venography (axial maximum-intensity-projection) revealed the mass draining into the venous system.

title: Antiphospholipid syndrome with major arterial thrombosis, presenting as pulmonary thromboembolism, cerebrovascular accident, and coronary artery disease: A case report and literature review
case_summary:
A 33-year-old man presented with sudden onset of dyspnea and right-sided pleuritic chest pain that began a day earlier at rest. Three weeks prior, he had undergone decompressive craniectomy for elevated intracranial pressure after a left middle cerebral artery thrombosis, which manifested as aphasia and right hemiparesis. Two years earlier, he had an acute coronary syndrome due to left anterior descending artery occlusion treated with angioplasty. He has no significant dyslipidemia, uses tobacco occasionally, and has a family history of ischemic heart disease in first-degree relatives at older ages.

On examination, he was alert, tachypneic (26 breaths/min), and tachycardic (124 beats/min) but hemodynamically stable without fever. Neurologic exam confirmed residual left-sided weakness. Lung and cardiac auscultation were normal, and healing craniectomy graft was noted on the scalp.

Initial laboratory tests including complete blood count, liver and renal function, urine analysis, electr

title: A case report of systemic embolic events associated with atrial fibrillation
case_summary: An 82-year-old woman with a history of non-ruptured cerebral aneurysm, bioprosthetic mitral valve replacement for regurgitation, atrial fibrillation, and prior right cerebellar infarction was found unconscious beside her bed by her son and brought to the emergency department. On arrival, her Glasgow Coma Scale score was 3. Vital signs were: blood pressure 139/85 mmHg; heart rate 100 b.p.m.; temperature 36.4  degreesC; respiratory rate 30 breaths/min; and oxygen saturation 85% on 10 L/min via bag-valve mask. Pupils were 2 mm in diameter, equally reactive to light. A subcutaneous forehead hematoma was noted. Lung auscultation was clear; a systolic murmur was present at the apex. The right arm was warm, whereas the left arm and both feet were cold. Pulses were normal in the right carotid and right radial arteries but weak in the left radial and bilateral femoral arteries. Muscle tone was decreased in all extremities. Current medications included warfarin, aspirin, spironolactone, tolvapta

title: Factor XIII Deficiency Presenting With Duodenal Hematoma in a 3?Year?Old Female: A Comprehensive Radiological Assessment
case_summary:
A 3-year-old girl presented with gradual-onset abdominal pain and non-bilious vomiting over 2 days. She was irritable but active; vital signs were stable (heart rate 98 beats/min, respiratory rate 22 breaths/min, blood pressure 90/65 mmHg, oxygen saturation 99% on room air); she was afebrile. The abdomen was soft, non-tender, with normal bowel sounds and no signs of obstruction. There was no history of fever, diarrhea, dysuria, trauma, or prior similar episodes. Family history was negative for bleeding or gastrointestinal disorders.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 7.3 g/dL (11.5-17.5), leukocyte count 16.1?10^9/L (5.0-15.0), MCV 72.4 fL (76-96), RBC count 3.69?10^6/?L (4-6), platelets 150?10^9/L (150-400), prothrombin time 12.7 s (11-15), activated partial thromboplastin time 27 s (25-40), and bleeding time 2 min (1-10). Liver and renal function tests were within normal limits except for an elevated AST of 337 U/L and CRP 43 U/L. Factor XIII activity and von Willebrand factor ant

title: Unexpected massive bleeding during the modified LeFort III advancement surgery for Crouzon syndrome: A case report
case_summary:
A 14-year-old girl with a known diagnosis of Crouzon syndrome presented for correction of midface and orbital hypoplasia. She had no cardiovascular, gastrointestinal, neurological, or endocrinological comorbidities and no family history of bleeding disorders; her surgical history included tonsillectomy and tooth extractions without bleeding complications, though she reported heavy menstrual bleeding. She underwent a modified LeFort III osteotomy. During surgery, an estimated 1 L of unexpected hemorrhage occurred, controlled intraoperatively with topical hemostatic agents and two units of packed red blood cells.

Postoperatively, in the intensive care unit, she developed tachycardia (heart rate 120-170 bpm), hypotension (blood pressure 50/30 mmHg), hypothermia (33.7  degreesC), and cyanosis of both hands. She received intravenous fluids and three additional units of packed red blood cells. Antibiotic therapy was initiated for possible septic shock with meropenem and vancomycin, and fres

title: Pulmonary tumour embolism and lymphangitis carcinomatosa: a case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 52-year-old man with no prior medical history initially presented with an eight-month history of diffuse abdominal pain, obstipation, and an 8-kg weight loss. Three weeks before admission he had a chronic cough; chest radiograph and spirometry were normal, and inhaler therapy relieved his symptoms. On admission, his C-reactive protein was elevated at 96.1 mg/L, white blood cell count was 13.85 ? 10^3/uL, and hemoglobin was 13.7 g/dL. He received empiric cefuroxime and metronidazole. Colonoscopy was unremarkable, and abdominal CT showed pathologically enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes and ascites.

While under evaluation for suspected lymphoma, he developed sudden?onset dyspnoea. Arterial blood gases showed pH 7.48, pO2 56 mmHg, pCO2 25 mmHg. ECG revealed new T-wave inversions in leads V1-V3. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a markedly dilated right ventricle, reduced RV function (TAPSE 1.5 cm), and estimated systolic pulmonary artery pressure of 70 mmHg. 

A CT pulmonar

title: Aneurysmal bone cyst of the skull: A case report
case_summary: A 14-year-old boy was referred for evaluation of a "cyst" on his right temporal scalp. He first noticed a bump several weeks earlier, and it had grown rapidly and become tender. On examination, there was a single, fixed, subcutaneous nodule measuring approximately 2.5 cm on the right temporal scalp. No overlying skin changes were noted. Palpation of regional lymph nodes revealed normal size and no tenderness. A noncontrast CT scan of the head was obtained. Imaging demonstrated a lytic lesion of the right parietal bone with full-thickness calvarial destruction, an associated periosteal reaction, and an adjacent heterogeneous soft-tissue mass in the right frontoparietal scalp. The ventricular system was normal in size, and there was no intracranial hemorrhage or infarction. The patient was referred to pediatric neurosurgery and scheduled for excision of the skull lesion.

title: Anterior Ischemic Optic Neuropathy Associated with Udenafil
case_summary:
A 54-year-old man presented with a 12-hour history of blurred vision and decreased visual field in his right eye. Three days earlier, he had taken his first dose of 100 mg udenafil; mild eye irritation and headache developed but resolved within hours. He took a second dose one day before presentation and awoke with visual symptoms. He had a 20-year history of smoking but no hypertension, hypotension, diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, or cardiovascular disease.

On examination, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/25 OD and 20/20 OS. A relative afferent pupillary defect was present in the right eye. Ocular motility and anterior segment biomicroscopy were normal. Dilated funduscopy of the right eye showed marked optic-disc swelling with a rim hemorrhage; the left eye had a crowded disc with a cup-to-disc ratio of 0.2. Fluorescein angiography demonstrated inferior choroidal filling delay, inferior sector filling delay of the optic disc in the arteriovenous phase, and diffuse late leakage

title: Renal clear cell carcinoma emerging in a transplanted kidney 3 years after return to dialysis. Case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 66-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with severe right flank pain of 9/10 intensity that had persisted for several hours and radiated to the right lower quadrant. She denied vomiting, changes in bowel habits, or recent trauma. Her medical history was notable for a deceased-donor kidney transplant placed in the right iliac fossa 20 years earlier for end-stage renal disease due to chronic glomerulonephritis; she had returned to hemodialysis 3 years ago because of chronic allograft nephropathy. Additional problems included recurrent biliary colic, chronic deep venous insufficiency, osteoporosis, hypothyroidism, and chronic hepatitis B carriage. Medications were lamivudine, levothyroxine, folic acid, omeprazole, low-dose aspirin, calcium, and vitamin D.

On examination she appeared uncomfortable. Vital signs were: temperature 36.7 degreesC, pulse 66/min, blood pressure 120/60 mmHg. Physical examination showed severe tenderness to palpation over the right lower abdomen

title: Innocent until proven guilty: acute on chronic tubal torsion mimicking pelvic inflammatory disease
case_summary:
A 44-year-old woman presented with a 1-week history of lower abdominal pain and a "feeling of pressure in the rectum" during bowel movements associated with an increased urge to defecate. She had not been sexually active for more than 10 years. On examination, she was afebrile; her lower abdomen was tender bilaterally, and cervical motion tenderness was elicited. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 12.4?10^9 per liter and a C-reactive protein level of 62 mg per liter; a pregnancy test was negative. She was diagnosed with pelvic inflammatory disease and started on intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics.  
  A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis showed two cystic structures in the pelvis. A transvaginal ultrasound demonstrated a 5.8-cm unilocular simple cyst in the right ovary and a 3-cm left tubo-ovarian complex with associated hydrosalpinx. Ovarian tumor markers, blood cultures, vaginal swabs, and tests for sexually transmitted infections were negative.  
  The patient's symp

title: Hepatic segmental arterial mediolysis: A case report and brief literature review
case_summary:
A 58?year?old woman with protein C and S deficiency, a history of deep?vein thrombosis on warfarin, systemic lupus erythematosus, and hypothyroidism presented with 3 days of right flank and right upper?quadrant abdominal pain. An urgent?care urinalysis showed blood and leukocyte esterase; she was treated for presumed urinary tract infection without improvement. In the emergency department, an abdominal CT scan was interpreted as consistent with a recently passed renal calculus, and she was discharged. Twelve hours later, she returned with worsening pain.

On examination, blood pressure was 154/80?mm?Hg, heart rate 85/min, respiratory rate 17/min, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. The abdomen was diffusely tender with right costovertebral?angle tenderness. Laboratory results showed white blood cell count 9.1?10^9/L, hemoglobin 13.0?g/dL, platelets 257?10^9/L, and an INR of 4.4. The basic metabolic panel was unremarkable. Liver enzymes were significantly elevated from 12?hours earl

title: A Rare Pancreatic Tumor with Adenomatoid Tumor-like Findings
case_summary: A 74-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of mildly elevated tumor markers on routine blood tests: carcinoembryonic antigen 7.4 ng/mL (normal 0-5.0) and carbohydrate antigen 19-9 44.2 U/mL (normal 0-37.0); all other markers were within normal limits. She was asymptomatic, with unremarkable glucose tolerance. Abdominal ultrasonography was limited by overlying gas. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen showed a 15 mm hypodense mass with a clear boundary in the pancreatic tail, containing multiple septal structures and demonstrating contrast enhancement in the portal venous phase; there was no calcification or main pancreatic duct dilation. On MRI, the lesion was hypointense on T1-weighted images, nonuniformly hyperintense on T2-weighted images, and exhibited mild diffusion restriction without hypercellularity. Dynamic MRI revealed lower early enhancement compared with surrounding pancreas and gradual enhancement thereafter. MR cholangiopancreatography did not visualize the lesion, ef

title: Ochroconis gallopava bronchitis mimicking haemoptysis in a patient with bronchiectasis
case_summary:
A 76-year-old woman with ulcerative colitis-related bronchiectasis presented with a 3-month history of increasing dark brownish-red sputum and dyspnea. She expectorated about a cup of brownish-red sputum each morning, without fever, weight loss, or other constitutional symptoms. Over the past year she had several brief, non-hypoxemic episodes described as haemoptysis.  
Her chest CT scan showed progressive cylindrical bronchiectasis predominant in the lower lobes. Ulcerative colitis was in remission on mesalazine and low-dose prednisolone. Past history included corticosteroid-induced osteoporosis, peripheral arterial disease, and remote alcohol abuse.  
On examination she was slightly malnourished, with normal vital signs; lungs had moist crackles at both bases and no clubbing. Current medications included prednisolone 7.5 mg/day, inhaled budesonide, mesalazine, calcium, vitamin D, azithromycin (250 mg three times weekly), clopidogrel, and levetiracetam.  
Laboratory studies revealed C

title: Increasing Alkaline Phosphatase as the Primary Manifestation of Disseminated Histoplasmosis in an AIDS Patient Without Pulmonary Disease
case_summary: A 51-year-old woman with AIDS (CD4 count 4 cells/mm^3, HIV-1 load >76 000 copies/mL), insulin-dependent diabetes, Crohn's disease on adalimumab, and a history of intravenous drug use presented with 4 days of weakness, night sweats, poor oral intake, and diarrhea. She was nonadherent to antiretroviral therapy. On admission, she had pancytopenia and disseminated intravascular coagulation; renal function was preserved. Liver tests showed an alkaline phosphatase level of 241 U/L (30-103 U/L), an AST level of 75 U/L (10-50 U/L), an ALT level of 8 U/L (10-55 U/L), and a total bilirubin level of 0.7 mg/dL (0.0-1.2 mg/dL); her baseline values were ALP 181, AST 31, ALT 14, and bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL. Initial imaging (CT head, chest x-ray, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) showed severe hepatic steatosis, periportal and mesenteric adenopathy, splenomegaly, and no pulmonary disease or biliary obstruction. Broad-spectrum antibiotics were started for presumed sepsis, but cultures remained negative and symptoms

title: Ultrasound diagnosis of a retroperitoneal fetus in fetu: A case report
case_summary:
A 45-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, presented at 37+4 weeks' gestation after an obstetric ultrasound suggested a teratoma. The initial ultrasound at 37+3 weeks showed a fetal-like echo in the thoracic cavity of the fetus. On repeat examination, there was a live singleton fetus in cephalic presentation with a right retroperitoneal mass of mixed echogenicity. Two parallel fetal-like signals were seen within the mass: one exhibited a weak heartbeat, and the other was acardiac.  
Both parents were healthy, there was no history of twin gestation, and the first child had no congenital abnormalities.  
Ultrasonography revealed:  
? A 4.7 ? 2.1 cm cystic-solid mass in the right retroperitoneum with intra-cyst septation and mixed echogenicity adherent to the cyst wall; no head, spine, upper limb, or heart echoes in this mass.  
? Continuous scanning showed one limb with skin covering, a long bone, distal footpad, and toe-like echoes. An umbilical cord-like vascular echo was connected to the

title: Paradoxical coronary embolism in Erdheim-Chester disease: invasive assessment and multidisciplinary management
case_summary: A 36-year-old white British man presented with three weeks of progressively worsening exertional central chest pain and breathlessness. He had a ten-year history of Erdheim-Chester disease, previously controlled with pegylated interferon; nine months earlier he had COVID-19 complicated by deep venous thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, for which he was taking apixaban. A recent disease flare had prompted increases in interferon and a course of prednisolone. He had no traditional cardiovascular risk factors. On admission, ECG showed sinus rhythm with <1 mm convex ST-segment elevation in V1-V6 and T-wave inversion in leads I and aVL. High-sensitivity troponin T was 51 ng/L, rising to 132 ng/L at six hours; D-dimer was 608 ng/mL. Transthoracic echocardiography was unremarkable. Contrast-enhanced gated CT coronary angiography demonstrated a zero calcium score but an ostial left anterior descending (LAD) non-calcified plaque 1.5 cm in length with positive remodelling and a low-density lipid

title: An Anomalous Cause of Deep Venous Thrombosis: A Case Report
journal: Clinical Practice and Cases in Emergency Medicine
case_summary: A 39?year?old woman with a history of alcohol use disorder presented to the emergency department with two days of atraumatic left leg swelling and pain. She had no risk factors for deep venous thrombosis and no personal or family history of thromboembolic disease or hypercoagulability. On examination, her left lower extremity had significant swelling with a positive Homans' sign and severe pitting edema extending from the foot to the groin; the extremity was well perfused, hyperemic, with palpable distal pulses. Doppler ultrasonography revealed a large occlusive thrombus in the left common femoral vein extending inferiorly to the great saphenous vein. Given the severity of her symptoms, lack of predisposing factors, and extensive thrombus burden, a CT venogram was obtained to evaluate for underlying structural pathology. The CT venogram showed a fully occlusive filling defect in the left common iliac and femoral veins extending into the distal inferior vena cava, with evidence of compr

title: Thromboelastometry-Guided Individualized Fibrinolytic Treatment for COVID-19-Associated Severe Coagulopathy Complicated by Portal Vein Thrombosis: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 48-year-old man with a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation off long-term anticoagulation presented with a 7-day history of diarrhea, generalized fatigue, dyspnea, and cough. A rapid antigen test confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection. 

On admission, he was hypoxemic (SpO2 80% on room air, improving with face-mask oxygen), tachyarrhythmic (heart rate 160/min, atrial fibrillation on ECG), and normotensive (119/100 mm Hg). Arterial blood gas analysis showed metabolic acidosis (pH 7.126, base excess -19 mmol/L) and elevated lactate (12.6 mmol/L). Laboratory tests revealed elevated C-reactive protein, ferritin, transaminases, D-dimer, and troponin I, consistent with infection-associated multiple organ dysfunction.

A thoracic CT scan showed bilateral ground-glass opacities with consolidation in the lower lobes. Abdominal CT scan identified a diffuse hepatic lesion without vascular flow abnormalities. Despite oxygen supplementation, volume resuscitation, and antiarrhythmics, he deteriorated

title: Multi-system bleeding risk with a cutaneous angiosarcoma at an arteriovenous fistula site
case_summary:
A 71-year-old man with ischaemic heart disease, insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and stage 4 chronic kidney disease presented with four weeks of progressive dyspnoea, cough, weight loss, and new haemoptysis. He had a left brachiocephalic arteriovenous fistula created 34 months earlier that had never been used for haemodialysis. Three months before presentation, he developed swelling and warmth at the fistula site; duplex ultrasound confirmed partial fistula thrombosis, and over the next month he reported pain and tenderness consistent with mild thrombophlebitis.

In respiratory clinic, he had a 60 pack-year smoking history, possible asbestos exposure, and a prominent swelling over the AVF. He was admitted with bilateral pleural effusions and severe anaemia (hemoglobin 64 g/L; baseline 110 g/L). CT of the thorax revealed bilateral pulmonary and pleural nodular and mass-like opacities with bilateral pleural and pericardial effusions. A chest drain yielded strongly blood

title: Colonic mesenteric lymphatic malformation presenting as an intraabdominal abscess in an infant: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: An 8-month-old male, born full term, was referred to the Emergency Department with a 9-day history of high-grade fevers. There were no vomiting, diarrhea, rash, seizures, sick contacts, or travel history. On exam, he had dry, red lips; temperature was 36.5  degreesC and heart rate 140 beats/min, with other vitals normal for age. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL, MCV 81 fL, WBC 31,500/mm3, platelets 618,000/mm3, CRP 21.53 mg/L, sodium 133 mmol/L, chloride 98 mmol/L, and a normal urinalysis. Blood cultures drawn at the primary care office showed no growth. A chest radiograph was normal. With an initial suspicion of Kawasaki disease, intravenous ceftriaxone was started and the patient was admitted to rheumatology; echocardiography showed no coronary aneurysms. Further exam revealed mild right-sided abdominal pain. Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated a large, heterogeneous right intra-abdominal mass concerning for neoplasm versus abscess. Serum alpha-fetoprotein, ?-human chorio

title: Multicentric Castleman's Disease in a Hepatitis C-Positive Intravenous Drug User: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 46-year-old man presented with pleuritic chest pain, a swollen painful right knee impairing weight bearing, and hip and back pain after a fall. He reported a 40-kg weight loss over 2 years, poor appetite, fever, night sweats, and increased infections. He has a 16-year history of untreated hepatitis C (genotype 3) and a history of intravenous heroin use. He had prior pulmonary embolism, disc prolapse, mild depression, osteomyelitis with digit amputation, and multiple fracture repairs.

On examination he was cachectic with rib and mediastinal tenderness, hepatomegaly (6 cm below costal margin), non-tender lymphadenopathy in the left jugular chain and bilateral groins, and a warm right knee effusion. Vital signs were normal. 

Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 102 g/L, elevated C-reactive protein, calcium 2.57 mmol/L, albumin 23 g/L, and a transient mild elevation of free T4. Liver function, renal function, coagulation, ?-fetoprotein, vasculitic screen, iron studies, B12, urate, and gl

title: A Forgotten Rare Cause of Unilateral Basal Ganglia Calcinosis Due to Venous Angioma and Complicating Acute Stroke Management: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 54-year-old man was admitted with sudden onset of speech impairment and right-sided weakness.  
Medical history: arterial hypertension, dyslipidemia, and lumbar-sacral spondylosis; no prior ischemic events.  
Family history: sibling with hypertension and intracerebral hemorrhage at age 54.  
On examination: mild central facial paresis on the right, mild right hemiparesis, right hemihypesthesia, motor aphasia; NIHSS score 10; mRS 4.  
Initial noncontrast head CT showed no acute ischemia or hemorrhage.  
CT angiography revealed occlusion of the left MCA M2 segment with leptomeningeal collaterals (Tan score 3).  
CT perfusion demonstrated a large penumbra without hemorrhage.  
Thrombolysis was deferred due to concern for an underlying neoplasm or basal ganglia calcification.  
One-day follow-up CT showed an ischemic lesion in the left insula, parietal lobe, and superior temporal gyrus, a hyperdense artery sign, and unilateral hyperdensity in the left basal ganglia.  
Brain MRI confirmed

title: COVID-19 in a patient implanted with a total artificial heart: a case report
case_summary:
A 27-year-old man with Marfan syndrome underwent ascending aorta replacement for a 46 mm aneurysm. Shortly after weaning from cardiopulmonary bypass, he developed refractory cardiogenic shock with biventricular dysfunction requiring veno-arterial ECMO. Ten days later, in the absence of a suitable donor, a total artificial heart was implanted as a bridge to transplantation. Two months after implantation, he developed persistent fever despite antibiotics and mediastinitis surgery, and then acute respiratory failure.

On examination he was tachypneic and hypoxemic. Chest X-ray showed diffuse alveolo-interstitial infiltrates. Arterial blood gas revealed PaO2 47 mmHg, pH 7.48, PaCO2 35 mmHg. CT pulmonary angiography showed diffuse peripheral ground-glass opacities with "crazy-paving," consolidation with atelectasis, and pleural effusion, without pulmonary embolism. Echocardiography was not feasible and natriuretic peptides were uninterpretable; interrogation of the TAH external device showe

title: Multifocal Warthin's Tumor: An Uncommon Presentation of Bilateral Cervical Lymphadenopathy
case_summary: A 71?year?old man presented with slowly enlarging bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy, parotid gland swelling, and night sweats. His medical history was notable for melanoma resected from the nasal bridge in 2015 and a 40-pack-year smoking history. Contrast-enhanced CT of the head, neck, and chest showed multiple enlarged bilateral parotid gland lesions, scattered level I and II cervical lymph nodes, and upper mediastinal (right paratracheal) lymphadenopathy. Ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration and core needle biopsy of a 4.0 ? 1.9 cm left cervical lymph node demonstrated cytology consistent with Warthin's tumor. A level IA neck dissection confirmed Warthin's tumor without evidence of malignancy or lymphoma. Six-month follow-up imaging showed stable bilateral parotid masses and cervical nodes but enlargement of right upper mediastinal lymph nodes. Endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial biopsy of the paratracheal node revealed absence of malignant cells, and flow cytometry w

title: Proximal Femoral Erosion: A Sequelae of Psuedoaneursym Formation Following Operative Fixation of an Intertrochanteric Neck of Femur Fracture
case_summary: A 72-year-old man with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease fell while intoxicated and presented with left hip pain and decreased mobility. Radiographs revealed a left intertrochanteric femoral-neck fracture, and he underwent fixation with a dynamic hip screw without immediate complication. Postoperatively, he reported medial thigh pain; radiographs were interpreted as normal and he was discharged. Fifteen months later, he returned with persistent left thigh pain and progressive swelling. Examination showed a large, fixed mass in the left anteromedial thigh; distal pulses and sensation were intact. Pelvic radiographs demonstrated union of the fracture and a well-positioned screw but revealed scalloping of the medial femoral cortex and protrusion of screws beyond the cortex. Ultrasound was inconclusive. A magnetic resonance image, performed to exclude sarcoma, showed an asymmetric expansion of the left proximal thigh by an 11 ? 11 ? 12 cm heterogeneous lesion impinging on the neurovasc

title: Spontaneous Neck Hematoma in a Patient with Fibromuscular Dysplasia: A Case Report and a Review of the Literature
case_summary:
A 69-year-old Caucasian woman was admitted with 3 days of throat and right neck pain and progressive dysphagia. The day before admission she noted a small neck hematoma that rapidly spread. She denied dyspnea. Her history included two internal carotid artery (ICA) aneurysms diagnosed in 2002, one coiled on the right and one managed conservatively on the left, with angiographic features of fibromuscular dysplasia noted in the left ICA, and later stenting of a dissecting left ICA aneurysm. Medications: aspirin 100 mg daily and amiodarone 100 mg daily for atrial fibrillation. 

On admission, vital signs were normal and she was afebrile. Examination revealed a large subcutaneous hematoma from the thyroid region to the right chest wall, tenderness over the right lateral neck above the cricoid, and slight tracheal deviation to the left. There were no signs of airway compromise. Fiber-optic laryngoscopy showed a normal glottis and no laryngeal compression. 

Laboratory studies, including a co

title: Atypical perifoveal exudative vascular anomalous complex (PEVAC) with multifocal and bilateral presentation
journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case_summary:
A 62-year-old man was referred for progressive bilateral blurred vision of long duration. He had no history of diabetes, hypertension, inflammatory disease, obstructive sleep apnea, carotid occlusion, or blood dyscrasias; he was on anticoagulation for a mitral valve replacement. Best-corrected visual acuity was 75 letters in the right eye and 80 letters in the left eye. Anterior segments were unremarkable; cataracts were not significant. 

Fundus examination revealed, in the right eye, three large perifoveal aneurysmal lesions with small hemorrhages and intraretinal hard exudates; the left eye had one similar isolated lesion. Multicolor imaging and autofluorescence showed no retinal pigment epithelium alteration. Fluorescein angiography of the peripheral retina was normal.

Macular FA and ICGA in early and late frames demonstrated only the perifoveal hyperfluorescent aneurysms, with no leakage. Optical coherence tomography showed cystoid intraretinal fluid in both eyes, with round intr

title: A rare case of MRI of myxoid hepatocellular adenoma with gadoxetic acid uptake
case_summary:
A 43-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of pelvic floor weakness, during which an incidental liver mass was palpated. She denied gastrointestinal symptoms, changes in bowel habits, weight loss, fevers, or night sweats. She reported drinking one bottle of wine nightly for 10 years but had no stigmata of cirrhosis and no family history of liver disease or malignancy.

Laboratory studies showed:
? Normal complete blood count and platelet count of 224?10^9/L  
? International normalized ratio 0.9  
? Plasma ammonia 32 ?mol/L  
? Normal liver function tests: albumin 50 g/L, bilirubin 17 ?mol/L, transaminases, alkaline phosphatase 63 U/L, ?-glutamyl transferase 32 U/L  
? Tumor markers (CEA, CA15-3, CA125, CA19-9) all within normal limits  
? Negative hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis C antibodies

Contrast-enhanced CT of the liver demonstrated a large exophytic mass in segment 5/6 with multiple central hypodense areas. On arterial phase, there was hypervascular curvilinear a

title: Hepatic angiosarcoma with clinical and histological features of Kasabach-Merritt syndrome
case_summary:
A 44-year-old Hispanic man presented with a 2-month history of worsening abdominal pain and jaundice. He had no significant past medical, family, or social history and denied exposure to vinyl chloride, arsenic, or thorium dioxide. On examination, he appeared icteric and had a distended, diffusely tender abdomen; there were no spider angiomata, palmar erythema, shifting dullness, hepatic bruit, caput medusae, or asterixis.

Laboratory studies revealed:
? WBC 13 400/mm3, hemoglobin 8.8 g/dL, MCV 99 fL, platelets 57 000/mm3  
? Alkaline phosphatase 118 IU/L, total protein 5.4 g/dL, albumin 2.7 g/dL  
? Total bilirubin 6.4 mg/dL (direct 3.1 mg/dL), AST 42 IU/L, ALT 72 IU/L  
? Prothrombin time 29.4 s (INR 2.88), PTT 40.7 s, fibrinogen < 60 mg/dL, d-dimer > 9.999 mg/dL  
Serologic tests for viral hepatitis and autoimmune liver disease were negative. Serum AFP, CA 19-9, and CEA were within normal limits.

Abdominal ultrasound showed multiple heterogeneous hepatic masses with peripheral echo

title: Metastatic neuroendocrine tumors mimicking as primary ocular disease
journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case_summary:
A 21-year-old man with no past medical history presented with two weeks of left eye pain and blurry vision. He denied flashes or floaters but reported acute loss of vision in the left eye four days before presentation. He also endorsed a one-month history of cough.

Visual acuity was 20/30 in the right eye and 20/70 in the left eye; intraocular pressure measured 12 mm Hg right and 9 mm Hg left. The left pupil was ovoid and nonreactive. Slit-lamp examination of the left eye revealed conjunctival injection, 3+ pigmented cells, 2+ flare, and a large pigmented ciliary body mass extending from 6 o'clock to 10:30 with internal vascularization. Dilated fundus examination showed extensive subretinal fluid, hemorrhage, and exudates; the right eye was normal. Gonioscopy demonstrated erosion of the iris root with invasion of the angle. B-scan ultrasonography of the left eye showed a dome-shaped tumor arising from the ciliary body measuring 11.2 ? 12.8 mm with low internal reflectivity and an infe

title: The curious case of a massive right heart thrombosis: a case report
case_summary:
A 49-year-old woman presented with worsening exertional dyspnoea. Her history included hypertension, diabetes, severe obesity (BMI 39.7 kg/m2), asthma, and a pulmonary embolism treated with 6 months of warfarin one year earlier (normal echocardiogram then). Family history was notable for a cousin with MTHFR and prothrombin mutations and pulmonary embolism; her daughter and sister carry the same MTHFR mutation without thromboembolic events.

On examination, she was hemodynamically stable (BP 100/60 mmHg; HR 89 bpm) but markedly dyspnoeic; there were muffled heart sounds without overt signs of right-heart failure. Laboratory tests showed a normal blood count and elevated B-type natriuretic peptide (1543 pg/mL). ECG demonstrated right-ventricular overload (tall R waves in V1-V2). 

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a normal left ventricle, a dilated right atrium (ESA 40 cm2), an enlarged right ventricle with qualitatively reduced function, and an intraventricular mass partially obli

title: Adult hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis causing multi organ dysfunction in a patient with multiple autoimmune disorders: when the immune system runs amok
case_summary:
A 52-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus (diagnosed at age 17, treated chronically with low-dose prednisolone) and muscle-specific kinase-positive generalized myasthenia gravis was admitted to the intensive care unit for respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation. She had received high-dose steroids, plasmapheresis, and intravenous immunoglobulin for an SLE flare with CNS involvement and her myasthenic crisis.

During her ICU stay, she developed a ventilator-associated pneumonia treated with broad-spectrum antibiotics, then presumed hepatitis E infection treated with ribavirin (complicated by agranulocytosis treated with G-CSF), and subsequently a severe pneumonia with pneumothorax leading to septic shock. Blood cultures grew multidrug-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae and Staphylococcus epidermidis, and she received appropriate antibiotics.

Despite a transient decline in procalcitonin levels, she remained febrile with elevated C-reactive protein, ongoing leukocyt

title: Squamous cell carcinoma of verrucous type in the setting of prior pyoderma gangrenosum: a case report
case_summary:
A 57-year-old Caucasian man with a history of Crohn's disease, colon cancer, bilateral deep vein thromboses, and a 13-year chronic nonhealing skin lesion over the right lateral tibia was evaluated. The lesion had been biopsy-proven pyoderma gangrenosum and was refractory to multiple treatments, with waxing and waning severity; the patient was maintained on Humira. Surgical history included venous thrombectomy, colon resection, splenectomy, cholecystectomy, and hernia repair.

On examination, there was an irregularly shaped, raised, fungating, exophytic lesion over the right lateral tibia measuring 12.0 cm ? 12.0 cm ? 1 cm, without bleeding or overt infection. Bilateral lower-extremity hyperpigmentation suggested chronic venous stasis. Abdominal exam was unremarkable. The patient denied tobacco or drug use and had ceased alcohol 30 years earlier.

Four years prior, a punch biopsy of this area showed "lobular vascular proliferation within the dermis with associated hemosiderin and spongi

title: The Wolf Hidden behind the Clots: Catastrophic Antiphospholipid Antibody Syndrome
case_summary: A 22-year-old Hispanic woman with a history of deep-vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism at age 16, systemic lupus erythematosus, acquired protein S deficiency, and secondary antiphospholipid syndrome presented with generalized weakness, malaise, recurrent fevers, and elevated blood pressure. She had a motor-vehicle accident and a diagnosed viral upper respiratory infection one week earlier and was noncompliant with fondaparinux and prednisone. On examination, temperature was 101.3  degreesF, blood pressure 140/115 mm Hg, and heart rate 130 beats/min. Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 6.5 g/dL, creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate 95 mm/h. She was treated empirically with broad-spectrum antibiotics without clinical improvement. Renal function worsened, hemoglobin fell further, and thrombocytopenia developed requiring transfusion. She then developed livedo reticularis, right upper-extremity weakness, memory loss, cyanotic left toes with diminished dorsalis pedis p

title: Brachial artery thrombosis secondary to fixation screw pullout: Case report?
case_summary: A 41-year-old Caucasian man presented with right arm pain, swelling, and bloody drainage following revision surgery for a rerupture of the distal biceps tendon. Two weeks earlier, he had undergone initial repair with a fixation screw at the radial tuberosity after a traumatic underwater sports injury. In the second postoperative week, he fell from standing height onto his unprotected arm, resulting in rerupture. Preoperative MRI showed pullout of the fixation screw with localized mass effect on the distal brachial artery but no occlusion. Examination revealed elbow edema, watery-serosanguineous drainage from the incision, and mild proximal forearm swelling. The limb was neurovascularly intact, with sensation to pinprick in all digits and palpable radial and ulnar pulses; a tendon cord was palpable without muscle retraction, and active and passive range of motion were preserved. An ultrasound with color Doppler demonstrated a 2.3 cm segment of complete occlusive arterial thrombosis of t

title: Hemangioma of the abdominal wall in infancy
case_summary:
A 6-month-old female infant was brought by her family after her grandmother noted a palpable, left-sided solid abdominal wall mass. There was no history of trauma, fever, or family history of similar conditions. The patient was born at 39 weeks' gestation with a birth weight of 3.2 kg after an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery; prenatal ultrasounds were normal and she was otherwise healthy.

An outpatient ultrasound showed a well-defined, heterogeneous, hypoechoic mass measuring approximately 3.7 ? 3.5 cm; it was unclear whether the lesion arose from the abdominal wall or intra-abdominally, but it was separate from the left kidney and spleen. Color Doppler imaging demonstrated detectable peripheral arterial vascularity; venous flow within the mass was not well assessed. The patient was referred for further evaluation.

On presentation, vital signs were normal, and physical examination was notable only for the firm mass in the left lateral abdominal wall. Laboratory studies revealed

title: An unexpected finding of hepatic lymphoma after emergent cholecystectomy
case_summary: A 53-year-old man presented with several weeks of fevers, joint pain, and abdominal pain. On admission, his temperature was elevated, and laboratory tests showed a total bilirubin of 1.8 mg/dL, aspartate aminotransferase 171 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 265 U/L, and alkaline phosphatase 466 U/L. An abdominal ultrasound demonstrated a thickened gallbladder wall without gallstones and a normal common bile duct. A CT scan of the abdomen was reportedly unremarkable. By hospital day 2, his white blood cell count had risen to 15,000/?L, and he was diagnosed clinically with acute cholecystitis. Because no biliary dilation was seen on imaging, he underwent laparoscopic cholecystectomy with intra-operative cholangiogram, which showed no choledocholithiasis. Gallbladder pathology revealed chronic cholecystitis without gallstones and no evidence of lymphoma. Postoperatively, his bilirubin rose to 10.2 mg/dL, white blood cell count increased to 16,000/?L, hemoglobin fell below 9 g/dL, and he had

title: Intramuscular Haemangioma with Diagnostic Challenge: A Cause for Strange Pain in the Masseter Muscle
case_summary: A 23-year-old woman presented with a 6-month history of right facial swelling. The mass remained stable in size for the first 3 months, then became more prominent, and she began experiencing intermittent pricking pain over the last 3 months. She had no significant medical history. On examination, there was a diffuse 2?2 cm warm, tender, soft swelling over the right mandibular body; it was mobile horizontally but limited in vertical movement, and the overlying skin was pinchable. The swelling became more prominent on clenching. Intraoral examination was unremarkable. Routine hematological tests were within normal limits. A posteroanterior mandibular radiograph showed no abnormalities. Ultrasonography revealed a 2.2?0.6 cm mixed echogenic lesion within the right masseter muscle with a speck of calcification. Colour Doppler ultrasound demonstrated dilated vascular channels with good flow and no interarterial or venous communications. MRI showed a well-defined T2-weighted mixed hypo- and h

title: A rare case of symptomatic vascular ring
case_summary:
A 58-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with acute chest pain radiating to the left arm and dyspnea. Her medical history included hypertension, right upper-lobe lung carcinoma with lymph node metastasis and secondary left-lung involvement, and prior endovascular treatment of a high-flow pelvic varicocele and large pelvic arteriovenous malformation. She also reported chronic dysphagia with pain on swallowing and left-upper-limb functional limitation with paresthesia.

On admission, cardiac markers were within normal limits, but the D-dimer concentration was elevated at 1153?ng/mL (reference ?500?ng/mL). CT pulmonary angiography demonstrated an occlusive defect in a segmental arterial branch of the right lower lobe, confirming pulmonary embolism. Incidentally, the aortic arch was anomalous: a right aortic arch of normal caliber gave rise independently to the right subclavian and right common carotid arteries; a smaller (9?mm) left arch, stenotic and hypoplastic in its p

title: A Case Report: The Challenging Diagnosis of Spontaneous Cervical Epidural Hematoma
journal: Clinical Practice and Cases in Emergency Medicine
case_summary:
A 59-year-old man presented with abrupt onset of severe upper back and neck pain that began while raising his arms in the shower. The pain worsened with arm movement, and he noted mild paresthesia in the left arm but denied initial weakness. He had no history of trauma. His medical history included hypertension, type 2 diabetes, peripheral vascular disease, and coronary artery disease. Medications were metformin, rosuvastatin, telmisartan-hydrochlorothiazide, fenofibrate, and aspirin 81 mg daily. He had quit smoking five years earlier and drank alcohol only occasionally. 

On examination, he was uncomfortable from pain. Vital signs: temperature normal, pulse 76/min, respiratory rate 15/min, blood pressure 156/82 mm Hg, oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Neck and posterior lower cervical/upper thoracic paraspinal regions were tender to palpation, and lowering the abducted left arm increased his pain. Initial neurologic exam showed normal strength in all extremities and left arm paresthe

title: Persistent respiratory failure after SARS-CoV-2 infection: The role of dual energy computed tomography. A case report?
case_summary: A 78-year-old woman with a history of hypertension, chronic bronchitis, and hepatitis C infection was hospitalized for SARS-CoV-2 pneumonia. She presented with 5 days of fever (to 37.5  degreesC) and increasing dyspnea; PCR testing was positive for SARS-CoV-2. On examination, she was alert and oriented, respiratory rate 20/min, and arterial blood gas analysis showed pH 7.46, pCO? 37.7 mm Hg, pO? 71 mm Hg, SpO? 95% on room air. Chest ultrasound revealed bilateral normal pleural sliding and B-lines; chest radiograph showed diffuse lung opacities. She received IV methylprednisolone, prophylactic LMWH, and oxygen (FiO? 36%-50%). CT of the chest demonstrated extensive bilateral ground-glass opacities typical of COVID-19 pneumonia; echocardiography showed preserved LV function and no pulmonary hypertension. Over 10 days, she nearly weaned off oxygen. On day 21 after symptom onset, she developed worsening dyspnea, chest tightness, and SpO? 87% requiring high-flow nasal cannula (40 L/min, FiO?

title: Abducens paralysis--a rare complication of spinal anesthesia at an emergency department: a case report
case_summary: A 38-year-old man presented to the emergency department with a 2-day history of increasing dizziness, binocular diplopia, and limited lateral gaze in his left eye. He also complained of an orthostatic headache. One week earlier, he had undergone endoscopic urological surgery under spinal anesthesia. He had no significant medical history. On examination, vital signs were within normal limits. Neurologic examination showed binocular diplopia and impaired abduction of the left eye, consistent with a unilateral sixth cranial nerve palsy; the remainder of the neurologic and fundoscopic examinations was normal. Noncontrast brain computed tomography, diffusion-weighted magnetic resonance imaging, and magnetic resonance venography showed no intracranial lesions, hemorrhage, or venous thrombosis. The following day, lumbar puncture was performed: opening pressure was 2.8 cm H2O; cerebrospinal fluid glucose was 61 mg/dL with a simultaneous blood glucose of 134 mg/dL; protein was 26.4 mg/dL; album

title: A Giant Silence - An?atypical association of sensorineural hearing loss with Giant Cell Arteritis
case_summary:
A 67-year-old independent Chinese woman presented with a 4-day history of profound, progressive bilateral hearing loss that began in the left ear, accompanied by tinnitus and vertigo. The hearing symptoms started 4 days after receiving the ChAdOx1-S (AstraZeneca) COVID-19 vaccine, with tinnitus initially in the left ear, extending to the right on day 2, and culminating in bilateral deafness by day 4. She reported subjective fevers for 4 days and a 5 kg weight loss over preceding months. She recalled hip and shoulder girdle pain 12 months earlier without a formal diagnosis, a dry cough that resolved weeks before admission, and mild bilateral pinna pain. She denied headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, or visual symptoms. Her medical history included ischemic heart disease, cervical degenerative disc disease, dyslipidemia, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and a >20 pack-year smoking history. 

On examination, she was febrile to 38.9  degreesC; other vital signs were normal. She h

title: Intraventricular neurocytoma: A diagnostic challenge with prognostic value
case_summary: A 20-year-old woman with no significant medical history presented with a three-week history of nausea, multiple episodes of vomiting, and moderate headache partially relieved by analgesics. There were no focal neurologic deficits. On noncontrast computed tomography of the brain, there was a partially calcified, soft-tissue density mass in the left lateral ventricle, broad-based on the septum pellucidum and occupying the foramen of Monro, with marked ventricular dilatation, effacement of sulci, and transependymal edema. Magnetic resonance imaging showed a voluminous intraventricular lesion that was isointense to gray matter on T1-weighted images and slightly hyperintense on T2-weighted images, with subtle peripheral cystic components and septations bridging to the ventricular wall, giving a scalloped contour. Susceptibility-weighted images revealed signal voids within the lesion corresponding to vascular structures and calcifications. Diffusion-weighted imaging and apparent diffusion co

title: Case report: An unusual presentation of renal hypertension after damage control surgery
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 20-year-old man with no prior medical history sustained blunt abdominal trauma from a motorcycle handlebar and arrived in hemorrhagic shock. CT showed a right?lobe liver laceration with active bleeding and free intraperitoneal fluid. He underwent damage?control surgery with placement of 20 surgical drapes for abdominal packing, received massive transfusion support, and was sedated and ventilated postoperatively.

On hospital day 2 he was transferred to our center. He underwent hepatic arterial embolization for ongoing bleeding, after which he stabilized. Overnight his blood pressure rose to 190/120 mmHg, attributed initially to pain. On day 3 he was extubated, reported no pain, yet maintained a blood pressure around 180/100 mmHg despite labetalol, metoprolol, furosemide, and amlodipine. His fluid balance was +10 L, and diuretics had only transient effects.

On day 4 he experienced six episodes of abrupt hypertensive crisis. The highest recorded blood pressure was 337/141 mmHg with he

title: A case of retroperitoneal fibrosis secondary to chronic periaortitis
case_summary:
A 33-year-old African man with an 11-month history of continuous, blunt low back pain, worse with walking and radiating to the loins and thighs bilaterally, presented with a 9-kg weight loss. He had no significant past medical history and was a smoker. On examination, he was normotensive, with unremarkable cardiovascular and respiratory findings and palpable lower extremity pulses bilaterally. 

Lumbar-spine MRI showed T1- and T2-weighted isointense circumferential soft-tissue thickening around the infrarenal aorta without disk pathology. A CT aortic angiogram confirmed thickening of the anterior and lateral aspects of the infrarenal aorta without aneurysm or distal narrowing. A diagnosis of periaortitis was considered. 

Six months later, he returned with severe back pain and bilateral lower limb swelling. Repeat CT of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated extensive soft-tissue thickening concentrically surrounding the abdominal aorta, both iliac arteries, the inferior vena cava, and bo

title: Rasmussen's pseudoaneurysm- case report
case_summary: A 28-year-old nonsmoking male prisoner presented with several weeks of fresh hemoptysis, fever, anorexia, and weight loss. He had known exposure to pulmonary tuberculosis but no risk factors for pulmonary embolism or bronchiectasis. On examination, he was tachycardic, ill-appearing, and underweight. Chest auscultation revealed bronchial breathing in both upper lobes and diffuse inspiratory and expiratory crackles. Chest radiography showed bilateral diffuse reticulonodular opacities with fibrotic changes in the apices. Sputum AFB and nucleic acid amplification confirmed pan-sensitive Mycobacterium tuberculosis, and standard first-line therapy was started. Despite treatment, he continued to have significant hemoptysis and required three units of packed red blood cells to maintain a hemoglobin around 9 g/dL. Fibreoptic bronchoscopy showed normal airways without an endobronchial mass or obvious bleeding source; bronchoalveolar lavage and biopsies revealed acute inflammation and AFB. A CT p

title: Retroperitoneal paraganglioma in a young patient presenting with hypertensive crisis and transient loss of sight; a rare case report and literature review
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 16-year-old male presented with a 1-month history of epigastric discomfort radiating to the right flank, which worsened over the 5 days before presentation. The pain was dull and continuous without clear relieving or aggravating factors. He also reported headaches associated with transient loss of sight. He was previously well, with no prior surgeries or significant medical history. 

On examination, blood pressure was 245/152 mm Hg and pulse 91 beats/min. Abdominal examination revealed tenderness in the right flank extending to the right lower quadrant without peritoneal signs; no masses were palpated. Funduscopic exam showed grade 1 papilledema. Initial laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10.1 g/dL, white-cell count 8.3 ? 10^9/L, platelet count 521 ? 10^3/mm^3, urea 2.17 mmol/L, creatinine 56.9 ?mol/L, sodium 143 mmol/L, and potassium 4.49 mmol/L. Twenty-four-hour urine collections revealed metanephrines 1148 nmol/day (reference 167-938) and normetanephrines 15,968 nmol/day (refer

title: Takayasu arteritis presenting as cerebral aneurysms in an 18 month old: A case report
case_summary:
An 18-month-old girl was brought to the hospital after a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. On arrival she was unresponsive, hypotonic, and mechanically ventilated. She was normotensive, afebrile, and had no audible bruits. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 32 400/ul, hemoglobin 9.6 g/dl, and platelets 342 000/ul; coagulation tests and metabolic panel were normal. ESR and CRP were not obtained. Her history included failure to thrive managed with supplemental tube feeding; family history was notable for an aunt with an asymptomatic cerebral aneurysm.

Noncontrast head CT revealed a frontal lobe hemorrhage with ventricular dilatation. Brain MR angiography showed an 8 mm aneurysm in the distal A1 segment of the right anterior cerebral artery and an additional aneurysm of the left internal carotid artery; these findings were confirmed by digital subtraction angiography. The ACA aneurysm was clipped and an external ventricular drain was placed. Postoperatively she developed vas

title: Unravelling a mystery of hypokalemic hypertension- a rare case report of a reninoma
case_summary:
A 19-year-old unmarried woman was found to have hypertension (200/120 mmHg) six months earlier, when she was hospitalized for a febrile illness. She has been on losartan 50 mg twice daily without blood-pressure control. She denies muscle weakness, polyuria, nocturia, weight gain, bruising, acne, hirsutism, episodic headaches, or rheumatologic symptoms. There is no family history of early hypertension or intracerebral hemorrhage. She takes no other medications or supplements.

On examination, her BMI is 22 kg/m2. Pulse is 88 beats/minute and regular. Cardiovascular examination is unremarkable; there are no radio-radial or radio-femoral delays, murmurs, or bruits. Fundoscopy shows silver wiring of retinal vessels; other systems are normal.

Laboratory studies:
? Potassium 2.8 mmol/L (3.5-5.2) despite replacement  
? Sodium 136 mmol/L (135-145)  
? Bicarbonate 24.8 mEq/L (22-26)  
? Overnight dexamethasone suppression test <11 nmol/L (normal <50)  
? Aldosterone 49.7 ng/dL (1.76-39.2)  
?

title: Mass-forming renal Crohn's disease: a case report with multimodality imaging
case_summary:
A 31-year-old woman with a 3-year history of Crohn's disease, previously treated with azathioprine and intermittent prednisolone after ileocolic resection, presented with a 2-week history of abdominal pain and diarrhoea. Physical examination was unremarkable aside from mild abdominal tenderness. Laboratory studies showed no leukocytosis and normal renal function. 

MRI of the abdomen performed to assess enterocolitis demonstrated active inflammation of the small and large bowel and revealed an incidental mass in the inferior pole of the left kidney measuring 2.5?2.9?2.3 cm. On MRI, the lesion was slightly hypointense to renal cortex on T2-weighted images, isointense on pre-contrast T1, and exhibited a honeycomb appearance after contrast with irregular enhancing septa around small (1-5 mm) non-enhancing foci; diffusion weighted imaging showed restricted diffusion. A dedicated contrast-enhanced CT scan confirmed a well-defined, isodense mass pre-contrast that was homogeneously hypoenhanc

title: Cholesterol-crystal embolism presenting with delayed graft function and impaired long-term function in renal transplant recipients: two case reports
case_summary:
Two men with end?stage renal disease--aged 57 and 39 years--received deceased?donor renal transplants. Underlying diseases were Alport syndrome (57?year?old) and chronic glomerulonephritis (39?year?old). Both had been on hemodialysis for 7 and 8.5 years and had hypertension. The donor was a 62?year?old man with hypoxic brain injury after a 10?minute cardiac arrest during an asthma attack, a history of smoking, insulin therapy in the ICU, an estimated GFR of 42.4 ml/min/1.73 m2, and atherosclerotic renal arteries.

After arterial end?to?side anastomosis to the external iliac artery, neither graft had primary function. Both patients experienced DGF--requiring 6 and 10 hemodialysis sessions, respectively. After recovery, the GFR stabilized at 26 and 23.9 ml/min/1.73 m2 by discharge and remained at that reduced level on follow?up. Doppler ultrasound repeatedly demonstrated homogeneous perfusion of both grafts, with resistance indices between 0.67 and 0.76.

Warm ischemia times were 46 and

title: Spontaneous cervical epidural haematoma: role of antiplatelets
case_summary: An 80-year-old man presented with sudden onset of right-sided neck pain at rest radiating to his right shoulder and back, immediately followed by numbness, tingling, and heaviness in his right arm. He reported no trauma or headache. His medical history included hypertension, diet-controlled diabetes, transient ischemic attacks, bilateral carotid endarterectomies, and peripheral vascular disease. His medications were aspirin 75 mg daily and dipyridamole sustained release 200 mg twice daily. On examination, he had 3/5 motor strength in the right upper limb with reduced sensation throughout, pain on right lateral neck movement, and no neck stiffness. Cranial nerve and lower limb examinations were normal. He was hemodynamically stable with a blood pressure of 140 mmHg systolic. An electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm with no acute features, and blood tests were unremarkable. The initial differential diagnosis included cerebral stroke, aortic dissection, and cervical myelopathy. An urgent

title: Orbital myeloid sarcoma misdiagnosed for subperiostal hematoma: a case report
case_summary:
A 3-year-old Caucasian boy presented with a 2-week history of left periorbital swelling. The swelling began gradually after blunt trauma in a traffic accident. On initial evaluation at another hospital, he had left periorbital ecchymosis, subconjunctival hemorrhage, and proptosis without visual loss. Two weeks later, persistent proptosis led to referral. Examination at our center revealed slight left proptosis and a tender mass in the inferior orbit displacing the globe upward. Extraocular movements and pupillary responses were normal, and slit-lamp and fundoscopic examinations were unremarkable. Intraocular pressures were within normal limits. Neurologic examination was nonfocal, and there was no hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, bone pain, or systemic symptoms.

Craniofacial CT showed a 29?19 mm spontaneously hyperdense, oval extraconal mass in the inferior orbit, displacing the inferior and lateral rectus muscles, with no bone erosion or significant contrast enhancement.

Given t

title: A Predictable Approach of a Rare and Frequently Misdiagnosed Entity: Laryngeal Nerve Schwannoma
case_summary: A 67-year-old woman presented with a 7-month history of a left-sided neck "lump" and mild discomfort on palpation. She denied odynophagia, dysphagia, or hoarseness. Her medical history was notable for hypertension and age-related cataracts; she was a non-smoker with a BMI of 34 kg/m2 and no family history of malignancy. Physical examination revealed an elastic, tender, mobile mass in the left lateral cervical region; the remainder of the head and neck examination was unremarkable. Prior fiberoptic laryngoscopy, bronchoscopy, and esophagoscopy were normal. Neck ultrasonography showed a 3.0 cm ? 2.06 cm well-defined, hypoechoic mass with no intratumoral vascularity on Doppler imaging. Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck demonstrated a well-circumscribed 4.5 cm ? 3.2 cm ? 2.7 cm mass located deep to the internal jugular vein, carotid artery, and vagus nerve. An ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration was performed, but the result was non-diagnostic.

title: Intravenous Lobular Capillary Haemangioma (Pyogenic Granuloma) of the Superior Vena Cava: Case Report and Literature Review
case_summary: A 30-year-old woman with no significant medical history presented for evaluation of worsening thoraco-abdominal venous collaterals that had developed during her pregnancy five years earlier. She had no other complaints. Vital signs were normal. Abdominal and cardiovascular examinations revealed no signs of portal hypertension or superior vena cava syndrome. Duplex ultrasound showed dilated collateral veins; the inferior vena cava was patent. Contrast-enhanced chest CT angiography demonstrated an intraluminal heterogeneous mass in the superior vena cava, extending from the left brachiocephalic venous trunk to the distal third of the vessel, with no other masses or adenopathy. Thoracic MRI confirmed an 80 ? 33 mm intraluminal tumour in the superior vena cava, with a cystic component showing heterogeneous T1- and T2-weighted signals; the azygos vein was not invaded. FDG-PET imaging showed moderate uptake (SUV max, 4.8). Transthoracic echocardiography revealed no extension into the right a

title: An Imperfect Marker: SLE and TTP-Like MAHA Without Low ADAMTS13
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_summary:
A 44-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus status post mechanical mitral valve replacement presented with two days of diffuse abdominal pain and black stools. Her outpatient SLE regimen--mycophenolate mofetil, prednisone, and azathioprine--had been reduced to azathioprine alone due to insurance issues. On arrival, vital signs were within normal limits. Physical examination was notable for mild abdominal tenderness without rebound or guarding; there was no rash or focal neurologic deficit.

Laboratory studies revealed pancytopenia (hemoglobin 8.2 g/dL, platelets 67,000/uL, white blood cells 2,300/uL) and acute renal failure (serum creatinine 3.0 mg/dL). A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated gallbladder wall thickening without pericholecystic fluid. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy showed only small gastric erosions. Serologic evaluation revealed elevated anti-double?stranded DNA titers and hypocomplementemia (C3 18 mg/dL, C4 2 mg/dL), consistent with an SLE flare. She re

title: An Unusual Case of Myonecrosis
case_summary:
A 58-year-old man presented with an 8-day history of bilateral, proximal upper-limb pain that progressed from a dull ache to excruciating pain, accompanied by swelling and restricted shoulder movement. He denied trauma, herbal or prescription drug use apart from occasional acetaminophen, and had no known history of hypertension or diabetes. He reported 2 years of polyuria and polydipsia but had not sought evaluation. His family history was notable for type 2 diabetes in his mother and sibling. 

On examination, he was afebrile and normotensive with a BMI of 29.4. There was warmth, tenderness, swelling, and mild erythema of both shoulders and the upper arms without induration or crepitus. All peripheral pulses were palpable and deep tendon reflexes were normal. Fundoscopy revealed retinal microaneurysms and blot hemorrhages. Other systems were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 13?g/dL, leukocytes 12.9 ? 10^9/L with 94% neutrophils, normal liver and renal function, rand

title: Primary liver actinomycosis in a pediatric patient: A case report and literature review
case_summary:
A 9-year-old Chinese girl presented with a one-month history of recurrent fevers (38-39  degreesC) and cough. She had been treated for presumed upper respiratory infection without improvement. Over the last 4 days, her caregivers noted a palpable mass in the right upper abdomen and right lower chest. There was no significant past medical history or known exposures.

On examination, she was febrile and appeared pale. A large tender mass involved the right lower chest and right upper abdomen. Breath sounds were diminished on the right side.

Laboratory studies showed WBC 24.5 ? 10^9/L (82% neutrophils), hemoglobin 70 g/L, hematocrit 24.6%, CRP 177 mg/L, normal liver enzymes, and albumin 29.6 g/L. Tumor markers revealed CA-125 45.5 U/mL; other markers, including CEA, CA-199, AFP, were within normal limits. Stool microscopy was negative for amoebae. Serologies for hepatitis A-E and HIV were negative.

Chest CT demonstrated a patchy, ill-defined area of uneven density in the right lower lun

title: Endovascular Treatment of Ruptured Renal Artery Aneurysm: A Case-Based Literature Review
case_summary:
A 55-year-old man with end-stage renal disease on thrice-weekly hemodialysis presented with sudden onset of cramping right flank pain radiating to the groin. He denied fever, nausea, vomiting, bowel changes, or prior similar pain, and had been anuric for two years. His medical history included diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and ESRD secondary to diabetic nephropathy and hypertensive nephrosclerosis; he had no history of trauma or prior renal procedures. On arrival, heart rate was 120 bpm, blood pressure 130/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 95% on room air. Examination revealed tenderness at the right renal angle; the remainder of the abdominal and systemic examination was unremarkable. 

Laboratory studies showed a hemoglobin of 6.5 g/dL and hematocrit of 28%, down from a baseline hemoglobin of 10.5 g/dL and hematocrit of 38% one month prior. A broad differential diagnosis was considered, including urinary calculus, acute pyelonephritis, renal in

title: Unilateral testicular seminoma with simultaneous contralateral torsion: a case report
case_summary: A 28-year-old previously healthy man presented with a two-week history of right-side scrotal swelling and a three-month history of left-side scrotal enlargement. He reported a single episode of dull right scrotal pain that resolved within a day. He had no history of scrotal surgery, cryptorchidism, or infections. On examination, the right hemiscrotum was mildly swollen, and the left hemiscrotum was markedly enlarged with a palpable hard mass. Scrotal ultrasound revealed bilateral heterogeneous testicular masses: the right mass was hypoechoic with a diffuse hypovascular area on Doppler imaging, and the left was hyperechoic with iso- to hypervascular flow. Contrast-enhanced CT of the pelvis showed the right testis to be normal in size with decreased enhancement, whereas the left testis was enlarged with heterogeneous enhancement. These findings raised a differential of bilateral testicular germ cell tumors, but the right testis did not have typical features of a germ cell tumor; therefor

title: A Multidisciplinary Approach to the Management of Eales Disease: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
