case_summary: A 34-year-old man presented with 2 weeks of decreased and blurred vision with floaters in the right eye. He had no prior ocular symptoms. His history was notable for untreated hepatitis B infection and a tick bite over one year earlier. On examination, best-corrected visual acuity was 0.4 in the right eye and 1.0 in the left eye; intraocular pressures were 12 mm Hg bilaterally. Anterior segments were unremarkable. Dilated fundus examination of both eyes showed vitreous cells, thick epimacular membranes, dilated veins with perivascular sheathing, focal peripheral venous occlusions, venous beading, dotted and flame-shaped intraretinal hemorrhages, and prominent sea-fan-shaped retinal and preretinal neovascularization. Fluorescein angiography revealed sharply demarcated peripheral non-perfused areas in the arterial phase; staining of inflamed veins and optic-disc hyperfluorescence in the early venous phase; and late leakage from neovascular lesions with macular leakage. Optical coherence

title: External jugular venous aneurysm: A rare case report
case_summary: A 58-year-old woman with no significant medical or surgical history presented with a right lateral neck swelling present since childhood, which had progressively enlarged over the past year with noticeable prominence in the last 2 months. She reported mild discomfort and intermittent episodes of dizziness but denied pain, dysphagia, voice changes, or a history of neck trauma. On examination, the mass in the right anterolateral neck was soft, compressible, non tender, nonpulsatile, and increased slightly in size with coughing and Valsalva maneuver; the overlying skin was normal. Doppler ultrasound of the neck revealed a 25 ? 11 mm lumen of the external jugular vein with a 1.8 mm defect in its wall, producing a 26 ? 3.6 mm saccular outpouching containing bidirectional flow with a yin-yang sign and a 3 mm thrombus within the lesion. No other venous channels communicated with the lesion, and the right common carotid artery appeared normal.

title: A case report of unilateral cerebral vasculitis in adults: keep in mind Lyme neuroborreliosis
case_summary: A 58-year-old man with no medical history or vascular risk factors presented with sudden right brachiofacial weakness. Facial palsy persisted after partial recovery. Brain MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging revealed acute cortical infarcts in the left middle cerebral artery territory (parieto-occipital and frontal lobes). Examination was otherwise unremarkable except for moderate hypertension. Cervical and intracranial CT angiography showed no stenosis, occlusion, or dilatation. Telemetry (48 hours), transthoracic echocardiography, and transesophageal echocardiography were notable only for a large patent foramen ovale. Laboratory testing, including homocysteine, blood glucose, lipid profile, anticardiolipin antibodies, and lupus anticoagulant, was normal. He was diagnosed with cryptogenic stroke and treated with aspirin, atorvastatin, and perindopril; PFO closure was planned. Two months later, he developed sudden aphasia. MRI showed a new acute ischemic lesion in the left MCA territor

title: Tumefactive demyelinating disease with isolated spinal cord involvement
case_summary:
A 31-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presented with acute onset of lumbar pain, bladder incontinence, and gait imbalance. Over several days, her symptoms progressed to paraplegia with complete loss of motor and sensory function below the T4 level. 

Initial MRI of the spine showed abnormal T2 hyperintensity extending from C6 through the upper thoracic cord, with mild cord expansion and faint enhancement in the lower thoracic region. She was treated with high-dose corticosteroids, intravenous immunoglobulin, and underwent rehabilitation. Two months later, her clinical status remained unchanged, but repeat MRI demonstrated significant improvement of the cervical and thoracic cord signal abnormalities, with only small residual foci.

Six months after the initial presentation, she experienced acute upper thoracic pain and dysesthesias radiating to the ulnar aspect of both upper extremities. MRI of the spine revealed marked interval worsening of signal abnormalities

title: Hemoptysis due to a lung metastasis of renal cell carcinoma: A case report
case_summary: A 53-year-old man was referred for evaluation of massive hemoptysis, estimated at approximately 200 mL per day for 1 week. His vital signs were stable, and his hemoglobin level was 11.5 g/dL. Two months earlier, he had undergone left nephrectomy for a 76 mm clear-cell renal carcinoma. His medical history was otherwise unremarkable, with no known bronchiectasis, cystic fibrosis, or exposure to tuberculosis, nontuberculous mycobacteria, or Aspergillus species. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography of the chest revealed a 16 mm nodule in the right middle lobe associated with a peritumoral hematoma. The nodule demonstrated intense enhancement in the arterial phase and washout in the venous phase. Right bronchial arteriography showed enhancement of the nodule but no evidence of contrast extravasation, arteriovenous shunting, or vascular malformation.

title: Atypical imaging presentation of a massive intracavitary cardiac thrombus: A case report and brief review of the literature
case_summary: A 26-year-old woman with obesity, newly diagnosed systemic lupus erythematosus complicated by catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome, and acute on chronic immune thrombocytopenic purpura presented with progressive cough, pleuritic chest pain, and worsening dyspnea on exertion and when supine. She reported a year-long cough that had become productive over 3 months and painful over the last day. She described parasternal chest pain radiating to her throat, worsened by coughing, cold exposure, and smoking. Examination in the outside emergency department revealed no fever; vital signs were within normal limits. Laboratory tests showed a D-dimer of 3.4 ug/mL, hemoglobin 18.9 g/dL, and platelet count 130 000/uL. A chest radiograph was initially read as normal. A CT pulmonary embolism protocol demonstrated a large heterogeneous, irregular, calcified mass extending from the right atrium across the tricuspid valve into the right ventricle and pulmonary outflow tract, along with a small to moder

title: Proteus syndrome with progressive paralysis of the unilateral lower limb: A rare case report and literature review
case_summary:
An 11-year-old boy was admitted for treatment of pressure ulcers in the buttock and sacrum. In 2016, he had sudden lower?limb weakness and paresthesia, from which he recovered with treatment. Since 2020, he noted progressive weakness of the left lower limb, and by 2022 he developed loss of touch, pain, and temperature sensation in that limb. Superficial sensation on the left declined, muscle strength worsened to grade 0, and the left lower limb hypertrophied rapidly, while the right lower limb underwent atrophy.

Family history was negative for hereditary disorders; parents were healthy and non-consanguineous; a brother has lymphocytic leukemia.

On examination, he appeared malnourished, with a long face and mildly downslanting palpebral fissures. Multiple soft, mobile, non-tender, adipose-like masses were present on the right chest wall, back, and left lower abdomen (largest 14?8?cm). He had thoracic scoliosis; the right lower-limb muscle was atrophic (strength grade 2) with intact se

title: Psychosis secondary to COVID?19 in pediatric sickle cell disease
case_summary: A 15-year-old female with sickle cell disease (HbSS) and a history of depression presented in November 2020 with shortness of breath and chest, leg, and back pain. She was afebrile, had an oxygen saturation of 100% on room air, and chest radiography showed a left basilar opacity. She received supplemental oxygen, antibiotics, analgesia, intravenous morphine via patient-controlled analgesia, and a packed red blood cell transfusion. On hospital day 2, she developed worsening dyspnea and tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 by PCR. Laboratory studies showed a C-reactive protein level of 252.6 mg/dL (normal 0.1-1.7), a procalcitonin level of 0.72 ng/mL (0.0-0.5), thrombocytopenia (38 K/uL), and a normal white-cell count and differential. A computed tomography angiogram of the chest revealed bilateral ground-glass opacities without pulmonary embolism. She was treated in the pediatric ICU with high-dose corticosteroids, remdesivir, enoxaparin, ceftriaxone, and platelet transfusion, improved, and w

title: Phrenic nerve injury secondary to extracorporeal membrane oxygenation in pregnancy: A case report
case_summary:
A 29-year-old G6P3023 at 25 weeks' gestation presented with chest and back pain and progressive shortness of breath. Her medical history included asthma, opioid use disorder, bipolar disorder, anxiety, seizures, anemia, hypothyroidism, rheumatoid arthritis, and fibromyalgia. She was on buprenorphine, gabapentin, quetiapine, alprazolam, levetiracetam, and albuterol; she smoked one pack per day and had a remote history of heroin use. 

Initial chest radiography showed bilateral airspace infiltrates without hemidiaphragm elevation. She developed ARDS, was intubated, and required broad-spectrum antibiotics, stress-dose steroids, vasopressors, sedation, and initiation of single-lumen veno-venous ECMO. After 13 days of ECMO support, she was decannulated. A chest radiograph obtained the next day demonstrated elevation of the right hemidiaphragm.

She underwent tracheostomy and was weaned to 2 L/min of oxygen via nasal cannula; after four days she maintained saturations on room air. A CT scan

title: Cerebellar large B-cell lymphoma: a case report
case_summary: A 71-year-old Caucasian man presented with a 1-week history of abrupt-onset blurry vision, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, and ataxia. He denied vertigo or headache and had no prior stroke history; he was taking aspirin for a patent foramen ovale. In the emergency department, a noncontrast head CT showed no masses or hemorrhage. MRI was contraindicated because of ocular shrapnel. Carotid Doppler ultrasound showed no stenosis, and CT angiography demonstrated no vascular compromise. The working diagnosis was cerebellar stroke, and he was transferred to inpatient stroke rehabilitation. Despite therapy, his symptoms worsened. A repeat noncontrast head CT 9 days after admission revealed indistinct, masslike lesions in the cerebellum--one lesion with hemorrhage, surrounding vasogenic edema, and mild hydrocephalus. Contrast-enhanced CT later that day showed three intensely enhancing masses in the right cerebellar hemisphere. He was started empirically on steroids for vasogenic edema, resulting i

title: Non-classical Celiac Disease as an Unrecognized Cause for Post-operative Knee Swelling following Patellofemoral Arthroplasty: A Case Report
case_summary: A 34-year-old woman with a history of recurrent patellar dislocations and progressive right anterior knee pain underwent patellofemoral arthroplasty with concomitant medial patellofemoral ligament reconstruction. The surgery was uneventful. Four weeks postoperatively, she noted an erythematous, blanchable rash around the arthrotomy incision, associated with warmth, swelling, and stiffness limiting range of motion to 0-30 degrees. She was afebrile, and vital signs were normal. A 7-day course of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole was initiated for presumed skin and soft-tissue infection. Two days later, she returned with increased pain, persistent rash with mild urticaria and warmth, tenderness along the joint line, and a trace effusion; the surgical incision sites were well healed. She was referred to the emergency department, where she received intravenous vancomycin and diphenhydramine. Laboratory tests showed normal inflammatory markers, and MRI of the knee showed no evidence of infection

title: Hepatic Perivascular Epithelioid Cell Tumor Mimicking Hepatocellular Carcinoma
case_summary: A 27-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of a right-sided abdominal mass. She was asymptomatic, with good performance status, no weight loss, and no comorbidities. On examination, there was a firm, nontender liver mass extending 6 cm below the right costal margin with rounded borders. Laboratory investigations, including liver function tests, viral hepatitis markers, and tumor markers, were normal (alpha-fetoprotein 0.4 ng/mL). Duplex ultrasound showed a 10 ? 8 cm heteroechoic mass in the right liver lobe with invasion of the right portal vein. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy and colonoscopy findings were normal. Contrast-enhanced abdominal CT demonstrated a 10 ? 10 ? 13 cm heterogeneously enhancing lesion in segments V through VIII, with early arterial enhancement that persisted in later phases, a prominent feeding right hepatic artery, and right portal vein thrombosis; no extrahepatic lesions were seen. A chest CT scan was unremarkable. Fine-needle aspiration cytology had suggested

title: Recurrent purpura due to alcohol-related Schamberg's disease and its association with serum immunoglobulins: a longitudinal observation of a heavy drinker
case_summary:
A 55-year-old white man with severe alcohol use disorder presented repeatedly over 2 years for inpatient detoxification. Upon each admission he had symmetric purpuric rashes on his lower extremities, appearing during periods of excessive ethanol intake (>300 g/day) and beginning to resolve within one week of abstinence. He denied itching, pain, fever, arthralgias, gastrointestinal symptoms, or other systemic complaints. Examination also revealed cerebellar ataxia, affective lability, mild sensorimotor polyneuropathy of the lower limbs, and hepatomegaly.

Routine blood tests over multiple admissions showed normal platelet counts and coagulation parameters, moderately elevated transaminases, and mildly increased C-reactive protein. Abdominal ultrasound showed fatty liver without cirrhosis. Serologic tests for hepatitis, HIV, and syphilis were negative. Neuroimaging, echocardiography, angiology, and capillary microscopy revealed no evidence of vasculitis or thrombosis. 

A skin biopsy of

title: Axillary Hibernoma in woman with Lobular breast cancer and MEN1 syndrome: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 42-year-old woman with known MEN1 syndrome was under surveillance. Two years earlier, she had undergone right mastectomy and axillary dissection for a 7-cm infiltrating lobular carcinoma (solid-alveolar variant, G2, ER 95%, PgR 80%, Ki-67 20%, Her2 negative; pT3 N9/18 M0). She completed adjuvant chemotherapy and hormonal therapy without evidence of recurrent disease. BRCA1/2 testing was negative.

Because she had a breast expander in place, she did not undergo MRI. Instead, a fluorodeoxyglucose PET-CT scan showed intense uptake (SUVmax 14.6) in a 25 ? 12 ? 50 mm elongated, thickened area along the left mid-axillary line adjacent to the 4th-6th ribs. There was also mild hypermetabolism in bilaterally enlarged adrenals.

Bilateral ultrasound and mammography of the left breast and axilla showed no focal lesions. A contrast-enhanced chest CT confirmed a 30-mm hyperdense mass contacting the pectoralis major muscle but without lymphadenopathy or imaging features typical of local recurrence

title: A case report of a teenager with hepatitis B surface antigen?positive multifocal hepatocellular carcinoma in a noncirrhotic liver
case_summary:
A 13-year-old girl presented with a 1-month history of right upper quadrant pain and abdominal distension, associated with fever, dark stools, weight loss, and scleral jaundice. She had no prior jaundice or history of transfusion. Immunization history was reportedly complete, but records were unavailable.

On examination, she was febrile (39.0  degreesC), tachycardic (134 bpm), mildly pale, wasted, and had scleral icterus. Abdominal examination revealed a tender, firm, nodular liver 23 cm below the right costal margin. There were no peripheral stigmata of chronic liver disease.

Laboratory studies showed WBC 20.9 ? 10^9/L, neutrophils 14.2 ? 10^9/L, hemoglobin 8.2 g/dL, platelets 644 ? 10^9/L, and ESR 136 mm/hr. Blood and urine cultures yielded no growth. AST was 55 U/L, ALP 448 U/L, GGT 170 U/L; ALT and albumin were normal. INR was 1.89 and aPTT 67.7 s. Serum alpha-fetoprotein was 1677.1 IU/mL; ?-hCG was normal. Renal function and electrolytes were unremarkable. Viral screening was po

title: Enlarged accessory spleen after splenectomy mimicking a pancreas tumor
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: A 38-year-old woman underwent a routine medical check-up. She had no weight loss, anorexia, or weakness. Twenty years earlier, she had undergone splenectomy for traumatic splenic rupture. Laboratory tests, including complete blood count, liver function, and alpha-fetoprotein, were within normal limits. An unenhanced abdominal CT scan showed no spleen and revealed a homogeneous mass at the splenic fossa. On arterial-phase CT, the mass enhanced heterogeneously; on venous-phase CT, the enhancement was homogeneous. There was no surrounding fat stranding. Coronal reconstructions demonstrated that the mass compressed the left kidney and received blood supply from branches of the splenic artery. Subsequent MRI confirmed a well-circumscribed, round mass at the expected splenic site. It was hypointense to liver on T1-weighted images without signal loss on opposed-phase, hyperintense on T2-weighted images, and exhibited restricted diffusion. In the arterial phase, the mass enhanced markedly and

title: Treatment of trigeminal and glossopharyngeal neuralgia in an adolescent: a case report
case_summary:
A 17-year-old previously healthy male presented with recurrent severe right pharyngeal pain. He described intense paroxysmal electric shock-like pain lasting seconds to minutes in a well-defined unilateral area from the right tongue root to the pharynx, occurring about 10 times per day and triggered by tongue movement or deglutition. There were no bradycardia or syncopal episodes. He rated the pain 8/10. 

Magnetic resonance imaging with CISS sequence demonstrated compression of the right glossopharyngeal nerve by the posterior inferior cerebellar artery. Examination by otorhinolaryngology and other cranial nerves was normal, and there were no signs of Eagle's syndrome. A diagnosis of glossopharyngeal neuralgia was made. High-dose carbamazepine, up to 800 mg daily, initially provided relief but only transiently; baclofen 5 mg daily was ineffective. Two years later, owing to refractory pain and imaging evidence of nerve compression, he underwent microvascular decompression with postoper

title: A rare case of tension pneumoperitoneum secondary to gastric perforation associated with severe aortic compression
case_summary:
A 36-year-old man was admitted to the emergency department with a 3-hour history of progressive central abdominal pain and distension. His blood pressure was 136/77 mmHg, and serum lactate was 14 mmol/L (reference, 0.5-2.2 mmol/L). On examination, his abdomen was tense and peritonitic, and femoral pulses were absent bilaterally. The initial differential diagnosis included pneumoperitoneum secondary to gastric or bowel perforation and vascular compromise such as aortic dissection, given the lack of femoral pulses. 

Arterial and portal-venous phase CT of the abdomen and pelvis extending to the mid-thigh demonstrated:
? Extensive free intraperitoneal fluid and severe pneumoperitoneum compressing the inferior vena cava and the abdominal aorta.  
? A short segment occlusion of the aorta at the level of the L4 superior endplate.  
? Minimal contrast opacification of the superior mesenteric artery and the bilateral common iliac and femoral arteries.  
? Poor opacification of the portal venou

title: Fatal Acute Hemorrhagic Encephalomyelitis and Antiphospholipid Antibodies following SARS-CoV-2 Vaccination: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 53-year-old man presented with acute confusion and unconsciousness evolving over a few hours. He had received his second dose of the Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine two days earlier. On examination, his Glasgow Coma Scale score was 7, with agitation and snoring. Neurologic findings included anisocoria (right-sided miosis), bilateral absence of pupillary light reflexes, and decreased voluntary movements of the left arm and leg. He was afebrile and showed no signs of systemic infection. His medical history was notable for rheumatoid arthritis treated with etanercept and methotrexate. 

Noncontrast head CT revealed a small hypoattenuating area in the left temporal lobe interpreted as an acute or subacute ischemic lesion, with reduced perfusion in the left hemisphere and a normal CT angiogram. Brain MRI demonstrated multiple cortical and subcortical T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesions with ubiquitous petechial hemorrhages. Follow-up MRI scans showed rapid development of widespread lesions in

title: Interdigitating Dendritic Cell Tumor of Submandibular Lymph Node: Case Report and Literature Review
case_summary: A 53-year-old man was referred for evaluation of left-sided neck swelling of seven months' duration. Ultrasound examination revealed an echo-free, well-circumscribed mass measuring 24.1?36.4 mm, located 3.9 mm beneath the skin over the left parotid region, with no adhesions to surrounding tissues and a leading consideration of a benign mass or lymphadenopathy. He had a lifelong history of photosensitivity and multiple skin lesions; at age 51, he was diagnosed with basal cell carcinoma of the zygomatic region and lip, metatypical carcinoma of the frontal skin, and keratoacanthoma-like squamous cell carcinoma of the nose. Family history was unremarkable. He underwent radical lymph node dissection; the submandibular specimen measured 4?2.5?1 cm and was effaced by a malignant mesenchymal tumor with a storiform pattern. Neoplastic cells were spindle-shaped with hyperchromatic, pleomorphic nuclei, vesicular chromatin, prominent nucleoli, and eosinophilic cytoplasm; mitotic figures were frequ

title: Case report: recurrent thrombosis of an old lead of a DDDR pacemaker mimicking lead infection
case_summary:
An 85-year-old man with a permanent DDDR pacemaker implanted 25 years earlier for complete heart block was evaluated for dyspnea on exertion (NYHA class III) when a routine transthoracic echocardiogram revealed a right atrial mass. His history included arterial hypertension and three-vessel coronary disease managed medically; he had refused surgical revascularization following coronary angiography 2 years prior. Six months earlier, he underwent an uncomplicated pacemaker generator replacement.

On admission, he was afebrile, with a heart rate of 60 b.p.m., blood pressure 120/65 mmHg, and pacing rhythm; examination showed no pocket-site infection. Laboratory studies, including CBC, C-reactive protein, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, liver and thyroid function tests, coagulation markers, and pacemaker interrogation, were normal. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated normal ventricular size and function, but the apical four-chamber view showed two mobile, elliptic, low-echogenicity

title: Vomiting-induced short gastric artery apoplexy
case_summary:
A 27-year-old man presented with severe epigastric and left hypochondriac pain of 1-day duration radiating to the left shoulder, worse on inspiration and when lying down. He reported drinking approximately 1.5 L of alcohol over 2 hours, followed by multiple episodes of vomiting, after which his abdominal pain intensified. There was no history of trauma, surgery, or significant past medical problems.

On examination, he was afebrile (35.7  degreesC), with stable vital signs: heart rate 69 beats/min, blood pressure 115/77 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, SpO? 100% on room air, and Glasgow Coma Scale 15/15. Abdominal examination revealed tenderness in the epigastric and left hypochondriac region without guarding or rebound; the remainder of the exam was unremarkable.

Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 13.7 g/dL, hematocrit 42.6%, leukocyte count 11.2 ? 10?/L, and normal prothrombin time, aPTT, and INR. Liver function tests and serum amylase were within normal limits.

Given his pre

title: Bilateral Renal Artery Thromboembolism During Thrombolytic Therapy for Embolism-Induced Acute Limb Ischemia
case_summary: A 59-year-old man with a history of heavy smoking presented with 5 hours of left lower leg pain. Vital signs were notable for sinus tachycardia on ECG without ST-T-wave changes; heart sounds were normal and lungs were clear. Examination of the left leg revealed it to be cold and pale, with a weak femoral artery pulse and absence of popliteal, dorsalis pedis, and posterior tibial pulses; neurologic function was intact. Duplex ultrasonography showed thrombotic occlusion of the proximal left common femoral artery (80%) and of three arteries below the knee (100%). Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated left ventricular systolic dysfunction (LVEF, 30%-35%), apical akinesia, and a mobile thrombus measuring 21 ? 15 mm in the left ventricular apex. He was diagnosed with embolism-induced acute left lower leg ischemia and received intravenous unfractionated heparin (100 U/kg) followed by catheter-directed thrombolysis with recombinant tissue-type plasminogen activator (1 mg/h) and heparin (

title: 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography/ computed tomography in the diagnosis of a rare mycotic aneurysm of the thoracic aorta in a patient with fever of unknown origin
case_summary:
A 49-year-old woman with a 10-year history of end-stage renal disease secondary to systemic lupus erythematosus and prior methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) bacteremia presented with prolonged, recurrent fevers. Four years earlier, she had an infected left upper-extremity hemodialysis graft. One month before this admission, she was hospitalized for MSSA bacteremia attributed to mastitis and was discharged on outpatient intravenous antibiotics. Despite compliance, she continued to have fevers up to 38.3  degreesC.

On readmission, laboratory studies showed a normal white-cell count, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 101 mm/h (reference range, 0-20), a C-reactive protein level of 10.7 mg/dL (reference range, 0.0-1.0), and persistent positive blood cultures for MSSA. Extensive clinical evaluation and conventional imaging failed to localize a source. She also reported left arm pain, prompting concern for infection of her abandoned left upper-arm vascular graft.

An 18F-f

title: Iron deficiency?induced thrombocytosis presenting with blue toe syndrome
case_summary:
A 38-year-old man with a history of laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy 10 years earlier presented with severe burning pain and blue-gray discoloration of the first three toes of his left foot. Seven days before admission, he had a transient 10-minute episode of left-sided upper and lower limb weakness without residual deficits. On the same day, he developed left foot pain radiating to the thigh, rated 9/10, partially relieved by paracetamol. Three days prior to presentation, the first and second toes discolored and improved, but the third toe remained markedly blue and tender.

He denied any history of stroke, thrombosis, coagulopathy, autoimmune disease, or similar episodes. Family history was notable only for a brother with leukemia. He worked as a businessman, had a 17-pack-year smoking history, and denied alcohol or drug use.

On examination, he was afebrile; heart rate 89 bpm; blood pressure 109/60 mmHg in both arms. The left third toe was bluish-purple and severely tender. Periphera

title: Plasmacytoma as a cause of small bowel obstruction in a virgin abdomen in a patient with multiple myeloma: a case report
case_summary: A 52-year-old African-American man with no prior abdominal surgeries and a history of multiple myeloma and venous thromboembolism presented with a 6-day history of nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. He had not passed flatus for 24 hours and denied fever, chills, or malaise. He was diagnosed with multiple myeloma 4 months earlier after a pathologic left humerus fracture and was undergoing chemotherapy (bortezomib, lenalidomide, dexamethasone) and localized radiation therapy; he completed his second cycle 10 days before presentation. His bone marrow biopsy had shown 25% clonal plasma cells with kappa restriction; FISH revealed a 17p13 deletion in 30% of cells and a t(14;16) rearrangement in 5.7% of cells. He also had stage II disease by the International Staging System. Other conditions included hypertension, obesity, sleep apnea, vitamin D deficiency, and a pulmonary embolism treated with enoxaparin. On examination, vital signs were normal. Abdominal examination revealed tenderness i

title: Pneumoscrotum: Value as an early diagnostic sign of tension pneumothorax in blunt thoracic trauma
journal: International Journal of Critical Illness and Injury Science
case_summary: A 37-year-old man is brought to the trauma bay after an unwitnessed motorcycle collision. EMS found him several feet from the motorcycle in asystole and initiated ACLS without return of pulses. On arrival, ACLS and ATLS were continued. He was intubated (GCS 3). Auscultation revealed bilateral anterior breath sounds with pulse oximetry >92%, but bag-mask ventilation met moderate difficulty and increased resistance. No carotid or femoral pulses were palpable and there was no jugular venous distention. He had large chest and abdominal abrasions without palpable crepitus or rib fractures. Pupils were equal, round, and reactive. During a pulse check, bedside ultrasound showed no cardiac activity or pericardial effusion. Breath sounds remained equal and no JVD was noted. After two rounds of epinephrine and 10 minutes of ACLS, it was noted that there was significant and rapid scrotal enlargement.

title: Traumatic air embolism; a case report and literature review of this rare complication of trauma
case_summary:
A 23-year-old man presented to a rural hospital with a single gunshot wound to the right chest, resulting in a sucking chest wound. He was managed according to Advanced Trauma Life Support protocols, including placement of an intercostal drain and closure of the bullet entry site. An initial CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed no additional injuries. One hour after presentation, he developed paresthesia of the left leg and experienced a generalized tonic-clonic seizure.

After seizure control, neurologic examination showed decreased sensation in the left leg. MRI of the brain demonstrated bilateral superior cerebellar infarcts and a small infarct in the left occipital lobe. MRI of the spine revealed focal cord infarcts at anterior C2-C3 and posterolateral left T6-T7, without evidence of traumatic injury at those levels. A transthoracic echocardiogram showed no valvular pathology or intracardiac shunt. Doppler ultrasound of the lower extremities and CT pulmonary angiograp

title: Arteriovenous malformation of the thyroid gland as a very rare cause of mechanical neck syndrome: a case report
case_summary:
A 64-year-old man with hypertension presented for evaluation of a 6-month history of intermittent cough, mild dyspnoea on exertion, and episodic hoarseness. Chest radiography showed a 10-mm rightward deviation of the trachea. On neck examination, the thyroid gland was enlarged, predominantly involving the left lobe. Thyroid?function tests were within normal limits.

Neck ultrasonography demonstrated:
? Right lobe: 20 ? 21 ? 49 mm, normal echotexture.  
? Left lobe: 80 ? 70 ? 55 mm, hypoechogenic, lobulated, markedly hypervascular, extending retrosternally, with several cystic areas and a vascular plexus in the lower portion (arterial diameters up to 12 mm).  
? No cervical lymphadenopathy.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck revealed a retrosternal left-lobe mass deforming and compressing the trachea, with a large inferior-pole nodule displacing the aortic-arch vessels and associated multiple venous plexuses up to 20 mm in width; cervical lymph nodes appeared benign. On auscultation over

title: Unmasking Aspergillosis: A Rare Cause of Facial Palsy and Vision Loss in an Immunocompetent Patient--A Case Report and Literature Review
case_summary: A 45-year-old woman was admitted with a 3-day history of right facial weakness and progressive vision loss in her right eye. She noticed facial asymmetry and an inability to close her right eyelid on awakening, accompanied by drooping of the right angle of her mouth, diplopia, and blurring of vision in the right eye. She also reported episodic, self-resolving vertigo but denied headache, fever, infection, nasal congestion, discharge, or sinus pain. There was no history of trauma. Baseline laboratory studies, including complete blood count, liver and renal function tests, and serum electrolytes, were within normal limits; C-reactive protein was 1.25 mg/dL (reference < 1 mg/dL) and erythrocyte sedimentation rate was 30 mm/h (reference < 20 mm/h). On examination, she was alert and oriented but had House-Brackmann grade IV right facial palsy with loss of forehead movement, orbicularis oculi function, and nasolabial fold. There was no light perception in the right eye, and funduscopic exami

title: Fat emboli and critical illness-associated cerebral microbleeds (CICMs) in a patient with sickle cell disease: Do these 2 entities coexist?
case_summary: A 30-year-old woman with homozygous sickle cell disease was transferred to the ICU for respiratory failure and new neurologic deterioration during an acute vaso-occlusive pain crisis. She had fever and lower-extremity pain similar to prior episodes. On admission she was lethargic; temperature 38  degreesC and heart rate 140 bpm. Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 8.3 g/dL, haptoglobin 17 mg/dL, white-cell count 23.1 ? 10^3/uL (neutrophils 18.7 ? 10^3/uL), CRP 215 mg/L, AST 121 U/L, ALT 64 U/L, ALP 450 U/L, LDH 3861 U/L, total bilirubin 3.9 mg/dL; renal function was normal. Arterial PO2 was 80%. A chest CT angiogram excluded pulmonary embolism and showed bilateral pulmonary consolidations. Unenhanced brain CT and CT angiography were unremarkable. SARS-CoV-2 RT-PCR and serology were negative. Brain MRI showed a few punctate foci of restricted diffusion with corresponding T2 FLAIR hyperintensities and numerous punctate low-signal foci on susceptibility-weighted images in both supra- and i

title: Multidisciplinary diagnostic dilemma in differentiating Madelung's disease -- the value of superb microvascular imaging technique: A case report
case_summary:
A 62-year-old man was referred for evaluation of slowly enlarging, symmetric subcutaneous masses over 2 years, most prominently in the parotid and submandibular areas, with more rapid growth over the past 7 months. He also reported 6 months of bilateral hand joint pain with morning stiffness, dry mouth, calf rash, and nail changes. His history was significant for metabolic liver cirrhosis (Child-Turcotte-Pugh B, MELD score 9), portal hypertension, and secondary megaloblastic anemia diagnosed in 2019. He had a history of moderate alcohol intake but had abstained for over 6 months.  
On examination, he was slightly overweight (BMI 25.5 kg/m2). There were firm, painless, egg-sized subcutaneous masses in the parotid and submandibular regions, softer mobile masses over the occiput and upper forearms, and mild gynecomastia. Skin examination revealed psoriatic plaques on the calves and onychodystrophy. Hand joint palpation was tender. Schirmer's test showed reduced tear production.  
Laborato

title: Enigmatic odyssey: A case of Behcet disease with atypical thrombotic complications
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 26-year-old woman with a 9-year history of Beh?et disease presented in January 2024 with a 2-week history of progressive shortness of breath, palpitations, fever, and dry cough that began after a dental extraction. She has experienced exertional dyspnea for the past year and recurrent oral and genital ulcers. In 2021, she was admitted for hemoptysis and recurrent oral ulcers; CT pulmonary angiography revealed a right pulmonary artery aneurysm and a left subsegmental pulmonary embolism. She was treated with intravenous infliximab and low-molecular-weight heparin, with resolution of symptoms. At that time, CT angiography also showed a 2.0 ? 1.8 cm filling defect in the right atrium, which was asymptomatic and managed with surveillance.

In 2022, she had a similar presentation; treatment was repeated, and CT angiography showed the right atrial filling defect had grown to 3.0 ? 2.0 cm. In 2024, echocardiography revealed a mobile right atrial mass measuring 4.0 ? 3.0 cm that appeared soft

title: Diagnosing a popliteal venous aneurysm in a primary care setting: A case report
case_summary:
A 68-year-old woman presented with several months of local discomfort and swelling in the right popliteal fossa that worsened with walking and climbing stairs. Her history included chronic bilateral venous insufficiency beginning after pregnancy, bilateral saphenectomy, and recurrence of varicosities about 12 years later. There was a strong family history of varicose veins. 

On examination, vital signs were normal. The right popliteal fossa showed a soft mass that was painful on deep palpation, without erythema, warmth, or bruit. There were signs of chronic venous insufficiency in both lower extremities. Chest and abdominal examinations were normal, and there were no signs of peripheral arterial disease. Chest X-ray, oxygen saturation, and electrocardiogram findings were unremarkable.

Colour Doppler ultrasonography of the right leg revealed a saccular dilatation measuring 2.3 ? 1.9 ? 2.4 cm in the distal popliteal vein. Spectral analysis showed an altered venous waveform during a cal

title: Iliacus Muscle Hematoma an Uncommon Complication in a Rehabilitation Unit: A Case Report Study
case_summary:
A 35-year-old woman, 30 days post-neurosurgical decompression for a cerebellar hemorrhage and cesarean delivery, was in a neurorehabilitation unit. She was on enoxaparin prophylaxis, antihypertensive therapy, anti-seizure drugs, and proton-pump inhibitors. After emerging from a minimally conscious state, she participated in passive and then active mobilization, including therapist-assisted bed-to-wheelchair transfers.

During one assisted transfer, she experienced acute severe right leg pain radiating to the mid-thigh, rated 10/10 on the numerical scale with both active and passive movement. Examination showed symmetric legs with normal skin and temperature, preserved peripheral arterial pulses, and reduced reflexes; passive hip extension was possible but elicited intense pain. Tenderness was noted in the right lower abdomen. Vital signs were normal (BP 120/80 mmHg, HR 90 bpm, SpO2 98%, T 36.5? degreesC). Laboratory studies revealed an acute drop in hemoglobin from 12.5 to 10.5 g/dL, p

title: Hyperacute Rejection of a Living Unrelated Kidney Graft
case_summary: A 59-year-old man with end-stage renal disease due to nephrosclerosis and a prior kidney transplant that failed after 10 years (chronic vascular rejection) was evaluated for a second living unrelated kidney transplant. His medical history included hypertension, coronary artery disease, deep venous thrombosis, a reversible ischemic neurologic deficit, and a central venous thrombosis of the right eye; he was maintained on phenprocoumon. Pretransplant testing revealed a panel-reactive antibody level of 34%, a negative T-cell crossmatch, and a positive B-cell crossmatch. He underwent induction with basiliximab and maintenance immunosuppression with tacrolimus, mycophenolate mofetil, and prednisone. The donor was a 53-year-old woman with normal renal anatomy and a calculated creatinine clearance of 80 mL/min. The left kidney (47% function) was procured uneventfully. After 143 minutes of cold ischemia, the graft was anastomosed to the left iliac vessels and urinary tract reconstruction was p

title: Coccidioidomycosis Masquerading as Eosinophilic Ascites
case_summary:
A 27-year-old man from Mexico (BMI 25.3) with a history of constipation presented with 9 days of progressive abdominal pain, distention, and dyspnea. He also reported fatigue, a one-month dry cough, and a 20-pound weight loss. He denied fever, chills, night sweats, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, hemoptysis, or chest pain. He had heavy alcohol use until 4 months prior, with no recent travel, sick contacts, transfusions, allergies, smoking, or illicit drug use.

On examination, his abdomen was distended with tense ascites and diffuse tenderness; there were no stigmata of chronic liver disease or pedal edema. He weighed 125 pounds on presentation; after paracentesis of 4 L, his weight decreased to 115 pounds.

Ascitic fluid analysis revealed 62% eosinophils. Abdominal ultrasound and CT scan showed large-volume ascites and a thickened small-bowel wall without evidence of obstruction, cirrhosis, splenomegaly, or portal/hepatic venous thrombosis. Imaging also showed basilar atelectasis of the l

title: Prolonged Hypoglycemic Encephalopathy: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 61-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension was brought to the emergency department with a decreased level of consciousness of unknown duration. He was last seen awake at 6 p.m. the previous evening after dinner and his usual medications, and was found unconscious at home at 2 a.m. Two recent falls were reported, one two days earlier and another the same morning. There was no history of fever, convulsions, chest pain, cough, rash, decreased urine output, or substance use.

On arrival, he was unresponsive with a snoring airway. Vital signs were: temperature 97  degreesF, blood pressure 140/70 mmHg, pulse rate 68 bpm, respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Neurologic examination revealed a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 7 (E1V2M4); pupils were 2 mm bilaterally with sluggish reaction to light; deep tendon reflexes were hypoactive, and plantar responses were extensor bilaterally.

A point-of-care glucose measurement was 19 mg/dL. After adm

title: Imaging findings of inflammatory pseudotumor-like follicular dendritic cell tumors of the liver: Two case reports and literature review
final_diagnosis: inflammatory pseudotumor-like follicular dendritic cell tumor
case_summary:
A 31-year-old Chinese woman was incidentally found to have multiple liver lesions. One month earlier, chest CT performed after an upper respiratory infection revealed two hypodense solid lesions in segment VII of the liver, measuring 36 ? 31 mm and 21 ? 16 mm. She had a 10-year history of chronic hepatitis B infection without antiviral therapy. She was asymptomatic, and vital signs and abdominal examination were normal.

Laboratory studies showed a low neutrophil count (1.53 ? 10^9/L; 40.6%), with normal white blood cell, red blood cell, platelet counts, hemoglobin, liver function tests, and tumor markers including AFP, CEA, CA 125, and CA 19-9. Hepatitis B surface antigen, core antibody, and e antibody were positive.

Unenhanced ultrasound demonstrated two heterogeneous hypoechoic lesions without internal blood flow in the same region. Triple-phase contrast-enhanced CT showed both tumors to be well-defined, hypodense on noncontrast images, with heterogeneous sustained hypoenhancement

title: Thrombosed vascular malformation within the flexor tendon sheath of the index finger in the palm
journal: Case Reports in Plastic Surgery & Hand Surgery
case_summary:
A 48-year-old woman presented with a 6-month history of a swelling in the palm of her left hand at the level of the index-finger ray. She denied any history of trauma, pain, neurological symptoms, triggering phenomena, or vascular complaints. Her medical history was otherwise unremarkable. 

On examination, there was a 3.5 ? 3.0 cm, soft, non-tender, subcutaneous mass in the mid-palm, without overlying skin changes. Distal sensation and perfusion were normal, and there were no palpable thrills or bruits. 

Ultrasound imaging demonstrated a well-circumscribed lesion enveloping both flexor tendons in the palm. Magnetic resonance imaging was not performed because of a documented allergy to gadolinium contrast.

title: Primary Hepatic Lymphoma Presenting as an Acute Abdomen in a Young Female Patient: A Case Report and Literature Review
final_diagnosis: Primary hepatic diffuse large B?cell lymphoma
case_summary:
A 19?year?old South Sudanese woman presented with right upper?quadrant abdominal pain and nonbloody vomiting for several days. She denied fever, night sweats, weight loss, jaundice, or bleeding. She had no significant medical or family history and did not smoke or drink. On examination she was pale, afebrile, and hemodynamically stable. There was tender hepatomegaly but no palpable lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 9?g/dL, normal white?cell and platelet counts, normal electrolytes, renal function, liver enzymes, bilirubin, albumin, and coagulation studies. Serologic testing was positive for hepatitis B virus (confirmed by PCR) and negative for hepatitis C and HIV. Alpha?fetoprotein and carcinoembryonic antigen levels were within normal limits. An abdominal ultrasound demonstrated a 10???13?cm hypoechoic mass in the left hepatic lobe and mild ascites; cytologic analysis of ascitic fluid was negative for malignant cells. A contrast-enhanced CT scan of

title:
A probable case of catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome: Should
high-dose steroids be given in the setting of polymicrobial
sepsis?
case_summary:
A 59-year-old woman with recently diagnosed ulcerative colitis presented with 3 days of bloody diarrhea, diffuse abdominal pain, anorexia, and weight loss. She was afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and on examination was cachectic with diffuse abdominal tenderness but no peritoneal signs. Initial laboratories showed: WBC 15.9???10^3/uL, hemoglobin 11.2?g/dL, platelets 128???10^3/uL, creatinine 0.6?mg/dL, INR 1.3, PT 16.6?s, PTT 42.8?s, lactate 2.11?mmol/L, ESR 20?mm/h, CRP 6.37?mg/dL, ferritin 93?ng/mL. She was treated for an ulcerative colitis flare with IV methylprednisolone and oral mesalamine.  
Contrast CT of the abdomen revealed a new occlusive main portal vein thrombosis extending into right and left branches with hepatic hypoperfusion and moderate-to-severe pancolitis. Unfractionated heparin was initiated. Shortly thereafter, she developed large-volume hematochezia, hypotension, and was transferred to the ICU. Repeat labs showed WBC 51???10^3/uL, hemoglobin 9.0?g/dL, PTT >150?s

title: Primary Angiosarcoma of the Kidney: Case Report and Comprehensive Literature Review
case_summary:
A 56-year-old man presented with left flank pain.  
Abdominal ultrasound revealed an 8 ? 10 cm round mass in the mid-lower pole of the left kidney.  
Contrast-enhanced CT showed an 11 cm lower-pole renal mass with poor vascularization, a large central collapsed area, infiltration of perirenal fat and the perirenal fascia, and several small (<1 cm) mesenteric lymph nodes.  
In the arterial phase, the lesion exhibited rapid peripheral enhancement with wash-out.  
There was no evidence of involvement of the renal vascular pedicle or distant metastases on CT and bone scan.  
The patient had one episode of gross hematuria after imaging.  
To reduce intraoperative bleeding, he underwent preoperative left renal artery embolization.  
He then underwent an open radical nephrectomy with hilar and para-aortic lymph node dissection, removal of perirenal fat, ipsilateral adrenal gland, a segment of ureter, and a portion of psoas muscle.  
Postoperative recovery was uneventful.

title: Atypical Valsalva retinopathy from habitual ear popping
journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case_summary:
A 57-year-old woman was referred for new retinal hemorrhages in the left eye. One week earlier, she experienced acute onset of "spots" in her left eye, similar to transient episodes she had for years that typically resolved within hours; this episode persisted, prompting evaluation in the emergency department. Neuroimaging, carotid Doppler ultrasound, and cardiac echocardiography were unremarkable. An optometrist diagnosed central retinal vein occlusion and referred her for retinal evaluation.

Her medical history included well-controlled hypertension and hyperlipidemia; she had no diabetes, smoking history, obesity, or other cardiovascular disease. On examination, visual acuity was 20/20 bilaterally; pupillary responses, intraocular pressures, extraocular motility, and anterior segment examination were all normal.

Dilated fundus examination of the left eye revealed multiple flame-shaped and round hemorrhages in the posterior pole. Late-phase fluorescein angiography demonstrated block

title: Thyroid Carcinoma Showing Thymic-Like Differentiation Causing Fracture of the Trachea
case_summary: A 76-year-old man with a history of COPD and active smoking presented with a 20-day history of rapidly worsening dyspnea and severe cough, associated with dysphagia and cervical pain. Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (19.27 K/uL), an elevated D-dimer level (2994 ug/L), and otherwise normal chemistries; tumor markers were within normal limits. Chest radiography demonstrated an imprint on the right side of the trachea. Thyroid ultrasound revealed a large, heterogeneous gland with a 4.76 ? 4.01 cm calcified mass in the right upper pole, showing high vascular perfusion. Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck and thorax showed a heterogeneous, irregularly bordered lesion extending from the sternoclavicular joints to the upper poles of the thyroid, surrounding the trachea anterolaterally (displacing it to the left) and displacing the right common carotid artery, without lymphadenopathy. Bronchoscopy revealed a fracture of the first tracheal ring but no tumor invasion; bronchial lavage cytolo

title: Coombs?negative hemolytic anemia in a male with COVID?19
case_summary:
A 51-year-old African-American man presented with a six-day history of fever, chills, myalgia, progressive shortness of breath, and one day of dark brown urine. He had no significant past medical or family history and took only over-the-counter vitamin supplements.  
On arrival: temperature 99 degreesF, blood pressure 135/75 mm Hg, heart rate 82 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 85% on room air, which normalized with 100% FiO? via a non-rebreather mask. Physical examination was unremarkable.  
Initial laboratory studies showed:  
? WBC 14.3 ? 10?/ul (83% neutrophils, 7% lymphocytes)  
? Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dl; hematocrit 36.4%; MCV 89.4 fl; platelets 200 ? 10?/ul; nucleated RBCs 1.7%  
? Total bilirubin 3.8 mg/dl (direct 0.3 mg/dl), haptoglobin < 8 mg/dl  
? AST 92 U/L; lipase 1066 U/L; CK 480 U/L  
? PT 10.2 s; INR 0.96; LDH 997 U/L  
? Ferritin 3527 ng/ml; D-dimer 3.94 mg/L FEU  
? SARS-CoV-2 PCR positive; influenza A/B PCR and urinary antigens for Legio

title: A Case of Turner Syndrome with Multiple Embolic Infarcts
case_summary: A 28-year-old woman with Turner syndrome (karyotype 45,X/46,X,+mar), diagnosed at age 16 for amenorrhea, was referred after abnormal brain MRI. She denied alcohol, tobacco, or drug use and had no family history of neurologic disease. On arrival, her temperature was normal, blood pressure 180/130 mm Hg, and heart rate 89 bpm. She was obese (BMI 27.3 kg/m2). Neurologic examination showed no focal deficits. She had had sudden-onset severe headache with a recorded blood pressure of 240/140 mm Hg. Noncontrast head CT was unremarkable. Diffusion-weighted MRI demonstrated restricted diffusion in multiple lesions involving different vascular territories. Cerebral MRA and carotid ultrasound revealed no extracranial or intracranial arterial stenosis. Chest radiograph and ECG were normal. Laboratory tests showed a fasting glucose of 219 mg/dL, hemoglobin A1C 11.0%, and LDL cholesterol 189 mg/dL.

title: Development of unilateral renal peripelvic lymphangiectasia after renal vein thrombosis
case_summary:
A 37-year-old woman presented with severe bilateral flank pain for 2 weeks. Her history was notable for systemic lupus erythematosus, hypertension, and membranous nephropathy complicated by left and right renal vein thromboses 9 and 5 months earlier. On arrival she was hemodynamically stable but hypertensive (167/85 mm Hg). Physical examination revealed left costovertebral-angle tenderness without other abnormalities. Laboratory evaluation showed a serum creatinine of 0.48 mg/dL, proteinuria, and an otherwise unremarkable renal panel. A comprehensive autoimmune workup (anti-dsDNA, ANCA, antihistone, antiphospholipid antibodies, CRP, ESR, complement) was negative.

An outside single-venous-phase contrast-enhanced CT scan was interpreted as right hydronephrosis. A multiphasic CT performed on admission demonstrated a fluid-density structure encasing the calyces on the excretory (delayed) phase, with normal-caliber calyces containing excreted contrast. Comparison with imaging one month ear

title: Post traumatic dural sinus thrombosis following epidural hematoma: Literature review and case report
case_summary: A 50-year-old man was hospitalized after a car accident in which he sustained a concussive head trauma. He had no significant past medical history. On arrival, he reported a mild headache but had no focal neurologic deficits; vital signs were stable. A noncontrast brain CT scan showed a combined right supra-subtentorial epidural hematoma. Given the location adjacent to the transverse sinus, a brain MRI with arterial and venous reconstruction was obtained. The MRI confirmed the supra- and subtentorial hematoma and revealed no appreciable signal of blood flow in the right transverse sinus, suggesting sinus occlusion.

title: Extensive migration of injected free liquid silicone for breast augmentation with related major complications
case_summary:
A 46-year-old woman presented to her general practitioner with a painless left supraclavicular mass that she had noticed several weeks earlier. On examination, there was a soft, oblong mass measuring approximately 11 cm in the left supraclavicular fossa. The left breast appeared slightly smaller than the right, but no other masses or abnormalities were detected. 

An ultrasound examination of the neck demonstrated a hypoechoic, approximately 11-cm oblong lesion in the left supraclavicular fossa. A noncontrast CT scan of the neck was then performed at the patient's request; interpretation was limited by the lack of intravenous contrast, and the preliminary differential diagnosis from the ultrasound included lymphangioma or cystic hygroma.

The patient was referred to interventional radiology for further evaluation. On targeted questioning, she reported that she had undergone an elective mastopexy in Russia three years earlier. Unsatisfied with the cosmetic result, she subsequently recei

title: Differential diagnosis for a ring-enhancing lesion on CT/MRI brain: retained cotton ball pledget
case_summary:
A 58-year-old man initially presented in 2003 with headache, vomiting, collapse, and new left-sided hemiparesis. CT of the head showed a right sylvian fissure and right frontoparietal subdural haematoma with mass effect and midline shift. He underwent burr-hole evacuation, coil embolization of a ruptured right middle cerebral artery aneurysm, and a subsequent craniotomy for evacuation of a further subdural haematoma. Postoperatively, his left-sided hemiparesis persisted and he developed dysaesthetic pain in his left upper and lower limbs. A postoperative CT scan demonstrated minimal residual clot, no hydrocephalus, and a marked reduction in midline shift. His ongoing deficits were attributed to ischaemic injury of deep grey structures.

At follow-up, he had headaches, poor short-term memory, decreased sensation on the left side, persistent hemiparesis, and a left upper motor neuron facial palsy. In 2005, a noncontrast CT scan showed a small, hyperdense cystic abnormality in the right e

title: Multiple hepatic infarctions secondary to diabetic ketoacidosis: A case report
case_summary: A 57-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus presented with 2 days of diffuse abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. He was tachycardic and hypotensive on arrival, and developed monomorphic ventricular tachycardia requiring synchronized cardioversion. Laboratory studies showed ketonuria, hyperglycemia (470 mg/dL), metabolic acidosis (pH 7.27, bicarbonate 15 mEq/L), AST 2356 U/L, ALT 2438 U/L, thrombocytopenia (9380 platelets/uL), and rising total bilirubin (1.59 mg/dL). Viral hepatitis and flavivirus serologies were negative. Physical examination revealed mild right upper-quadrant tenderness without fever; other vital signs were stable. On day 2, abdominal ultrasound with Doppler showed multiple heterogeneous nodular lesions in both hepatic lobes without portal vein or hepatic artery thrombosis. Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated multiple hypoattenuating, heterogeneous liver lesions with slight peripheral enhancement and a wedge-shaped transition between affected and

title: Segmental arterial mediolysis with 5 splenic artery aneurysms. A rare finding of a rare disease: Case report and literature review
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 54-year-old multiparous Kuwaiti woman (gravida 8, para 8) with diabetes mellitus, hypothyroidism, and nephrolithiasis was referred after an incidental finding on imaging. She had left-sided abdominal pain attributed to renal stones, and a contrast-enhanced CT scan revealed a 2.5 ? 2.5 cm splenic artery aneurysm. She had no relevant surgical history or family history of vasculopathy. Physical examination was unremarkable.

Diagnostic angiography was performed with possible therapeutic intent; it demonstrated a tortuous splenic artery with five saccular aneurysms. The largest, measuring 2.5 cm, was located in the proximal splenic artery near the celiac axis, and the smallest lay in the splenic hilum embedded in the parenchyma. No other abdominal vascular abnormalities were identified.

In multidisciplinary review, endovascular management was deemed technically unfeasible because of extensive proximal and distal vessel involvement. The patient received pneumococcal and meningococcal vac

title: Radiological evaluation of extrahepatic and intrahepatic portal vein aneurysms: A report of two cases
case_summary:
A 60-year-old woman presented with vague epigastric pain and dyspepsia of one week's duration. She had no history of liver disease, pancreatitis, trauma, or prior abdominal surgery. Her abdomen was soft and nontender, and there were no stigmata of portal hypertension. She had well-controlled diabetes treated with oral hypoglycemic agents. Complete blood count and liver and pancreatic function tests were normal.

Abdominal ultrasound revealed a 2.5 cm anechoic lesion in direct continuity with the main portal vein. Color Doppler demonstrated a monophasic, nonpulsatile venous flow pattern within the lesion, and there was no evidence of intraluminal thrombus. Other abdominal organs appeared normal.

To further evaluate her persistent pain and to characterize the lesion, a triple-phase CT scan of the abdomen was obtained. In the portal venous phase, there was a focal, well-defined, smoothly marginated, homogenously enhancing mass near the hepatic hilum in direct continuity with the portal v

title: Late-Onset Metastatic Malignant Spindle Cell Tumour Presenting with Massive Intra-Abdominal Haemorrhage
case_summary: A 40-year-old woman with a history of curative resection of a malignant spindle cell tumour from the right thigh 5 years earlier (clear margins, no adjuvant therapy, no recurrence) presented with sudden onset of severe generalized abdominal pain and collapse. She had no external bleeding, no trauma history, and was otherwise well. On arrival, she was in class III hemorrhagic shock (pulse 125 beats/min, blood pressure 90/60 mmHg), with a distended, diffusely tender abdomen. She was resuscitated with crystalloids and blood. Serum ?-Human Chorionic Gonadotropin was negative. Hemoglobin was 5 g/dL; other blood counts and clotting studies were normal. Abdominal ultrasound showed a moderate amount of free fluid and a mass near the proximal pancreas; there was no abdominal aortic aneurysm and other solid organs appeared normal. A CT scan was not obtained due to hemodynamic instability. An emergency midline laparotomy revealed 4.5 L of hemoperitoneum, a linear tear in the gastrocolic omentum w

title: Progressive edemas and generalized telangiectasia: A presentation of Intravascular B?cell Lymphoma
case_summary:
A 91-year-old woman was admitted with a 2-month history of progressive leg, abdominal, and breast edema, along with asthenia and declining ability to perform daily activities. Her medical history included Parkinson disease, mild cognitive impairment, polymyalgia rheumatica, and diverticulitis. Home medications were omeprazole, levodopa/carbidopa, furosemide, quetiapine, rivastigmine, and allopurinol. She was partly dependent (Barthel index 60/100) and required assistance to walk.

On examination, there was severe pitting edema of the legs, abdomen, and breasts, and telangiectatic vascular lesions on the chest, back, and abdomen. Heart rate and rhythm were regular; lung auscultation was normal; there were no signs of ascites.

Laboratory studies showed a normocytic anemia (RBC count 3.00?10^12/L, hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, MCV 92 fL), elevated lactate dehydrogenase (969 U/L), elevated ferritin (892 ng/mL), and NT-proBNP of 1800 pg/mL. Total serum protein was 6.2 g/dL with normal albumin. Tumo

title: Spontaneous regression of gastric gastrinoma after resection of metastases to the lesser omentum: A case report and review of literature
case_summary:
A 37-year-old man collapsed outdoors from sudden cardiopulmonary arrest and was resuscitated with bystander CPR and two external defibrillator shocks. His only past gastrointestinal history was gastric mucosal erosions diagnosed 9 years earlier. He reported chronic abdominal pain and intermittent reflux despite long-term proton-pump inhibitor therapy. He denied alcohol or tobacco use and had no known allergies.

On arrival, his temperature was 36.2  degreesC, blood pressure 139/111 mmHg, heart rate 130 beats/min, respiratory rate 32 breaths/min, and Glasgow Coma Scale score was E4V1M4; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable. Laboratory evaluation showed leukocytosis, mild elevations of liver enzymes and creatinine, lactic acidosis (arterial pH 7.118, lactate 8.1 mmol/L), and a D-dimer >100 ug/mL. 

Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest and abdomen revealed an incidental 8 cm mass in the lesser omentum composed of three fused solid tumors, one with a nonenhancing low-density area

title: Spontaneous renal tract rupture from obstructing vesico?ureteric junction calculus
case_summary:
A 77-year-old man presented with a 6-day history of progressive left-sided abdominal pain. The pain began suddenly, was constant, rated 8/10 on a numerical scale, radiated to the back, and had no clear exacerbating or relieving factors, worsening markedly over the preceding 3 days. He was independent with a WHO performance status of 0. His medical history included hypertension, a prior pulmonary embolism, chronic work-related back pain, and a suprapubic catheter placed for multiple urethral strictures after green light prostatectomy. He initially attributed his new pain to his chronic back condition.

On examination, he appeared comfortable at rest. Blood pressure was 220/90 mmHg in the left arm and 224/89 mmHg in the right arm. A suprapubic catheter was in situ without signs of local infection. The abdomen was soft but tender in the suprapubic region; there was no costovertebral angle tenderness, and bowel sounds were normal.

Laboratory studies, including complete blood count, renal

title: COVID?associated non?vasculitic thrombotic retiform purpura of the face and extremities: A case report
case_summary:
A 55-year-old woman presented with 6 days of worsening cough, shortness of breath, and chest pain with coughing. She also reported myalgias, arthralgias, sore throat, anosmia, and dysgeusia. One day before presentation, she began to develop painful, tender (but nonpruritic) skin changes on her face and extremities; she denied recent exposure to cold or damp weather. She had hypertension, uncontrolled type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hypercholesterolemia. She was fully vaccinated against COVID-19 6 months earlier.

In the emergency department, her oxygen saturation was 90% on room air, respiratory rate 20/min, blood pressure 140/66 mm Hg, and she was afebrile. She appeared nontoxic. Lung examination was clear. Skin examination showed numerous nonpalpable purpuric macules over the glabella, nose, cheeks, dorsal forearms, and shins, with confluent purpuric plaques on distal fingers and toes. Lesions were nonscaly, without erosion or ulceration. Pulses were 2+ and capillary refill <3 s.

Naso

title: Extended spectrum beta lactamase producing Escherichia coli eustachian valve infective endocarditis
case_summary:
A 47-year-old woman with hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, seizures, and end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis presented from her dialysis session with bleeding from her right upper-arm arteriovenous graft. On arrival, she was febrile to 103.0  degreesF, hypotensive (74/45 mm Hg), tachycardic (heart rate in the 120s beats/min), tachypneic (respiratory rate 23 breaths/min), and had altered mental status (oriented only to person). She was in septic shock, requiring a norepinephrine infusion, and received vancomycin and cefepime. An arterial catheter and two peripheral IV lines were placed, and she was admitted to the ICU.

In the ICU, she underwent hemodialysis, continued on vancomycin and cefepime, received levetiracetam for seizure prophylaxis, insulin for glycemic control, and up to 4 L/min of oxygen by nasal cannula. Vascular surgery, neurology, nephrology, and infectious disease were consulted. Ultrasound of the arteriovenous graft showed no abscess. Over the

title: Magnetic resonance and ultrasound imaging of a patient with carotydinia
case_summary: A 47-year-old woman presented with a 6-month history of a left-sided neck mass and ipsilateral headache. She denied fever, myalgia, sore throat, or head and neck trauma. Neurological and general physical examinations were unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 18 mm/h and a C-reactive protein level of 0.2 mg/dL (both within normal limits). Carotid ultrasonography revealed a hypoechoic soft?tissue mass encircling the upper half of the left common carotid artery, with mild luminal narrowing but without accelerated blood flow. Magnetic resonance imaging of the head and neck demonstrated a circumferential soft?tissue halo around the left common carotid artery and bulb from D1 to just below the bifurcation at C5, measuring up to 12 mm in thickness anteriorly, without luminal distortion or an intimal flap. The perivascular tissue was hypointense on T1- and T2-weighted sequences and showed homogeneous enhancement on fat-saturated postcontrast T1-weighted

title: Endometrial Stromal Sarcoma Presented as an Incidental Lung Mass with Multiple Pulmonary Nodules
case_summary:
A 42-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of an incidentally discovered lung mass and multiple pulmonary nodules on chest radiography performed during a routine medical inspection one month earlier. She had undergone a myomectomy seven years ago for a benign uterine leiomyoma. She was asymptomatic, and physical examination was unremarkable. Chest radiography showed a right perihilar mass with tiny peripheral nodules in the right mid-lung field. Chest CT revealed a 4.5-cm well-demarcated right perihilar mass and multiple variably sized peripheral nodules in both lungs.

Laboratory studies, including complete blood count and blood biochemistry, were within normal limits. Tumor markers (CEA, SCC antigen, alpha-fetoprotein, and ?-hCG) were also normal. The imaging features suggested either a primary lung malignancy with intrapulmonary metastases or metastatic disease from an extrathoracic origin.

Given her history of uterine surgery, a gynecologic evaluation was performed. Pelvic ex

title: Lipofibromatous hamartoma of a digital branch of the median nerve: A case report and review of literature
case_summary:
An 18-year-old woman fell from a horse, sustaining forced traction of her left middle finger. Over 4 months, she reported insidious burning pain and numbness over the dorsal proximal interphalangeal joint of that finger, worsening with direct pressure. She denied fever or weight loss.

On examination, there was a 1 ? 1 cm diffuse, mobile mass on the ulnar-dorsal aspect of the left middle proximal phalanx. There was pain on palpation and a positive Tinel's sign at that site. Range of motion of the finger joints was limited to mild flexion; no skin changes, lymphadenopathy, or paresthesias were noted. Sensation was intact.

Plain radiographs of the middle finger revealed a 6.5 ? 5 mm circular radiolucent area with thinning of the posterior cortex of the proximal phalanx. Ultrasound showed vascular trajectories with ectasic venous flow communicating with an arterial branch. MRI without gadolinium demonstrated a 36 ? 5 ? 11 mm lesion on the dorsal proximal phalanx displacing the extensor t

title: Mycobacterium intracellulare Infection Mimicking Progression of Scleroderma
case_summary:
A 41-year-old woman with a history of myalgias, elevated C-reactive protein, and a prior steroid-responsive necrotic ankle wound presented with thickened, painful fingers, dyspnea, and arthritis of the knees. She also had a painful, red, dorsal swelling behind her right knee. Deep venous thrombosis was excluded. High-resolution CT of the lungs showed subpleural reticulation predominantly in the lower lobes, discrete ground-glass changes, and traction bronchiectasis. Pulmonary function testing revealed a forced expiratory volume in 1 second of 2.5 L (77% predicted), total lung capacity of 4.7 L (82% predicted), and transfer factor for carbon monoxide of 93% predicted. Bronchoalveolar lavage and transbronchial biopsies showed no fungal infection. Serologic tests demonstrated a nuclear speckled ANA pattern and high-titer anti-SSA antibodies. Laboratory studies revealed eosinophilia (0.52 ? 10^9/L) and elevated IgG (33 g/L).

Her right leg skin elements progressed in thickness and tightnes

title: A case report of staged testicular infarction
case_summary:
A 60-year-old man with a history of atrial fibrillation and prior cerebral infarction presented with 38 hours of progressive left testicular pain without trauma. Physical examination findings were not detailed in the report. Emergency scrotal ultrasound was performed:

? 2D gray-scale ultrasonography showed a well-defined, heterogeneous hypoechoic area measuring approximately 16 ? 11 ? 10 mm within the left testis; the surrounding parenchyma appeared homogeneous.  
? Color Doppler flow imaging and color Doppler energy detected no blood flow or energy signal within the lesion, with normal signals in the remainder of the testis.  
? Contrast-enhanced ultrasound revealed a 20 ? 15 mm patchy area of no enhancement in the left testicular parenchyma during the arterial, venous, and delayed phases; contrast filled the surrounding tissue starting at 45 seconds, peaked at 60 seconds, and faded by 80 seconds.  
? Quantitative CEUS parameters comparing the lesion to adjacent normal tissue showed

title: Duplication of the inferior vena cava and thrombosis: A rare case
journal: Journal of Research in Medical Sciences : The Official Journal of Isfahan University of Medical Sciences
case_summary: An 18-year-old woman presented with a 3-day history of right leg pain and swelling extending to the inguinal area, accompanied by dyspnea and respiratory distress. On examination, she was febrile and ill-appearing, with pitting edema of the right leg and a marked difference in circumference between the two legs. Later the same day, she developed sudden dyspnea and severe right-sided chest pain; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable. She denied any history of deep-vein thrombosis, clotting disorders, recurrent miscarriage, peripheral vascular disease, nonhealing extremity ulcers, cerebrovascular accident, or use of oral contraceptive pills, and she had no other predisposing factors for venous thrombosis. Laboratory studies showed mild normochromic, normocytic anemia; an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 75 mm/h (reference <25); and a C-reactive protein level of 16 mg/L (reference <8). Renal, electrolyte, liver, and urine tests were normal. Color duplex ultrasonography demons

title: "Recurrent Papillary Necrosis and Nephrocalcinosis Induced by Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs for Gouty Arthritis Associated with Congenital Chloride-Losing Diarrhea: A Major Risk for Kidney Loss"
case_summary:
A 30-year-old man presented with progressive nausea and vomiting over several weeks. He reported a history of watery diarrhea since the age of 6 months; at that time, investigations showed a stool chloride concentration >90 mmol/L, low serum and urinary chloride and potassium levels, metabolic alkalosis, and elevated serum uric acid. He had been managed with a high-salt diet and oral potassium and calcium supplements. Twelve years earlier, he developed recurrent gouty arthritis treated with multiple NSAIDs.

On examination, he was alert and oriented, with a blood pressure of 180/110 mmHg, afebrile, and a body weight of 104 kg. There was no jugular venous distension, edema, or abnormal findings on systemic examination, including the lungs.

Laboratory results revealed:  
? Hemoglobin 104 g/L, normal MCV, transferrin saturation, and vitamin B12  
? White-cell and platelet counts within normal limits  
? Serum urea 40 ?mol/L, creatinine 650 ?mol/L  
? Sodium 129 mmol/L, chloride 93 mmol/L

title: Fatal primary amoebic meningoencephalitis in a Norwegian tourist returning from Thailand
case_summary:
A 71-year-old woman was admitted to a local hospital in Oslo hours after returning from a four-week holiday in Thailand. She had a two-day history of fatigue and developed rapid onset of nausea, vomiting, fever, and exhaustion. On examination, she was febrile (39.5? degreesC) with a respiratory rate of 26 breaths per minute. Laboratory tests showed a leukocyte count of 12?10^9 cells/L and a C-reactive protein level of 9 mg/L. Intravenous ampicillin and gentamicin were started for presumed urosepsis, but later that day she became confused, developed speech disturbances, and was noted to have nuchal rigidity.

She was transferred to the tertiary care center and received 4?g of ceftriaxone. On arrival, she had obvious nuchal rigidity, incoherent speech, motor agitation, and confusion. Her Glasgow Coma Scale score was initially 12, dropping to 9, at which point she was intubated. A cerebral CT scan with angiography showed no acute ischemia, hemorrhage, or vascular occlusion. 

A lumbar pun

title: Pediatric Mesenchymal Hamartoma of Liver: A Case Report with Histomorphological Differential Diagnosis and Review of Literature
case_summary:
A three-year-old boy, born full term with normal development, presented with an eight-month history of a gradually enlarging, painless abdominal lump and distention. He also had a one-month history of low-grade fever and a diffuse rash. There was no history of vomiting, jaundice, or significant past medical or family history.

On examination, a large, non-tender abdominal mass was palpable in the right hypochondrium, epigastrium, and periumbilical region, crossing the midline. There was no splenomegaly or other stigmata of chronic liver disease.

Laboratory studies showed a normal complete blood count, normal liver and renal function tests, and a normal serum alpha-fetoprotein level. Viral markers for hepatitis B and C and an echinococcal antigen test were negative.

Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated an avascular, hypoechoic liver mass with internal septations. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed a 10.0 ? 5.9 cm, well-defined, multiloculated, variably sized, peripherally enhan

title: Septic Bleeding of the Common Carotid Artery Following Total Thyroidectomy: An Atypical Complication
final_diagnosis: Septic rupture of the common carotid artery
case_summary: A 16-year-old girl underwent surgical excision of a midline cervical mass suspected to be a median cervical or thyroglossal duct cyst. Family history was unremarkable. Histopathologic examination unexpectedly revealed papillary thyroid carcinoma. Preoperative magnetic resonance imaging, radioisotope scanning of the thyroid gland, and clinical examination showed no evidence of residual thyroid tissue, lymphadenopathy, or distant metastases. Four weeks later, she underwent a total thyroidectomy with systematic dissection of the cervicocentral lymph node compartment under magnification. Both recurrent laryngeal nerves and all four parathyroid glands were identified; one parathyroid gland was autotransplanted into the left sternocleidomastoid muscle. On postoperative day 1, she developed symptomatic hypocalcemia requiring intravenous calcium. On postoperative day 4, she developed local erythema at the Kocher incision, fever, and elevated inflammatory markers; opening of the wound yielded a

title: Extensive abdominal wall ulceration as a late manifestation of antiphospholipid syndrome: a case report
case_summary:
A 58-year-old woman with a 27-year history of systemic lupus erythematosus and secondary antiphospholipid syndrome presented with painful, non-healing ulcers. Her initial lupus manifestations included recurrent pneumonitis, malar rash, and biopsy-proven lupus nephritis. She had recurrent deep venous thromboses at diagnosis, was positive for lupus anticoagulant on two occasions 3 months apart, and was anticoagulated with warfarin targeting an INR of 2.0-3.0. Additional thrombophilia testing (factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene mutation, protein C/S, antithrombin III) was negative.

Her comorbidities included obesity, obstructive sleep apnea, hypertension, diet-controlled type 2 diabetes, stage 3B chronic kidney disease, and previous endometrial cancer in remission. Medications were warfarin, hydroxychloroquine, atenolol, and analgesics.

Two months before admission, she noted small bruises at sites of abdominal ultrasound probe pressure, which progressed to ulcerative lesions on the anter

title: Primary Chemotherapy in a 47-Year-Old Patient with Giant Ulcerative and Necrotizing Nonseminomatous Testicular Germ Cell Tumor
case_summary:
A 47-year-old man was transferred with an ulcerating left testicular mass that he had noted growing over 3 years. In the days before admission, he developed scrotal ulcerations, fever, and 3 weeks of left leg swelling, redness, and pain. He had no prior medical history or medications. 

Initial CT showed an approximately 22 ? 18 cm ulcerating left testicular mass with bilateral para-aortic, iliac, and inguinal lymphadenopathy, and extensive deep venous thrombosis of the left leg with subcutaneous and perifascial edema; there were no parenchymal distant metastases. He received antibiotics and tinzaparin.
