During hospitalization, he developed hemorrhagic shock from tumor bleeding and bilateral acute pulmonary embolism with right heart strain; anticoagulation was managed despite bleeding. MRI of the head and SPECT bone scan excluded brain and bone metastases.

Serum tumor markers were markedly elevated: lactate dehydrogenase, beta-human chorionic gonadotropin, and alpha-fetoprotein. Hormon

title: Severe Vaginal Bleeding in a Case of Renal Cell Carcinoma
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
case_summary:
A 60-year-old woman presented with two months of left abdominal pain radiating to the midline, moderate in intensity, without aggravating or alleviating factors. She also reported intermittent postmenopausal vaginal bleeding, dysuria, and generalized weakness, but denied fever, night sweats, or headache. Her surgical history included right radical nephrectomy for renal cell carcinoma 6 years ago, a cesarean delivery, and tubal ligation. Six months earlier she had been diagnosed with a plasma cell neoplasm, and one month earlier her nephrologist noted anemia. 

An outpatient ultrasound had shown an enlarged left kidney with a multifocal mass thought to be neoplastic. A follow-up CT scan demonstrated near?complete replacement of the left kidney by an enhancing mass with internal neovascularity. The patient did not complete a scheduled renal biopsy and presented three weeks later to the emergency department. 

On admission, hemoglobin was 6.9 g/dL with a mean corpuscular volume of 79.2 fL

title: Sclerosing Angiomatoid Nodular Transformation of the Adrenal Gland: A Case Report of a Novel Histopathological Entity
case_summary:
A 54-year-old postmenopausal woman was referred for evaluation of a left adrenal mass discovered incidentally during workup for a spontaneous intracerebral hemorrhage. She had no smoking history and a family history significant for hypertension and ischemic heart disease. In November 2016, she underwent craniotomy for evacuation of a right parietal hematoma; concurrent abdominal ultrasound showed a 15-mm left adrenal lesion. Initial urinary catecholamine testing revealed elevated adrenaline (64 ?g/day; normal 1.7-22.4 ?g/day) with normal noradrenaline and dopamine. Random aldosterone was 9.4 ng/dL (normal 3.7-31.0 ng/dL). She was discharged on ramipril, amlodipine, doxazosin, metoprolol, and lamotrigine.

At endocrinology clinic: BMI 21.2 kg/m2, blood pressure 130/85 mmHg, heart rate 60 bpm, no signs of hypercortisolism. Serum cortisol after 1-mg dexamethasone suppression was 0.9 ?g/dL (normal), adrenal androgens were normal, and tumor markers (CEA, CA19-9, CA125, CA15-3, neuron-specif

title: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis in pregnancy presenting with hemiplegia: A case report
case_summary:
A 22-year-old primigravid woman at 10 weeks' gestation, with no prior medical history, presented to the emergency department with a 7-day history of left-sided headache associated with nausea and nonbloody, nonbilious vomiting. She had had similar episodes previously and responded to paracetamol, metoclopramide, and intravenous fluids. A noncontrast head CT was reportedly normal, and she was discharged with instructions to return if symptoms persisted or worsened.

The next morning, she awoke with decreased sensation and weakness of the left side, blurry vision, and worsening headache, prompting return to the ED. She was alert and oriented; vital signs were stable. Neurologic examination showed motor strength of 1/5 in the left upper and lower extremities, with decreased sensation in the left face and left upper and lower extremities. There were no cerebellar signs, dysarthria, or cranial nerve deficits. A stroke code was activated. 

A repeat noncontrast head CT revealed an abnormally

title: Pseudotumoral portal cavernoma: A rare case that challenges diagnosis
case_summary: A 42-year-old woman presented with a 3-month history of asthenia. She denied fever or lymphadenopathy. On examination, she had hepatosplenomegaly without other abnormalities. Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 16?g/dL, platelets 480 000/uL, AST 55 U/L, ALT 60 U/L, total bilirubin 2 mg/dL, and LDH 300 U/L; serum electrolytes were normal. A bone marrow biopsy revealed a myeloproliferative disorder. An abdominal ultrasound showed hepatosplenomegaly and portal vein thrombosis. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen demonstrated a mass encircling the common bile duct and compressing it, with enhancement only during the portal venous phase; hepatosplenomegaly, a portosystemic shunt, and a hepatic perfusion defect were also noted. Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography revealed a sleeve of tissue around the common bile duct at the hepatic pedicle with intermediate T2 signal, no arterial enhancement, and intense, homogeneous portal-phase enhancement causing compression of the duct.

title: Internal jugular venous aneurysm--A report of two cases with literature review
journal: Journal of Vascular Surgery Cases and Innovative Techniques
case_summary: An 81-year-old man with atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and diabetes presented with a painless, palpable soft-tissue mass in the left submandibular area. On examination, the mass was compressible and non-tender without overlying skin changes. Duple x ultrasound demonstrated a large, blood-containing structure in the left mandibular region with mixed intraluminal arterial and venous flow. CT venography confirmed a saccular dilation of the internal jugular vein with multiple feeding collaterals and noted arterial flow within the mass, raising concern for an arteriovenous fistula. To clarify the vascular connections, internal jugular venography and carotid angiography were performed, and both studies showed no arterial connection to the lesion.

title:
Epithelioid sarcoma with retained INI1 expression as a cause of a
chronic leg ulcer
case_summary:
A 66-year-old man with a history of smoking, arterial hypertension, and hypercholesterolemia underwent an aorto-bifemoral bypass in 2002 for critical right-leg ischemia, at which time a superficial ulcer was noted on the right calf. Over the next 14 years, the ulcer failed to heal and progressively enlarged. In July 2016, angiography for worsening calf pain demonstrated occlusion of the right superficial femoral artery. In October 2016, a right femoro-popliteal bypass was performed, and tissue from the ulcer bed was submitted for histologic examination.

Microscopic examination showed pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia at the ulcer edges and malignant structures at the base composed of spindle-shaped and epithelioid cells, with focal necrosis, secondary vascular thrombosis, and up to 8 mitoses per 10 high-power fields (French Federation of Cancer Centers Sarcoma Group grade 3).

Immunohistochemical studies demonstrated diffuse positivity for cytokeratins (CK AE1-3) and early growth resp

title: Transient ischemic jejunitis due to symptomatic isolated superior mesenteric artery dissection: case report and review of literature
case_summary: A 57-year-old man with no prior medical history or cardiovascular risk factors presented with moderate diffuse abdominal pain, repeated vomiting, and two non-bloody watery stools that began 72 hours earlier after a heavy meal. Vital signs were blood pressure 130/70 mmHg, heart rate 80 bpm, respiratory rate 18/min, and temperature 36.6 degreesC. The abdomen was slightly distended with epigastric and left middle quadrant tenderness without peritonitic signs. Laboratory tests showed an inflammatory syndrome (CRP 11.98 mg/dl, ESR 65 mm/h, fibrinogen 759.1 mg/dl), a white-cell count of 8.1?10^9/L with 92% neutrophils, hemoglobin 14 g/dl, and normal amylase, lipase, lactate, and liver function tests. A plain abdominal radiograph was unremarkable. Abdominal ultrasound revealed a 6.6-cm jejunal segment with a 7.5-mm thickened wall, preserved peristalsis, and adjacent mesenteric lymph nodes. Color duplex sonography showed a normal SMA origin with Vmax 0.86 m/sec, no turbulent flow or dissection

title: A case of pituitary apoplexy masquerading as subarachnoid hemorrhage
case_summary:
A 75-year-old man presented 4 days after the sudden onset of a severe frontal headache. He reported no nausea, vomiting, or photophobia. His medical history included atrial fibrillation, bronchiectasis, lumbar stenosis, previous cerebral infarction, and ischemic heart disease; his medications included aspirin, ramipril, doxazosin, bisoprolol, simvastatin, and topical latanoprost.

On examination, his Glasgow Coma Scale score was 15, temperature 38 degreesC, and blood pressure 135/85 mmHg. He had mild neck stiffness; cardiovascular, respiratory, abdominal, and neurological examinations were otherwise unremarkable. A provisional diagnosis of subarachnoid hemorrhage was made.

A noncontrast CT scan of the brain showed a pituitary macroadenoma but was initially reported as showing no hemorrhage. Lumbar puncture revealed a CSF protein of 1.44 g/L (reference range 0.15-0.45), glucose 4.8 mmol/L (2.2-3.9), no organisms on gram stain, and xanthochromia; based on these findings, SAH was presume

title: Bilateral simultaneous thalamic hematomas - Unusual presentation of intracerebral hemorrhage: A case report
case_summary:
A 52-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with a 2-hour history of progressive somnolence, disorientation, and weakness of the right side. Her medical history was notable for ischemic heart disease and essential hypertension that had been untreated for decades; she had not taken any prescribed antihypertensive or antithrombotic medications for a prolonged period. Family history was remarkable only for hypertension.

On examination, her blood pressure was 200/110 mm Hg. She was somnolent but arousable, disoriented to time and place, and had 4/5 strength in the right upper and lower extremities. No other focal neurologic deficits were noted initially.

An urgent noncontrast cranial CT scan revealed bilateral thalamic hemorrhages with extension into the lateral ventricles and a subarachnoid component over the frontal, parietal, and occipital lobes. Small hemorrhages were also seen in the pons and the fourth ventricle. CT angiography showed no active contrast extravasation

title: Recurrent "transient ischemic attacks" caused by pulmonary arteriovenous malformation
case_summary:
A 39-year-old woman with no prior medical history presented repeatedly with brief episodes of aphasia, dysarthria, and tingling in her right arm. She was a never-smoker, had no known cardiovascular risk factors, and denied epistaxis, hemoptysis, or exertional dyspnea. Physical examination showed no platypnea-orthodeoxia, clubbing, or mucocutaneous telangiectasia. Arterial blood gas on room air revealed a pO2 of 10.6 kPa. Hemoglobin was normal, and there was no polycythemia. An electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm without arrhythmia. Brain MRI demonstrated lesions compatible with old ischemia. She was diagnosed with recurrent transient ischemic attacks.  
Given her age, lack of risk factors, and recurrence of events, a bubble-contrast transesophageal echocardiogram was performed; more than 30 bubbles appeared in the left atrium after six cardiac cycles, indicating a right-to-left shunt. A patent foramen ovale was thought to be the source, and the patient was scheduled for PFO cl

title: Unsuspected Pneumocystis pneumonia in an HIV-seronegative patient with untreated lung cancer: circa case report
case_summary:
A 43-year-old nonsmoking woman presented with a 6-week history of intermittent mild-to-moderate fever, progressive dyspnoea, increasing expectoration of gel-like sputum, and paroxysmal wheezing. She was treated for presumed community-acquired pneumonia with intravenous levofloxacin followed by ceftriaxone, and received intermittent dexamethasone (10-20 mg prn) and aminophylline for dyspnoea and wheezing. Four weeks after symptom onset, she developed orthopnoea, high-grade fever, and copious gel-like phlegm and was transferred for further care.

On examination, she was orthopnoeic and cyanotic with finger clubbing and nonpitting lower-extremity edema. Vital signs: temperature 38.8  degreesC, heart rate 124 beats/min, respiratory rate 32 breaths/min, blood pressure 124/76 mmHg. Fine crackles were heard at both lung bases with resonant wheezing throughout inspiration and expiration. Laboratory studies showed WBC 15.4 ? 10^9/L with 86% neutrophils; normal RBC and platelets; CEA 13.4 ug/L;

title: Perifoveal exudative vascular anomalous complex (PEVAC) resembling lesion in a patient with multiple myeloma
journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case_summary:
A 56-year-old man with a 12-year history of IgA lambda multiple myeloma, currently on daratumumab and dexamethasone, presented with sudden moderate vision loss in his right eye for one day. He denied diabetes, uncontrolled hypertension, prior radiation, or carotid disease. Recent laboratory evaluation showed an elevated IgA monoclonal peak.  
On examination, best corrected visual acuity was 20/25 in the right eye and 20/400 in the left eye. Anterior segment examination was unremarkable in the right eye; the left eye had a known central corneal scar. No intraocular inflammation was present.  
Fundus examination of the right eye revealed a discrete vascular irregularity in the perifoveal region, seen as a red dot on binocular ophthalmoscopy, without hemorrhages, cotton-wool spots, or arteriovenous changes.  
Fluorescein angiography demonstrated an isolated perifoveal aneurysm with mild late leakage and no evidence of neovascularization. A few peripheral microaneurysms were noted bilatera

title: A Rare Case of Fatal Hemorrhagic Stroke in a Young Female with Early Mixed Connective Tissue Disease
case_summary:
A 43-year-old healthy Caucasian woman presented with 7-10-day recurrent flares of lower extremity tenderness, swelling, and redness over several months. She also reported upper back and neck myalgias and a one-year history of headaches with transient, curtain-like visual disturbances in her right eye that ophthalmology diagnosed as ocular migraines. Initial laboratory testing showed an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 31 mm/H, a positive antinuclear antibody, and elevated ribonucleoprotein antibodies (2.4 AI). Double-stranded DNA, antichromatin, anti-Smith, SM/RNP, and Lyme disease antibodies were negative. She received a presumptive diagnosis of undifferentiated connective tissue disease, was started on prednisone for flares, and was referred to rheumatology.

Two months later, she experienced erythema nodosum of the lower extremities, Raynaud's phenomenon, chest pain, and visual disturbances suggestive of amaurosis fugax. She kept a diary of transient fatigue and painful hand and fi

title: Paroxysmal Sympathetic Hyperactivity Syndrome in the Setting of Fat Emboli Syndrome Secondary to Polytrauma
case_summary:
A 15-year-old girl with no prior medical history was brought to the emergency department after a high-speed front-end motor-vehicle collision. She was a restrained passenger; airbags deployed. On arrival, her Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) was 15, though she had brief loss of consciousness and amnesia. Injuries included a displaced comminuted fracture of the left femur, bilateral tibia/fibula fractures (left side comminuted), multiple pelvic fractures, a small left apical pneumothorax (<5%), a grade 2 liver laceration, two left rib fractures, and scattered ecchymoses. Initial head and cervical-spine CT scans showed no intracranial hemorrhage or edema. The left lower extremity was placed in traction, and she was admitted to the ICU.

Nine hours after arrival, her GCS fell to 7 (eyes open to pain only, extensor posturing). She was intubated for airway protection. Repeat CT angiography of the head, neck, and thorax showed no new hemorrhage, edema, or central pulmonary embolism. Laboratory studi

title: The role of T2*-weighted gradient echo in the diagnosis of tumefactive intrahepatic extramedullary hematopoiesis in myelodysplastic syndrome and diffuse hepatic iron overload: a case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 52-year-old man with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, a 15 pack-year smoking history, moderate alcohol use, and no significant family history presented after a presyncopal episode. He was found to have pancytopenia. Vital signs were normal and the physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory testing showed a normal metabolic profile and urinalysis. Complete blood count confirmed pancytopenia; peripheral smear demonstrated a myeloid left shift with 5% blasts. Bone marrow biopsy revealed erythroid dyspoiesis, left-shifted granulopoiesis, and grade 3+ fibrosis consistent with myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) with fibrosis. Genetic testing showed wild-type JAK2 and MPL, normal karyotype, absence of BCR-ABL1 translocation, and a negative MDS FISH panel. His hemoglobin was 7.8 g/dL, platelet count was 32?10?/L, and absolute neutrophil count was 11.3?10?/L, yielding a high-risk IPSS-R score. He received multiple transfusions and was treated with ruxolitinib. 

Three months later, d

title: Laparoscopic Splenectomy for Splenic Hamartoma: A Case Report
case_summary: A 28-year-old man presented with a several-week history of diarrhea and fever that did not respond to antibiotics. On examination he was afebrile, with normal vital signs. Laboratory studies were unremarkable except for mild transaminase elevations (ALT 65 U/L, AST 46 U/L). Two weeks later, abdominal ultrasonography showed a normal liver, biliary ducts, and pancreas, but an enlarged spleen (15 cm) containing a 70 ? 77 mm hypoechoic, asymptomatic solid mass at its upper pole. Color Doppler imaging revealed blood flow along the periphery of the lesion, with no significant internal signals. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen demonstrated a round splenic lesion that was hypodense relative to normal parenchyma, with mild arterial-phase enhancement and complete washout in the late venous phase; there were no prominent internal vascular structures. Given the difficulty of obtaining a definitive preoperative diagnosis, the high risk of bleeding with biopsy, and the potential for spontaneous r

title: Metronidazole induced encephalopathy: A rare side effect with a common drug
journal: The Indian Journal of Radiology & Imaging
case_summary:
A 45-year-old man with chronic alcohol use presented with 12 days of high-grade fever, right upper-quadrant abdominal pain, and abdominal distension. On examination, he had tender hepatomegaly and ascites. Abdominal ultrasound showed a ruptured liver abscess with ascites, and the abscess was drained. He was started on intravenous metronidazole (800 mg three times daily) and piperacillin-tazobactam (2.25 g four times daily).

By hospital day 12, he had one generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting 30 seconds, regained consciousness within 10 minutes, and was loaded with levetiracetam. Blood glucose and electrolytes were within normal limits. A noncontrast head CT was performed "to rule out new-onset infarct/intracranial hemorrhage or other gross organic pathology" and was unremarkable.

The next day, he had two further seizures, became poorly responsive (Glasgow Coma Scale score, 8/15), and was intubated for airway protection. Given his history of liver abscess and alcohol use, hepatic e

title: Primary extraskeletal intradural Ewing sarcoma with acute hemorrhage: a case report and review of the?literature
case_summary:
A 58-year-old man with no significant past medical history presented with a 2-month history of mild lower back pain that responded to celecoxib. Over that time, he developed bilateral numbness along the back of his legs, worse on the left, consistent with S1 radiculopathy. A lumbosacral CT scan was unremarkable, and symptoms persisted.

Two weeks before admission, his back pain and radiculopathy dramatically worsened, with non-dermatomal numbness and distal lower-limb weakness more pronounced on the left, impacting his daily activities. He also reported constipation and urinary incontinence. On examination, bilateral straight-leg-raise tests were positive; sensory examination revealed hypoesthesia in the S1 dermatomes. Patellar reflexes were present; Achilles reflexes were decreased. Muscle strength (manual muscle test) was 3/5 for left foot dorsiflexion and plantarflexion and 4/5 on the right; proximal muscles were intact. Pre- and post-void ultrasound suggested neurogenic bladder.

M

title: Lipoleiomyoma: a potential mimic of an ovarian dermoid cyst on MRI--a case report
case_summary: A 39-year-old woman (gravida 3, para 2) with no significant medical or surgical history and a normal menstrual cycle presented with isolated pelvic pain. On examination, the uterus was normal in size, and a left latero-uterine mass separate from the uterine corpus was palpated. Serum CA-125 was within normal limits. Pelvic ultrasound showed an oval, well-defined left pelvic mass with regular contours, heterogeneous hyperechoic echostructure, cystic areas, and no vascularity on color Doppler imaging. Contrast-enhanced CT of the pelvis revealed a predominantly fatty mass containing soft-tissue components without calcifications and heterogeneous enhancement, suggesting initially a mature teratoma. Pelvic MRI demonstrated a round suprauterine mass adjacent to the left ovary with a thin wall, containing a fatty component (hyperintense on T1 and T2, suppressed on fat-saturated sequences) and a solid component (T2 hyperintense, T1 hypointense, diffusion hyperintense, with strong postcontrast

title: Joining forces: a complex cardio-obstetrics case report of severe ergometrine-induced vasospasm
case_summary:
A 31-year-old primigravida with an uncomplicated IVF-conceived pregnancy presented in spontaneous full-term labor. Her past history was notable only for a recovered eating disorder and secondary amenorrhea; there was no family history of thrombosis. After an assisted forceps vaginal delivery of a healthy daughter, she developed hemorrhagic shock from a retained placenta. She received 5 L intravenous fluids, six units of packed red cells, two units of fresh frozen plasma, one pool of platelets, 2 g of tranexamic acid, intramuscular ergometrine, and intrauterine carboprost. As she became increasingly unstable (arterial lactate 8 mmol/L, hemoglobin 7.3 g/dL), she was intubated and taken to the operating room.  

In the ICU, she was diagnosed with disseminated intravascular coagulation: platelets 59 ? 10^9/L, fibrinogen 1.3 g/L, prothrombin time 17.2 s, aPTT 117 s, and D-dimer 35 mg/L. Two days later, after extubation, she had difficulty following commands; the stroke team diagnosed speech

title: Stiff person syndrome presenting with sudden onset of shortness of breath and difficulty moving the right arm: a case report
case_summary:
A 27-year-old Hispanic woman presented with sudden-onset shortness of breath and increased difficulty moving her right arm. Over the preceding two months she had noted progressive right-arm weakness with limited strength and range of motion. On the evening before admission, while lying down, she developed acute shortness of breath, worsening right-arm weakness, and substernal chest pressure described as non-radiating, with associated nausea, palpitations, and lightheadedness. She denied headache, loss of consciousness, vomiting, diarrhea, or vertigo. Two months earlier, she had been evaluated for right-arm weakness and dysphasia; brain MRI was unremarkable, and cervical MRI showed only mild canal narrowing; she had been diagnosed with hemiplegic migraine headache. 

On examination, her vital signs were stable, and she was afebrile. She appeared anxious. Cardiac and pulmonary exams were normal. Abdominal palpation revealed a firm abdomen. The right upper extremity was rigid on active an

title: The choroidal structure changes in a case with hypertensive choroidopathy
journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case_summary: A 27-year-old woman at 33 weeks' gestation was diagnosed with hypertensive disorders of pregnancy after presenting with proteinuria and elevated blood pressure (maximum 172/112 mmHg). Despite intravenous nicardipine and labor induction, her hypertension remained uncontrolled and laboratory tests revealed disseminated intravascular coagulation (platelets 24,000/?L; PT-INR 1.44; fibrinogen 65 mg/dL; FDP 15.8 ?g/mL). She underwent an emergency cesarean delivery at 36 weeks. Twelve hours postpartum, she noted changes in color vision and metamorphopsia. On day 5 after delivery, she was treated with oral amlodipine (5 mg) and referred for ophthalmic evaluation. Her best-corrected visual acuity was 1.2 in both eyes, intraocular pressures were 13 mmHg, and she denied meningismus, tinnitus, alopecia, vitiligo, or other systemic symptoms. Slit-lamp and funduscopic examination showed subconjunctival hemorrhage in the right eye but no anterior-chamber or vitreous inflammation; funduscopy revealed

title: CT perfusion: stroke, seizure or both?
case_summary: An independent octogenarian with hypertension, coronary artery disease, and prior Bell's palsy was last seen well at 10 :00 AM but found 'confused' at 12 :40 PM with new speech disturbance, right facial droop, and right?sided weakness. On arrival at 1 :40 PM, his NIH Stroke Scale score was 19, with severe aphasia, impaired command following, visual inattention, near?unintelligible speech, and right hemiparesis. Blood pressure was 155/88 mm Hg. Baseline blood tests and electrolytes were unremarkable; finger?prick glucose was normal. Noncontrast CT of the head showed chronic small?vessel ischemic changes and arterial calcification without hemorrhage or acute infarct. Intravenous alteplase was administered 26 minutes after referral. During infusion, CT angiography revealed no large?vessel occlusion. CT perfusion imaging demonstrated relatively reduced time to peak and time to maximum, and increased relative cerebral blood volume and flow in the left hemisphere compared with the right, fin

title: Transient osteoporosis of the knee in pregnancy: A case report and literature review
case_summary: A 38-year-old woman at 38 weeks' gestation (G3P2) reported an 8-week history of progressively worsening right knee pain localized to the lateral side. She denied trauma, prior joint disorders, or corticosteroid use. Her current pregnancy was otherwise uneventful except for a mild SARS-CoV-2 infection. She became unable to bear weight on the right leg and required a walking aid. On examination, there was tenderness and swelling over the lateral femoral condyle and tibia, with pain-limited range of motion. Anterior cruciate ligament and meniscal tests were negative. Laboratory studies were within normal limits except for an elevated D-dimer level of 1.29 mg/L (reference <0.50). Venous Doppler ultrasonography twice showed no deep-vein thrombosis, and low-molecular-weight heparin was discontinued. Knee ultrasound demonstrated a small intraarticular effusion. After spontaneous vaginal delivery, a knee radiograph revealed patchy demineralization of the lateral femoral condyle and tibia, with

title: Clear cell sarcoma of the kidney in a 62-year-old patient presenting with generalized pruritus
final_diagnosis: clear cell sarcoma of the kidney
case_summary:
A 62-year-old man presented with a 45-day history of generalized pruritus. Initial treatment with antihistamines and emollients provided incomplete relief. He had no history of tobacco or alcohol use and a medical history significant only for hypertension. On examination, vital signs were normal, and the only finding was mild scratch marks on the skin. 
Laboratory studies showed:
- Normal complete blood count, urinalysis, chest radiograph, liver chemistry, and renal and thyroid function tests
- Erythrocyte sedimentation rate: 35 mm/h
Imaging studies revealed:
- Color duplex sonography: a large hypervascular, low-level echo, heterogeneous mass in the right kidney
- Intravenous pyelogram: irregularity of the margins of the right renal calices
- Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis: a heterogeneous mass arising from the upper pole of the right kidney infiltrating the calices
- CT angiography: a tumor thrombus in the right renal vein without extension into the inferior vena cava

title: Intracerebral hemorrhage as initial presentation of metastatic choriocarcinoma: A case report
case_summary:
A 24-year-old woman was admitted with sudden headache and left-sided weakness 6 hours earlier. She had no history of hypertension. Initial head CT showed an irregular right frontal lobe hematoma. Laboratory studies revealed a serum ?-human chorionic gonadotropin (?-HCG) level of 2266 mIU/mL; CA 19-9 was 37.13 U/mL. She received mannitol and supportive care and was discharged.  

Two weeks later, she was transferred for a generalized seizure. On examination, she was alert; pupils were equal and reactive. She had limited leftward gaze bilaterally, symmetric forehead wrinkles, tongue deviation to the left, and left upper and lower limb strength of grade IV-; right limbs were grade V. Obstetric history included a normal delivery 2 years ago, an abortion 1 year ago, an intrauterine device placed 7 months ago, and regular menses for the last 2 months.  

During hospitalization, she had two more seizures and her serum ?-HCG rose from 63 148 to 980 893 mIU/mL. Gynecologic ultrasonography showe

title: Can a breast hematoma lead to hemorrhagic shock in elderly trauma patients with multiple comorbidities and reduced physiological reserve? Examining the risks and management strategies
case_summary: A 65-year-old woman was brought to the emergency department as a full trauma alert after a single-vehicle accident. She was a restrained driver, found ambulatory next to her crashed vehicle. Medical history included juvenile rheumatoid arthritis, intraductal carcinoma and lobular carcinoma in situ of the left breast, and major depressive disorder. On arrival, her airway was intact; breath sounds were slightly diminished over the right anterior chest; radial pulses were strong; extremities were warm and well perfused; and Glasgow Coma Scale score was 15. E-FAST examination showed no free fluid in the abdominal quadrants or pelvis, though cardiac views were unobtainable. Secondary survey revealed a large hematoma and tenderness over the right superolateral chest wall and shoulder; the abdomen and spine were nontender. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with intravenous contrast demonstrated a large right breast hematoma with contrast blush, indicating active hemorrhage, and no acute ab

title: Late-onset pseudoaneurysms of lower limb arteries following late complications of orthopaedic constructs - Two cases and review of literature
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
An 86-year-old woman presented with right thigh pain and a swelling that she first noted one year earlier, with progressive enlargement over the past three months. She denied fever, erythema, or tenderness at the site. On examination, there was a tense, non-pulsatile, non-tender swelling on the medial aspect of the right thigh; distal pulses were intact, and there was no neurological deficit. Seventeen months earlier, she had sustained a right femoral shaft fracture after a fall and underwent dynamic compression plate fixation two days later without immediate complications. She did not attend subsequent follow-up visits.  
Plain radiographs of the femur showed a healed lateral cortex fracture with cortical erosion on the medial side and protrusion of screws through the medial cortex. A duplex ultrasound scan of the thigh revealed a cystic lesion with a "yin-yang" colour-flow pattern and a to-and-fro Doppler waveform at its neck, as well as a large internal thrombus.

title: A case report of confusing meningoencephalocele
case_summary:
A female infant was born at 38 weeks' gestation by uncomplicated vaginal delivery. The Apgar scores were 7 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes, respectively; birthweight was 2540 g (10th percentile). Prenatal history included treated maternal hypothyroidism and daily folic acid supplementation; there was no family history of congenital anomalies.

On initial examination, the infant appeared well. Examination of the skull revealed a 3?3 cm, well-circumscribed mass over the occipital scalp. The overlying skin was intact and of normal color; palpation revealed a soft, smooth, nonfluctuant, nonreducible lesion. There were no signs of neck rigidity or neurologic deficits.

Differential consideration included caput succedaneum, subgaleal hemorrhage, and cephalohematoma. A cranial ultrasound showed no intracranial abnormalities. On day 2, phototherapy was administered for hyperbilirubinemia attributed to the presumed cephalohematoma. Over the next 5 days of hospitalization, the occipital swelling remain

title: Dealing with Pheochromocytoma during the First Trimester of Pregnancy
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
case_summary: A 34-year-old woman, gravida 1 para 0, at 9 weeks' gestation was evaluated for recurrent episodes of hypertension. Over the previous 8 months, she had experienced 2-3 paroxysmal spikes per month lasting 1-2 minutes, with systolic pressures of 220-240 mm?Hg and diastolic pressures of 140 mm?Hg. Each episode was accompanied by palpitations, headache, sweating, nonspecific gastrointestinal discomfort, and a persistent heavy sensation in the right renal area. Physical examination of the abdomen was unremarkable. Transabdominal ultrasonography revealed a 9.0 ? 7.5 cm mixed-echogenic mass with low vascularity between the right kidney and liver. Magnetic resonance imaging confirmed a well-defined, 9 cm round mass lateral to the aorta and between the right kidney and liver, displacing the kidney. On T1-weighted images, the lesion was heterogeneously hypointense relative to muscle with isointense areas suggesting hemorrhage. On T2-weighted images, peripheral regions were markedly hyperintense (

title:
A rare case of bilateral spontaneous indirect caroticocavernous
fistula treated previously as a case of conjunctivitis
case_summary:
A 66-year-old woman was referred to the eye casualty with a 3-week history of bilateral red eyes that did not improve with chloramphenicol 0.5% drops. She also reported intermittent headaches, a "thumping" sensation in her head, and scratchy sounds in both ears at night. Her only past medical history was hypothyroidism controlled on levothyroxine, with no history of trauma.

On examination, uncorrected visual acuity was 6/12 in the right eye and 6/6 in the left, with no improvement on pinhole. Both eyes showed large, diffusely engorged scleral vessels. Confrontation visual fields were full. Pupils were equal and reactive without an afferent pupillary defect. Intraocular pressures were 19 mm Hg OD and 20 mm Hg OS. Eye alignment was orthophoric in primary gaze; however, there was mild bilateral horizontal extraocular-movement restriction with slow saccades. There was no proptosis, audible bruit, or diplopia initially. Fundus examination was normal.

Initial differential diagnoses include

title: Cerebral Fat Embolism After Traumatic Long?Bone Fracture: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 26?year?old man with no prior medical history was in a motorcycle-car accident. He sustained open fractures of the left femur and tibia and a right shoulder dislocation. Initial trauma CT scans of the head, spine, chest, pelvis, and abdomen were normal. He was transferred several hours later for orthopedic surgery. On arrival, CT angiography confirmed the long?bone fractures without vascular injury or pulmonary thromboembolism. Active bleeding from the fractures caused hemodynamic instability, and he underwent uneventful external fixation of the femur and tibia and shoulder reduction under general anesthesia. He was taken to the ward in stable condition.

Three hours postoperatively, he developed altered consciousness with aphasia, leftward gaze deviation, inability to follow commands, and grimacing to pain. He was spontaneously breathing (SpO2 96%) and hemodynamically stable. He was transferred to the ICU after a head CT excluded hemorrhage. In the ICU, he became hypoxemic, his Glas

title: Diffuse alveolar damage and e-cigarettes: Case report and review of literature
case_summary:
A 47-year-old woman with a history of actinic keratosis, seborrheic dermatitis, anxiety, deep vein thrombosis, and depression presented with 4 days of progressive shortness of breath, nasal congestion, fevers, chills, cough, lower-extremity swelling, and body aches. She denied sick contacts, tuberculosis exposure, or recent travel. Her home medications included alprazolam, amlodipine, buspirone, clonazepam, dicyclomine, losartan, ondansetron, warfarin, escitalopram, ibuprofen, methadone, and topical tretinoin. 

In the emergency department, she became hypoxemic and required endotracheal intubation. Auscultation revealed bilateral crackles and rhonchi. She was treated empirically with broad-spectrum antibiotics and, later, steroids and azathioprine without improvement. 

Laboratory studies showed WBC 9.97 ? 10^3/uL, hemoglobin 9.9 g/dL, hematocrit 35.8%, and platelets 266 ? 10^3/uL. Blood cultures showed no growth. Influenza A and B antigens and cryptococcal antigen were negative. Bronc

title: Neuro-Beh?et leading to coma: A case report
case_summary: A 35-year-old woman with a history of recurrent oral ulcers and panuveitis presented with acute loss of consciousness. Five years earlier she had been treated with azathioprine and cyclosporine for Beh?et's disease but had been lost to follow-up for one year. Two weeks before admission she developed a sore throat and low-grade fever (38  degreesC) treated with antibiotics; the fever then rose to 39  degreesC, and over the ensuing three days she developed photophobia, right-sided weakness, and gait difficulty. On arrival she was unconscious (Glasgow Coma Scale 8/15: E2 V3 M3), with bilateral vertical nystagmus, right-foot myoclonus, brisk deep-tendon reflexes, and bilateral Babinski signs. Examination of other systems was unremarkable. Laboratory studies--including complete blood count, C-reactive protein, vitamin B12 and B9 levels, thyroid function tests, serum electrolytes, and renal and hepatic panels--were normal. Noncontrast CT of the brain showed a hypodense lesion centered in the

title: Endovascular Repair of Ruptured Popliteal Artery Aneurysms: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_summary: A 96-year-old man with benign prostatic disease and dementia presented after a collapse at home. On admission, he had normocytic anemia (hemoglobin 119 g/L), a D-dimer of 1095 ng/mL, normal troponin, normal C-reactive protein, and neutrophilia (10.44 ? 10^9/L). A diagnosis of venous thromboembolism was considered but excluded on the basis of Well's score, and he was discharged with antibiotics for a presumed urinary tract infection. The next day, he returned with a swollen right knee and thigh and extensive bruising, findings that were suspicious for deep vein thrombosis. Lower limb ultrasound showed a patent venous system and identified a 45 mm popliteal artery aneurysm with an 11.4 ? 5.2 cm medial leg hematoma. Seven days later, he was admitted emergently with worsening right-leg pain and difficulty walking. Examination revealed extensive bruising behind the right knee and posterior-medial thigh, a pulsatile mass extending into the mid-thigh, and a pulseless right foot without signs

title: Purtscher-like retinopathy in patients with COVID-19
journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case_summary:
A 43-year-old woman with a history of migraines presented with acute bilateral central vision loss. One week earlier, she had severe headaches, vomiting, and diarrhea; during that visit she tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 by PCR, received IV fluids and antiemetic, and was discharged. The following morning she awoke with central vision loss in both eyes. She denied any trauma; review of systems was otherwise unremarkable.

On examination, blood pressure was 154/62 mm Hg. Visual acuity was hand motion in the right eye and count fingers in the left eye, with no afferent pupillary defect. Intraocular pressures were 14 mm Hg OD and 12 mm Hg OS. Dilated fundus examination showed diffuse peripapillary cotton-wool spots extending into the macula in both eyes, without optic disc edema. There was a flame hemorrhage along the inferior arcade in the right eye and pinpoint hemorrhages along the superior arcade in the left eye.

Neurovascular imaging--including non-contrast CT of the head, CT angiogr

title: A case of SIADH following uncomplicated mild traumatic brain injury: Did cognitive bias delay treatment?
case_summary:
A 55-year-old Spanish-speaking man with a history of asthma and chronic migraines was struck on the occiput by a falling tree branch, experienced a 1-minute loss of consciousness, and was amnestic to the event. In the emergency department his Glasgow Coma Scale score was 15, vital signs were stable, and there were no focal neurologic deficits. Head and spine CT scans were normal. He had a small temporal laceration that was repaired at the bedside and mild leukocytosis. He was diagnosed with an uncomplicated mild traumatic brain injury, post-traumatic headache, and a stress-related leukocytosis, and was admitted for observation with a plan to discharge after 3?days.

During his hospital stay he reported severe, persistent headaches. Given an unchanged neurologic examination and his history of chronic migraines, the team deferred further testing and continued headache management, attributing his symptoms to post-traumatic headache and migraine exacerbation.

On day 3, the day of planned

title: A patient with diffuse alveolar hemorrhage: A diagnostic dilemma
case_summary: A 73-year-old man with a history of abdominal aortic aneurysm repair and former smoking presented initially with two weeks of rectal bleeding. Abdominal CT showed sigmoid diverticulosis and pericolic hypervascularity; hemoglobin fell from 13.5 to 12.5 g/dL. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy and sigmoidoscopy were unrevealing, and he was discharged with a presumed diverticular bleed. One month later, he was readmitted with fatigue, cough, hemoptysis, and dyspnea. He was pale, had bilateral pedal edema, and hemoglobin was 7.0 g/dL. Chest radiography showed bilateral infiltrates interpreted as pulmonary edema, and echocardiography revealed an ejection fraction of 45%. Repeat abdominal CT showed no bleeding source. Contrast CT of the chest excluded pulmonary embolism but demonstrated bilateral airspace disease with large pleural effusions. Thoracenteses yielded hemorrhagic fluid, prompting bilateral chest-tube placement. Bronchoscopy revealed diffuse alveolar hemorrhage. He was treated empiricall

title: An intimal sarcoma of pulmonary artery mimicking pulmonary embolism: a case report and literature review
case_summary: A 63-year-old man presented with a 2-week history of right-sided chest pain that had gradually worsened. He reported mild chest congestion with a mild cough but denied haemoptysis, dyspnoea, or fever. On presentation, his arterial oxygen tension was 84 mmHg and oxygen saturation was 95% on room air. Electrocardiography showed a left anterior fascicular block. A noncontrast CT of the chest demonstrated a suspicious filling defect in the right pulmonary artery. Computed tomographic pulmonary angiography (CTPA) revealed filling defects in the right pulmonary artery and its upper branch, with possible focal pulmonary infarction. Laboratory studies showed a D-dimer level of 0.49 mg/L (reference range 0.02-0.80 mg/L), with normal complete blood count, hepatic and renal function tests, serum electrolytes, cardiac biomarkers, autoantibodies, and coagulation parameters; there was no evidence of internal bleeding. The patient was treated with low-molecular-weight heparin for 2 weeks. On transfer

title: Castleman disease mimicking accessory spleen on imaging: A case report
case_summary:
A 19-year-old man presented with mild left upper quadrant pain. He had no significant medical history, was a nonsmoker, and did not consume alcohol. Physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory tests were within normal limits except for an elevated alkaline phosphatase level of 210 U/L (normal range, 44-147 U/L). 

Abdominal ultrasound showed a 45 ? 55 mm hypoechoic round mass anterior to the pancreatic tail and medial to the splenic hilum, with significant vascularity on color Doppler. Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated a retroperitoneal mass adjacent to the pancreatic tail that showed a similar enhancement pattern to the spleen in the arterial and portal phases. 

To assess the origin of the lesion, Tc99m-denatured red blood cell scan and octreotide scintigraphy were performed; neither study showed uptake in the mass. MRI with intravenous contrast revealed that the lesion had heterogeneous early arterial and portal enhancement comparable to the spleen, was hyperintense on T2-weig

title: Lower limb lymphedema and cellulitis as a complication of COVID?19 vaccine: A case report
case_summary: A 68-year-old man with a history of severe COVID-19 (75% pulmonary involvement treated with remdesivir) received his first dose of the Sinopharm (BBIBP-CorV) COVID-19 vaccine without complication. One month later, he received the second dose. Three days after the second injection, he developed edema of the left foot and ankle, which progressed to involve the entire left leg. Color-Doppler sonography (CDS) showed extensive soft-tissue swelling and fat edema in both legs, suggesting a lymphatic drainage disorder, with no evidence of clot or deep vein thrombosis and normal flow in the popliteal and femoral veins; a small Baker's cyst was noted in the right popliteal fossa. Five days later, he developed similar edema, redness, and warmth in the right foot and ankle. Examination revealed bilateral pitting edema confined below the knees, without ischemia, ecchymosis, arthritis, or trauma; distal pulses were normal. He had no history of diabetes, heart disease, hypertension, thyroid disease,

title: Unusual Primary Ocular Manifestation of Sarcoidosis
case_summary:
A 23-year-old previously healthy man presented with slightly blurred vision and "some spots" in his right visual field. Best-corrected visual acuity was 0.7 in the right eye and 1.0 in the left eye. Examination showed distinct optic disc prominence with cotton-wool spots and tortuous retinal vessels in the right eye and mild disc prominence in the left eye. Inflammatory cells were present in the anterior chamber and vitreous, and there were cell deposits on the corneal endothelium.

Given the disc edema and vasculitis-like appearance, a contrast-enhanced cranial MRI was obtained, which "excluded encephalitis, sinus vein thrombosis, recent infarcts, and intracranial hemorrhages." Blood pressure was regular. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) opening pressure was 21 cm H?O, and imaging of CSF spaces was normal, "thus a pseudotumor cerebri could be excluded." Initial blood tests were notable only for a D-dimer of 895 ng/mL (norm <500) and calcium of 2.62 mmol/L (norm 2.09-2.54). CSF analysis showe

title: Hepatobiliary case report and literature review of hepatic reactive lymphoid hyperplasia with positive anti-smooth muscle antibody and anti-nuclear antibody tests
case_summary:
A 69-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of a hepatic mass discovered on CT one month earlier. She had experienced intermittent abdominal distention for two months but no other symptoms. Her medical history included hypertension for 40 years and type II diabetes mellitus for 10 years; she had no history of hepatitis B or tuberculosis.  

On examination, findings were unremarkable. Laboratory tests showed normal blood counts, normal liver function (AST, ALT, bilirubin, PT), and an albumin/globulin ratio of 0.9. Antinuclear antibody testing was positive (nucleolar type, 1:640; cytoplasmic type, 1:80), and smooth muscle antibody was positive at 1:320. Other hepatopathic autoimmune antibodies were negative. Serum IgG (18.2 g/L) and IgA (4.58 g/L) were mildly elevated. Tumor markers (AFP, CA242, CEA, CA19-9) and thyroid parameters were normal.  

Dynamic CT revealed a 1.0-cm solid, well-delineated mass in segment II with slight arterial-phase enhancement and venous-phase washout. MRI

title: Aneurysmal bone cyst of the mandible affecting the articular condyle: a case report
case_summary:
A 10-year-old girl with no prior medical history presented with progressive left facial asymmetry over several months. On examination, there was a firm mass over the left temporomandibular area, marked lateral deviation of the mandible, and reduced mouth opening. Intraorally, the mucosa was intact but both the internal and external cortices of the left mandibular ramus were palpably bulging. There were no neurological deficits.

An orthopantomograph demonstrated a poorly defined, multilobulated radiolucent lesion involving the left mandibular ascending ramus and condyle. Subsequent CT imaging confirmed an expansile, multiseptated, osteolytic intraosseous lesion in the left condyle and ramus with cortical thinning and bulging but no soft-tissue invasion. Multiple vascular-appearing "lagoons" were noted within the lesion, prompting a provisional diagnosis of a suspected aneurysmal bone cyst.

title: Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome and Guillain-Barr? Syndrome after Head Injury: Case Report
case_summary: A 74?year?old man with dementia was brought to the emergency department one day after a mountain fall. He had no headache, seizure, or visual symptoms but had multiple bruises and an abrasion on the head. There was no history of infection. On arrival his Glasgow Coma Scale was 13 (E3V4M6); heart rate 94/min; blood pressure 149/75 mmHg; and temperature 37.9 degreesC (persisting near 38 degreesC for two weeks). Physical examination showed bruises on the chest and abdomen and right lower?leg tenderness; muscle strength was 4/5 in the right lower leg. Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL; leukocytes 19,200/?L (neutrophils 15,398/?L); urea 25 mmol/L; creatinine 0.76 mg/dL; potassium 3.8 mmol/L; sodium 141 mmol/L; chloride 108 mmol/L; creatine kinase 20,518 IU/L; and C?reactive protein 13.43 mg/dL. He received a tetanus vaccine and intravenous tazobactam/piperacillin from hospital day 6 to 20. Plain radiographs showed no fractures. Head CT demonstrated subarachnoid hemorrhage, an

title: Neurosarcoidosis Presentation as Adipsic Diabetes Insipidus Secondary to a Pituitary Stalk Lesion and Association with Anti-NMDA Receptor Antibodies
case_summary:
A 22-year-old woman presented with a two-month history of headache accompanied by incoherent speech, aggressive behavior, disinhibited conduct, and visual and auditory hallucinations. On the day before admission, she developed a progressive decrease in awareness. She had no significant past medical history, denied drug use, toxin exposure, or recent travel, and reported minimal oral intake of food and water over the preceding days.

On examination, she was tachycardic with a weak pulse, normal blood pressure, dry mucous membranes, and prolonged capillary refill. Neurologic examination revealed disorientation, retrograde amnesia, impaired judgment, and loss of abstract thinking. A chest radiograph and noncontrast head CT scan were unremarkable.

Laboratory evaluation showed severe hypernatremia (serum sodium 180 mEq/L) and acute kidney injury (creatinine 2.0 mg/dL; baseline 0.6 mg/dL). Serum electrolytes, liver function tests, complete blood count, urinalysis, and toxicology screen were

title: Revisiting hereditary tyrosinemia Type 1--spectrum of radiological findings
case_summary:
An 18-month-old boy was evaluated for intermittent spiking fevers, constipation, and gradually progressive abdominal distension since 1 month of age. His birth history and immunizations were unremarkable. A sibling had died at age 2 with a similar illness.

On examination, he was below the fifth percentile for height and weight, jaundiced, and had abdominal distension. The liver and spleen were palpably enlarged.

Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 8.0 g/dL, white blood cell count 12,000/uL, alkaline phosphatase 2117 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 121 U/L, ?-glutamyl transferase 301 U/L, elevated total bilirubin, and prolonged prothrombin time/international normalized ratio. TORCH screening was negative. Serum ?-fetoprotein was markedly elevated at 14,831 ng/mL.

Abdominal ultrasound revealed a coarse, nodular-appearing liver with multiple small hypoechoic avascular nodules in a cirrhotic background. Contrast-enhanced CT confirmed multiple non-enhancing hepatic nodules on arterial, ve

title: Segmental retinal arteritis in a case of presumed bilateral rifabutin-associated hypopyon uveitis
journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case_summary:
A 63-year-old woman with HIV/AIDS (on dolutegravir, emtricitabine, tenofovir), hepatitis B, and a history of pulmonary tuberculosis presented with two days of pain and acute vision loss in the left eye. She had a TB relapse seven months earlier and had been on rifabutin 450 mg, isoniazid, and pyridoxine daily; her body weight was 62.3 kg. Three weeks before presentation, her CD4 count was 166 cells/mm3 and HIV viral load was <20 copies/mL.

On examination, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/20 in the right eye and light perception in the left; intraocular pressures were 9 mm Hg OD and 8 mm Hg OS. The right eye was normal. Slit-lamp examination of the left eye showed a fibrinous anterior chamber reaction with 4+ cell and flare, a pupillary membrane, and a 1 mm hypopyon. B-scan ultrasonography of the left eye revealed vitritis.

Concerned about endogenous endophthalmitis, she received intravenous vancomycin and cefepime, oral trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole and valacyclovir, intravitreal

title: Treatment of a pneumatocele in a COVID-19 patient with endobronchial valves
case_summary: A Caucasian man in his 40s was admitted in spring 2021, six days after the onset of fever, cough, and progressive dyspnea due to COVID-19. His medical history included hemithyroidectomy, thymoma resection, Mycoplasma pneumoniae pneumonia, and treated visceral leishmaniasis. A routine CT of the thorax one year earlier was unremarkable except for dependent atelectasis. On this admission, he required intensive care with noninvasive ventilation and high-flow nasal cannula oxygen but never invasive ventilation. Three weeks after admission, while improving, he experienced a forceful cough or sneeze followed by acute worsening of dyspnea. A CT pulmonary angiogram showed no embolism but revealed a right-sided air collection and widespread ground-glass opacities. Multiple appropriately placed chest drains failed to evacuate any air. Four days later, at referral to our center, he was on 8-10 L/min of oxygen and desaturated to 65% on minimal exertion. An ultrasound-guided pigtail drain in the bas

title: Prostate Cancer Presenting as Huge Mediastinal and Retroperitoneal Masses: Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_summary:
A 67-year-old man presented with acute urinary retention and left leg swelling. A urinary catheter was placed, and ultrasonography revealed an extensive left femoral vein deep venous thrombosis, for which he received anticoagulation and an inferior vena cava filter. At that time, his prostate-specific antigen (PSA) level was 77.2 ug/L; no prostate biopsy was performed.

Six months later, he developed two days of shortness of breath and productive cough with whitish sputum. Pulmonary embolism was excluded by blood tests and imaging. Chest radiography showed a left-sided mediastinal mass. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest and abdomen (oral contrast only, no IV contrast because of renal impairment) demonstrated lobulated mediastinal masses extending into both supraclavicular regions (largest 9.8 cm) and large retroperitoneal masses (15 ? 15 cm) from the upper abdomen to the aortic bifurcation, with bilateral hydronephrosis and hydroureters. The radiologic impression was lymphoma.

A core-

title: Successful Treatment of Uterine Arteriovenous Malformation due to Uterine Trauma
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
case_summary: A 35-year-old woman (gravida 2, para 1, abortion 1) underwent dilation and curettage at 9 weeks' gestation for a missed abortion two weeks earlier. One week later, she returned for repeat D/C because of a suspected hematoma, but the procedure was halted due to hemorrhage. On admission to our hospital, her hemoglobin was 11.2 g/dL, hematocrit 35.1%, and ?-hCG 3518 mIU/mL. There was no active vaginal bleeding. Transvaginal ultrasonography revealed a 60 ? 60 ? 56 mm hyperechogenic, heterogeneous mass in the anterior uterine wall extending to the left, with minimal fluid in the endometrial cavity and normal adnexa. Color Doppler imaging demonstrated prominent venous vascular signals within the lesion.

title:
A rare suspected case of chronic nodular granulomatous herpes simplex
encephalitis in an adult
case_summary:
A 61-year-old woman presented with acute onset dysphasia and a 10-day history of fever, vertigo, and lethargy. Her only medical history was hypothyroidism on thyroxine. On examination, she had severe expressive and receptive dysphasia with phonemic paraphasias and jargon, could follow only one-step verbal commands, and had right-arm pronator drift with brisk ipsilateral reflexes but no sensory or motor weakness. Stroke and encephalitis were both considered, and empirical antibacterial and antiviral therapies were started.

Noncontrast CT of the brain on admission showed hypoattenuation in the left insula and external capsule without haemorrhage or intravascular thrombus. CSF analysis revealed lymphocytic pleocytosis, protein 1.62 g l??, normal glucose, negative bacterial cultures, no malignant cells, and negative cryptococcal antigen and acid-fast bacilli. Autoantibodies, including voltage-gated potassium channel antibodies, were normal. A viral PCR panel of the CSF was positive for HS

title: A rare case of adenocarcinoma of an ectopic pancreas: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: A 69-year-old man presented to the emergency department with a 2-day history of feculent vomiting. His past medical history included appendicular peritonitis, coronary bypass surgery, hypertension, chronic renal failure, hypercholesterolemia, and insulin-dependent diabetes. He reported nocturnal and diurnal vomiting without abdominal pain, along with months of eructation and burping; his appetite was preserved. On examination, vital signs were normal; the abdomen was soft and non-tender. Laboratory testing revealed a creatinine level of 1.8 mg/dL and a C-reactive protein level of 19.1 mg/L. Given his renal impairment, a non-contrast abdominal CT scan was performed, which demonstrated a distended stomach without duodenal or intestinal abnormality and an infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm measuring > 3 cm in diameter. He was admitted for further evaluation, placed on nil per os with parenteral nutrition, intravenous fluids, high-dose proton-pump inhibitors, and nasogastric decompressio

title: Rare Presentation of an Ulnar Artery Aneurysm in a Six-Month-Old Baby: Case Discussion
case_summary: A 6-month-old boy was brought to the vascular surgery clinic with a 2-month history of a gradually enlarging swelling on the hypothenar eminence of his left hand. The parents noted no unusual crying or signs of pain. On examination, the mass measured approximately 4?2 cm, was pulsatile on palpation, and had no overlying skin changes. There were no motor deficits in the left hand, both radial and ulnar pulses were palpable, and capillary refill was normal. Further history revealed that the infant habitually banged his left hand against hard surfaces while feeding. Given the location and pulsatile character of the swelling, an aneurysm arising from the ulnar artery or superficial palmar arch was suspected. An arterial duplex ultrasound confirmed the presence of an aneurysmal sac; detailed vascular anatomy could not be delineated due to the patient's age, and no additional imaging studies were obtained.

title: Parasellar epidermoid cyst with unique radiological features: A case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 39-year-old woman presented with periodic left facial convulsions for more than 3 months, accompanied by a mild headache, twitching of the left masticatory muscle, and difficulty opening her mouth during episodes. There was no dizziness, facial numbness, nausea, or vomiting. Her medical history was notable for a partial thyroidectomy 2 years earlier for papillary thyroid cancer with lymph node metastases. Vital signs were within normal limits. On examination, she was alert and cooperative, with normal heart, lung, abdominal, and neurologic findings. Facial electromyography, lateral spread response, and somatosensory evoked potentials were approximately normal.

Noncontrast CT of the head showed a circular, well-circumscribed mass in the left parasellar region that was predominantly hyperdense with patchy hypodense areas, without surrounding edema. Bone-window images demonstrated compression of adjacent bone without destruction.

Multimodal MRI revealed that the lesion was heterogeneo

title: Multimodality Imaging of Primary Hepatic Lymphoma: A Case Report and a Literature Review
case_summary: An 84-year-old man underwent abdominal ultrasonography for diabetes screening, which incidentally identified a 15-mm hypoechogenic, lobular nodule in segment 7 of the liver. He was asymptomatic, with a history of diabetes mellitus and hypertension, no alcohol use, no blood transfusions, and an unremarkable family history. Laboratory tests showed a normal blood count, normal liver-function and cholestasis markers, negative viral hepatitis markers, and normal levels of alpha-fetoprotein and des-gamma-carboxy prothrombin; soluble interleukin-2 receptor was mildly elevated at 599 U/mL. Initial color Doppler ultrasonography demonstrated linear blood flow within the nodule and a strand of intermediate echogenicity penetrating the lesion. Low-mechanical-index contrast-enhanced ultrasonography (CEUS) revealed reticular vessels with gradual arterial-phase enhancement, lower enhancement than liver parenchyma in the portal phase, and complete wash-out in the late phase. Noncontrast CT showed a hy

title: A "Catastrophic" Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia
case_summary: A 63-year-old woman underwent left knee replacement and was discharged on enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously once daily for thromboprophylaxis without planned platelet monitoring. Fifteen days later she presented with fever (39.5  degreesC), headache, acute right-sided weakness, and a 2-minute episode of altered consciousness with focal tremors in her right leg and abdomen. Noncontrast head CT was unremarkable. Brain MRI showed an acute infarct in the left precentral and postcentral gyri and a subacute infarct in the right parietal lobe. Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL, platelet count 43 000 ? 10^9/L, and D-dimer 34 800 ?g/L; other values were within normal limits. She had no history of hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, smoking, or estrogen use. Physical examination noted right upper quadrant pain without hepatosplenomegaly or mucocutaneous bleeding. ECG demonstrated sinus rhythm, and echocardiography showed a nondilated left atrium with a normal ejection fraction (

title: Traumatic Basilar Artery Entrapment without Longitudinal Clivus Fracture: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_summary: A 67-year-old man fell approximately 1 m from the cargo bed of a truck and was transported with quadriplegia and neurogenic shock, managed with an adrenergic vasopressor. He was alert on arrival (GCS score 14 [E3,V5,M6]) with a respiratory rate of 23 breaths/min, blood pressure 106/69 mmHg while receiving dopamine (5.5 ?g/kg/min), heart rate 70 bpm (sinus), and SpO? 99% on 1 L O?. Head CT showed a small epidural hematoma over the frontal bone. His history included a bifrontal craniotomy for aneurysm clipping 3 years earlier. He also had fractures of the C4-6 spinous processes, T2-3 vertebral bodies, bilateral first ribs, and the sternum. T2-weighted MRI demonstrated high-intensity signals in the spinal cord at C3-4 and T2 levels. Whole-body 3D-CT angiography revealed occlusion of the basilar artery trunk and poor visualization of the left vertebral artery distal to the posterior inferior cerebellar artery; there were no abnormal findings such as arterial dissection in the vertebral art

title: An elderly woman with a mediastinal granulosa cell tumour: a rare presentation
case_summary: An 81?year?old woman with asthma presented for routine pulmonary evaluation. She had undergone resection of a "benign" ovarian mass 40 years earlier. She was a non?smoker and asymptomatic on review of systems. Vital signs and physical examination were normal. A surveillance CT scan of the chest performed to follow an ascending aortic aneurysm revealed a right paratracheal mediastinal lesion measuring 2.6 ? 2.0 ? 4.0 cm, which had increased in size since prior imaging and demonstrated regions of hypoattenuation and peripheral calcification. A PET?CT scan showed a right paratracheal nodal conglomerate without hypermetabolic activity and otherwise normal physiological 18?FDG uptake. Given the interval enlargement, the patient underwent endobronchial ultrasound with transbronchial needle aspiration. The EBUS revealed a well?circumscribed mass in the 4R lymph node region, and the TBNA samples demonstrated an epithelioid neoplasm without evidence of lymphoid tissue.

title: Embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma of the biliary tree in a paediatric patient - A rare cause of obstructive jaundice
case_summary:
A 4-year-old boy presented with a 3-month history of yellow discoloration of his eyes, dark urine, and pale stools, and 2 months of progressive abdominal distension. On examination, he was pale, had scleral icterus, and a distended abdomen with a large palpable liver.

Admission laboratory tests showed elevated cholestatic liver enzymes, conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, an elevated international normalized ratio, and iron-deficiency anemia.

Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated a heterogeneous periportal mass with internal flow on color Doppler and dilation of the common bile duct (CBD), cystic duct, gallbladder, and intrahepatic bile ducts.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis revealed a 60 ? 45 ? 89 mm fusiform-shaped mass with heterogeneous contrast uptake running parallel to the expected CBD course; its superior aspect was inseparable from the porta hepatis and proximal central bile ducts. The portal vein was partially attenuated without thrombosis. The mass deviated and part

title: Atypical presentation of fat embolism syndrome: A case report
case_summary:
A 21-year-old previously healthy man was involved in a motorcycle collision. Upon transfer to a trauma center and ATLS evaluation, he was hemodynamically stable but had an open left femur fracture and comminuted fractures of the left humerus and left tibia. A whole-body CT scan ruled out cranial, thoracic, abdominal, and spinal injuries. Fourteen hours after the injury, he underwent debridement and external fixation of the open femur fracture and stabilization of the other fractures.

Twelve hours postoperatively, in the orthopedic ward, he developed hypoxia, tachypnea, fever, and tachycardia; there were no neurologic deficits or skin findings. Oxygen was given by nasal cannula, intravenous fluids were administered, blood cultures were obtained, and ceftriaxone was started. An initial chest CT angiogram showed no pulmonary embolism and only discrete bilateral consolidations at the lung bases. Tests for bacterial and viral pathogens, including COVID-19, were negative.

He was transferre

title: Spontaneous perforation of small intestine followed by rupture of the cystic artery: the natural history of Vascular Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome
case_summary: A 30-year-old man presented with left lower abdominal pain associated with nausea and vomiting. His medical history was notable for aneurysms of the abdominal aorta and several of its branches, a surgically repaired pseudoaneurysm of the left subclavian artery, coil embolizations of the superior ophthalmic veins and cavernous sinuses, and a recent coil embolization of a splenic artery aneurysm. His father also had multiple aneurysms. On examination, he had tenderness and guarding in the lower abdomen. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 15.1 g/dL (normal, 12-16 g/dL) and a white-cell count of 8800 cells/mm3 (normal, 4800-10 800 cells/mm3) with 84.2% neutrophils. Contrast-enhanced abdominal CT revealed ascites and findings consistent with his prior aneurysms. A repeat CT scan demonstrated a right pleural effusion and nonspecific enteritis involving small-bowel loops in the left abdomen and descending colon. A clinical diagnosis of intestinal obstruction was made; symptoms improved afte

title: Arterial Thrombosis in an Unusual Site (Ulnar Artery) after COVID-19 Vaccination--A Case Report
case_summary:
A 69-year-old man presented four days after receiving his third dose of the Spikevax vaccine (mRNA-1273) with a 4-cm area of redness and swelling over the hypothenar eminence of his left hand, the same side as the injection. The swelling was painless, with only mild discomfort on pressure, and had a firm, rope-like consistency. He denied any trauma or repetitive pressure to the hand. Musculoskeletal ultrasound revealed a 30-mm thrombus in the arcuate branch of the ulnar artery. 

Vital signs were blood pressure 133/82 mm Hg, heart rate 70 beats/min, oxygen saturation 99% on room air; BMI was 23.4 kg/m2. Examination showed normal distal perfusion and pulses, without signs of ischemia. His history was notable for hypothyroidism (post-thyroidectomy) and benign prostatic hyperplasia; medications included levothyroxine and tamsulosin. In September 2021, routine blood tests--including complete blood count, glucose, creatinine, liver enzymes, PSA, and TSH--were within normal limits. A lipid p

title: Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy Complicated by Pulmonary Edema in the Postpartum Period
case_summary:
A 42-year-old woman, gravida 4, para 1, presented to the emergency department seven days after an uneventful cesarean delivery with severe shortness of breath.  
On examination, jugular venous pressure was elevated, there was bilateral pitting edema of the lower extremities, and bibasilar crackles were heard on lung auscultation.  
Chest radiographs showed bilateral pleural effusions and perihilar airspace opacities consistent with pulmonary edema.  
A thoracic CT pulmonary angiogram was performed, which ruled out pulmonary embolism but confirmed pulmonary edema and demonstrated asymmetric thickening of the interventricular septum measuring up to 22 mm.  
Transthoracic echocardiography revealed normal left ventricular systolic function, an elevated E/E? ratio of 19 mmHg indicating raised left atrial pressure, and a mild provocable left ventricular outflow tract gradient of 33 mmHg with amyl nitrate.

title: Rectus Abdominis Endometrioma after Caesarean Section
case_summary:
A 37-year-old woman presented with a painful mass in the left hypogastric area at the site of a Pfannenstiel incision from a cesarean section performed 3 years earlier. She reported that the intensity of pain and the size of the mass increased during menstruation. She had no other significant medical history. On examination, a firm, pseudotumorous mass approximately 2?cm in diameter was palpated at the left side of the postoperative scar. Serum CA 125 measured on the first day of her menstrual cycle was 25?U/mL (reference range, 0-35?U/mL).  
Ultrasound with Doppler imaging revealed a hypoechoic, heterogeneous, roundish nodule at the lateral end of the postoperative scar, with a single vascular pedicle entering at the periphery of the mass. Magnetic resonance imaging showed a 21?23?28 mm heterogeneous mass within the lower left rectus abdominis muscle containing focal areas of high and low signal intensity, suggestive of old hemorrhage or fibrosis, with no involvement of the abdominal

title: COVID-19 and severe pulmonary alveolar proteinosis (PAP): A case report
case_summary: A 55-year-old man presented with 3 months of progressive exertional dyspnea, generalized weakness, and productive cough with brown sputum. One month earlier he had been evaluated elsewhere, treated with levofloxacin and prednisolone, and discharged on home oxygen without improvement. Medical history included type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and a 40-pack-year smoking history. Two months before presentation he had dry cough and dyspnea attributed to COVID-19; PCR was negative at this admission, but chest imaging showed diffuse bilateral infiltrates with ground-glass opacities and crazy-paving predominantly in the lower lobes. He was treated for presumed post-COVID fibrosis with superimposed infection, discharged on oxygen, antibiotics, and steroids, but returned 2 weeks later with worsened dyspnea, occasional dry cough, weakness, and fever to 38.6  degreesC. On exam his SpO2 was in the 70s on room air, with decreased air entry at both lung bases and minimal crackles. Laboratory s

title: Physiotherapy Screening for Referral of a Patient with Peripheral Arterial Disease Masquerading as Sciatica: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 67-year-old man was referred by a neurosurgeon to our physiotherapy clinic for bilateral sciatica. His history included:
? Four years earlier: left medullary stroke (Wallenberg syndrome) with right-sided pain and temperature sensory loss; on aspirin, antihypertensive, statin, and antidiabetic medications.  
? One year later: coronary artery bypass surgery followed by abdominal aortic aneurysm repair.  
? Risk factors: diabetes, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, smoking, sedentary lifestyle, high-red meat diet, and alcohol use.  

Symptom history:
? One year before physiotherapy: right knee pain with low back pain evolving into burning pain posteriorly in the knee, then anterior tibia and toe; fluctuating in location and intensity.  
? Orthopedic evaluation: knee MRI and thigh ultrasound were normal.  
? Subsequent left posterior thigh-knee pain prompted spine MRI and CT, which showed L4-5 and L5-S1 disc bulging with L5 root impingement.  
? Neurosurgeon diagnosed bilateral lumbosac

title: Superior mesenteric artery aneurysm with rupture: an atypical cause of abdominal pain
case_summary:
A 60-year-old woman presented to the emergency department after a syncopal episode during which she struck her head. She reported the syncope was associated with acute central abdominal pain radiating to her back. On arrival she was initially hemodynamically stable but had a transient drop in blood pressure to 75/60 mmHg that responded to intravenous fluids. Her heart rate was 70 bpm in sinus rhythm, and all other vital signs were within normal limits. She had no significant medical history or cardiovascular risk factors.  

On examination, her abdomen was soft with central tenderness but no signs of peritonism. No abdominal masses were palpable, and the abdominal aorta was not palpable. Initial laboratory studies showed a hemoglobin concentration of 7.8 g/dL and an international normalized ratio of 1.3; all other laboratory values were unremarkable.  

The initial working diagnoses included aortic dissection, perforated viscus, and severe pancreatitis. A contrast-enhanced CT angiogram

title: Eales' disease with secondary coats'-like reaction: a case report
case_summary:
A 25-year-old healthy Malay man noted painless blurred vision in his right eye for 2 months after a trivial blunt injury. He smoked 4 pack-years, had no prior ocular or systemic disease, and no relevant family history. On exam, a right relative afferent pupillary defect was present. Best-corrected visual acuity was 3/60 in the right eye and 6/6 in the left. Anterior segments were unremarkable bilaterally; intraocular pressures were 14 mmHg in each eye. Fundus examination of the right eye revealed multiple aneurysmal dilatations at the macula with loss of the foveolar reflex, 360 degrees perivascular sheathing, and telangiectatic changes extending from the equator to the periphery; there were no venous tortuosity, hemorrhages, exudates, vitritis, retinitis, or choroiditis. The optic disc was pink with a cup-to-disc ratio of 0.4. The left fundus was normal.

Spectral-domain OCT of the right macula showed disorganization of inner retinal layers, thinning of the inner and outer nuclear lay

title: Solitary juvenile xanthogranuloma in the parotid gland
case_summary:
A 1-month-old girl was found to have a firm, palpable mass anterior-inferior to the right external auditory canal over the right parotid gland. There was no overlying skin lesion; the mass did not appear adherent to bone or muscle. The patient was otherwise healthy; she was born at term by cesarean delivery as one of a dichorionic twin pair.

Ultrasound of the parotid gland showed a well-defined, encapsulated heterogeneous mass in the superficial lobe measuring 39.4 ? 23.6 ? 33 mm with posterior attenuation and multiple peripheral echogenic round spots. Color-Doppler imaging revealed peripheral vascularization in an incomplete spoke-wheel pattern; arterial vessels did not reach the lesion's center and the resistance index was 0.62. No abnormal lymph nodes were seen.

Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated an intraparotid lesion that was isointense to muscle on T1-weighted and STIR images, with a peripheral rim of enhancement after intravenous contrast.

Fine-needle aspiration revealed

title: Visual impairment associated with choroidal macroaneurysm in a patient with presumed anomalous short posterior ciliary artery
journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case_summary: A 62-year-old asymptomatic woman was referred for suspected age-related macular degeneration. Her history was unremarkable except for well-controlled hypertension. Best-corrected visual acuity was 20/20 in both eyes, and intraocular pressures were normal. Slit-lamp examination showed unremarkable anterior segments. Funduscopic examination revealed an epiretinal membrane with retinal folds in the right eye. In the left eye, thick choroidal vessels were visible at the level of the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) from the fovea to the temporal macula, and there was an area of pigmentary alteration inferotemporal to the macula. Spectral-domain optical coherence tomography of the left eye showed RPE elevation without intra- or subretinal fluid or hemorrhage. Fluorescein angiography and indocyanine green angiography demonstrated anomalous choroidal vessels in the arterial phase; late fluorescein angiography showed background hyperfluorescence corresponding to the area of pigmentary alterati

title: A survival after cardiac arrest during CT scan: The critical imaging findings
case_summary: A 15-year-old boy with no significant medical history was transferred after a motorbike accident. On admission, he was intubated and agitated, with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 11, blood pressure stable, respiratory rate 22 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air.
