Examination revealed severe head and maxillofacial trauma and a left tibia?fibula fracture. Abdominal ultrasound raised suspicion for hemoperitoneum in Morrison's pouch. To assess for abdominal organ injury, a contrast-enhanced abdominal CT scan was planned. Before imaging, 5 mg of midazolam was administered for sedation because of patient agitation. During the arterial phase of the CT scan, there was intense contrast opacification of the right atrium, right ventricle, inferior vena cava, and hepatic veins, whereas the left heart chambers and descending aorta were poorly opacified.

title: Iatrogenic Injury of Profunda Femoris Artery Branches after Intertrochanteric Hip Screw Fixation for Intertrochanteric Femoral Fracture: A Case Report and Literature Review
case_summary: A 92?year?old woman was referred to vascular surgery for an enlarging hematoma of the left thigh. Two months earlier, she had undergone dynamic hip screw fixation for an intertrochanteric femoral fracture. On admission, she was febrile (temperature >38 degreesC), mildly anemic (hematocrit 32.9%, hemoglobin 10.7 g/dL), but hemodynamically stable with palpable peripheral pulses in both lower extremities. During the postoperative period, despite repeated blood transfusions, her hematocrit and hemoglobin levels declined progressively to 22.5% and 7.1 g/dL, respectively. Serial ultrasound examinations showed the thigh hematoma enlarging from an initial diameter of 9 cm to over 20 cm. By postoperative day 25, the hematoma enlargement had plateaued and there were no signs of lower?extremity ischemia, leading to her discharge. On representation, the hematoma continued to be a concern, prompting further evaluation.

title: Radiation-Induced Alopecia after Endovascular Embolization under Fluoroscopy
case_summary:
A 46-year-old Thai man presented to the outpatient clinic with an asymptomatic, sharply demarcated rectangular balding patch on the occipital scalp of 2 weeks' duration. Ten months earlier, he had sustained a traumatic right carotid-cavernous fistula and optic neuropathy in a motor vehicle accident. Six months ago, he underwent transarterial balloon embolization without complication. One month before presentation, magnetic resonance imaging and angiography demonstrated a residual shunt, and he subsequently underwent a cerebral angiogram with transvenous coil and glue embolization under fluoroscopy. The procedure lasted 150 minutes, with a radiation exposure time of 67 minutes and a peak skin dose of 2.9 Gy. Two weeks after the procedure, he noted the occipital hair loss. 

On examination, there was a sharply demarcated rectangular nonscarring alopecic patch measuring 10 ? 12 cm on the occipital scalp without erythema or scaling. A hair pull test at the periphery revealed 100% telogen h

title: Do you know the diagnostic importance of susceptibility?weighted imaging on MRI for patients with cerebral fat embolism?
case_summary:
A 75-year-old woman, previously independent in daily activities, was found collapsed on her driveway after arriving home by car. Her medical history was notable for left renal cancer in remission and a vertebral compression fracture; she was taking sodium alendronate and calcium supplements. When paramedics arrived, she was alert and complained only of left hip pain, but en route to the local hospital she developed progressive loss of consciousness. On arrival there, her Glasgow Coma Scale score was E4V3M5 and oxygen saturation was 80% on room air. CT of the pelvis revealed a left trochanteric femur fracture. She was transferred for further evaluation.

On admission, her GCS remained E4V3M5. Vital signs were temperature 37.1 degreesC, heart rate 107 bpm, respiratory rate 29 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 92% on 4 L/min of oxygen. On exam, she had no motor deficits, pupils were equal and reactive, and there was no rash. Chest auscultation revealed bilateral crackles with diminished

title: Case Report: Intracranial peripheral primitive neuroectodermal tumor - Ewing's sarcoma of dura with transcalvarial-subgaleal extension: An unusual radiological presentation
journal: The Indian Journal of Radiology & Imaging
case_summary:
An 11-year-old girl presented with a 3-month history of a midline frontal, non-tender scalp swelling that had recently grown rapidly. She also had recurrent headaches, giddiness, mild behavioral changes, epiphora, diplopia, and blurring of vision. Fundoscopic examination revealed bilateral papilledema; no focal neurologic deficits were detected.

Non-contrast CT of the head showed a 7.0 ? 5.0 ? 5.0 cm, densely calcified, mushroom-shaped, parasagittal hyperdense mass arising from the anterior falx. The non-calcified portion of the lesion enhanced markedly with intravenous contrast. There was destruction of both the inner and outer tables of the overlying calvarium with transcalvarial-subgaleal extension and extensive perilesional vasogenic white-matter edema.

On MRI, the mass was predominantly hypointense on T1-weighted images with internal hyperintense foci corresponding to calcifications and was heterogeneously isohyperintense on T2-weighted images. Diffusion-weighted imaging and ADC

title: Coexistence of disseminated tuberculosis and peripheral deep vein thrombosis in a child with newly diagnosed celiac disease: A rare entity
journal: Journal of Family Medicine and Primary Care
case_summary: A 13-year-old girl presented with 5 days of generalized weakness and pain and swelling of the left lower limb. One month earlier, celiac disease had been diagnosed by duodenal biopsy and tissue transglutaminase IgA. On examination, she was thin and emaciated (height 144 cm, weight 22 kg), with pallor and enlarged cervical, axillary, and inguinal lymph nodes. The left lower limb was swollen with dilated tortuous veins; vital signs were stable. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 7 g/dL; white cells 4,000/mm3 (80% neutrophils); AST 157 IU, ALT 160 IU; total protein 4.9 g/dL; prothrombin time 23.7 s, PTTK 69.5 s, INR 2.03; vitamin D3 12 ng/dL. Doppler ultrasound revealed an echogenic thrombus in the left common femoral and deep femoral veins. She was started on intravenous antibiotics and fluids for high-grade fever and early shock. Despite therapy, fever persisted. Chest radiograph showed multiple nodular opacities. Abdominal ultrasonography revealed multiple enlarged paraaortic, pre-ao

title: One-Year Outcome of Aflibercept and Photodynamic Therapy in a Caucasian Patient with Polypoidal Choroidal Vasculopathy Refractory to Ranibizumab and Photodynamic Therapy
case_summary:
A 75-year-old woman presented in July 2015 with a 5-month history of irregularities on her Amsler grid. Five months earlier, at a routine visit, visual acuity was 0.6 in the right eye and 0.8 in the left eye, and fundus examination had shown scattered drusen in both eyes. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) at that time had shown no neovascular membrane. 

On return in July, visual acuity remained 0.6 in the right eye. Fundus examination revealed persistent drusen and a small juxtapapillary hemorrhage in the superior temporal arcade of the right eye. OCT now showed subretinal fluid and a pigment epithelial detachment beneath the superior temporal arcade. Fluorescein angiography demonstrated significant pigment epithelial detachments in the perifoveal capillary network during the venous phase but no obvious polypoidal lesions. An indocyanine green angiogram was scheduled to clarify the diagnosis, and intravitreal ranibizumab was begun at 4-week intervals.

title: Peritoneal loose body presenting as a hepatic mass: A case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 69-year-old man was referred for evaluation of a 3-month history of abdominal discomfort predominantly in the upper quadrants. He denied other gastrointestinal or systemic symptoms. His medical history was notable only for hypertension for more than four years. On physical examination, vital signs were normal, the abdomen was soft, and no masses were palpable. Laboratory studies, including a complete blood count and liver function tests, were within normal limits.  

Abdominal ultrasound revealed a hypoechoic mass with a clear boundary and regular shape in the second hilar region; the lesion contained hyperintense echogenic foci with posterior acoustic shadowing, suggestive of dense calcifications, and no internal blood flow signal. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen demonstrated a well-circumscribed, round solid lesion with punctate central calcification and no enhancement on venous-phase imaging. On T1- and T2-weighted MRI, the mass appeared distinctly hypointense--comparable to m

title: A peculiar case of large primary cutaneous Ewing's sarcoma of the foot: Case report and review of the literature
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 37-year-old man presented with a 3-month history of a rapidly enlarging mass on the left first toe. He denied trauma or infection; a prior dermatologist had treated the lesion as a viral wart without improvement. On examination, there was a 9.5?8?cm polypoid, necrotic mass with irregular margins, ulceration, and intermittent bleeding. Ambulation was limited by pain and tumor bulk. Radiographs of the foot showed no bony involvement. There was no palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy, and ultrasound of the groin was normal. Routine blood tests were within normal limits. The patient underwent wide local excision, which was extended to amputation of the toe to achieve clear margins. 
Histological examination of the excised specimen revealed a tumor in the dermis and subcutis composed of cohesive sheets and trabeculae of uniform small to medium round cells with hyperchromatic nuclei, scant pale cytoplasm, indistinct borders, prominent mitotic activity (30-35 mitoses per 10 high-power fields),

title: Run or Die: A Didactique Case Report of a Rare Cause of Lactic Acidosis in Emergency Medicine
case_summary: A 23-year-old man with no known medical history was found near the entrance of the emergency department, confused and sweating, with visible blood around him. On arrival, his vital signs were: heart rate 140 beats per minute, blood pressure 125/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 55 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 96% on room air, and temperature 37.2  degreesC. A venous blood gas demonstrated severe lactic acidosis, which was confirmed on an arterial sample obtained five minutes later. He received 500 mL of 0.9% sodium chloride and was preoxygenated for intubation. A focused assessment with sonography for trauma (FAST) showed no intrathoracic or intraabdominal fluid. His scalp laceration was closed with staples, and after sedation, a whole?body CT scan revealed no acute injuries. One hour after admission, his respiratory rate began to improve. Repeat arterial blood gases at one and two hours showed a rapid decline in lactate levels, and by two hours he was asymptomatic.

title: Uterine arteriovenous malformations, clinical and radiological considerations: A report of two cases
case_summary: A 21-year-old G2P2 woman presented with profuse per vaginal bleeding. She denied any prior surgical procedures, dilatation and curettage, or use of pharmacologic contraceptives. On examination, she appeared pale with a hemoglobin concentration of 3.2 g/dL; she was afebrile and hemodynamically stable. Transabdominal ultrasound revealed an enlarged uterus with an anechoic mass in the posterior lower uterine segment; the endometrial lining was thin, and there were no signs of intra- or extra-uterine gestation. Color Doppler imaging showed a hypervascular mass with a yin-yang flow pattern, color aliasing, and flow reversal. Spectral Doppler interrogation demonstrated a peak systolic velocity of 71.5 cm/s and a resistive index of 0.42. A contrast-enhanced CT angiogram of the pelvis showed a complex cystic lesion with vivid central enhancement within the posterior myometrium, multiple dilated tortuous arterial feeders from bilateral uterine and gonadal arteries, and early venous drainage int

title: Splenic artery pseudoaneurysm: Challenges of non-invasive and endovascular diagnosis and management
case_summary: A 47-year-old man with chronic pancreatitis secondary to alcohol excess and multiple pancreatic pseudocysts presented with acute epigastric abdominal pain. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed a 3.8 cm expansile lesion at the pancreatic tail with peripheral low density and a centrally enhancing component of 1.3 cm; this component was slightly hypodense to the splenic artery in the arterial phase and slightly hyperdense on delayed phase images, raising the suspicion of a splenic artery pseudoaneurysm. He was transferred for angiography, which demonstrated no evidence of pseudoaneurysm or active hemorrhage. The patient was discharged with plans for repeat imaging in one month. On re-admission, triple-phase CT showed that the lesion had increased to 5.3 cm with the central component now measuring 2.6 cm; it remained hypodense relative to the splenic artery on arterial phase images and isodense to the portal vein on delayed phase images. Given the prior negative angiograph

title: Subarachnoid hemorrhage due to middle cerebral artery dissection mimicking aneurysm - Case report
case_summary: A 74-year-old woman presented with a 4-day history of severe headache and neck pain. Her Glasgow Coma Scale score was 15, and she had no focal neurological deficits. Brain MRI showed subarachnoid hemorrhage in the left Sylvian fissure without acute infarction. Hunt and Hess grade was II. CT angiography demonstrated a 5 mm conical-shaped outpouching at the left middle cerebral artery bifurcation and a separate 3 mm saccular aneurysm at the basilar tip. Routine blood tests, including electrolytes, blood counts, coagulation profile, and lipid panel, were normal. Neurosurgical consultation recommended surgical clipping of the presumed ruptured MCA aneurysm.

title: Inferior Vena Cava Thrombosis in a Patient with Factor V Leiden Syndrome Presenting with Scrotal Pain
case_summary:
A 39-year-old man presented with right flank pain that progressed over two days to severe back pain, bilateral flank pain, scrotal pain, and bilateral thigh pain. He had no significant medical history. Analgesics administered at a local health center relieved his back pain, but his testicular and thigh pain worsened.  
On examination, blood pressure was 120/80 mmHg, pulse 130 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, and temperature 37 degreesC. The leg-raising test was negative, and the neurovascular status of both legs was intact. Scrotal examination was unremarkable; both the cremasteric reflex and Prehn's sign were negative. There was no edema or swelling of the lower limbs.  
Laboratory studies showed a D-dimer level of 17 mg/L (normal, 0-0.49 mg/L), creatinine 124 umol/L (normal, 64-110 umol/L), white blood cell count 10?10^3/L (normal, 4-10?10^3/L), and C-reactive protein 5 mg/L (normal, 0-5 mg/L). Prothrombin time was 13.8 seconds (normal, 9.4-12.5 seconds), with normal APTT

title: A case of a paediatric chondromyxoid fibroma-like osteosarcoma
case_summary:
A 12-year-old girl with no significant medical history presented with a 3-month history of a painful swelling of the right thigh, causing a limp. She denied fever, weight loss, or other systemic symptoms. On examination, there was a prominent, non-erythematous swelling at the distal end of the right femur with restricted knee mobility but no signs of local inflammation.

Plain radiographs of the right femur showed a diaphyseal-metaphyseal periosteal reaction with a sunburst-like appearance at the distal femur. CT imaging revealed a hypodense, septated cortico-medullary lesion extending from the metaphysis into the diaphysis, with medullary hyperdensity, cortical irregularities, periosteal reaction, circumferential peri-osseous extension, and displacement of adjacent soft tissues. 

MRI demonstrated an expansive, osteolytic lesion involving the metaphysis, diaphysis, and epiphysis of the distal femur, infiltrating both cortical and medullary bone and extending into adjacent soft tissues

title: Dynamic thromboembolic left ventricular outflow tract obstruction after aggressive procoagulant treatment in hemorrhagic shock: a case report
case_summary: A 56-year-old woman with no prior medical conditions suffered an out-of-hospital cardiac arrest. She had complained of right calf pain after a long-distance flight. Emergency crews found pulseless electrical activity and administered tenecteplase (100 mg) for presumed pulmonary embolism. Spontaneous circulation returned, and she was transferred intubated and unresponsive (GCS 3) with hemodynamic shock (MAP 92 mm Hg, HR 133 beats/min) requiring epinephrine (5.5 ug/kg/min) and norepinephrine (4.5 ug/kg/min). A CT scan showed no pulmonary embolism. Soon after, hemoglobin dropped markedly; repeat CT revealed a hemoperitoneum from a traumatic liver rupture. She underwent emergency laparotomy, hepatic repair, and abdominal packing, during which she received massive transfusion (packed red blood cells, fresh-frozen plasma, platelets) and procoagulant factors (fibrinogen, antithrombin, four-factor PCC). The next day she developed abdominal compartment syndrome, acute liver failure, and progres

title: A rare case of asymptomatic retroperitoneal and thigh femoral nerve schwannoma
case_summary:
A 29-year-old man underwent a routine health examination. He was asymptomatic, with unremarkable vital signs and physical examination. Medical history included surgical repair of an inguinal hernia at age 3, foam sclerotherapy for superficial varicose veins at 19, and left varicocele repair at 20. Family history was notable for premature aortic dissection in his mother and hematologic malignancies in other relatives. Laboratory studies showed mild elevations of total cholesterol, total bilirubin, urate, and iron; all other values, including markers for lymphoma, were within normal limits.

Abdominal ultrasound revealed a 12 ? 7 cm inhomogeneous, minimally vascularized mass in the lower left abdominal quadrant and fatty liver changes. A multiphasic CT scan of the abdomen, pelvis, and upper thighs demonstrated a sharply marginated, slightly lobulated mass in the left retroperitoneum, extending from the lower pole of the left kidney to the distal third of the left thigh. The lesion measur

title: Hodgkin lymphoma presenting as paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration: A case report
case_summary: A 49-year-old man with bipolar disorder, depression, and migraines presented with a 4-week history of progressive ataxia, dysarthria, and vertigo. An MRI of the brain and spinal cord was unremarkable. Lumbar puncture revealed a CSF pleocytosis (WBC 159 cells/uL; 78% lymphocytes), elevated protein, and positive oligoclonal bands; extensive infectious testing in blood and CSF was negative. He received intravenous steroids for presumed autoimmune cerebellar syndrome with minimal improvement. Repeat CSF analysis still showed pleocytosis (WBC 40 cells/uL); cytology and lymphoma markers were negative. An autoimmune movement-disorder panel was positive for anti-Tr antibodies in the CSF (titer 1:128) and glutamic acid decarboxylase (GAD) antibodies in the serum. Malignancy screening with bone marrow biopsy and flow cytometry, CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis, and testicular ultrasound revealed no abnormalities. A whole-body PET scan showed four small periaortic and aortocaval lymph nodes (

title: Primary Spinal Germ Cell Tumors: A Case Analysis and Review of Treatment Paradigms
case_summary:
A 45-year-old man reported one year of gradually worsening numbness and weakness in both lower extremities, with marked decline over the past four months leading to dependence on a walker. He also had intermittent urinary incontinence and chronic constipation.  

On examination, sensation to light touch, pinprick, and proprioception was diminished from the T2 level downward, more on the right than the left. Motor testing revealed 0/5 strength in the right leg and 4/5 in the left leg. Bilateral clonus and muscle spasms were noted in the lower extremities.  

Magnetic resonance imaging of the spine showed an intramedullary lesion in the upper thoracic cord with diffuse contrast enhancement and cord expansion. Imaging of the remainder of the neuraxis was unremarkable. Testicular ultrasound and CT scans of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis were normal. Serum and cerebrospinal fluid levels of ?-fetoprotein and ?-HCG were within normal limits.  

Differential diagnoses included primary spinal c

title: A large vaginal stone on top of ureteric stent migration through a poucho-vaginal fistula after radical cystectomy and ileal neobladder reconstruction - A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 69-year-old woman underwent radical cystectomy with orthotopic ileal neobladder reconstruction in August 2020 for T3bN0 grade I squamous-cell carcinoma of the bladder. Postoperatively, she developed a left ureteric stricture managed with a ureteric stent and bilateral percutaneous nephrostomies (PCNs). In January 2021, she had an extensive right lower-limb deep venous thrombosis treated with anticoagulation. That same month, the left PCN slipped, and the ureteric stent protruded through the urethra while its upper coil remained in the renal hilum; the stent was pushed back into the pouch but the patient then defaulted on follow-up for three years.

In March 2024, she presented with a firm right subcostal anterior abdominal wall mass. Ultrasound showed a well-defined hypoechoic solid lesion (10.3 ? 4 cm) within the right rectus muscle, with internal vascularity, calcific foci, and cystic areas, suggesting a desmoid tumor or a secondary deposit from her bladder cancer. Ultrasound-guide

title: From Gut to Gray Matter: A Case Report of Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome in a Pediatric Patient With Celiac Disease
case_summary: A 9-year-old child with a history of celiac disease and dermatitis herpetiformis presented to the emergency department with a 2-month history of vomiting and loose stools and a recent 20-minute seizure. On examination, the child was pale, emaciated, and drowsy (Glasgow Coma Scale score 10/15), with a generalized rash, increased muscle tone, and hyperreflexia; the rest of the systemic examination was unremarkable. In the ER, the patient was tachycardic, hypertensive, and had an oxygen saturation of 65% on room air; supplemental oxygen, intravenous ceftriaxone, acyclovir, and hydralazine were administered. Laboratory tests revealed: hemoglobin 8.8 g/dL, hematocrit 28.5%, red-cell distribution width 24.2%, white-cell count 17.7?10^9/L (81.6% neutrophils), sodium 135 mmol/L, potassium 3.3 mmol/L, calcium 7.1 mmol/L, C-reactive protein 102 mg/L; arterial blood gas showed pH 7.27, PCO2 55.6 mmHg, PO2 105.1 mmHg, bicarbonate 25.1 mEq/L. After intubation in the pediatric ICU, a noncontrast CT

title: Diffuse neonatal hemangiomatosis with a single atypical cutaneous hemangioma
case_summary: A 39-week-gestation male infant was born to a healthy primiparous woman admitted for preeclampsia and a suspicious fetal brain mass. A 36-week fetal US was normal; at 39 weeks, US for fetal weight estimation revealed a  hyperechoic mass in the fetal right frontal lobe without detectable vascular flow. The infant was delivered vaginally, birth weight 3156 g, Apgar scores 9/10. Physical examination was unremarkable; no skin lesions were noted. On day 1, noncontrast cranial CT showed three well-circumscribed, round hyperdense intra-axial lesions of varying size in the right frontal lobe with extensive surrounding edema and a smaller hyperdense lesion in the left frontal lobe. On day 6, skin discoloration of the right scrotal sac prompted scrotal and abdominal US, which revealed a right hydrocele and a 4-cm predominantly hyperechoic liver mass with central hypoechoic area in the left hepatic lobe. On day 13, contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen demonstrated an avid peripheral nodular arteri

title: Diagnosis of spontaneous secondary tension pneumothorax following apparent recovery from coronavirus disease 2019 pneumonitis: a case report
case_summary:
A 51-year-old man presented initially with a 4-day history of shortness of breath, dry cough, and weakness. A lateral flow test was positive for SARS-CoV-2; chest x-ray showed bilateral patchy opacification. His oxygen saturation remained above 94% on room air, and he was discharged home with a pulse oximeter. 

Twelve days after symptom onset, he re-presented with oxygen saturation of 78% and chest x-ray-documented progression of pneumonitis. He was treated with oxygen, dexamethasone, doxycycline, and amoxicillin; the antibiotics were stopped when procalcitonin was < 0.25 ?g/L and he remained afebrile. A CTPA ruled out pulmonary embolism. After 3 days, he maintained saturation on room air with improved inflammatory markers and was discharged to complete a 10-day course of dexamethasone.

Twenty-three days after symptom onset (8 days post-discharge), he experienced 48 hours of progressively worsening shortness of breath. In the emergency department, examination revealed reduced breath

title: Pediatric Ovarian Yolk Sac Tumor Mimicking Appendicitis: A Case Report From a Low?Resource Setting
case_summary: A 6?year?old girl presented with a one?month history of abdominal pain that began in the right inguinal region and later became generalized. She reported slight abdominal fullness and intermittent low?grade fevers, and for the past two weeks she experienced postprandial, non?projectile vomiting. She denied weight loss, night sweats, cough, chest pain, abnormal vaginal discharge, bowel or urinary changes. On examination, she was alert but mildly pale. Vital signs: temperature 39.4  degreesC, heart rate 149 beats/min, respiratory rate 23 breaths/min, blood pressure 98/67 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Abdominal examination revealed slight distension with symmetrical contours, generalized tenderness most pronounced in the right iliac region, and reduced bowel sounds. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations were normal. Laboratory studies showed a white?cell count of 12.07 ? 10^9/L, hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL, and platelets 440 ? 10^9/L. Serum creatinine was 30 ?mol/L, blood ur

title: Iatrogenic uterine injury leading to uterine arteriovenous malformation
case_summary:
A 34-year-old woman presented with persistent vaginal bleeding for 2 weeks after undergoing a dilation and curettage for abortion. Her vital signs were stable, and hemoglobin was 11.6 mg/dL. Internal examination was deferred because of active bleeding. Transvaginal ultrasound showed a linear defect in the right anterior uterine wall, interpreted as uterine wall perforation, and endometrial thickening, thought to be reactionary to the procedure. Contrast-enhanced CT of the pelvis demonstrated a partial-thickness defect in the same uterine wall without hematoma or free air; she was discharged for conservative management.

Four weeks later, she returned with ongoing vaginal bleeding. Hemoglobin was 10.8 mg/dL. Repeat transvaginal ultrasound again showed a thickened endometrium. Color Doppler imaging revealed tortuous vessels in the myometrium extending into the endometrium. Spectral Doppler demonstrated high vascularity with a peak systolic velocity of 45 cm/s.

title: Spontaneous hepatic artery dissection--a rare presentation of fibromuscular dysplasia
case_summary:
A 43-year-old Caucasian woman was admitted for investigation of a transient ischaemic attack characterized by expressive dysphasia and left hemiparesis following cardiac stress testing. Her medical history included primary hypertension diagnosed at age 19, an iatrogenic left main coronary artery dissection treated with bypass grafting in 2007, an internal carotid artery dissection in 2001, and recurrent TIAs. 

While inpatient, she developed sudden severe right upper quadrant abdominal pain associated with dizziness and hyperventilation. Initial liver function tests were normal, but subsequent tests showed a mixed hepatocellular pattern: alkaline phosphatase 246 U/L (53-141), GGT 433 U/L (<38), ALT 508 U/L (<34), AST 760 U/L (<31), and total bilirubin 22 ?mol/L (<20). 

Extensive evaluation for common causes of liver injury revealed:  
? Negative viral hepatic serology.  
? Paracetamol level <10 mg/L.  
? Autoimmune serologies, including ANA, ANCA, anti-smooth muscle, and anti-mitochon

title: Dermatofibrosarcoma presenting as a nodule in the breast of a 75-year-old woman: a case report
case_summary: A 75-year-old woman presented with a new lump in her left breast. She had undergone right mastectomy, axillary dissection, radiotherapy, and reconstruction for invasive ductal carcinoma 21 years earlier. Her medical history included hypertension, a pacemaker for arrhythmia, and anticoagulation with acenocoumarol for pulmonary embolism. On physical examination, a firm, nodular lesion was palpable in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast without overlying skin changes. Mammography revealed a dish-shaped skin nodule measuring approximately 1.4 cm in the upper outer quadrant. Breast ultrasound confirmed a 14 ? 8 mm lesion in the dermal/subcutaneous layer, and Doppler imaging showed marked hypervascularity. Magnetic resonance imaging could not be performed because of the pacemaker. On the basis of these imaging features, a probable diagnosis of angiosarcoma was made.

title: Metastatic yolk sac tumor masquerading as multifocal hepatocellular carcinoma in a young adult: A case report
case_summary:
A 19-year-old man with no chronic illnesses presented with a 2-month history of vague, colicky abdominal pain, reduced appetite, weight loss, and intermittent diarrhea with occasional blood mixed in the stools. There was no family history of inflammatory bowel disease or malignancy, and no significant past surgical history. He smoked only briefly in the past.  
On examination, he was afebrile with mild right upper quadrant tenderness. There was no lymphadenopathy, and both digital rectal and scrotal examinations were unremarkable.  
Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL, white blood cells 12.2?10^3/?L, platelets 375?10^3/?L, ALT 61 U/L, AST 44 U/L, ALP 120 U/L, bilirubin 12 ?mol/L, INR 1.4, alpha-fetoprotein 2492 IU/mL (reference 0-6), beta-hCG 4 mIU/mL, and negative viral serologies including HIV, HBsAg, and anti-HCV.  
Abdominal ultrasound revealed an enlarged liver (19.7 cm) with multiple heterogeneous hyperechoic lesions (largest 9.9?9.7 cm in the right lobe and 10?8.3 cm

title: Delayed ethylene glycol poisoning presenting with abdominal pain and multiple cranial and peripheral neuropathies: a case report
case_summary:
A 53-year-old Afro-Caribbean man presented with a two-day history of vomiting, hiccups, and abdominal pain. He was initially treated for presumed gastroenteritis and discharged, but four days later he reported continued epigastric and lower abdominal pain, ongoing vomiting, and no bowel movements or urine output for four days. His history was notable only for hypercholesterolemia; he smoked ten cigarettes per day and denied alcohol or recent travel. 

On examination he was apyrexial and clinically dehydrated, with a pulse of 108 beats per minute and blood pressure of 145/82 mm Hg. The abdomen was tender but soft, without peritoneal signs. A urethral catheter was inserted; urine dipstick was strongly positive for blood and protein, and positive for glucose and leukocytes. 

Laboratory studies showed:  
- Sodium 131 mmol/L, potassium 6.7 mmol/L  
- Urea 45.2 mmol/L, creatinine 1885 umol/L  
- C-reactive protein 421 mg/L, neutrophils 25.9 ? 10^9/L, ESR 100 mm/h  
- Arterial blood gas on 2

title: A Rare Finding of Falciform Ligament Thrombosis as a Sequel of Acute Pancreatitis
case_summary:
A 55-year-old woman presented with a one-day history of colicky epigastric and right-upper-quadrant pain accompanied by vomiting. She had known cholelithiasis and noted that the pain resembled her previous biliary colic; she had no prior episodes of pancreatitis. She was a nonsmoker, consumed nine units of alcohol per week, and had no family history of bleeding or clotting disorders. Her only regular medication was combined hormone replacement therapy.

On admission, vital signs were normal. Abdominal examination revealed point tenderness in the right upper quadrant without organomegaly or peritonism. Laboratory studies showed: alanine aminotransferase 275 U/L, bilirubin 54 ?mol/L, amylase 1900 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 80 U/L, C-reactive protein <6 mg/L, and white-cell count 18 ? 10^9/L.

An abdominal ultrasound demonstrated cholelithiasis without evidence of common bile duct obstruction. She was treated for presumed gallstone pancreatitis with intravenous fluids and analgesia.

Three

title: A rare cause of chronic mesenteric ischemia from fibromuscular dysplasia: a case report
case_summary: A 44-year-old Caucasian woman with a history of hypertension and preeclampsia who had taken oral contraceptive pills for 15 years presented with two weeks of intractable colicky abdominal pain that worsened with oral intake and was associated with diarrhea and nonbilious vomiting. The diarrhea was sometimes watery and hemoccult positive; gastroccult testing of vomitus was negative. On examination, she had stage III hypertension out of proportion to her risk factors and diffuse periumbilical tenderness without rebound or guarding. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 16.1?10^9/L with 91% neutrophils, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 79 mm/hr, and a C-reactive protein level of 100 mg/L. An abdominal CT scan demonstrated nonspecific colitis. She received intravenous metronidazole and ciprofloxacin, but all microbial cultures, including three for Clostridium difficile, were negative. Urinalysis revealed nephritic-range proteinuria. Tests for P-ANCA, C-ANCA, ASCA, ANA, celiac

title: Intramuscular Arteriovenous Hemangioma of Thigh: A Case Report and Review of Literature
case_summary: An 8-year-old boy presented with a swelling in the anterolateral aspect of the right lower thigh of 1 year's duration. The mass had gradually increased in size and had become painful over the past 2 months. There was no history of trauma, no constitutional symptoms, and no bleeding tendencies. Physical examination revealed an asymmetric, boggy, non-compressible, non-pulsatile swelling in the anteromedial right lower thigh measuring approximately 10 cm ? 7 cm. The overlying skin was normal without discoloration, venous engorgement, or inflammatory signs. There was fullness of the medial para-patellar fossa without joint effusion, tenderness over the quadriceps (more anteromedial), no distal neurovascular deficit or bruit, equal limb lengths, and full knee range of motion. Initial differential diagnoses included lipoma, cold abscess, suprapatellar bursitis, synovitis, sarcoma, fibroma, or other extra-articular soft tissue tumors. Magnetic resonance imaging of the thigh showed a soft tiss

title: Prenatal Diagnosis of Adrenal Neuroblastoma: A Case Report with a Brief Review of the Literature
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
case_summary: A 38-year-old gravida 2, para 1 woman was referred at 37 weeks of gestation for evaluation of a fetal abdominal mass. Her medical history was unremarkable. Detailed ultrasonography showed a 45?45 mm left suprarenal cystic mass with homogeneous content and intracystic septations suggesting hemorrhage; color Doppler imaging demonstrated peripheral vascularization without internal flow. The fetal spleen and left kidney appeared normal, and no other structural anomalies were identified. Maternal urine homovanillic acid and vanillylmandelic acid levels were within normal limits. Two weeks later, follow-up sonography revealed a mass of unchanged size, and the fetal biophysical profile was 8 of 8. The infant was delivered by cesarean section for breech presentation at 39 weeks 4 days, weighing 3100 g, with Apgar scores of 8 and 10 at 1 and 5 minutes. Postnatal sonography confirmed a 48?50 mm left adrenal mass; one week later, the lesion had increased to 65?70 mm in size.

title: Artery of Percheron infarction a rare anatomical variant and a diagnostic challenge: Case report
case_summary: A 55-year-old man was found unconscious in front of his home and brought to the emergency department. He had no significant medical history and took no long-term medications. On arrival, his Glasgow Coma Scale score was 4. He was afebrile, blood pressure was 100/55 mm Hg, pulse 45-55 beats/min, respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, and temperature 32.5  degreesC. Pupils were poorly reactive bilaterally, with a dilated right pupil. He flexed his arm and extended his leg to pain. Routine laboratory tests, including complete blood count, electrolytes, renal and liver function, and arterial blood gases, were all within normal limits. Cardiovascular exam and ECG were unremarkable. A noncontrast head CT showed no hemorrhage or ischemia. Transcranial Doppler demonstrated patency of the carotid, basilar, and vertebral arteries. He was admitted to the ICU for respiratory support. Four hours later, he regained consciousness but remained somnolent. He had bilateral ptosis, and examination revealed ver

title: Giant Chondroid Syringoma Radiologically Mimicking Malignancy
case_summary:
A 59-year-old man presented with a painless mass on the anterior aspect of his upper thigh, slowly enlarging over 5 years. On examination, there was a firm, mobile, 5???3 cm nodule covered by normal skin. He was otherwise healthy. Laboratory studies--including complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, urinalysis, aspartate aminotransferase, alanine aminotransferase, blood urea nitrogen, and creatinine--were normal. 

Sonography revealed a mixed echogenic solid mass measuring approximately 4.5???3???2 cm. Doppler ultrasound showed vessels predominantly at the periphery of the mass. Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated a well-circumscribed, lobular, subcutaneous 4.7???2.9???2.2 cm mass with heterogeneous enhancement, predominantly in its peripheral portion, after intravenous contrast administration. Diffusion-weighted imaging showed areas of increased signal intensity compatible with a highly cellular tumor.

Based on these imaging findings, the differential diagnosis incl

title: Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome secondary to chronic cocaine abuse: Case report
case_summary: A 36-year-old man with a history of chronic headache presented with a 24-hour history of worsening global headache reaching 10/10 intensity, followed by a focal tonic-clonic seizure of the right upper limb, blurred vision, and transient loss of consciousness without sphincter relaxation. On arrival, he was alert, hypertensive, and had no focal neurologic deficits. Noncontrast cranial CT showed a Fisher II subarachnoid hemorrhage without aneurysmal dilations. A follow-up CT scan demonstrated a left basal temporal subcortical intra-axial hyperdense lesion consistent with intraparenchymal hemorrhage surrounded by hypodense vasogenic edema. Cerebral panangiography revealed irregular contours of the right cervical internal carotid artery with a 5 mm lateral pseudoaneurysm and slight distal stenosis, multiple areas of stenosis in medium and small caliber pial arteries across all vascular territories, and evidence of an old dissection of the right cervical internal carotid artery. Autoimmune

title: Inferior vena cava resection for a leiomyosarcoma: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
final_diagnosis: Leiomyosarcoma of the inferior vena cava
case_summary: A 53-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presented with a two-year history of vague, paroxysmal abdominal pain localized to the right hypochondrium, occurring throughout the day. She denied fever, vomiting, or jaundice but reported a 10-kg weight loss over this period. Her general condition was well preserved; vital signs were stable. On abdominal examination, a fixed, well-limited 4-cm mass was palpable in the right hypochondrium; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable. Laboratory tests, including liver and renal function, were within normal limits. Six months earlier, an abdominal ultrasound had shown no abnormalities. A contrast-enhanced CT scan revealed a heterogeneous, lobulated soft-tissue mass arising from the anterior wall of the inferior vena cava, measuring 50 ? 37 ? 46 mm, with invasion of the right renal vein. On MRI, the lesion was of low signal on T1, T2, and diffusion sequences, demonstrated intraluminal extension, and showed enhancement af

title: Huge Intravascular Tumor Extending to the Heart: Leiomyomatosis
case_summary: A 49-year-old woman was admitted with intermittent dyspnea, palpitations, and dizziness. Four years earlier, she had undergone a myomectomy for uterine leiomyoma. Nine months before admission, she was diagnosed with right iliac vein thrombosis and was receiving warfarin. On examination, vital signs were within normal limits except for bilateral pretibial edema. Chest radiography was unremarkable. Magnetic resonance venography demonstrated a mass originating in the right internal iliac vein and extending into the inferior vena cava. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a mass extending from the inferior vena cava into the right atrium. Transesophageal echocardiography showed two small membrane-like attachments of the mass to the right atrial endocardium. Thoracic, abdominal, and pelvic magnetic resonance imaging confirmed an intravascular mass continuous from the right iliac vein through the inferior vena cava into the right atrium, with venous occlusion.

title: Cystic adventitial disease of the popliteal artery: A case report with review of literature
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 40-year-old man presented with a 3-month history of left calf pain that began after walking short distances and was relieved by rest. He had no significant medical history, history of trauma, or known vascular risk factors. On examination, there was no skin discoloration or swelling of the lower extremities, and bilateral femoral, popliteal, and posterior tibial pulses were palpable at rest.  

A lower-limb arterial Doppler ultrasound showed a focal hypoechoic area around the popliteal artery with a regular arterial wall, consistent with extrinsic compression and significant stenosis of the artery during plantar flexion of the foot. Peak systolic velocity at the stenotic segment was 400 cm/s, with pronounced damping of flow downstream. The popliteal vein was mildly dilated over a short segment without thrombosis.  

Subsequent computed tomography angiography of the lower limbs demonstrated an approximately 25 % caliber discrepancy of the popliteal artery, with an intraparietal, fluid

title: Differential diagnosis of a ring-enhancing brain lesion in the setting of metastatic cancer and a mycotic aneurysm
case_summary:
An 80-year-old man with terminal, poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the base of the tongue (cT2N2b), status post tracheostomy, gastrostomy, and arterial embolization of multiple branches of the bilateral external carotid arteries 2 weeks earlier for hemoptysis, presented with 3 days of left upper- and lower-extremity weakness. He denied sensory changes, headache, fever, or other neurologic symptoms. He took only acetaminophen. Vital signs were within normal limits. Neurologic examination showed 4/5 strength in the left upper and lower extremities; NIH Stroke Scale score was 2. Laboratory tests were unremarkable. No blood cultures were obtained.

Noncontrast CT of the brain revealed a hypodense region at the right parietal vertex with a 15 mm focal circular lesion surrounded by edema, plus a smaller adjacent focus; there was no hemorrhage or acute infarct. CT angiography of the head and neck showed a newly developed large, eccentric, saccular aneurysm of the distal right

title: Tubulocystic Renal Cell Carcinoma of the Native Kidney in a Renal Transplant Recipient: A Rare Case Report
case_summary: A 65-year-old African American man with end-stage renal disease secondary to hypertension and insulin-dependent diabetes underwent a right nephrectomy for renal cell carcinoma 25 years earlier. In 2010, during evaluation for kidney transplantation, contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen showed multiple low-attenuation renal cysts and a 3.1?2.6 cm nonenhancing lesion in the upper pole of the left native kidney, consistent with a Bosniak class II renal cyst; no further follow-up was recommended. He received a living-related kidney transplant in 2012 with induction rabbit antithymocyte globulin followed by maintenance tacrolimus, mycophenolate mofetil, and prednisone. Five months posttransplant, he presented with gross hematuria and swelling of the left leg. Abdominal CT revealed an enlarging, minimally enhancing hypodense 3.3?3.8 cm cystic mass in the left native kidney upper pole (Bosniak class IV) that was "suspicious for RCC." Venous duplex ultrasonography of the left leg demonstrated de

title: Multiple liver abscesses secondary to Listeria monocytogenes complicated by hepatic artery mycotic aneurysm
case_summary: A woman in her 50s with no known medical history presented with 5 days of right upper quadrant abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. The pain was gradual and accompanied by multiple episodes of yellow emesis and one episode of hematemesis. She denied fever, chills, night sweats, diarrhea, or constipation. She took no medications and denied tobacco, alcohol, or drug use. On examination, she was afebrile (36.7 degreesC), with a heart rate of 87/min, blood pressure 139/77 mm Hg, respiratory rate 16/min, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. The abdomen was soft, non-tender, non-distended, without guarding. Laboratory studies showed a leukocytosis of 15.0?10^9 cells/L (78% neutrophils), HbA1c 14.2%, and normal transaminases (AST 14 U/L, ALT 14 U/L), alkaline phosphatase 126 U/L, total bilirubin 1.1 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 0.1 mg/dL, and albumin 3.0 g/dL. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis and a multiphase liver protocol showed three peripherally enhancing, cystic lesio

title: Ischemic stroke in a young patient with Fahr's disease: a case report
case_summary:
A 36-year-old previously healthy Indonesian man presented with sudden onset of left-sided weakness while working. He denied any prior medical or neurologic disorders and had no family history of cognitive impairment, movement disorders, or early stroke. On examination in the emergency department, his blood pressure was 156/95 mmHg, pulse 91 beats/min, respirations 16 breaths/min, and temperature 36  degreesC. General examination was unremarkable. Neurologic examination showed left hemiparesis (muscle power grade 4), mild dysarthria, and left central facial palsy; there were no cognitive deficits, extrapyramidal signs, or ataxia. NIH Stroke Scale score was 4.

Noncontrast head CT revealed extensive, symmetric calcifications in the bilateral basal ganglia, internal capsules, thalami, cerebral subcortical white matter, and cerebellar dentate nuclei. Initial laboratory studies including blood count, coagulation profile, and chemistry were normal. Serum calcium, phosphorus, magnesium, alkal

title: Gallbladder metastasis of renal cell carcinoma presenting as a hypervascular polypoid lesion: case report of two cases with immunohistochemical analysis
case_summary: A 73-year-old man, who underwent right nephrectomy 7 years earlier for stage I clear cell renal cell carcinoma, was asymptomatic when a follow-up CT scan at 6 years after surgery detected a 6.4-mm polypoid lesion in the body of the gallbladder. One year later, repeat CT showed that the lesion had more than doubled in size. Laboratory tests, including serum CA19-9, were within normal limits. Ultrasonography revealed a 13.9 ? 10 mm smooth-surfaced lesion in the gallbladder body. Color Doppler US demonstrated hypervascularity within the lesion. Contrast-enhanced CT in the arterial phase showed a polypoid mass with very high and homogeneous contrast enhancement. Based on these clinical backgrounds and imaging findings, the possibility of gallbladder malignancy, including metastatic cancer from RCC, was considered, and laparoscopic cholecystectomy was performed.

title: Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumors--Diagnostic Pitfalls of Non-Diabetic Severe Hypoglycemia: Literature Review and Case Report
case_summary:
A 57-year-old woman was admitted for a 1-month history of daily episodes of dizziness, fatigue, cold sweats, tremors, changes in behavior, anxiety, and occasional seizures or altered consciousness. During a recent psychiatric hospitalization, a random capillary glucose measurement was 19 mg/dL. She denied alcohol use and any use of insulin or oral hypoglycemic agents. Her medical history was unremarkable, with no diabetes, endocrine syndromes, or significant illnesses. Her body-mass index was 25.6 kg/m2, with abdominal obesity; the rest of the physical examination was unremarkable.

On admission, she had repeated hypoglycemic episodes (plasma glucose < 55 mg/dL) not related to food intake, accompanied by confusion, dizziness, cold sweats, and tremor. Episodes were refractory to oral carbohydrates and required intravenous boluses of 33% glucose and continuous infusion of 10% glucose. Two incidental plasma glucose readings off infusion were ? 200 mg/dL (fasting 209 mg/dL, postprandial 22

title: Secondary hyperhomocysteinemia-related occlusive retinal vasculopathy: A case report
journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case_summary:
A 43-year-old man presented with a 2-year history of slowly progressive vision loss in both eyes, which had markedly worsened over the past 3 months. He also noted floaters and a rapid decrease in vision in the left eye. His medical history was notable for chronic kidney failure from post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (on peritoneal dialysis and erythropoietin) and well-controlled systemic hypertension (blood pressure 115/80 mmHg on beta- and alpha-blockers). He denied diabetes, prior ocular surgery, or significant eye disease; he was allergic to trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole.

On examination, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/200 in the right eye and 20/50 in the left eye. Intraocular pressure was 12 mmHg bilaterally. Slit-lamp examination showed clear corneas, rubeosis iridis, and mild nuclear sclerotic cataracts. Funduscopic examination revealed engorged, pale retinal arterioles; attenuated venules; diffuse RPE pigmentary changes; small vitreous hemorrhage and subhyaloid hemorr

title: Solid pseudopapillary neoplasm of the pancreas causing obstructive jaundice: Case report of a rare entity
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: A 32-year-old woman with no comorbidities presented with 12 hours of dull, aching epigastric pain radiating to the back. She had undergone laparoscopic cholecystectomy 3 months earlier for chronic cholecystitis. On examination, she was febrile (101  degreesF), tachycardic (110 bpm), and icteric. Abdominal examination revealed a 3 ? 3 cm firm, tender, ill-defined lump in the epigastrium beneath a right upper quadrant scar. Laboratory studies showed elevated serum bilirubin, ALT, AST, ALP, and GGT; disproportionate elevation of ALP with direct hyperbilirubinemia indicated cholestatic jaundice. Serum CA 19-9 (13.79 U/mL) and IgG4 (41.74 mg/dL) were within normal limits. Empiric intravenous piperacillin/tazobactam was started for presumed cholangitis. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen demonstrated an ill-defined, isodense solid mass in the pancreatic head with gradual delayed enhancement, causing obstruction of the main pancreatic and bile ducts and associated obstructive pancreatopathy

title: Post-thrombotic syndrome of the lower extremity associated with arteriovenous fistula: Three case reports
journal: Journal of Vascular Surgery Cases, Innovations and Techniques
case_summary: A 91-year-old woman presented with a 6-month history of deep vein thrombosis in her left common and external iliac veins and progressive worsening of left lower extremity swelling and pain. On examination, the left leg was swollen with overlying inflammation but no skin ulceration. Duplex ultrasonography of the left femoral vein demonstrated an occluded iliac segment and a high-velocity arterialized waveform within the femoral vein. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography in the arterial phase showed early opacification of the left common femoral vein, and in the venous phase revealed occlusion of the left common and external iliac veins with multiple enlarged collateral vessels.

title: Bizarre Parosteal Osteochondromatous Proliferation (Nora's Lesion) of the Hand: A Report of Two Atypical Cases
case_summary:
A 39-year-old woman underwent resection of a dorsal surface lesion on her right third metacarpal that had been diagnosed elsewhere as an osteochondroma. Within months she noted rapid recurrence of a firm, painful mass with increased swelling and progressive limitation of metacarpophalangeal motion (0 degrees to 40 degrees). She had no history of trauma. On examination, there was a well-healed dorsal incision overlying a prominent, non-tender, firm 2- to 3-cm mass; neurovascular function was intact. 

Plain radiographs showed an ossified mass bordering the dorsal aspect of the distal third metacarpal. CT scanning demonstrated continuity between the medullary cavity of the lesion and that of the underlying bone. MRI revealed a juxtacortical mass with mature ossification, containing both cortical and medullary components. Review of imaging obtained before the initial resection showed an exophytic lesion with a corticated rim and a cartilaginous cap and no medullary continuity. 

The diffe

title: Successful Treatment of Clostridium difficile Bacteremia with Aortic Mycotic Aneurysm in a Patient with Prior Endovascular Aortic Aneurysm Repair
case_summary: A 52-year-old man with a history of congestive heart failure, hypertension, alcohol abuse, and endovascular abdominal aortic aneurysm repair 4 years earlier initially presented to an outside hospital with abdominal pain and diarrhea. CT of the abdomen showed an expanding abdominal aortic aneurysm with a type II endoleak, and coil embolization of the inferior mesenteric artery was performed with thrombosis of the leak. He was discharged home but returned 1 month later with persistent abdominal pain. A follow-up CT scan showed further expansion of the aneurysm and a few gas bubbles around the endovascular graft. His white blood cell count was 14,000/mm3 (normal 4,000-10,000/mm3). He was transferred to our center, where two sets of blood cultures were drawn, and he was started on intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam and vancomycin. Vascular surgery recommended explantation of the endovascular graft with surgical bypass reconstruction.

title: Traumatic Aneurysm Involving the Posterior Communicating Artery
case_summary:
A 41-year-old man was brought to the emergency department after being punched and kicked multiple times in the head during an assault. He was comatose on arrival (GCS score of 3). Vital signs were: temperature 36.8  degreesC, heart rate 90/min, blood pressure 140/85 mm Hg, respiratory rate 14/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Physical examination showed facial bruises, fractures of the bilateral mandible and left mastoid process, and tenderness over several left ribs.

Toxicology on admission revealed cocaine at 0.224 mg/L and ethanol at 1.35 g/L in the blood. Noncontrast head CT demonstrated diffuse subarachnoid and subdural hemorrhages, areas of contusion, and skull fractures. CT angiography revealed a blister-like aneurysm of the left posterior communicating artery with active contrast extravasation. Given the high surgical risk, he was treated pharmacologically and underwent endovascular coiling of the left internal carotid artery and branches of the left posterior and an

title: Pseudoaneurysm Accompanied by Crowe Type IV Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip: A Case Report
case_summary: A 72-year-old woman with bilateral Crowe type IV developmental dysplasia of the hip and a history of mitral valve replacement on warfarin (1 mg daily) and aspirin (100 mg daily) presented with progressive enlargement and severe pain of her left buttock over several weeks without trauma. She had no relevant family history. Initial presentation to an emergency hospital occurred after self-injury in the setting of severe pain; she was found to be in hemorrhagic shock and had a hemoglobin level of 5.1 g/dL. After transfer for workup of anemia, including colonoscopy without a source of bleeding, she was referred for evaluation of a suspected hip-joint hematoma versus suppurative arthritis. On admission, her blood pressure was 93/58 mmHg, pulse 62/min, and temperature 35.9  degreesC. She had severe left hip motion pain with flexion contracture; the left buttock, groin, and proximal thigh were markedly enlarged with tense overlying skin. Laboratory studies showed WBC 10?700/uL, CRP 10.8 mg/dL

title: Incidental littoral cell angioma of the spleen
case_summary: A 52-year-old woman was evaluated for intermittent right upper-quadrant pain without jaundice, nausea, vomiting, or bowel-habit changes. Liver-function tests showed a cholestatic pattern (alkaline phosphatase 139 U/L, ?-glutamyltransferase 67 U/L; total bilirubin 6 ?mol/L). Complete blood count was normal. Abdominal ultrasound revealed cholelithiasis, a normal biliary tree, fatty liver infiltration, and incidentally a 3 cm, well-circumscribed, hyperechoic lesion at the inferior splenic pole without significant Doppler flow. Contrast-enhanced CT showed a hypodense, well-defined splenic lesion with some internal arterial and venous enhancement, becoming isodense on delayed images. On MRI, the lesion was hypointense on T1, hyperintense on T2, showed internal linear enhancement in the portal venous phase after gadolinium, and became isointense on delayed sequences. A Tc-99m-labeled red-blood-cell scan demonstrated a "cold" splenic defect. Ultrasound-guided core biopsy was nondiagnostic, sh

title: Gastrointestinal infantile hemangioma: A rare cause of digestive tract bleeding in children to consider
case_summary:
A 10-month-old female infant was evaluated for recurrent hematochezia. The first episode at 7 months was of little quantity and treated empirically as an intestinal infection with antibiotics without resolution. Over the next 2 months, there were two more episodes of rectal bleeding. An abdominal ultrasound excluded intussusception. A possible food allergy was suspected, and she was switched from dairy formula to an elemental diet, but hematochezia persisted. At 10 months, she was referred to pediatric gastroenterology.

On evaluation, vital signs were normal. Physical examination showed a well-colored infant with a soft, nondistended, nontender abdomen and no visceromegaly or masses. Laboratory tests revealed hemoglobin 8.9 g/dL, hematocrit 26.8%, leukocytes 9,260/mm?, platelets 243,000/mm?; clotting times were normal (PT 15.8 s, PTT 23.4 s, INR 1.0). She was admitted to the pediatric ICU for transfusion support.

A colonoscopy showed small angiodysplasia lesions in the colon at the s

title: Dapsone?induced methemoglobinemia--A case report
case_summary:
A 23-year-old woman presented with a 1-week history of worsening blurry vision and mild headache that progressed to ataxia and difficulty ambulating. On examination, she had nystagmus, left-eye ophthalmoplegia, and diplopia; the remainder of her neurologic examination and a CT scan of the brain were normal. She was admitted to neurology for further workup to exclude multiple sclerosis. Her medical history was notable for fibromyalgia and pityriasis lichenoides et varioliformis acuta, for which she had been taking prednisone (30-50 mg/day) for 2 years and dapsone (150 mg/day). 

Three days into her admission, she developed ongoing hypoxia with pulse-oximetry saturations of 88%-92% while receiving 8 L of oxygen via Hudson mask. She complained of dyspnea and appeared tachypneic but denied cough, hemoptysis, chest pain, or any history of deep-vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, or asthma. Examination revealed mild central cyanosis of the lips and fingertips. Lung auscultation was clear, h

title: Infantile Hemangioendothelioma of the Parotid Gland
case_summary: A 4-month-old girl was brought for evaluation of a swelling over the left angle of the mandible that had been present for several weeks. The lesion was noted to be painless, soft, and nontender, with normal overlying skin and no regional lymphadenopathy. An ultrasound examination of the left parotid region using a 4-7-MHz linear transducer demonstrated a largely homogeneous, isoechoic mass replacing the parotid gland, with lobulated margins and internal echogenic septae. Color Doppler imaging revealed multiple dilated vascular channels within the lesion. Subsequent magnetic resonance imaging of the neck showed a lesion in the left parotid gland that was hyperintense on fat-suppressed T2-weighted and STIR sequences, contained thin internal septae, and demonstrated prominent flow voids. On post-contrast T1-weighted images, the mass enhanced homogeneously, with thin nonenhancing septae. The right parotid gland and overlying skin appeared normal.

title: Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy with Negative JC Virus PCR following Treatment of Follicular Lymphoma: Implications for Biologics in the Era of Targeted Cancer Therapy
case_summary: A 69-year-old man with stage IIIB follicular lymphoma, atrial fibrillation, and pulmonary sarcoidosis presented with two months of progressive dysarthria and right-sided weakness. He had completed six cycles of rituximab and bendamustine one year earlier for lymphoma. MRI of the brain showed multiple confluent T2-weighted FLAIR hyperintensities in the inferior frontal lobes and left corona radiata, and vascular imaging demonstrated no flow-limiting stenosis, occlusion, or aneurysm. Admission laboratory tests, including CBC, metabolic panel, rheumatologic markers (ANA negative; ANCA titer <1:20; complement C3 and C4 normal), inflammatory markers (CRP 0.16 mg/dL; ESR 17 mm/hr), and ACE level (27 mcg/L), were within normal limits. HIV and HCV screens were nonreactive; immunoglobulin levels were normal; syphilis and Lyme serologies were negative. Evaluation for lymphoma recurrence--including beta2-microglobulin, LDH, PET scan, and skin biopsy--revealed no evidence of disease. Lumbar punctu

title: Calcifying Nested Stromal-Epithelial Tumor: An Extremely Rare Hepatic Tumor
case_summary:
A 24-year-old woman presented with several months of constant, nonradiating right hypochondriac pain that progressed from mild to moderate intensity over 3 weeks and was temporarily relieved with analgesics. She also reported abdominal fullness and dyspepsia. She denied use of oral contraceptives or herbal medications and had no significant medical or surgical history. On examination, her liver border was palpable below the right costal margin; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed normal liver function tests (AST 27 IU/L, ALT 24 IU/L, alkaline phosphatase 97 IU/L, GGT 40 IU/L), a normal serum alpha-fetoprotein level, and negative hepatitis B and C serologies.

A triphasic dynamic CT scan of the liver demonstrated a smoothly marginated, well-defined lesion in segments V, VI, and VII, with an inferior exophytic component, central coarse, chunky intralesional calcifications, no convincing washout in the venous or delayed phases, and tumor thrombus

title: Primary diffuse leptomeningeal gliomatosis as a rare cause of pain in cervical spine
case_summary:
A 56-year-old woman with seropositive rheumatoid arthritis on long-term methotrexate presented in June 2013 with fluctuating cervical spine pain radiating to the occipital region. By early November, pain severity necessitated a 2-week hospitalization for intensive analgesic therapy. On October 27, she abruptly became noncommunicative and fixed her gaze. Examination revealed receptive and expressive aphasia and cervical paravertebral muscle tenderness without meningeal signs. Noncontrast brain CT was normal. Suspecting an acute ischemic stroke within the therapeutic window, she received intravenous thrombolysis, with rapid full recovery. Partial complex seizure was also considered. Neuropsychological testing showed mildly impaired memory and executive function. Internal, ophthalmologic, EEG, carotid/vertebral Doppler ultrasound, and CT angiography were unremarkable. She was discharged for thrombophilia screening and planned MRI.

On December 18, she was readmitted with sudden aphasia, d

title: A 39-year-old woman with transient convulsions and vision disturbances: a case report
case_summary:
A 39-year-old Chinese woman was admitted to the intensive care unit with acute pancreatitis after presenting three hours earlier with sudden abdominal pain. Three hours after admission, she had a brief generalized convulsion lasting approximately 10 seconds that resolved spontaneously. Fifteen minutes later, she reported complete loss of vision in both eyes. An ophthalmologic examination revealed no abnormalities.

Her history was notable for two prior episodes of pancreatitis. She also had untreated hypertension and hyperlipidemia, and a history of anxiety treated intermittently with propranolol, alprazolam, and long-term venlafaxine. She denied other systemic, infectious, or psychiatric illnesses.

On examination, her blood pressure was 145/77 mmHg, and other vital signs were within normal limits. She was alert and oriented. Pupils were equal, 3 mm in diameter, and reactive to light, but she was unable to see objects or count fingers at 30 cm, although she could perceive light. Optok

title: Diffuse Pancreatic Carcinoma with Hepatic Metastases
case_summary:
A 54-year-old man presented with 4 days of diffuse abdominal pain and distention. The pain began in the epigastrium, became generalized, and was associated with nausea but no vomiting or jaundice. He reported a 4-kg weight loss over 3 months. He drank alcohol only rarely, did not smoke, and had no pertinent family history.

On examination, his blood pressure was 120/60 mmHg, pulse 75/min, respiratory rate 21/min, and temperature 37.7 degreesC. There were no stigmata of biliary obstruction; palpation elicited diffuse abdominal tenderness.

Laboratory studies showed normal serum amylase, lipase, and IgG4 levels. Tumor markers were elevated: CA19-9 at 69.6 U/mL (normal ?37 U/mL) and CA242 at 82.1 U/mL (normal ?20 U/mL). Bilirubin, alpha-fetoprotein, and HBsAg were within normal limits.

Abdominal ultrasound revealed a diffusely enlarged, heterogeneous, hypoechoic pancreas with slight peripancreatic fat infiltration and no fluid collection. The liver contained multiple solid hypo- or hyper

title: Transhepatic Embolization of Congenital Intrahepatic Portosystemic Venous Shunts with Associated Aneurysms
case_summary:
A 45-year-old woman presented with several months of progressively worsening right upper quadrant abdominal pain and swelling, accompanied by confusion and an altered level of consciousness. She reported no fever, nausea, vomiting, or changes in bowel habits. Her medical history was unremarkable for hepatic, renal, or cardiovascular disease, and she denied prior abdominal surgery, trauma, liver biopsy, or alcohol abuse.

On examination, she was somnolent but hemodynamically stable. There was slight swelling and mild tenderness in the right upper quadrant without hepatosplenomegaly, rebound tenderness, or rigidity. Bowel sounds were normal. Laboratory studies showed normal liver-function tests and a normal blood ammonia level.

Abdominal ultrasound with color Doppler revealed a communication between the right portal vein and the right hepatic vein through an aneurysmal segment in liver segment VII. The liver contour was regular, parenchyma homogeneous, and no ascites was present. Contra

title: Ectopic pancreas appearing as a giant gastric cyst mimicking gastric lymphangioma: a case report and a brief review
final_diagnosis: Ectopic pancreas with serous oligocystic adenoma
case_summary:
A 44-year-old woman presented with a 6-month history of paroxysmal, dull epigastric pain that intensified and sometimes radiated to her back. She denied loss of appetite, postprandial vomiting, or gastrointestinal bleeding. Proton-pump inhibitors failed to relieve her symptoms. Physical examination and routine laboratory tests were unremarkable.

Contrast-enhanced CT showed a 5.8 ? 3.9 cm heterogeneous, cystic and solid submucosal mass arising from the thickened posterior wall of the gastric antrum proximal to the pylorus. The lesion had irregular mixed cystic and solid components with thick walls and intracystic fluid; the solid portion enhanced unevenly in the arterial phase, and the cystic wall showed persistent enhancement into the venous phase.

Esophagogastroduodenoscopy revealed a submucosal elevation from the posterior antral wall to the gastric angle with normal overlying mucosa. Endoscopic ultrasound demonstrated a smooth 5.5 ? 3.8 cm mixed cystic and solid, anechoic lesion i

title: A step-by-step diagnosis of exclusion in a twin pregnancy with acute respiratory failure due to non-fatal amniotic fluid embolism: a case report
case_summary:
A 34-year-old healthy primigravida with a dichorionic twin pregnancy at 31 weeks and 6 days presented with premature uterine contractions and rupture of membranes. She received an initial ritodrine infusion (0.10-0.3 mg/min) for 24 hours, then oral ritodrine, plus two doses of dexamethasone. Within 24 hours she developed tachycardia (140 bpm), bilateral basal crackles, mild fever (37.6  degreesC), and clinical pulmonary edema; ritodrine was stopped and she improved after intravenous furosemide. Antibiotics (amoxicillin/clavulanic acid and erythromycin) and nadroparin were started. Forty-eight hours later an uneventful cesarean delivery under spinal anesthesia (sensory level T4; 8 mg ropivacaine) was performed after 1.5 L IV preload. A few hours postoperatively she acutely developed dyspnea (RR 35/min), hypotension (75/45 mmHg), sinus tachycardia (123 bpm), severe hypoxemia (PaO? 6.25 kPa, cyanosis), and was intubated.  
Chest radiography showed bilateral pulmonary edema with pleural ef

title: Kawasaki Disease: Unusual Presentation with Retropharyngeal Involvement
case_summary: A 4-year-old boy presented with left submandibular swelling, odynophagia, and left head flexion. He had no rhinitis or nasal obstruction. In the emergency room, he had elevated WBC, CRP, and fibrinogen; ALT, AST, and LDH were normal; SARS-CoV-2 nasal swab was negative. Neck ultrasound showed multiple enlarged lymph nodes in left levels II-IV, with preserved hila and no colliquation. Examination revealed laterocervical lymphadenopathy, asymmetric tonsils (left > right), lateral oropharyngeal wall swelling, and purulent nasopharyngeal discharge. CT scan, performed for suspected parapharyngeal abscess, showed asymmetry of the nasopharyngeal airspace and multiple oval laterocervical lymphadenopathies bilaterally. The team treated him with intravenous antibiotics without surgery. Follow-up ultrasound showed increased lymph node size; MRI confirmed persistent obliteration of the left deep parapharyngeal and carotid spaces with retropharyngeal lymphadenopathy. Blood cultures, nasal secretion

title: Intravascular leiomyomatosis with cardiac extension: a case report
case_summary:
A 47-year-old multiparous woman was referred for evaluation of a mass in her inferior vena cava discovered on abdominal ultrasound. Over the preceding 2 months, she had experienced nausea, anorexia, and progressive bilateral lower-limb swelling. Her history was notable for partial hysterectomy and right adnexectomy 3 years earlier for menometrorrhagia and uterine leiomyomas; she had no other comorbidities and was not taking medications.

On examination, vital signs were normal. Prominent jugular venous distention and bilateral lower extremity edema were present. Laboratory tests and coagulation studies were unremarkable, and the D-dimer level was within normal limits.

Transthoracic echocardiography showed a large, highly mobile mass extending from the inferior vena cava into the right atrium and through the tricuspid valve into the right ventricle, causing severe functional tricuspid stenosis. The mass was stalk-free and nonadherent to the chamber walls. There was severe right ventric

title: Epipericardial Fat Necrosis: A Case Report and a Review of the Literature
case_summary:
A 22-year-old healthy man presented with a 2-day history of left-sided chest pain that was constant, "stinging," rated 7-8/10 on the VAS, and worsened when lying down or with inspiration; the pain radiated to his left shoulder. He reported shortness of breath and an increased resting heart rate but no cough, fever, or other systemic symptoms. On initial examination by the community physician, he was slightly hypertensive (159/92 mm?Hg), tachycardic (102 beats/min), and tachypneic (20 breaths/min), with possibly diminished breath sounds on the left. Point-of-care CRP was 23 mg/L (<5). ECG showed sinus rhythm at 92 bpm, Q waves in aVF and V3-V6, and peaked T waves in V2-V6. He was referred for chest X-ray and admitted for further evaluation.

On arrival at the ED, vital signs were unchanged. Laboratory studies revealed:  
? CRP 31 mg/L (<5)  
? D-dimer 0.8 mg/L (<0.5)  
? Troponin I <10 ng/L (<10)  
? High-sensitivity troponin <3 ng/L (<47)  
? NT-proBNP 35 ng/L (<85)  
Venous blood gas

title: Recurrent Intra-articular Synovial Hemangioma - A Case Report
case_summary:
A 10-year-old girl presented with a 3-year history of recurrent swelling, pain, and deformity of her right knee. She had undergone excision of a similar swelling at another center and remained asymptomatic for one year before the swelling recurred. On examination, there was a soft, 3?4-cm lump in the suprapatellar region and a fixed flexion deformity of 10 degrees.

Plain radiographs of the knee showed a poorly defined soft-tissue density over the infrapatellar fat pad without joint changes. Magnetic resonance imaging revealed a lobulated hyperintense lesion involving Hoffa's fat pad, extending from the anterior margin of the patella to the anterior cruciate ligament and menisci, with mild cortical irregularity and subchondral marrow edema in the patellofemoral joint. These MRI features were suggestive of a hemangioma or a venous malformation.

title: Epithelioid hepatic angiomyolipoma in pregnancy: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: A 24-year-old woman, at 15 weeks of her second pregnancy, presented with a 4-5-month history of right upper-quadrant pain and nausea. She had no significant medical or surgical history and had used norethisterone two years earlier. An abdominal ultrasound at 11 weeks' gestation revealed an 8?6 cm heterogeneous lesion in segment III of the liver with internal vascularity. Laboratory studies--including complete blood count, liver-function tests, and hepatitis B and C serologies--were within normal limits. MRI deferred during pregnancy was obtained three months postpartum because of recurrent pain. MRI showed a well-defined, 9.1?7.5 cm exophytic lesion in segment III that was hyperintense on T2-weighted imaging, demonstrated homogeneous arterial-phase enhancement post-contrast, and remained isointense to slightly hyperintense in portal-venous and delayed phases without washout. A 12 mm gallstone was also noted. Serum alpha-fetoprotein and other preoperative tests were within reference ran

title: Liver metastasis as the initial clinical manifestation of sublingual gland adenoid cystic carcinoma: A case report
case_summary:
A 51-year-old Chinese woman was referred after a routine health examination detected a hepatic mass. She was asymptomatic.  
History of present illness: An incidental, painless right hepatic-lobe mass was noted on imaging.  
Past medical history: Unremarkable.  
Physical examination: No abnormalities.  
Laboratory studies: Serum alpha-fetoprotein, carcinoembryonic antigen, and carbohydrate antigen 19-9 levels were normal.  
Ultrasound of the liver showed a 3.8 ? 3.6 cm hypoechoic lesion with well-defined borders in the right anterior lobe; Doppler imaging revealed internal vascular flow.  
Contrast-enhanced CT scan demonstrated a 4.1 cm low-density, well-circumscribed mass in the anterior-superior segment of the right lobe, with heterogeneous enhancement and a central ring of enhancement; the right hepatic artery coursed through the lesion, the adjacent portal vein branch was abutted, and there was mild intrahepatic bile-duct dilation.

title: Complications of interosseous infusion resulting in a diagnostic dilemma
case_summary:
A 58-year-old obese woman was brought to the emergency department with a 2-3-day history of abdominal pain, fever, decreased oral intake, and oliguria. She had a history of hypertension managed with medication. On arrival she was confused, tachypneic, and tachycardic. Several minutes later, she developed pulseless electrical activity and underwent cardiopulmonary resuscitation according to ACLS protocols. Peripheral vascular access was difficult despite multiple attempts; resuscitation included adrenaline, endotracheal intubation, and chest compressions.  

Initial laboratory studies showed leukocytosis, an elevated C-reactive protein level, and marked hyperglycemia in a patient without a prior diagnosis of diabetes. A working diagnosis of septic shock was made, and broad-spectrum antibiotics were started and then tailored to culture sensitivities. A femoral arterial line was placed for monitoring and infusions. title: Behcet's Disease Presenting as Pancolonic Ulcerations in a Patient With Kikuchi-Fujimoto Disease: A Rare Gastrointestinal Manifestation
case_summary: A 24-year-old man with a history of Kikuchi-Fujimoto disease diagnosed at age 13 and a recent dural venous sinus thrombosis on apixaban presented after an episode of syncope. He reported 1 week of nonbloody vomiting, diarrhea, and blood in stools, accompanied by mild lower abdominal pain, loss of appetite, and recurrent oral ulcers. On examination, he was febrile, tachycardic, and tender in the lower abdominal quadrant. Initial laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 5.7 g/dL (later dropping to 4.8 g/dL despite transfusion), platelets 334,000/?L, white blood cell count 19,000/?L with an elevated band count, elevated international normalized ratio, and mildly increased AST and ALT levels. An abdominal and pelvic CT scan revealed multifocal high-density areas throughout the small and large bowel, suggesting acute hemorrhage, and a 3.7 cm incidental liver lesion.
